**Recognition** is the cognitive-level process of identifying and interpreting the perceived stimulus using memory (e.g., recognizing the same icon as a "**Wi-Fi**" symbol). It is **top-down**, slower, and knowledge-dependent.
**HMI link:** A graphical button must be both *visible* (perception) and *understandable* (recognition). Visibility requires adequate contrast and size; understandability requires culturally correct iconography.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** State **Weber's Law** and write the Weber fraction for **loudness**.

**Model Answer:**
Weber's Law states that the *just-noticeable difference* ($\Delta I$) is a constant fraction $k$ of the original stimulus intensity $I$:
$$
\frac{\Delta I}{I} = k
$$
For **loudness**, the Weber fraction is approximately $k \approx 0.10$–$0.15$. Hence, if a sound is at 50 dB, an increase of ~5–7 dB is the minimum that a listener can reliably detect. **HMI implication:** Audio alert volume steps should be sized in proportion to current level (logarithmic scaling) rather than linearly.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Module 1 ESE Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **human perception pipeline** from stimulus to recognition. (7 Marks)
**(b)** Apply **Gestalt principles** to critique the layout of a hypothetical user interface containing 12 buttons in a 3×4 grid for a medical infusion pump. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Perception Pipeline (7 marks) — Stepwise**

1. **Stimulus:** Physical energy (light, sound, touch) impinges on the sensory organ.
2. **Reception & Transduction:** Photoreceptors (rods, cones), hair cells (cochlea), or mechanoreceptors (skin) convert energy to graded receptor potentials.
3. **Neural Encoding:** Spike trains travel along dedicated neural pathways (optic nerve ~1×10⁶ fibers).
4. **Thalamic Relay & Filtering:** All senses (except smell) synapse in the thalamus; attention acts as a filter.
5. **Primary Cortical Processing:** V1 detects edges/orientation; A1 detects pitch; S1 detects pressure.
6. **Feature Integration & Gestalt Grouping:** Brain binds features into unified percepts via Proximity, Similarity, Closure.
7. **Recognition:** Activated long-term-memory schema identifies the percept; meaning is assigned.
8. **Decision & Response:** Motor cortex executes the appropriate action.

*Examiner scoring key:*
- 1 mark per pipeline stage above; 1 additional mark for an accurately labeled block diagram.

**(b) Gestalt Critique of the 3×4 Infusion-Pump Grid (7 marks)**

| **Gestalt Principle** | **Layout Flaw in 3×4 Grid** | **Suggested Fix** |
|---|---|---|
| Proximity | All buttons equally spaced → all feel equally important. | Group Start/Stop; Alarm Ack; Silence; numeric keypad with extra spacing. |
| Similarity | All buttons look identical → critical "**Emergency Stop**" blends with routine keys. | Make Emergency Stop **red, large, and mushroom-shaped**. |
| Closure / Figure-Ground | Boundary-free flat surface → errors of mis-clicks rise under stress. | Use raised bezels, contrasting borders, drop shadows. |
| Common Region | No grouping by region → users search linearly. | Enclose "**Rate**," "**Volume**," "**Time**" groups in distinct bordered regions. |
| Continuity | A 3×4 grid implies equal reading direction (L→R, top→bottom). | Place **most-used functions along expected scan path** (top-left). |
| Common Fate (motion) | No visual feedback for active infusions. | Animate / pulse the running infusion button. |

*Examiner scoring key:* 1 mark per principle correctly identified with a concrete HMI recommendation.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

### **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Module 1 ESE Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** Derive the expression for **Fitts' Law** and use it to estimate the **movement time** for a finger to tap a 12 mm wide target located 96 mm away. Assume $a = 50$ ms, $b = 100$ ms/bit. (7 Marks)
**(b)** Explain the role of **bottom-up** and **top-down** processing in human recognition, with a real-world HMI example for each. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Fitts' Law Derivation & Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Index of Difficulty (ID):**
$$
ID = \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)
$$
$$
ID = \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{96}{12} + 1\right) = \log_{2}(9)
$$
$$
ID = \log_{2}(9) = \frac{\ln 9}{\ln 2} = \frac{2.1972}{0.6931} \approx 3.17\;\text{bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Movement Time (MT):**
$$
MT = a + b \cdot ID
$$
$$
MT = 0.050 + 0.100 \times 3.17 = 0.050 + 0.317
$$
$$
MT \approx 0.367\;\text{seconds}
$$

**Result:** The user will need approximately **367 ms** to tap the target.

**Step 3 — Design implication:**
If we double the target width to 24 mm (keeping $D = 96$):
$$
ID' = \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{96}{24} + 1\right) = \log_{2}(5) \approx 2.32\;\text{bits}
$$
$$
MT' = 0.050 + 0.100(2.32) = 0.282\;\text{s}
$$
A **2× larger target** reduces tap time by **~23%** — strong evidence for "**fat-finger-friendly**" touch targets.

*Examiner scoring key:* 2 marks for correct formula; 2 marks for ID; 2 marks for MT; 1 mark for design discussion.

**(b) Bottom-Up vs Top-Down Processing in Recognition (7 marks)**

| **Processing Type** | **Definition** | **HMI Real-World Example** |
|---|---|---|
| **Bottom-Up** | Data-driven; perception built purely from sensory features. | A new user recognizes a red square as the **Universal Stop** sign because of the *color* and *shape* features alone. |
| **Top-Down** | Knowledge-driven; expectations from prior experience complete the percept. | A power-user sees a **floppy-disk icon** in Microsoft Word and immediately recognizes it as "**Save**", despite floppy disks being obsolete for 30 years. |
| **Combined Interaction** | The two streams converge to form a single, robust percept. | A user glances at a map UI: bottom-up features (color gradient, contour lines) are interpreted top-down as "**elevation map of Kerala**" using prior geographic knowledge. |

*Examiner scoring key:* 1 mark for clear definitions; 4 marks for the two example categories with correct mechanisms; 2 marks for the combined-interaction synthesis.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Conflating perception with recognition.** Examiners explicitly test this distinction. Saying "seeing an icon is recognition" will lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Writing Weber's Law without units or context.** Always state the *modality* (brightness, loudness, weight) and the *value* of the constant.
> 3. **Forgetting the "+1" in Fitts' Law.** The Shannon form $\log_2(D/W + 1)$ is the only one accepted in KTU 2024; the older $D/W$ form loses marks.
> 4. **Listing Gestalt laws without engineering consequences.** Examiners want *applied* answers, e.g., "**Closure is violated, hence the Save button lacks a clear boundary, causing mis-taps**."
> 5. **Confusing Stevens' Power Law with Fechner's Log Law.** Use Stevens for *brightness* ($b \approx 0.33$) and *heaviness* ($b \approx 1.45$); Fechner only for the idealized *log* model.
> 6. **Missing the Snellen/visual-angle calculation steps.** Examiners award partial credit per algebraic step; skipping the small-angle approximation loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Perception** = sensory registration (bottom-up, ~50–150 ms); **Recognition** = cognitive identification (top-down, ~200–500 ms).
- **Snellen acuity** = 1 arc-minute; design UI text at $\geq$ 30 arc-minutes visual angle.
- **Weber's Law:** $\Delta I / I = k$ — JND scales with stimulus magnitude.
- **Fechner's Law:** $S = k \log(I/I_0)$ — perceived magnitude is logarithmic.
- **Stevens' Power Law:** $S = a I^b$ — gives modality-specific exponents.
- **Flicker Fusion Frequency:** 50–70 Hz — set display refresh ≥ 60 Hz.
- **Fitts' Law:** $MT = a + b \log_2(D/W + 1)$ — bigger targets → faster targeting.
- **Reaction Time hierarchy:** Auditory (~150 ms) < Visual (~200 ms) < Cognitive (~300 ms).
- **Field of View:** ~200° horizontal; foveal region ~2° — important for VR/HUD design.
- **Gestalt Laws (7 main):** Proximity, Similarity, Closure, Continuity, Common Region, Figure-Ground, Common Fate.
- **Recognition models:** Template, Prototype, Feature-analysis (Pandemonium), Interactive Activation.
- **Norman's Two Gulfs:** Gulf of Execution (intention → action) and Gulf of Evaluation (perception → interpretation).
- **HMI Rule of Thumb:** Make all critical stimuli at least **2 × JND** above background; design icons that are **both** perceptually salient and culturally recognizable.
- **Cultural caveat:** Icons and colors are *not* universal — 🚫 = "**Stop**" in the West, "**Good Luck**" in East Asia. Always localize.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Psychology of Users
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT831 - HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Psychology of Users

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)** and the **KTU 2024 OEBRT831 syllabus**, *Psychology of Users* refers to the systematic study of the cognitive, perceptual, emotional, and behavioral characteristics of human beings as they interact with interactive systems. It encompasses the mental processes—**attention, perception, memory, learning, reasoning, problem-solving, and motor control**—that govern how a user understands, operates, and responds to a designed interface.

The discipline draws heavily from **Cognitive Psychology** and **Ergonomics**, forming the scientific foundation upon which usable, accessible, and efficient interfaces are engineered. As per the course outcome **CO1** of the 2024 scheme, the student must be able to *"analyze the psychological principles underlying human-machine interaction"*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The psychology of users is a prerequisite pillar for understanding *usability heuristics, user-centered design, and interaction models*. Every interface design decision is ultimately a hypothesis about how the user's mind works.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **tourist standing at a ticket vending machine at a railway station** in a foreign country. The tourist carries a **mental model**—a private, internal understanding—of what a ticket machine *should* look like (perhaps from a previous country). The designer of the machine, however, has built it according to a *system image* (button labels, icons, screen flow).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Translation Problem:** Psychology of Users is essentially the study of the *translator* sitting between two very different "languages"—the language of the **Human Mind** (intuition, pattern recognition, fuzzy thinking) and the language of the **Machine** (precise, binary, deterministic logic). A good HCI designer is bilingual.

When the tourist presses a wrong button, it is rarely because the user is "stupid." It is because the **designer's model of the user** failed to predict the tourist's actual mental model. The *Psychology of Users* provides the formal vocabulary to predict, model, and design around this gap.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

The following empirical constants from cognitive psychology are treated as **laws** within HCI:

| Symbol | Constant / Law | Value | Originator |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **7 ± 2** | Miller's Magical Number | Short-term memory chunk capacity | George A. Miller (1956) |
| **150 ms** | Human reaction time baseline | Simple visual-motor reaction | Card, Moran & Newell (1983) |
| **100 ms** | Perceptual fusion threshold | Below this, events feel simultaneous | Sensory psychology |
| **50–200 ms** | Cognitive (decision) cycle | Time per mental operation | GOMS / Model Human Processor |

> [!TIP]
> These numbers are **not** approximate guesses. They are the result of thousands of controlled laboratory experiments. Memorize them—KTU frequently tests these empirical values as 1- or 2-mark items.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Triad of User Psychology

The psychology of any user interacting with a system can be decomposed into three interlocking components. A deep understanding of each is **mandatory** for Module 1.

#### A. Cognitive Processes (The "Thinking" Layer)
- **Perception:** How raw sensory data is interpreted. Governed by Gestalt principles (proximity, similarity, closure, continuity).
- **Attention:** Selective concentration on aspects of the environment. Limited resource; bottleneck for information processing.
- **Memory:** Divided into Sensory Memory → Short-Term Memory (Working Memory) → Long-Term Memory.
- **Learning & Reasoning:** Schema formation, analogical reasoning, mental model construction.

#### B. Physical / Motor Processes (The "Doing" Layer)
- Motor control, dexterity, response time, fatigue, biomechanics of the hand/eye.
- Fitts's Law governs aimed movements; Steering Law governs constrained movements.

#### C. Emotional / Affective Processes (The "Feeling" Layer)
- Frustration, satisfaction, trust, flow, cognitive load stress.
- Now formally included in **Affective Computing** (Picard, MIT).

### 2.2 The Model Human Processor (MHP)

Proposed by **Stuart Card, Thomas Moran, and Allen Newell (1983)** in the seminal work *The Psychology of Human-Computer Interaction*, the **Model Human Processor (MHP)** is the cornerstone cognitive architecture for HCI.

- The MHP posits three interacting processors:
  1. **Perceptual Processor** (handles sensory input).
  2. **Cognitive Processor** (handles thinking, memory).
  3. **Motor Processor** (handles physical action).
- Connected by three memories: **Sensory Store, Working Memory, Long-Term Memory**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why MHP matters for KTU:** It is the *theoretical justification* for predictive formulas like Fitts's Law and Hick's Law. Every time you predict task time using a formula, you are implicitly using the MHP.

### 2.3 Norman's Action Cycle (The Seven Stages)

**Donald Norman (1988)** in *The Design of Everyday Things* described user interaction as a **7-stage cycle** divided into two gulfs:

**Execution Side (Doing):**
1. Forming the Goal
2. Forming the Intention
3. Specifying an Action
4. Executing the Action

**Evaluation Side (Checking):**
5. Perceiving the System State
6. Interpreting the State
7. Evaluating the Outcome

> [!NOTE]
> **Gulf of Execution:** The gap between the user's *intention* and the *physical actions* the system allows. Narrow this gap → better usability.
> **Gulf of Evaluation:** The gap between the system's *actual state* and the user's *understanding* of that state. Narrow this gap → better feedback.

### 2.4 Mental Models and Conceptual Models

- **Mental Model:** The user's internal, personal, often-incomplete model of how a system works.
- **Conceptual Model:** The designer's external model of how the system *should* be understood.
- **System Image:** What the system actually presents (UI, manuals, icons).
- The fundamental HCI rule: **The system image should accurately convey the conceptual model**, so that the user can construct a correct mental model.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!WARNING]
> The vertical pipe symbol is replaced below with `\vert` and `\mid` to comply with markdown table integrity rules.

| Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Fitts's Law** | $T = a + b \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | $T$ = move time (s); $D$ = distance to target (m); $W$ = target width (m); $a, b$ = empirically fitted constants (s, s/bit) | Predicts pointing/selection time. Drives **button size, menu placement, and touch-target design** in mobile/web UIs. |
| **Hick's Law** | $T = b \cdot \log_{2}(n + 1)$ | $T$ = decision time (s); $n$ = number of equally probable choices; $b$ = empirical constant (~150–200 ms/bit) | Predicts decision time given choice count. Drives **menu depth vs. breadth** trade-offs. |
| **Steering Law** | $T = a + b \cdot \dfrac{D}{W}$ | $T$ = time to traverse a tunnel of length $D$ and width $W$ | Predicts time for constrained motion (scrollbars, sliders, hierarchical menus). |
| **Miller's Law** | $\text{Chunk Capacity} = 7 \pm 2$ | Applies to Short-Term Memory only | Drives **grouping of menu items, phone-number chunking (3-3-4), navigation limits**. |
| **Power Law of Practice** | $T_n = T_1 \cdot n^{-\alpha}$ | $T_n$ = time on trial $n$; $T_1$ = first-trial time; $\alpha \approx 0.2\text{–}0.6$ | Predicts learning curves; used in **training simulations and HCI modeling tools (GOMS)**. |

### 2.6 Why This Matters in Engineering

- **Mobile UI Design:** Fitts's Law tells Apple/Google engineers why the thumb-reach "reachability zone" matters—smaller targets far from the thumb increase error and time.
- **Web Design:** Hick's Law explains why the **Hamburger menu** (collapsed choices) trades off discoverability for decision speed.
- **Automotive UX:** Steering Law informs the design of in-car touchscreen sliders for HVAC control.
- **Avionics / Medical Devices:** Miller's 7±2 governs how many cockpit/clinical indicators can be grouped on a single display.
- **AI & Chatbots:** Mental models now guide the design of **prompt-engineering affordances** and LLM conversational UIs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since *Psychology of Users* is a conceptual core topic, the "derivations" below refer to the **logical / mathematical justification of the predictive laws** introduced in Section 2. Each algebraic transition is shown explicitly.

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation of Fitts's Law

Fitts's Law was first published by **Paul Fitts (1954)** as an **information-theoretic analogy** to Shannon's channel capacity theorem. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step derivation of the Index of Difficulty ($ID$).

**Step 1 — Shannon's Channel Capacity (1948).**
Shannon defined the maximum information rate through a noisy channel as:

$$C = B \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{S}{N} + 1\right)$$

where $B$ is bandwidth and $S/N$ is the signal-to-noise ratio. The information transmitted is:

$$I = C \cdot T$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Analogy.**
Fitts analogized:
- The **target width $W$** corresponds to the **noise level $N$** (uncertainty in endpoint).
- The **distance $D$** corresponds to the **signal amplitude $S$** (movement magnitude).

**Step 3 — Map Variables to Information Theory.**
Substituting $S = D$ and $N = W$ into the Shannon information expression:

$$I = \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) \quad \text{[bits]}$$

This quantity is called the **Index of Difficulty (ID)**.

**Step 4 — Linearize Movement Time with ID.**
Empirically (and theoretically by Fitts), movement time is *linearly proportional* to the information processed:

$$T = a + b \cdot ID$$

**Step 5 — Combine Step 3 and Step 4.**

$$T = a + b \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$$

This is the **canonical Fitts's Law** equation.

**Step 6 — Define Throughput (Index of Performance).**
The constant $b$ (slope) has units of *seconds per bit* and is called the **Index of Performance (IP)**:

$$IP = \frac{ID}{T} \quad \text{[bits/s]}$$

Typical human IP is **4–10 bits/s** for a mouse and **3–6 bits/s** for a finger on a touchscreen.

**Step 7 — Logical Interpretation of the "+1" Term.**
The "+1" prevents $ID \to -\infty$ as $D \to 0$ (infinitely small distance to an infinitely wide target). Without it, Fitts's Law would predict negative movement times, which is physically impossible. This is mathematically guaranteed by Shannon's original formulation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A *larger* $W$ (wider target) or a *smaller* $D$ (closer target) **decreases $ID$** and therefore **reduces task time**. This is why *Material Design* recommends a minimum touch target of **48 × 48 dp** and why iOS uses 44 × 44 pt.

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation of Hick's Law

**Step 1 — Define Choice Uncertainty.**
For $n$ equally probable choices, the probability of each is $p = 1/n$. Each choice carries information:

$$H = \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{1}{p}\right) = \log_{2}(n) \quad \text{[bits]}$$

**Step 2 — Add the Mandatory Null Choice.**
Empirically, even with one choice ($n=1$), users take a small but non-zero decision time (the "I am ready" delay). Shifting $n$ to $n+1$ accounts for this:

$$H = \log_{2}(n + 1) \quad \text{[bits]}$$

**Step 3 — Linearize Decision Time with Information.**
Hick (1952) and Hyman (1953) found decision time is a linear function of $H$:

$$T = b \cdot H$$

**Step 4 — Final Form.**

$$T = b \cdot \log_{2}(n + 1)$$

**Step 5 — Practical Implication.**
- Doubling the number of menu items from 8 to 16 adds only **$\log_2(2) = 1$ bit**, i.e., one constant $b$ of additional decision time.
- However, going from 8 to 256 (doubling thrice) adds **3 bits**, i.e., 450–600 ms of extra time.
- This non-linear cost justifies **chunking and progressive disclosure** in modern UIs.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cognitive Load Across Memory Types

| Memory Type | Capacity | Duration | Encoding Mode | HCI Implication |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sensory Registers** | Large (raw data) | ~250 ms (visual), ~150 ms (auditory) | Modal (visual, auditory, haptic) | Tooltips must be < 250 ms; animation cues must align with persistence. |
| **Short-Term (Working) Memory** | $7 \pm 2$ chunks | ~15–30 s without rehearsal | Primarily phonological; also visuospatial | PIN entry, CAPTCHA, and verification codes must respect 7±2. |
| **Long-Term Memory** | Effectively unlimited | Years to lifetime | Semantic; episodic; procedural | User's *expertise* lives here. Affects learnability vs. efficiency trade-off. |

> [!TIP]
> KTU frequently tests the **specific durations** of memory types. Rehearse: Sensory = ~250 ms, STM = 15–30 s, LTM = permanent.

### 3.4 Why-How Analytical Breakdown: Norman's Action Cycle

| Stage | User Cognitive Operation | System Must Provide |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. Forming the Goal | High-level intent | Discoverable entry points |
| 2. Forming the Intention | Choosing strategy | Clear navigation |
| 3. Specifying the Action | Sequencing inputs | Predictable controls |
| 4. Executing the Action | Motor execution | Ergonomic layout (Fitts's) |
| 5. Perceiving the State | Sensory capture | Timely feedback (< 1 s) |
| 6. Interpreting the State | Pattern recognition | Meaningful visualization |
| 7. Evaluating Outcome | Compare to goal | Confirmation / undo |

> [!WARNING]
> Common student mistake: treating all 7 stages as equivalent. The **Execution Gulf (1–4)** and **Evaluation Gulf (5–7)** are *separated* in time. A system can have zero Execution Gulf and yet a wide Evaluation Gulf (e.g., a fast command-line tool that gives no output).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Model Human Processor (MHP) Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> Node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `procP`, `memSTM`) to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguard. Labels are kept as raw uppercase text, free of markdown formatting.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ExtStim[EXTERNAL STIMULUS] -->|Visual Audio Haptic| Sens[PERCEPTUAL PROCESSOR]
    Sens -->|Encode| WMem[WORKING MEMORY]
    LMem[LONG TERM MEMORY] <-->|Retrieve Store| WMem
    WMem --> Cog[COGNITIVE PROCESSOR]
    Cog -->|Motor Commands| MotProc[MOTOR PROCESSOR]
    MotProc -->|Physical Action| SysResp[SYSTEM RESPONSE]
    SysResp -->|Feedback| ExtStim
    Cog -.->|Decisions| LMem
    WMem -.->|Temporary Storage| Sens
```

**Reading Guide:**
- The **outer cycle** (ExtStim → Sens → WMem → Cog → MotProc → SysResp → ExtStim) is the **perception-action loop**.
- The **dotted lines** indicate **storage / retrieval operations** between cognitive states.
- The **processor cycle time** (≈ 70 ms perceptual, ≈ 50 ms cognitive, ≈ 70 ms motor) defines the **fastest possible interaction cadence**.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Norman's Seven Stages of Action

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1[STAGE 1: FORMING THE GOAL] --> G2[STAGE 2: FORMING THE INTENTION]
    G2 --> G3[STAGE 3: SPECIFYING AN ACTION]
    G3 --> G4[STAGE 4: EXECUTING THE ACTION]
    G4 -->|User acts on system| G5[STAGE 5: PERCEIVING THE SYSTEM STATE]
    G5 --> G6[STAGE 6: INTERPRETING THE STATE]
    G6 --> G7[STAGE 7: EVALUATING THE OUTCOME]
    G7 -.->|Outcome vs. Goal| G1
    subgraph EXECUTION GULF
        G1
        G2
        G3
        G4
    end
    subgraph EVALUATION GULF
        G5
        G6
        G7
    end
```

**Reading Guide:**
- The **top half** (Execution Gulf) is the *Doing* side: where the user translates thoughts into physical action.
- The **bottom half** (Evaluation Gulf) is the *Checking* side: where the user interprets the system's response.
- The **dashed feedback line** from Stage 7 back to Stage 1 closes the action loop and represents iterative learning.

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Mental Model / Conceptual Model / System Image Alignment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Des[DESIGNERS CONCEPTUAL MODEL] -->|Encoded as| SysImg[SYSTEM IMAGE UI DOCS MANUALS]
    SysImg -->|User infers| UsrMM[USERS MENTAL MODEL]
    Des -.->|Should match| UsrMM
    UsrMM -->|Drives| Behavior[USER BEHAVIOR AND ERRORS]
    SysImg --> Behavior
```

**Reading Guide:**
- The designer holds the **Conceptual Model** (an idealized truth about the system).
- The user *never* sees the conceptual model directly. They infer a **Mental Model** from the **System Image** (the interface).
- A good HCI design ensures the **System Image is a faithful projection** of the Conceptual Model. When the lines between Designer and User do not align, **user errors and frustration** result.

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Cognitive Memory Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Stim[RAW STIMULUS] -->|≤ 250 ms| SMem[SENSORY MEMORY]
    SMem -->|Attention filter| WMem[WORKING MEMORY 7 plus minus 2]
    WMem -->|Rehearsal Encoding| LMem[LONG TERM MEMORY UNLIMITED]
    LMem -->|Retrieval| WMem
    WMem -->|Decay 15 to 30 s| Decay[(Forgotten)]
```

**Reading Guide:**
- The single forward path (Stim → SMem → WMem → LMem) is the *encoding* path.
- The reverse path (LMem → WMem) is the *retrieval* path that brings expertise into active problem-solving.
- The decay node represents the **forgetting curve** of Ebbinghaus, which HCI designers counter with **microcopy, help text, and progressive disclosure**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Bank, Module 1]**
*(3 Marks)*
**Explain the Model Human Processor (MHP) proposed by Card, Moran, and Newell. List its three processors and the cycle times associated with each.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Model Human Processor (MHP)** is a cognitive architecture proposed by **Card, Moran, and Newell (1983)** in their foundational book *The Psychology of Human-Computer Interaction*. It models the human as an information-processing system composed of three interacting subsystems:

1. **Perceptual Processor:** Handles sensory input (visual, auditory, haptic). Perceptual cycle time ≈ **100 ms**.
2. **Cognitive Processor:** Handles memory operations, decision-making, and problem solving. Cognitive cycle time ≈ **70 ms**.
3. **Motor Processor:** Handles physical action execution. Motor cycle time ≈ **70 ms**.

The three processors communicate via three memory stores: **Sensory Registers, Working (Short-Term) Memory, and Long-Term Memory**. The MHP provides the theoretical foundation for predictive human-performance models such as **GOMS (Goals, Operators, Methods, Selection rules)**, which engineers use to estimate task completion time in interface design.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank, Module 1]**
*(3 Marks)*
**State and explain Fitts's Law. Discuss its significance in graphical user interface (GUI) design.**

**Model Answer:**

**Fitts's Law (1954)** quantifies the time required to move a pointer (e.g., mouse cursor, finger) to a target. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$T = a + b \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$$

where $T$ is movement time, $D$ is the distance from starting point to target center, $W$ is the target width, and $a, b$ are empirically fitted constants.

**Significance in GUI Design:**
- **Larger targets** reduce selection time → justifies Apple's 44 pt and Material Design's 48 dp minimum touch-target guidelines.
- **Smaller distances** reduce time → justifies placing frequently used menu items near the user's dominant hand or thumb's natural arc.
- **Corners and screen edges** are effectively *infinitely wide targets* (the pointer cannot overshoot) → OS menus are deliberately placed there.
- Fitts's Law is the **engineering basis for hit-region optimization** in icon design, button placement, and crosshair targeting in games.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper Pattern, Module 1]**

**(a)** *Explain in detail Donald Norman's Seven Stages of Action model. Clearly differentiate between the **Gulf of Execution** and the **Gulf of Evaluation**, providing a real-world example of each from a smartphone interface.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *With neat diagrams and appropriate equations, derive Fitts's Law from Shannon's information-theoretic channel capacity formula. Show how the Index of Difficulty (ID) and Index of Performance (IP) are computed, and explain the physical meaning of the "+1" term in the formula.* **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

Donald Norman's **Seven Stages of Action** model, introduced in *The Design of Everyday Things* (1988), describes the cognitive stages a user traverses when interacting with any system. The seven stages are:

**Execution Side (Stages 1–4):**
1. **Forming the Goal:** The user formulates a high-level objective. *Example: "I want to send a WhatsApp message to Arun."*
2. **Forming the Intention:** The user decides *what general action* to take. *Example: "I will use the Messages app."*
3. **Specifying the Action:** The user identifies the *exact sequence* of inputs. *Example: "Tap Arun's chat thread, then tap the message field."*
4. **Executing the Action:** The user performs the physical input. *Example: Finger taps on the screen.*

**Evaluation Side (Stages 5–7):**
5. **Perceiving the System State:** The user observes the system's response. *Example: Sees the keyboard appear.*
6. **Interpreting the State:** The user decodes what the response means. *Example: "Ah, the cursor is now in the message field."*
7. **Evaluating the Outcome:** The user compares actual state to goal. *Example: "Yes, I can now type the message."*

**Gulf of Execution:** This is the gap between *what the user wants to do* (Stages 1–3) and *the physical actions the system permits* (Stage 4). A **wide Gulf of Execution** means the user struggles to translate their intention into a sequence of system commands.

*Smartphone Example:* A *narrow* Gulf of Execution is found in the iOS Control Center—swiping down from the top-right corner gives instant access to Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, brightness, etc. A *wide* Gulf of Execution is found in a deep Android settings menu requiring 5 taps to reach "Developer Options."

**Gulf of Evaluation:** This is the gap between *the system's actual state* (Stage 5) and *the user's understanding* of that state (Stages 6–7). A **wide Gulf of Evaluation** means the user cannot easily tell what the system has done or is doing.

*Smartphone Example:* A *narrow* Gulf of Evaluation is exemplified by the iOS AirPods pairing popup—the moment the lid is opened, an animation confirms the connection. A *wide* Gulf of Evaluation is found in a TV remote where pressing "Source" produces no immediate on-screen feedback, leaving the user uncertain whether the command was received.

**Design Rule:** *A good interface minimizes BOTH gulfs simultaneously.*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1:** Shannon (1948) defined the maximum information that can be transmitted across a channel of bandwidth $B$ and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$ in time $T$ as:

$$I = B \cdot T \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{S}{N} + 1\right)$$

**Step 2:** Fitts (1954) made the **information-theoretic analogy** that human motor movement is equivalent to signal transmission through a noisy channel:
- The **signal $S$** corresponds to the **distance $D$** the user must traverse.
- The **noise $N$** corresponds to the **target width $W$** (uncertainty in the endpoint).
- The **bandwidth $B$** is absorbed into the empirical constant $b$.

**Step 3:** Substituting $S = D$ and $N = W$ into Shannon's equation and lumping $B$ into $b$:

$$I = \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) \quad \text{[bits]}$$

This is the **Index of Difficulty (ID)**.

**Step 4:** Fitts's empirical law states that movement time $T$ is *linearly proportional* to the information processed:

$$T = a + b \cdot ID$$

**Step 5:** Substituting ID from Step 3:

$$T = a + b \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$$

**Step 6:** The **Index of Performance (IP)** is defined as:

$$IP = \frac{ID}{T} \quad \text{[bits per second]}$$

Typical IP for a mouse is **4–10 bits/s**; for a finger touch, **3–6 bits/s**.

**Step 7:** **Physical meaning of "+1":** The "+1" prevents the Index of Difficulty from going negative when $D$ is very small relative to $W$ (e.g., the user is already inside the target area). Mathematically, it ensures $ID \geq 0$ for all $D, W > 0$, which guarantees $T \geq a > 0$, consistent with the non-negativity of physical time. Empirically, it accounts for the *residual* targeting error that always remains even with perfect aiming, ensuring the model is well-posed.

**Conclusion:** Fitts's Law is thus derived as a direct **information-theoretic analogy** to Shannon's channel capacity theorem, with movement time increasing logarithmically with task difficulty.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Pattern, Module 1]**

**(a)** *Discuss the differences between **Sensory Memory, Short-Term Memory, and Long-Term Memory**. For each, state its capacity, duration, and encoding mode. Explain how Miller's "Magical Number Seven, Plus or Minus Two" applies to interface design.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Explain Hick's Law and the Power Law of Practice with their mathematical formulations. For each law, provide **two real-world UI design applications** that demonstrate the principle.* **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

The human memory system is commonly modeled as a **three-stage information-processing architecture** introduced by **Atkinson and Shiffrin (1968)**.

**1. Sensory Memory (Sensory Registers):**
- **Capacity:** Very large (effectively unlimited; raw sensory buffer).
- **Duration:** ~250 ms (visual/iconic memory); ~150–300 ms (auditory/echoic memory).
- **Encoding:** Modal — preserves the *sensory modality* of the stimulus.
- **HCI Implication:** Animations, tooltips, and transitional cues must complete their informative function within ~250 ms; otherwise the sensory trace decays and the user loses context.

**2. Short-Term Memory (Working Memory):**
- **Capacity:** **$7 \pm 2$ chunks** (Miller, 1956).
- **Duration:** ~15–30 seconds without active rehearsal.
- **Encoding:** Primarily *phonological* (verbal) and *visuospatial*.
- **HCI Implication:** Verification codes, PIN entries, and on-screen navigation must respect chunking (e.g., phone numbers grouped 4-3-4 or 5-3-3; CAPTCHA strings of 6–8 characters).

**3. Long-Term Memory:**
- **Capacity:** Effectively unlimited.
- **Duration:** Minutes to a lifetime.
- **Encoding:** *Semantic* (meaning-based), *episodic* (event-based), and *procedural* (skill-based).
- **HCI Implication:** User's expertise and prior experience live here. Designers must consider the *knowledge in the head* (LTM) vs. *knowledge in the world* (UI affordances) trade-off (Don Norman).

**Miller's Magical Number $7 \pm 2$ in Interface Design:**
George Miller (1956) showed that the span of immediate recall is approximately **seven items, give or take two**. This finding has two principal applications:

1. **Menu Grouping:** Navigation menus should be chunked into 5–9 top-level items to avoid overwhelming working memory. A 25-item menu should be grouped into 3–5 categories of 5–9 each.
2. **Information Density:** Dashboards, instrument panels, and data tables should display 5–9 primary visual elements at once. If more information is essential, it must be chunked, paginated, or placed behind progressive disclosure.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Hick's Law (Hick, 1952; Hyman, 1953):**
Hick's Law relates the **decision time** to the **number of equally probable choices** available to the user:

$$T = b \cdot \log_{2}(n + 1)$$

where $T$ is decision time, $n$ is the number of choices, and $b$ is an empirical constant (~150–200 ms/bit).

**Two Real-World UI Applications:**

1. **Hamburger Menus in Mobile Apps:** Collapsing 12 navigation items into a hamburger icon reduces the *visible* choice count, lowering $n$ and thus $T$ in the main interface. The trade-off is reduced discoverability.

2. **Search Engines with Auto-Suggest:** Google Search's autocomplete reduces the *effective* $n$ from millions of pages to 5–10 suggestions, dramatically lowering decision time per keystroke.

**Power Law of Practice (Newell & Rosenbloom, 1981):**
This law describes how performance improves with practice. If $T_1$ is the time to perform a task on the first trial and $T_n$ is the time on trial $n$:

$$T_n = T_1 \cdot n^{-\alpha}$$

where $\alpha$ is the **learning rate parameter**, typically between **0.2 and 0.6**, and $n$ is the trial number.

**Two Real-World UI Applications:**

1. **Keyboard Shortcut Disclosure:** Apps like Photoshop or VS Code initially show menu commands (slow path). As the user practices, they switch to keyboard shortcuts (fast path). The Power Law predicts the steep drop in latency with each repetition, which is why such tools progressively *hide* the slow path.

2. **Onboarding Tutorials and Guided Tours:** First-time users take much longer to complete a task (large $T_1$). By trial 5–10, latency drops dramatically. Designers can use this law to estimate *how many onboarding sessions* are needed to reach a target performance threshold.

**Conclusion:** Hick's Law governs *choice-driven* interaction design, while the Power Law of Practice governs *learning-driven* interaction design. Both are derived from the **Model Human Processor** and are essential quantitative tools for predictive HCI modeling.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps in the Psychology of Users topic:**
> 1. **Confusing the Three Memory Types:** Students often interchange "Sensory Memory" and "Short-Term Memory." Remember: *Sensory* is pre-attention, modal, sub-second; *Short-Term* is post-attention, chunked, 7±2, lasts 15–30 s.
> 2. **Forgetting the "+1" in Fitts's Law:** Writing $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W)$ *without* the "+1" is a frequent error that loses 1–2 marks. The "+1" is mathematically and physically essential.
> 3. **Mixing up the Gulfs:** *Gulf of Execution* = user-intent → system-action. *Gulf of Evaluation* = system-state → user-perception. They are **not** interchangeable.
> 4. **Hick's Law units:** Decision time depends on $b$ in **ms/bit** and $\log_2$ must be base-2, not natural log. Wrong base loses the derivation.
> 5. **Citing Miller's Law incorrectly:** The "7±2" applies **only to short-term memory** for **chunked** items. It does not apply to long-term memory or to single, non-chunked digits in the same way.
> 6. **Skipping the MHP link:** When asked to derive Fitts's or Hick's Law, examiners expect a brief mention that the law is **empirically supported by the MHP** (Card, Moran, Newell). Omitting this loses a half-mark on linkage.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Psychology of Users**

- [ ] **Model Human Processor (MHP)** has three processors: Perceptual (≈100 ms), Cognitive (≈70 ms), Motor (≈70 ms).
- [ ] **Three Memory Stores:** Sensory (≤ 250 ms, modal), Short-Term (7±2 chunks, 15–30 s), Long-Term (unlimited, semantic/episodic/procedural).
- [ ] **Miller's Law:** $7 \pm 2$ applies *only* to **chunks in STM**.
- [ ] **Fitts's Law:** $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$. Larger $W$, smaller $D$ → faster $T$. The "+1" is non-negotiable.
- [ ] **Hick's Law:** $T = b \cdot \log_2(n + 1)$. More choices → slower decisions (logarithmically).
- [ ] **Steering Law:** $T = a + b \cdot (D/W)$. Applies to constrained motion (tunnels, sliders).
- [ ] **Power Law of Practice:** $T_n = T_1 \cdot n^{-\alpha}$, with $\alpha \approx 0.2\text{–}0.6$.
- [ ] **Norman's Seven Stages:** 4 Execution stages (Goal → Intention → Specify → Execute) and 3 Evaluation stages (Perceive → Interpret → Evaluate).
- [ ] **Gulf of Execution:** Gap between user intent and system action. **Gulf of Evaluation:** Gap between system state and user understanding.
- [ ] **Mental Model** (user) ≠ **Conceptual Model** (designer) ≠ **System Image** (UI). The System Image must faithfully project the Conceptual Model.
- [ ] **Gestalt Principles:** Proximity, Similarity, Closure, Continuity, Common Region — drive perceptual grouping in UI layouts.
- [ ] **Cognitive Load Theory:** Intrinsic, Extraneous, and Germane load must be balanced. Minimize extraneous load (unnecessary UI clutter).
- [ ] **Affective Computing:** Picard (MIT) — emotion is now a first-class HCI variable. Affect-aware systems adapt to user frustration/fatigue.
- [ ] **Cross-cultural psychology:** User psychology varies by culture (Hofstede's dimensions); HCI designs must localize.
- [ ] **Universal design principle:** Account for users with disabilities, age-related decline, and varying expertise levels.
- [ ] **KTU Hot Keywords:** *MHP, GOMS, Fitts, Hick, Miller, Norman, Gulf, Mental Model, Working Memory, Cognitive Load, Gestalt* — expect at least 2–3 in every ESE paper.

> **End of Notes — Psychology of Users | OEBRT831 | Module 1 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT831 - HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Attention

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Attention

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)** and **Human Machine Interface (HMI)**, **Attention** is formally defined as the cognitive process of selectively concentrating on specific aspects of the environment while temporarily ignoring other perceivable information. It is the mechanism by which the human information-processing system allocates its finite mental resources to the most salient, relevant, or expected stimulus among the vast array of inputs received through sensory channels.

According to **James (1890)**, attention is *"the taking possession of the mind, in clear and vivid form, of one out of what seem several simultaneously possible objects or trains of thought."*

In modern **Cognitive Engineering** and **KTU 2024 HMI syllabus terminology**, attention is treated as a **resource allocation function** governed by two primary mechanisms:
- **Bottom-Up (Stimulus-Driven) Attention** – Exogenous, driven by physical saliency of stimuli.
- **Top-Down (Goal-Driven) Attention** – Endogenous, driven by the user's task, expectations, and prior knowledge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Attention is one of the foundational cognitive constraints in HMI design. Any interface that demands more attentional resources than the human operator possesses will result in **cognitive overload**, increased error rates, and degraded task performance.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are at a noisy railway station trying to hear your friend call your name. Hundreds of people are talking, trains are arriving, announcements blare — yet your brain "zooms in" like a **spotlight on a dark stage** and isolates your friend's voice. That mental spotlight is **Attention**.

In HMI design, this is precisely why a well-designed interface:
- Uses **color, contrast, and motion** to draw the eye (exogenous cues).
- Provides **predictable layouts** so the user knows where to look (endogenous cues).
- Avoids placing **critical alerts** next to decorative animations that steal focus.

## 1.3 Core Properties of Attention

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Properties Used in KTU Board Examinations:**
> - **Selectivity** – Choosing one stimulus over others.
> - **Capacity Limitation** – Limited mental resources (~±7 chunks, Miller's Law).
> - **Vigilance / Sustained Attention** – Maintaining focus over time.
> - **Switching Cost** – Time penalty when shifting focus between tasks.
> - **Automaticity** – Skilled tasks require less attention.
> - **Inattentional Blindness** – Failure to notice an unexpected stimulus when attention is engaged elsewhere.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants

The following are the **standard empirical benchmarks** used in HMI attention studies:

| Metric | Standard Value / Range | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Miller's Magic Number | **7 ± 2 chunks** | Working memory / attention span |
| Foveal Visual Angle | **~2° of arc** | High-acuity attention zone |
| Useful Field of View (UFOV) | **~30° horizontal, ~20° vertical** | Effective attention cone |
| Flicker Fusion Threshold | **~50–60 Hz** | Beyond this, motion appears continuous |
| Attention Switch Cost | **~200–300 ms** | Penalty for task switching |
| Visual Reaction Time (Simple) | **~180–250 ms** | Baseline attentional response |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Attention (KTU Frequently Tested)

1. **Selective Attention** – Ability to focus on a particular stimulus while suppressing others.
2. **Sustained Attention (Vigilance)** – Maintaining focus on a stimulus or task over a prolonged period.
3. **Divided Attention** – Allocating attention to multiple tasks simultaneously (limited capacity).
4. **Focused Attention** – Responding to specific sensory input (e.g., a beep tone).
5. **Alternating Attention** – Switching focus between tasks with different cognitive demands.
6. **Spatial Attention** – Directing attention to a specific location in the visual field.

## 2.2 Major Theoretical Models of Attention (Exhaustive)

### A. Broadbent's Filter Model (1958) — *Early Selection Theory*
- Based on **Donald Broadbent's** work at the **Applied Psychology Unit, Cambridge**.
- All incoming sensory information is held in a brief **sensory buffer** (~0.5 seconds).
- A **selective filter** selects information based on **physical characteristics** (e.g., pitch, loudness, spatial location, color).
- The filter is a **bottleneck** — only one channel passes through.
- The unattended channel is completely blocked (all-or-nothing).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critique:** Cannot explain the **Cocktail Party Effect** — how a person hears their own name in the unattended channel. This led to the **Attenuation Model**.

### B. Treisman's Attenuation Model (1960)
- Anne **Treisman's** modification of Broadbent.
- The unattended channel is **not blocked** but **attenuated** (weakened).
- Attenuated information may still be processed, especially if highly relevant (e.g., one's name).
- Introduces the concept of a **Dictionary Unit** that checks semantic content of both channels.
- Threshold for unattended channel is **lower** than for attended channel.

### C. Deutsch & Deutsch Late Selection Model (1963)
- **All** sensory information is fully processed for **meaning** before selection.
- Selection occurs **after** semantic analysis, just before response.
- Attention chooses the response, not the input.

### D. Feature Integration Theory (FIT) — Treisman & Gelade (1980)
- Visual scene is first decomposed into **elementary features** (color, orientation, motion) processed in parallel.
- A **master map** (saliency map) integrates these features.
- **Focused attention** is required to **bind** features into a single perceptual object.
- A **conjunction search** (e.g., "red AND vertical") requires serial attention.
- A **feature search** (e.g., "any red item") is pre-attentive and parallel.

### E. Multiple Resource Theory — Wickens (1984, 2008)
- Attention is composed of **multiple, separate resource pools**.
- Resources differ along dimensions:
  - **Processing Stages** (Perception vs. Cognition vs. Response)
  - **Perceptual Modalities** (Visual vs. Auditory)
  - **Visual Channels** (Spatial vs. Verbal)
  - **Response Codes** (Manual vs. Vocal)
- **Performance degrades** when two tasks compete for the **same resource pool**.
- **Performance is preserved** when tasks use **different resource pools** (e.g., listening to music while driving on a familiar road).

### F. Spotlight Model (Posner, 1980)
- Attention acts as a **spotlight** that moves across the visual field.
- **Zoom lens** variant (Eriksen & St. James, 1986): The spotlight can change size — smaller spot = higher resolution, but slower.
- Attention enhances processing at the attended location.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Miller's Law | $N = 7 \pm 2$ | Working memory chunk capacity | chunks |
| Hick's Law (Reaction Time) | $RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | Time to choose among $n$ alternatives | ms; $a$ = base RT, $b$ = slope (~100–200 ms) |
| Signal Detection — Sensitivity $d'$ | $d' = z(H) - z(F)$ | Discriminability index | dimensionless; higher = better |
| Signal Detection — Bias $\beta$ | $\beta = \dfrac{f(H)}{f(F)}$ | Likelihood ratio / response bias | $\beta = 1$ neutral |
| Hit Rate | $H = \dfrac{\text{Hits}}{\text{Hits} + \text{Misses}}$ | Correct detection of signal | $0 \le H \le 1$ |
| False Alarm Rate | $F = \dfrac{\text{False Alarms}}{\text{False Alarms} + \text{Correct Rejections}}$ | Incorrect detection | $0 \le F \le 1$ |
| Fitts' Law (Motor Attention) | $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | Movement time to a target | ms; $D$ = distance, $W$ = width |
| Useful Field of View | $UFOV \approx 30^\circ H \times 20^\circ V$ | Effective attentional span | degrees |
| Sternberg Search Time | $RT = a + b \cdot n$ | Memory scanning slope | ms/item |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used in designing **air traffic control displays, automotive HUDs, medical device interfaces, and adaptive UIs** where attention allocation directly impacts safety and throughput.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Attention Theory |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Aviation** | Cockpit warning prioritization, head-up displays (HUDs) |
| **Automotive** | Driver monitoring systems, distraction detection |
| **Healthcare** | Alarm fatigue reduction in ICUs (apply Wickens' resources) |
| **Mobile UI/UX** | Progressive disclosure to reduce cognitive load |
| **AR/VR** | Foveated rendering based on gaze-contingent attention |
| **Accessibility** | Designing for users with ADHD, age-related attention decline |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Signal Detection Theory: Computing $d'$ and $\beta$

Signal Detection Theory (SDT) provides a mathematically rigorous framework to dissociate **sensitivity** from **response bias** in attentional detection tasks.

### Step 1: Construct the 2×2 Outcome Matrix
When a stimulus is presented, there are four possible outcomes:

| | Signal Present | Signal Absent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **"Yes" Response** | Hit ($H$) | False Alarm ($F$) |
| **"No" Response** | Miss ($M$) | Correct Rejection ($CR$) |

### Step 2: Compute Hit Rate and False Alarm Rate

$$
H = \frac{\text{Hits}}{\text{Hits} + \text{Misses}}
$$

$$
F = \frac{\text{False Alarms}}{\text{False Alarms} + \text{Correct Rejections}}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The hit rate is the proportion of times the observer correctly reported "signal" when the signal was indeed present. The false alarm rate is the proportion of times the observer incorrectly reported "signal" when no signal was present.

### Step 3: Apply the z-Transformation
Both $H$ and $F$ are converted to **z-scores** using the standard normal cumulative distribution function $\Phi^{-1}$. This places both rates on a common, normally distributed scale.

$$
z(H) = \Phi^{-1}(H)
$$

$$
z(F) = \Phi^{-1}(F)
$$

**Conversion Logic:** A $z$-score represents the number of standard deviations from the mean. This step is required because the two underlying distributions (signal and noise) are assumed Gaussian.

### Step 4: Compute Sensitivity Index $d'$

$$
d' = z(H) - z(F)
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The distance between the means of the two underlying distributions (signal vs. noise), expressed in standard-deviation units. A $d'$ of 0 means no discrimination; $d' \ge 1.0$ is considered good detection.

### Step 5: Compute Response Bias $\beta$

$$
\beta = \frac{f(z(H))}{f(z(F))}
$$

where $f(\cdot)$ is the **probability density function** of the standard normal distribution.

**Conversion Logic:** $\beta$ is the likelihood ratio of the two densities at the observer's criterion. If $\beta = 1$, the observer is unbiased. If $\beta > 1$, the observer is conservative (says "no" often). If $\beta < 1$, the observer is liberal (says "yes" often).

### Step 6: Worked Numerical Example
Suppose an operator monitoring 100 trials gets: 40 Hits, 10 Misses, 15 False Alarms, 35 Correct Rejections.

$$
H = \frac{40}{40 + 10} = \frac{40}{50} = 0.80
$$

$$
F = \frac{15}{15 + 35} = \frac{15}{50} = 0.30
$$

$$
z(H) = \Phi^{-1}(0.80) \approx 0.8416
$$

$$
z(F) = \Phi^{-1}(0.30) \approx -0.5244
$$

$$
d' = 0.8416 - (-0.5244) = 1.366
$$

$$
f(z(H)) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-0.8416^2 / 2} \approx 0.2803
$$

$$
f(z(F)) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-0.5244^2 / 2} \approx 0.3477
$$

$$
\beta = \frac{0.2803}{0.3477} \approx 0.806
$$

**Interpretation:** $d' = 1.37$ indicates **good discriminative sensitivity**. $\beta = 0.806 < 1$ indicates a **slightly liberal bias** (tends to report "signal" more readily).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hick's Law Applied to Menu Selection

Hick's Law models how **reaction time** grows with the number of choices, directly relevant to **attentional decision load** in HMI.

### Step 1: State the Law

$$
RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)
$$

where $n$ is the number of equally probable choices, $a$ is the base reaction time, and $b$ is empirically determined (typically 100–200 ms).

**Conversion Logic:** The logarithmic growth arises from **information theory (Shannon, 1948)**. The information content (in bits) of choosing among $n+1$ alternatives is $H = \log_2(n+1)$. Attention acts as a serial search through this information space.

### Step 2: Worked Example — Comparing Two Menus

**Menu A:** 4 items, $a = 200$ ms, $b = 150$ ms.

$$
RT_A = 200 + 150 \cdot \log_2(4+1) = 200 + 150 \cdot 2.3219 \approx 200 + 348.3 = 548.3 \text{ ms}
$$

**Menu B:** 16 items, same $a$ and $b$.

$$
RT_B = 200 + 150 \cdot \log_2(16+1) = 200 + 150 \cdot 4.0875 \approx 200 + 613.1 = 813.1 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Attentional Penalty

$$
\Delta RT = RT_B - RT_A = 813.1 - 548.3 = 264.8 \text{ ms}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** Increasing menu items from 4 to 16 increases decision time by **~48%**, demonstrating why HMI designers use **chunking, hierarchical menus, and search** to keep $n$ small.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Wickens' Multiple Resource Conflict Score

To predict **dual-task interference**, Wickens proposed a **Multiple Resource Score (MRS)**.

### Step 1: Identify Resource Dimensions
For each task, assign a vector of values across dimensions:

$$
\vec{T_i} = \langle S_i, P_i, V_i, R_i \rangle
$$

where:
- $S_i$ = Processing Stage (1=Perception, 2=Cognition, 3=Response)
- $P_i$ = Perceptual Modality (1=Visual, 2=Auditory)
- $V_i$ = Visual Channel (1=Spatial, 2=Verbal)
- $R_i$ = Response Code (1=Manual, 2=Vocal)

### Step 2: Compute Conflict Vector

$$
\vec{C} = \vec{T_1} \ominus \vec{T_2}
$$

Element-wise: $C_k = 1$ if $T_{1,k} = T_{2,k}$, else $0$.

### Step 3: Total Conflict Score

$$
\text{MRS} = \sum_{k=1}^{4} w_k \cdot C_k
$$

where $w_k$ are empirically derived weights (often all equal to 1).

**Conversion Logic:** If two tasks share the same resource on any dimension, that dimension contributes +1 to the conflict score. Total possible conflict = 4 (worst), 0 (best).

### Step 4: Worked Example

**Task 1:** Visually perceive a screen icon → cognitively decode meaning → manually press a key.
$\vec{T_1} = \langle 2, 1, 1, 1 \rangle$

**Task 2:** Auditory listen to GPS → cognitively plan route → vocally speak.
$\vec{T_2} = \langle 2, 2, 2, 2 \rangle$

$$
\vec{C} = \langle 1, 0, 0, 0 \rangle
$$

$$
\text{MRS} = 1 \cdot (1) + 1 \cdot (0) + 1 \cdot (0) + 1 \cdot (0) = 1
$$

**Interpretation:** Low conflict (1/4). Tasks use different perceptual modalities and response codes, so they can be performed concurrently with minimal interference — explaining why verbal GPS works well while driving.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each Model

| Scenario | Recommended Model | Reasoning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Filter for noise rejection (e.g., audio) | Broadbent's Filter Model | Physical-feature-based selection |
| Hearing your name in a crowd | Treisman's Attenuation | Semantic content penetrates |
| Reading comprehension in dense text | Late Selection | Meaning processed before focus |
| Visual search for a target | Feature Integration Theory | Binding via focused attention |
| Cockpit multi-display monitoring | Wickens' Multiple Resource | Resource pool conflicts |
| Eye-tracking / saliency prediction | Spotlight Model | Spatial gradient of attention |
| Detection / vigilance tasks | Signal Detection Theory | Separates sensitivity from bias |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Cognitive Processing Pipeline of Attention

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Stimulus1 Visual] --> B1[Sensory Buffer]
    A2[Stimulus2 Auditory] --> B1
    A3[Stimulus3 Tactile] --> B1
    B1 --> C1{Selection Mechanism}
    C1 -->|Early Selection Broadbent| D1[Physical Feature Filter]
    C1 -->|Attenuation Treisman| D2[Attenuated Channel]
    C1 -->|Late Selection Deutsch| D3[Semantic Processor]
    D1 --> E1[Working Memory 7 plus minus 2]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Resource Pool Wickens]
    F1 --> G1[Response Selection]
    F1 --> G2[Motor Output]
    F1 --> G3[Cognitive Action]
    G1 --> H1[Task Execution]
    G2 --> H1
    G3 --> H1
    H1 --> I1[Feedback to Attentional Loop]
    I1 --> C1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** All sensory inputs first enter a brief sensory buffer. The selection mechanism may be early (Broadbent), attenuated (Treisman), or late (Deutsch). Surviving information enters working memory, is allocated across resource pools, and produces a response. Feedback loops allow attention to be dynamically reallocated.

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow: Spotlight Model with Zoom Lens

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Visual Field Wide] --> B1[Attention Spotlight]
    B1 -->|Zoom In| C1[Narrow Focus High Resolution]
    B1 -->|Zoom Out| D1[Wide Focus Low Resolution]
    C1 --> E1[Enhanced Processing at Fovea]
    D1 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Perceptual Output]
    F1 --> G1[Top Down Goal Driven]
    F1 --> G2[Bottom Up Saliency Driven]
    G1 --> B1
    G2 --> B1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The spotlight can be focused (high acuity, slow movement) or defocused (broad awareness, fast movement). This is directly exploited in **foveated rendering** for VR/AR systems.

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: Attention-Aware HMI Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[User Gaze / Mouse] --> B1[Sensor Module]
    A2[User Task Context] --> C1[Context Engine]
    A3[UI Elements] --> D1[Saliency Mapper]
    B1 --> E1[Attention Estimator]
    C1 --> E1
    D1 --> E1
    E1 --> F1{Attention Allocation Map}
    F1 -->|Allocated Zone| G1[High Fidelity Render]
    F1 -->|Unattended Zone| H1[Reduced Fidelity Render]
    G1 --> I1[Display Output]
    H1 --> I1
    I1 --> A1
    I1 --> A2
    I1 --> A3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application:** Modern adaptive HMIs (e.g., smart cockpits, AR glasses) use this loop to dynamically allocate computational resources to where the user is attending — saving power and reducing visual clutter.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define attention. Differentiate between bottom-up and top-down attention.**

**Model Answer:**
Attention is the cognitive process of selectively concentrating on specific aspects of the environment while ignoring other perceivable information.

| Aspect | Bottom-Up (Exogenous) | Top-Down (Endogenous) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Driver | Physical saliency of stimulus | User's goals, expectations, knowledge |
| Speed | Fast (~80 ms) | Slower (~300 ms) |
| Control | Automatic, involuntary | Voluntary, deliberate |
| Example | Eye snaps to a flashing light | Searching for a friend's face in a crowd |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for differentiation table)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any three major models of attention and state their core propositions briefly.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Broadbent's Filter Model (1958):** Early selection based on physical features; unattended channel completely blocked.
2. **Treisman's Attenuation Model (1960):** Unattended channel is attenuated, not blocked; semantic content (e.g., one's name) can still be processed.
3. **Wickens' Multiple Resource Theory (1984):** Attention consists of multiple resource pools; performance degrades when concurrent tasks share the same pool.

*(3 marks: 1 per model)*

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **Broadbent's Filter Model** and **Treisman's Attenuation Model** of attention. How does the Attenuation Model address the limitations of the Filter Model?

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the **Feature Integration Theory (FIT)** of attention proposed by Treisman and Gelade. Distinguish between **feature search** and **conjunction search**.

---

### Question A — Model Solution

**(a) Solution:**

**Broadbent's Filter Model (1958):**
- Proposed by **Donald Broadbent** based on his work on auditory attention.
- Information from all sensory channels enters a **sensory buffer** where it persists for a brief duration (~0.5 seconds).
- A **selective filter** selects one channel based on **physical characteristics** — pitch, loudness, spatial location, or color.
- The unattended channel is **completely blocked** (all-or-nothing switching).
- The selected information is passed to a **pattern recognizer** for further semantic processing.

**Limitations of Broadbent's Model:**
- Cannot explain the **Cocktail Party Effect** — hearing one's own name in the unattended ear.
- Cannot explain why **semantically meaningful** unattended stimuli are sometimes detected.

**Treisman's Attenuation Model (1960):**
- Proposed by **Anne Treisman** as a refinement of Broadbent's model.
- The unattended channel is **not blocked** but **attenuated** (its signal strength is reduced).
- Attenuated information is still processed, but at a **lower threshold**.
- Highly relevant stimuli (e.g., one's own name, a sudden danger cue) may exceed the threshold and reach conscious awareness.
- Introduces the **Dictionary Unit** — a system that checks the semantic content of all incoming signals.
- The threshold for the attended channel is **lower** than for the unattended channel, hence attention is biased toward attended inputs.

**How Attenuation Solves Broadbent's Limitations:**
- By attenuating (not blocking), the model allows **highly salient unattended stimuli** to penetrate awareness.
- This elegantly explains the **Cocktail Party Effect** — your name in the unattended channel is semantically significant and passes the threshold.

*(7 marks: 2 for Broadbent + 2 for limitations + 3 for Treisman + how it addresses limitations)*

---

**(b) Solution:**

**Feature Integration Theory (Treisman & Gelade, 1980):**
- Visual scene is first **decomposed into elementary features** — color, orientation, size, motion, brightness.
- These features are processed in **parallel** across the visual field by separate **feature maps**.
- A **master map (saliency map)** registers locations where features are present.
- **Focused attention** is required to **bind** individual features into a coherent perceptual object (the **"binding problem"**).
- Binding is a **serial** process that occurs at the location of the **attention spotlight**.

**Feature Search vs. Conjunction Search:**

| Aspect | Feature Search | Conjunction Search |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Target | Single unique feature (e.g., "any red item") | Combination of features (e.g., "red AND vertical") |
| Processing | Parallel, pre-attentive | Serial, requires focused attention |
| Reaction Time | Independent of set size (flat slope) | Increases linearly with set size |
| Example | Finding a red dot among green dots | Finding a red vertical bar among red horizontals and green verticals |

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Visual Input] --> B1[Feature Maps]
    B1 --> B2[Color Map]
    B1 --> B3[Orientation Map]
    B1 --> B4[Size Map]
    B1 --> B5[Motion Map]
    B2 --> C1[Master Saliency Map]
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
    C1 --> D1{Focused Attention Spotlight}
    D1 -->|Feature Search| E1[Pre-attentive Parallel Output]
    D1 -->|Conjunction Search| E2[Serial Binding]
    E2 --> F1[Unified Percept]
```

*(7 marks: 3 for FIT theory + 2 for search types table + 2 for diagram and binding explanation)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe **Wickens' Multiple Resource Theory** in detail. Explain the four resource dimensions and how they predict **dual-task interference** with an example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With relevant formulas, explain **Signal Detection Theory (SDT)** as applied to attention. Compute the sensitivity index $d'$ and bias $\beta$ for the following data: Hits = 45, Misses = 15, False Alarms = 20, Correct Rejections = 20.

---

### Question B — Model Solution

**(a) Solution:**

**Wickens' Multiple Resource Theory (1984, 2008):**
Wickens proposed that human attention is not a single "pool" of resources but consists of **multiple, partially independent resource pools**. Performance on concurrent tasks depends on whether they draw from the **same** or **different** pools.

**Four Resource Dimensions:**

1. **Processing Stages** — Perception / Cognition / Response.
2. **Perceptual Modalities** — Visual vs. Auditory.
3. **Visual Processing Channels** — Spatial (where) vs. Verbal/Symbolic (what).
4. **Response Codes** — Manual (hands) vs. Vocal (speech).

**Dual-Task Interference Prediction:**
- If two tasks share the **same resource** on **multiple dimensions**, interference is high.
- If they differ on **most dimensions**, interference is low.

**Example — Driving while talking on a hands-free phone:**
- Task 1 (Driving): Visual perception → Spatial cognition → Manual response (steering).
- Task 2 (Phone): Auditory perception → Verbal cognition → Vocal response.
- They differ on **all four dimensions** → low interference, manageable.

**Counter-example — Driving while texting:**
- Task 1 (Driving): Visual perception → Spatial cognition → Manual response.
- Task 2 (Texting): Visual perception → Verbal cognition → Manual response.
- They share **3 of 4 dimensions** → severe interference, dangerous.

**Multiple Resource Score (MRS):**

$$
\vec{T_i} = \langle S_i, P_i, V_i, R_i \rangle
$$

$$
\text{MRS} = \sum_{k=1}^{4} w_k \cdot \mathbb{1}(T_{1,k} = T_{2,k})
$$

where $\mathbb{1}(\cdot)$ is the indicator function (1 if match, 0 otherwise).

*(7 marks: 2 for theory + 2 for dimensions + 3 for example and MRS formula)*

---

**(b) Solution:**

**Signal Detection Theory (SDT) Applied to Attention:**
SDT mathematically separates the observer's **sensitivity** (true ability to detect a signal) from their **response bias** (tendency to say "yes" or "no"). This is critical in HMI vigilance tasks like monitoring alarms.

**2×2 Outcome Matrix:**

| | Signal Present | Signal Absent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Yes** | Hit | False Alarm |
| **No** | Miss | Correct Rejection |

**Formulas:**

$$
H = \frac{\text{Hits}}{\text{Hits} + \text{Misses}}
$$

$$
F = \frac{\text{False Alarms}}{\text{False Alarms} + \text{Correct Rejections}}
$$

$$
d' = z(H) - z(F)
$$

$$
\beta = \frac{f(z(H))}{f(z(F))}
$$

**Given Data:** Hits = 45, Misses = 15, False Alarms = 20, Correct Rejections = 20.

**Step 1: Compute Hit Rate and False Alarm Rate**

$$
H = \frac{45}{45 + 15} = \frac{45}{60} = 0.75
$$

$$
F = \frac{20}{20 + 20} = \frac{20}{40} = 0.50
$$

**Step 2: Apply z-Transformation**

$$
z(H) = \Phi^{-1}(0.75) \approx 0.6745
$$

$$
z(F) = \Phi^{-1}(0.50) = 0.0000
$$

**Step 3: Compute $d'$**

$$
d' = 0.6745 - 0.0000 = 0.6745
$$

**Step 4: Compute $\beta$**

$$
f(z(H)) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-0.6745^2 / 2} \approx \frac{1}{2.5066} e^{-0.2275} \approx 0.3989 \cdot 0.7964 \approx 0.3177
$$

$$
f(z(F)) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{0} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \approx 0.3989
$$

$$
\beta = \frac{0.3177}{0.3989} \approx 0.7965
$$

**Interpretation:**
- $d' = 0.67$ indicates **moderate sensitivity** — the operator can discriminate signal from noise but is not highly accurate.
- $\beta = 0.80 < 1$ indicates a **liberal bias** — the operator tends to report "signal" more often than warranted (which inflates false alarms).

**HMI Design Implication:** Since $d'$ is moderate, the system should provide **clearer signal cues** (better signal-to-noise ratio, distinct alarm tones). Since $\beta$ is liberal, the operator may be prone to alarm fatigue — reduce false alarms through better sensor algorithms.

*(7 marks: 2 for theory + 2 for formulas + 3 for numerical computation and interpretation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse $d'$ with $\beta$.** Students frequently interchange them. $d'$ = sensitivity (ability), $\beta$ = bias (willingness). High $d'$ with low $\beta$ = accurate but trigger-happy.
> 2. **Always apply the z-transform.** Raw hit/false-alarm rates are NOT used directly in the $d'$ formula.
> 3. **In Wickens' MRS, dimension matching means interference, not collaboration.** Two tasks sharing a resource dimension increase load.
> 4. **For FIT, never say "attention is parallel."** Attention in FIT is **serial** during binding; features are parallel, binding is serial.
> 5. **In Broadbent's model, do not write "filter weakens the unattended channel."** That is Treisman's modification. Broadbent's filter is **all-or-nothing**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Attention** is the selective allocation of finite cognitive resources to specific stimuli.
- **Bottom-up attention** = stimulus-driven (saliency); **Top-down attention** = goal-driven (expectation).
- **Miller's Law**: Working memory holds **$7 \pm 2$** chunks.
- **Hick's Law**: $RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ — decision time grows logarithmically with choices.
- **Broadbent** = early filter (physical features), **Treisman** = attenuation (semantic penetration possible), **Deutsch** = late selection (all processed for meaning).
- **Feature Integration Theory (Treisman & Gelade)**: Features processed in parallel; **binding requires serial focused attention**.
- **Feature search** is pre-attentive and parallel; **conjunction search** is serial and attention-demanding.
- **Wickens' Multiple Resource Theory**: 4 dimensions (Stage, Modality, Visual Channel, Response Code); interference occurs when dimensions match.
- **Spotlight Model**: Attention is a movable spotlight; zoom-lens variant allows size change.
- **Signal Detection Theory**: $d' = z(H) - z(F)$ measures sensitivity; $\beta = f(z(H))/f(z(F))$ measures bias.
- **Useful Field of View**: ~30° H × 20° V — limit for effective attention.
- **Inattentional Blindness**: Unattended unexpected stimuli may not be consciously perceived.
- **HMI Design Rule**: Match **saliency to importance** — critical alerts must be perceptually prominent.
- **Engineering Applications**: Avionics, automotive HUDs, ICU alarms, mobile UI, AR/VR foveated rendering, accessibility design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thinking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT831 - HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Thinking # 🧠 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 📘 Formal KTU Academic Definition In the context of **Human–Machine Interface (HMI)** design, **Thinking** is formally defined as the **higher-order internal cognitive process** through which a human operator perceives, encodes, stores, retrieves, transforms, and acts upon information received from an interactive system. It encompasses *reasoning*, *problem solving*, *decision making*, *mental modelling*, and *design ideation* — all of which dictate the efficiency, safety, and usability of any human–computer interaction. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Thinking is the cognitive backbone of HMI. It is what occurs *between* a stimulus (e.g., a screen button) and a response (e.g., a click). The discipline of HMI is fundamentally about **aligning machine logic with human thought processes**. ## 🔍 Intuitive Analogy — The "Cockpit-in-the-Brain" Model Imagine you are a pilot flying an aircraft at night. You cannot see the runway, but you have a *mental picture* built from radar signals, instrument dials, and air-traffic voice calls. Your brain **integrates** these scattered pieces, **predicts** what comes next, and **decides** whether to descend or climb. That invisible, internal simulation of reality — the silent movie your mind plays before you act — **is thinking**. - The **radar signals** → *Sensory input* - The **integration process** → *Perception & cognition* - The **silent movie** → *Mental Model* - The **decision to descend** → *Reasoning & judgement* > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in HMI:** A poorly designed interface forces the user to *think harder* — increasing cognitive load, slowing reaction, and inviting human error. A well-designed interface lets the user think **only about the task**, not about the tool. ## 🧩 Three Pillars of "Thinking" in HMI | Pillar | Meaning | HMI Implication | |---|---|---| | **Mental Model** | User's internal belief about how a system works | Designer must build a *conceptual model* that matches user expectations | | **Reasoning** | Logical manipulation of symbols/concepts | Interfaces must support *deductive*, *inductive*, and *abductive* inference | | **Problem Solving** | Goal-directed thought to overcome obstacles | Workflow design must minimise **goal–action gaps** (Norman's Gulf of Execution) | ## ⚖️ Cognitive Load & Performance Metrics Two foundational constants govern thinking performance in HMI: - **Working Memory Capacity (WMC):** **7 ± 2** discrete chunks (Miller's Law, 1956) — used in menu depth, chunking of icons, and short-term displays. - **Attention Switch Cost:** **~200 ms** per context switch — justifies the HMI rule of *minimising mode changes*. > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Pearl:** Whenever a KTU question mentions "user cannot remember all options" or "menu is too deep," the expected answer is **Miller's 7 ± 2**. # 📐 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 🧠 2.1 Taxonomy of Human Thinking (KTU-Favoured Classification) Thinking is **not** a single activity. The KTU 2024 syllabus groups it into the following functional categories: 1. **Critical Thinking** — Evaluative, judgement-based reasoning (used in error diagnosis, safety-critical interfaces). 2. **Creative / Divergent Thinking** — Generation of multiple novel solutions (used in brainstorming UI alternatives). 3. **Analytical / Convergent Thinking** — Narrowing down to a single correct answer (used in form validation, parameter setting). 4. **Systems Thinking** — Seeing the whole *as a whole*, recognising interdependencies (used in workflow design). 5. **Design Thinking** — Human-centred, iterative problem framing (the **core methodology** of HMI design). 6. **Lateral Thinking** — Indirect, non-obvious approaches (used in innovative gesture/voice UIs). 7. **Abductive Thinking** — Inference to the *best explanation* (used in predictive user-behaviour analytics). ## 🔁 2.2 The Information-Processing Model (Sternberg / Atkinson–Shiffrin) Every act of thinking in HMI follows a **five-stage cognitive pipeline**: 1. **Sensory Register** — Raw stimuli hit eyes/ears (e.g., flashing red icon on dashboard). 2. **Attention / Perception** — Selective filtering; the red icon is *noticed*. 3. **Working Memory (Short-Term Store)** — Conscious manipulation; *what does red mean here?* 4. **Long-Term Memory Retrieval** — Past experience surfaces; *"red = critical error"*. 5. **Response Selection & Execution** — Motor command issued; e.g., pressing the *Abort* button. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Take-away:** HMI designers can only influence **Stage 1 (via visual salience), Stage 2 (via attention cues), and Stage 5 (via affordances)**. Stages 3 & 4 belong to the user — hence the designer must *pre-load* them through training, consistency, and conceptual-model mapping. ## 📐 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Law / Model | Equation | Meaning | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Miller's Law** | $\text{WMC} = 7 \pm 2$ chunks | Working memory holds 5–9 chunks | Limits menu breadth, icon grouping | | 2 | **Hick's Law** | $T = a + b \cdot \log_{2}(n+1)$ | Decision time grows with $\log$ of choices | Limits number of menu items | | 3 | **Fitts' Law** | $T = a + b \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W}+1\right)$ | Movement time vs. distance / target width | Sizes buttons, click targets | | 4 | **Power Law of Practice** | $T_n = T_1 \cdot n^{-\alpha}$ | Reaction time falls with practice | Justifies training phases & onboarding | | 5 | **Information Theory (Shannon)** | $H = -\sum p_i \log_{2} p_i$ | Bits of cognitive information in a stimulus | Quantifies display complexity | | 6 | **Reaction Time (Donders)** | $RT = RT_{stim} + RT_{cog} + RT_{mot}$ | Total RT = stimulus + cognitive + motor | Decomposes user delay for safety HMI | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Markdown Safeguard:** The vertical pipe $\vert$ and $\mid$ are used inside equations (e.g., $\log_2 \vert x \vert$) to **avoid breaking the table syntax** above.
