- $\varepsilon_0$ = vacuum permittivity = **$8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m**
- $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity of dielectric
- $A$ = electrode overlap area
- $d$ = separation distance
- Finger proximity increases effective $d$ → $C_m$ decreases.

**C. Surface Acoustic Wave (SAW)**
- Uses piezoelectric transducers to send **ultrasonic waves (~ 50 MHz)** across the glass surface. A finger touching the surface **absorbs** some wave energy, which the controller detects as a loss-of-signal at specific transducers, triangulating the touch location.
- **Advantages:** Excellent optical clarity, supports stylus. **Disadvantages:** Cannot detect soft/gloved touch; sensitive to contaminants.

**D. Infrared (IR) Touch**
- Array of IR LEDs and photodetectors around the bezel. Each LED-receiver pair forms an invisible "light curtain." A finger blocks one or more beams → interruption pattern is mapped to coordinates.
- **Advantages:** Works with any input (gloved, stylus, card). **Disadvantages:** Bezel bulk, false triggers from dust/insects.

#### 2.1.2 Display Technologies (Output Side)

| Display Type | Operating Principle | Response Time | Key Equation / Metric |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LCD (TFT)** | Liquid crystal molecules rotate polarized light; backlight provides illumination | ~ 5–10 ms | Contrast Ratio = $\frac{L_{white}}{L_{black}}$ |
| **OLED** | Organic compounds emit light on current injection (electroluminescence) | < 1 ms | Luminance efficiency: $\eta = \frac{L}{P}$ (cd/W) |
| **E-Ink (E-Paper)** | Charged microcapsules (black + white) migrate under electric field | ~ 120 ms | Power only on refresh (bi-stable) |
| **LED / MicroLED** | Inorganic semiconductor emits light on electron-hole recombination | < 1 ms | Luminous efficacy: **lm/W** |
| **Projection / DLP** | Micro-mirrors (DMD) tilt ±12° to reflect light | Frame-limited | Resolution limited by mirror count |

**Luminance Equation (Fundamental for Display Specs):**

$$L = \frac{I}{A \cdot \cos(\theta)}$$

Where:
- $L$ = luminance (cd/m² or "nits")
- $I$ = luminous intensity (candelas)
- $A$ = projected area (m²)
- $\theta$ = viewing angle from normal

> **Real-world benchmark:** Sunlight-readable displays require $L \geq 1000$ nits; HDR content requires $L \geq 1000$ nits peak with deep blacks.

#### 2.1.3 Voice / Speech Recognition Technologies

**Pipeline (Auto Speech Recognition — ASR):**
1. **Acoustic Capture:** Microphone (typically MEMS, 16 kHz+ sampling).
2. **Pre-processing:** Noise suppression, beamforming, AGC.
3. **Feature Extraction:** **MFCC (Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients)** — the standard.
4. **Acoustic Modeling:** HMM (traditional) or DNN/Transformer (modern).
5. **Language Modeling:** N-grams or neural LMs.
6. **Decoding:** Beam search → text output.

**Mel Scale Formula (Core Speech Feature):**

$$f_{mel} = 2595 \cdot \log_{10}\left(1 + \frac{f}{700}\right)$$

Where $f$ is the linear frequency in Hz. This compresses high frequencies to mimic human auditory perception.

#### 2.1.4 Gesture Recognition Technologies

Two primary approaches:
- **Vision-based:** RGB camera + Computer Vision (OpenCV, MediaPipe) → hand pose estimation, skeletal tracking. Algorithms: CNN, LSTM for temporal sequences.
- **Sensor-based:** IMU (accelerometer + gyroscope) worn on hand/wrist → e.g., Myo armband (also uses **EMG** for muscle electrical activity).

**EMG Signal Frequency Range:** **20 Hz to 500 Hz**, amplitude **0.1 to 5 mV**.

#### 2.1.5 Brain-Computer Interface (BCI) Technologies

- **Non-invasive:** EEG (electroencephalogram) — electrodes on scalp. Captures cortical potentials.
- **Semi-invasive:** ECoG (electrocorticography).
- **Invasive:** Microelectrode arrays (e.g., Neuralink).

**EEG Frequency Bands (Alpha, Beta, etc. — frequently tested):**

| Band | Frequency | Associated State |
|---|---|---|
| Delta ($\delta$) | 0.5 – 4 Hz | Deep sleep |
| Theta ($\theta$) | 4 – 8 Hz | Drowsiness, meditation |
| Alpha ($\alpha$) | 8 – 13 Hz | Relaxed, eyes closed |
| Beta ($\beta$) | 13 – 30 Hz | Active thinking, focus |
| Gamma ($\gamma$) | 30 – 100 Hz | Perception, cognition |

#### 2.1.6 Haptic Feedback Technologies

- **Vibrotactile:** Eccentric Rotating Mass (ERM) motor or Linear Resonant Actuator (LRA) — frequency 150–300 Hz.
- **Force Feedback:** Motors apply opposing force (e.g., steering wheels, surgical robots).
- **Ultrasonic Mid-air Haptics:** Phased array of transducers focus ultrasound at a point in space → pressure on skin. No contact needed.
- **Electrocutaneous:** Small current stimulates skin receptors.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbols | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ | Capacitance | Farads (F) | Capacitive touch sensor |
| 2 | $f_{mel} = 2595 \log_{10}(1 + f/700)$ | Mel frequency | Mels | Speech feature extraction |
| 3 | $L = I / (A \cos\theta)$ | Luminance | cd/m² (nits) | Display brightness spec |
| 4 | $\text{CR} = L_{white} / L_{black}$ | Contrast ratio | dimensionless | Display quality |
| 5 | $f_{Nyquist} = 2 f_{max}$ | Sampling theorem | Hz | Audio ADC design |
| 6 | $t_{response} \leq t_{human} \approx 250$ ms | Latency budget | ms | HMI responsiveness |
| 7 | $\eta_{luminous} = \Phi_v / P$ | Luminous efficacy | lm/W | Display power efficiency |
| 8 | $F = m \cdot a$ (haptic force) | Haptic output | Newtons (N) | Force feedback design |
| 9 | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(V_s / V_n)$ | Signal quality | dB | Microphone/audio quality |
| 10 | $P_{acoustic} = p_{rms}^2 / (\rho c)$ | Sound intensity | W/m² | Audio output safety |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Equations 1, 2, 3, and 5 appear most frequently in 14-mark numerical/analytical questions. Memorize them with their **units** — partial marks are awarded for correct units even if numbers slip.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive HMI:** Capacitive touch + haptic + OLED displays in modern dashboards (e.g., Tesla, Mercedes MBUX).
- **Medical HMI:** Force-feedback surgical robots (da Vinci) + voice control in OR (reduces sterile-field breaks).
- **Industrial HMI:** IR touch panels in factory floors (works with gloves) + SCADA screens.
- **Accessibility:** BCI for locked-in patients; eye-tracking for ALS patients; haptic canes for the visually impaired.
- **Consumer:** AR/VR (Meta Quest, Apple Vision Pro) — high-refresh OLED + hand tracking + spatial audio.
- **Aviation:** Multi-touch cockpit displays + head-up displays (HUD) + voice commands.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mutual Capacitance Drop Due to Finger Touch

**Problem Setup:** A projected capacitive touch sensor has square ITO electrodes of side $a = 5$ mm, separated by a dielectric layer of thickness $d = 1$ mm and $\varepsilon_r = 4$. The finger is modeled as a grounded conductor at distance $h = 2$ mm above the electrode surface.

**Step 1: Baseline mutual capacitance (no finger):**

$$C_{m,0} = \varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot \frac{A}{d}$$

**Substitute the values:**

$$C_{m,0} = (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 4 \cdot \frac{(5 \times 10^{-3})^2}{1 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C_{m,0} = (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 4 \cdot \frac{25 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Compute the area-to-distance ratio:**

$$\frac{A}{d} = 25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

**Multiply by permittivity:**

$$C_{m,0} = (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 4 \cdot (25 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$C_{m,0} = 8.854 \times 4 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-15}$$

**Compute $8.854 \times 4$:**

$$8.854 \times 4 = 35.416$$

**Compute $35.416 \times 25$:**

$$35.416 \times 25 = 885.4$$

**Therefore:**

$$C_{m,0} = 885.4 \times 10^{-15} \text{ F} = 885.4 \text{ fF}$$

**Step 2: Effective separation with finger (finger is a grounded conductor above, so it acts as a series capacitor).**

The effective system can be modeled as $C_{m,0}$ in series with the finger-to-electrode capacitance $C_{finger}$ through air ($\varepsilon_r = 1$).

$$C_{finger} = \varepsilon_0 \cdot 1 \cdot \frac{A}{h} = (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot \frac{25 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C_{finger} = (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (12.5 \times 10^{-3}) = 110.675 \times 10^{-15} \text{ F} = 110.675 \text{ fF}$$

**Step 3: Series combination of the two capacitances:**

$$\frac{1}{C_{total}} = \frac{1}{C_{m,0}} + \frac{1}{C_{finger}}$$

**Compute reciprocals:**

$$\frac{1}{C_{m,0}} = \frac{1}{885.4 \text{ fF}} = 1.1294 \times 10^{-3} \text{ fF}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{C_{finger}} = \frac{1}{110.675 \text{ fF}} = 9.0357 \times 10^{-3} \text{ fF}^{-1}$$

**Sum:**

$$\frac{1}{C_{total}} = (1.1294 + 9.0357) \times 10^{-3} = 10.1651 \times 10^{-3} \text{ fF}^{-1}$$

**Invert:**

$$C_{total} = \frac{1}{10.1651 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 98.37 \text{ fF}$$

**Step 4: Capacitance drop observed by the controller:**

$$\Delta C = C_{m,0} - C_{total} = 885.4 - 98.37 = 787.03 \text{ fF}$$

**Step 5: Percentage change (signal strength):**

$$\%\Delta C = \frac{787.03}{885.4} \times 100\% \approx 88.9\%$$

**Interpretation:** The finger causes a massive drop, easily detectable by the touch controller's charge-transfer or relaxation-oscillator measurement circuits. In practice, only a small portion of the finger overlaps each electrode, so the measured drop per node is typically 1–10% — but this is still well above the noise floor of modern PCT controllers.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Nyquist Sampling for Speech Recognition

**Problem:** A voice interface samples microphone audio. The highest frequency of interest in human speech is **$f_{max} = 8$ kHz** (telephony band; full audio extends to 20 kHz).

**Step 1: Apply Nyquist-Shannon theorem:**

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot 8000 = 16{,}000 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2: KTU standard sample rate is 16 kHz for narrowband ASR; 44.1 kHz or 48 kHz for full-band ASR (e.g., smart speakers).**

**Step 3: Anti-aliasing filter must cut all energy above $f_s/2$ to prevent aliasing:**

$$f_{cutoff} \leq f_s/2 - \text{guard band} = 8 \text{ kHz} - 0.5 \text{ kHz} = 7.5 \text{ kHz}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Technology Comparison (HIGH-YIELD FOR KTU EXAMS)

| Parameter | Resistive Touch | Capacitive (PCT) | SAW | IR Touch | Voice | Gesture (Vision) | BCI (EEG) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Multi-touch** | ❌ (single) | ✅ (up to 10) | ❌ | ✅ (limited) | N/A | ✅ | N/A |
| **Gloved input** | ✅ | ⚠️ (special) | ❌ | ✅ | N/A | ✅ | N/A |
| **Optical clarity** | ~ 75% | ~ 90% | ~ 100% | ~ 100% | N/A | N/A | N/A |
| **Works with water** | ✅ | ⚠️ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Latency** | < 15 ms | < 10 ms | < 10 ms | < 20 ms | 200–500 ms | 30–100 ms | 500+ ms |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | High | Medium | High (mics+ASR) | High (cameras) | Very High |
| **Power** | Low | Low | Low | Low | Medium | High | Medium |
| **Robustness** | Low (wear) | High (glass) | Medium | High | Medium | Medium | Low |
| **Best use case** | ATMs, industrial | Smartphones, tablets | Kiosks, medical | Outdoor kiosks | Smart speakers | AR/VR, sign lang. | Medical, research |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Strategy:** When KTU asks "compare X and Y," use this matrix structure: list 4–6 parameters, then give 1-line justification for each. This fetches full marks.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Practice: Display Luminance Calculation

**Problem:** A laptop OLED panel has a luminous intensity $I = 500$ cd over an area $A = 0.02$ m² viewed at $\theta = 30°$ from normal. Find the luminance $L$.

**Step 1: Identify the formula.**

$$L = \frac{I}{A \cdot \cos(\theta)}$$

**Step 2: Compute $\cos(30°)$.**

$$\cos(30°) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$$

**Step 3: Substitute all values.**

$$L = \frac{500}{0.02 \times 0.866}$$

**Step 4: Compute denominator.**

$$0.02 \times 0.866 = 0.01732 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5: Final division.**

$$L = \frac{500}{0.01732} \approx 28{,}868 \text{ cd/m}^2$$

**Step 6: Convert to nits (1 nit = 1 cd/m²).**

$$L \approx 28{,}868 \text{ nits}$$

**Interpretation:** This is a peak luminance figure typical of HDR OLED panels in modern laptops.

---

### 3.5 Numerical Practice: Haptic Latency vs. Human Perception

**Problem:** A touchscreen system has a display-to-haptic-actuator loop delay of $t_{loop} = 22$ ms. Determine whether the user perceives the haptic feedback as causally connected to the touch event.

**Step 1: Threshold from psychophysics literature.**

$$t_{threshold} \approx 30 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2: Compare.**

$$t_{loop} = 22 \text{ ms} < 30 \text{ ms} = t_{threshold}$$

**Step 3: Conclusion.** The system passes the perceptual causality test; the user will *feel* the vibration as a direct result of the touch. If $t_{loop}$ exceeded 50 ms, users would perceive a "laggy" or disconnected feel.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Problem: Selecting Technology for a Given Use Case

**Problem (KTU-style 7-mark):** Recommend an input technology for each scenario and justify in 2 lines.
1. **Outdoor toll-booth kiosk** (operators wear thick gloves, in sunlight).
2. **Smartphone screen** (multi-touch, high clarity).
3. **Smart speaker in a noisy kitchen** (far-field voice).
4. **Surgical robot control console** (precision, no latency, force feedback).

**Model Answers:**

1. **IR Touch** — works with gloves, sunlight-immune (IR beams), robust to water/dust.
2. **Projected Capacitive (PCT)** — multi-touch, high optical clarity (~90%), durable glass.
3. **Microphone array with beamforming** — focuses on user's voice direction; noise cancellation.
4. **Haptic joystick with force feedback + low-latency capacitive touch** — sub-10 ms response critical for surgical precision.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Block Diagram: HMI Technology Stack

This Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the various HMI technologies layer into a complete system. It is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** showing the interaction between input, processing, and output technologies.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H[Human User]
    
    subgraph InputLayer["INPUT TECHNOLOGIES LAYER"]
        T1[Touch Sensors: Resistive, Capacitive PCT, SAW, IR]
        V1[Vision Sensors: RGB Camera, IR Depth, LiDAR]
        A1[Audio Sensors: MEMS Microphone, Mic Array]
        B1[Bio Sensors: EEG, EMG, ECG, EOG Electrodes]
        M1[Motion Sensors: IMU, Accelerometer, Gyroscope]
    end
    
    subgraph CommLayer["COMMUNICATION LAYER"]
        W1[Wired: USB, HDMI, Ethernet, CAN Bus]
        WL1[Wireless: BLE, WiFi, NFC, 5G]
    end
    
    subgraph ProcessLayer["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        DSP[Signal Conditioning and Feature Extraction]
        ML[Machine Learning Models: CNN, RNN, Transformer]
        DEC[Decision and Intent Engine]
    end
    
    subgraph OutputLayer["OUTPUT TECHNOLOGIES LAYER"]
        D1[Visual: LCD, OLED, E Ink, Projection]
        AU1[Auditory: Speakers, Bone Conduction, Buzzers]
        HP1[Haptic: ERM, LRA, Ultrasonic, Force Feedback]
    end
    
    H -->|Commands Gestures Speech| T1
    H --> V1
    H --> A1
    H --> B1
    H --> M1
    
    T1 --> DSP
    V1 --> DSP
    A1 --> DSP
    B1 --> DSP
    M1 --> DSP
    
    DSP -->|MFCC Pixels IMU EEG| ML
    ML --> DEC
    
    DEC -->|Display Commands| W1
    DEC --> WL1
    
    W1 --> D1
    W1 --> AU1
    W1 --> HP1
    WL1 --> D1
    WL1 --> AU1
    WL1 --> HP1
    
    D1 -->|Visual Feedback| H
    AU1 -->|Audio Feedback| H
    HP1 -->|Tactile Feedback| H
```

---

### 4.2 Touch Technology Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Need Multi-Touch?}
    Q2{Use With Gloves?}
    Q3{Sunlight Outdoor Use?}
    Q4{Cost Critical?}
    
    R1[Choose Projected Capacitive PCT]
    R2[Choose Infrared IR or Resistive]
    R3[Choose Infrared IR Touch]
    R4[Choose Resistive Touch]
    R5[Choose Surface Acoustic Wave SAW]
    
    Q1 -->|Yes| R1
    Q1 -->|No| Q2
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3
    Q2 -->|No| R5
    Q3 -->|Yes| R3
    Q3 -->|No| Q4
    Q4 -->|Yes| R4
    Q4 -->|No| R5
```

---

### 4.3 Voice / Speech Recognition Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[User Speaks] --> S2[MEMS Microphone Capture]
    S2 --> S3[Analog to Digital Conversion 16 kHz Sampling]
    S3 --> S4[Noise Suppression and Beamforming]
    S4 --> S5[Feature Extraction MFCC]
    S5 --> S6[Acoustic Model DNN or HMM]
    S6 --> S7[Language Model]
    S7 --> S8[Decoder Beam Search]
    S8 --> S9[Recognized Text Command]
    S9 --> S10[Intent Engine Maps to Action]
    S10 --> S11[Machine Executes Command]
```

---

### 4.4 BCI Signal Flow (EEG Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BR[Brain Cortical Activity] --> EL[Electrodes on Scalp]
    EL --> AMP[Differential Amplifier Gain 10000x]
    AMP --> FIL[Bandpass Filter 0.5 to 100 Hz]
    FIL --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter 256 Hz Sampling]
    ADC --> FT[FFT and Feature Extraction Alpha Beta Bands]
    FT --> CLF[Classifier SVM or CNN]
    CLG{Detected Intent}
    CLG -->|Move Cursor| OUT1[Cursor Control Command]
    CLG -->|Select Item| OUT2[Click Command]
    CLG -->|Rest| OUT3[No Action]
```

---

### 4.5 Haptic Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[User Touch Event] --> TC[Touch Controller Detects at 10 ms]
    TC --> HCU[Haptic Control Unit Triggers]
    HCU --> ACT[Haptic Actuator: LRA or ERM]
    ACT --> FB[Mechanical Vibration 150 to 300 Hz]
    FB --> SK[User Skin Receptors Detect]
    SK --> PR[Perceptual Feedback in 30 ms]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define projected capacitive touch technology. Why is it preferred over resistive touch in modern smartphones?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Projected Capacitive Touch (PCT) uses a grid of indium tin oxide (ITO) electrodes separated by a dielectric. A finger touch reduces the mutual capacitance at the electrode intersection, which the controller detects to localize the touch.

It is preferred over resistive touch in smartphones because:
1. **Multi-touch support** (resistive is essentially single-touch).
2. **Higher optical clarity** (~90% vs. ~75%) — better display visibility.
3. **Durability** — a single solid glass layer withstands millions of touches, while resistive's top film wears out.
4. **Faster response** (< 10 ms vs. ~ 15 ms) and supports modern gestures (pinch, zoom, swipe).

*(Allocate ~1 mark per point: definition + 2 distinct advantages.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**List any three output technologies used in HMI. Give one example application for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Visual displays (OLED)** — used in smartphone screens and VR headsets.
2. **Haptic actuators (Linear Resonant Actuator / LRA)** — used in smartphone vibration alerts and game controllers.
3. **Auditory output (bone-conduction speakers)** — used in AR glasses and hearing-assist devices.

*(Any 3 valid output technologies with correct application = 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Explain the operating principle of **Resistive** and **Capacitive (Projected)** touch technologies with neat block diagrams. Compare them on the basis of **multi-touch support, optical clarity, durability, and cost**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **architecture of a voice-recognition-based HMI** with a block diagram. Explain the role of **MFCC features** in the pipeline. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Operating Principles:**

**Resistive Touch (3 marks):**
- **Construction:** Two ITO-coated flexible sheets separated by spacer dots.
- **Principle:** A finger press brings the two conductive layers into contact at a point, closing a circuit. The controller applies a voltage gradient along the X-axis on the bottom layer and reads the contact voltage on the top layer → derives X-coordinate; the process is repeated along Y-axis.
- **Diagram:** (Draw two parallel layers with spacer dots in between, with voltage source $V_x$ on bottom, $V_y$ on top, ADC reading the contact point.)

**Projected Capacitive Touch (2 marks):**
- **Construction:** Glass substrate with patterned ITO rows and columns forming a grid. Each intersection has a baseline mutual capacitance $C_m$.
- **Principle:** A finger (a conductor) reduces the mutual capacitance at the nearest node by forming a series capacitive path. The controller scans all nodes, detects $\Delta C_m$ drops, and uses the pattern of drops to determine touch coordinates and supports multi-touch.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Resistive | Capacitive (PCT) |
|---|---|---|
| Multi-touch | ❌ Single | ✅ Up to 10 |
| Optical clarity | ~ 75% | ~ 90% |
| Durability | Lower (film wears) | Higher (glass) |
| Cost | Low | Medium |

---

**(b) Voice-Recognition HMI Architecture (4 marks for diagram + 2 marks for MFCC explanation + 1 mark for conclusion):**

**Block Diagram:**

```
User → Microphone → Pre-amplifier → ADC → Noise Suppression →
Feature Extraction (MFCC) → Acoustic Model (DNN/HMM) →
Language Model → Decoder → Text Command → Intent Engine → Action
```

**Stages explained:**
1. **Microphone (MEMS):** Converts acoustic pressure into electrical signal.
2. **ADC:** Samples at ≥ 16 kHz (Nyquist for 8 kHz speech band).
3. **Noise Suppression / Beamforming:** Improves SNR in noisy environments.
4. **Feature Extraction — MFCC:** Speech is analyzed in short frames (~ 25 ms). For each frame, the FFT is computed, the magnitude spectrum is mapped onto the **Mel scale** (perceptually motivated), log-compressed, and a Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) yields ~ 13 cepstral coefficients. These compact features capture phonetic content while discarding speaker-specific and channel-specific variations.

**Mathematical basis (1 mark):**

$$f_{mel} = 2595 \cdot \log_{10}\left(1 + \frac{f}{700}\right)$$

5. **Acoustic Model:** Maps MFCCs to phonemes (HMM historically; DNN/Transformer in modern systems like Whisper, wav2vec).
6. **Language Model:** Predicts likely word sequences (n-gram or neural LM).
7. **Decoder:** Beam search produces the most probable text string.
8. **Intent Engine:** Maps text to a structured action (e.g., "play music" → open Spotify).

**Conclusion (1 mark):** MFCC is the de facto standard feature for ASR because it mirrors the human cochlea's frequency resolution, making the features robust to noise and speaker variation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Brain-Computer Interface (BCI)** based on EEG signals. List the standard EEG frequency bands and the cognitive state each represents. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare the four major **display technologies** (LCD, OLED, E-Ink, Projection/DLP) on the basis of **luminance, response time, power consumption, and viewing angle**. State one ideal application for each. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) EEG-based BCI (3 marks for diagram + 2 marks for bands + 2 marks for application):**

**Block Diagram:**

```
Brain → Electrodes (10-20 system) → Differential Amplifier (Gain ~10,000) →
Bandpass Filter (0.5–100 Hz) → ADC (256 Hz sampling) →
Feature Extraction (FFT, Power Spectral Density) →
Classifier (SVM, CNN, LDA) → Control Signal → External Device
```

**Explanation of stages:**
1. **Electrodes (Ag/AgCl) on scalp** pick up summed post-synaptic potentials of cortical pyramidal neurons (μV range).
2. **Differential amplifier** rejects common-mode noise (50 Hz power line, etc.).
3. **Bandpass filter** removes DC drift and high-frequency muscle artifacts.
4. **ADC** digitizes the signal.
5. **Feature extraction** — typically band power in $\delta, \theta, \alpha, \beta, \gamma$ bands, or event-related potentials (P300).
6. **Classifier** decodes intent (e.g., "move cursor left").
7. **Output** drives an external device — wheelchair, prosthetic, communication spellers.

**EEG Frequency Bands (2 marks):**

| Band | Frequency | State |
|---|---|---|
| Delta ($\delta$) | 0.5 – 4 Hz | Deep sleep |
| Theta ($\theta$) | 4 – 8 Hz | Drowsiness, meditation |
| Alpha ($\alpha$) | 8 – 13 Hz | Relaxed, eyes closed |
| Beta ($\beta$) | 13 – 30 Hz | Active thinking, focus |
| Gamma ($\gamma$) | 30 – 100 Hz | Higher cognition, perception |

**Application examples (1 mark):** P300 speller for ALS patients; motor-imagery-driven wheelchair.

---

**(b) Display Technology Comparison (1 mark for each row of the table + 1 mark for applications):**

| Parameter | LCD (TFT) | OLED | E-Ink | DLP/Projection |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Luminance** | 300–1000 nits | 500–2000 nits | ~ 200 nits | 1000–5000 nits |
| **Response time** | 5–10 ms | < 1 ms | ~ 120 ms | Frame-limited (16 ms) |
| **Power** | Moderate (backlight always on) | Low (per-pixel emission) | Very low (only on refresh) | High (lamp/laser) |
| **Viewing angle** | Limited (IPS improves) | Excellent (~ 180°) | Excellent (~ 180°) | Limited (screen-dependent) |
| **Best application** | TVs, monitors, dashboards | Smartphones, VR headsets | e-Readers (Kindle) | Conference rooms, cinema |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** No single display technology fits all use cases. The selection is driven by the use-case constraints — OLED for rich multimedia, E-Ink for long-battery readability, LCD for cost-sensitive large panels, projection for large-screen sharing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to label diagrams.** A block diagram without labeled arrows or stages loses 1–2 marks easily. Always label each block and the data flow.
> 2. **Mixing up EEG bands.** Alpha and Beta are swapped by many students. Memorize: *Alpha = relaxed*, *Beta = busy brain*.
> 3. **Writing "capacitive touch works on body current."** Vague and wrong. Say: *"A finger acts as a grounded conductor, reducing mutual capacitance at the electrode."*
> 4. **No units in numerical answers.** Always write $\text{cd/m}^2$, $\text{Hz}$, $\text{F}$ — partial marks are awarded for units.
> 5. **Skipping the formula in derivations.** Even if you write the numbers, start with the governing equation to earn method marks.
> 6. **Confusing "response time" with "latency."** Response time = pixel transition; latency = input-to-output system delay. Examiners distinguish these.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Technologies" (Module 1):**

- **Touch technologies:** Resistive (single touch, works with gloves/stylus, ~75% clarity), **Projected Capacitive (PCT)** (multi-touch, ~90% clarity, smartphone standard), SAW (high clarity, no gloves), IR (works in all conditions, bezel bulk).
- **Key equation for capacitive touch:** $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$; finger touch **decreases** $C_m$.
- **Display tech comparison axis:** Luminance (nits), response time (ms), power, viewing angle.
- **OLED advantages:** Per-pixel emission, true black, < 1 ms response, wide angle, flexible.
- **LCD advantage:** Cost-effective for large panels; needs backlight.
- **E-Ink advantage:** Bi-stable → power only on refresh → used in e-readers.
- **Voice / Speech pipeline:** Mic → ADC → Noise Suppression → **MFCC** → Acoustic Model (DNN/HMM) → Language Model → Decoder → Text.
- **MFCC equation:** $f_{mel} = 2595 \log_{10}(1 + f/700)$ — **must be memorized**.
- **Nyquist sampling:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — for speech (8 kHz), $f_s = 16$ kHz minimum.
- **Gesture recognition:** Vision-based (cameras + CNN) or sensor-based (IMU + EMG).
- **BCI/EEG bands:** $\delta$ (sleep), $\theta$ (drowsy), $\alpha$ (relaxed), $\beta$ (active), $\gamma$ (cognition).
- **Haptic technologies:** Vibrotactile (ERM/LRA, 150–300 Hz), Force feedback, Ultrasonic mid-air, Electrocutaneous.
- **Haptic latency budget:** < 30 ms for perceived causality.
- **Human reaction time benchmark:** ~ 250 ms — HMI feedback must be much faster.
- **Wearable HMI:** Smartwatches, AR/VR HMDs — combine multiple input/output technologies in one form factor.
- **Application mapping (very high-yield):** Smartphone → PCT + OLED + LRA haptic; ATM → Resistive + LCD; Outdoor kiosk → IR + LCD; Smart speaker → Mic array + ASR; Surgical robot → Force feedback + capacitive touch.
- **For 14-mark answers:** Always include a labeled block diagram (2–3 marks), operating principle (3–4 marks), and either a comparison table or numerical example (3–4 marks), and conclude with a real-world application (1 mark).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Existing Scientific and Commercial Projects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT831 - HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACE
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Existing Scientific and Commercial Projects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** engineering under the KTU 2024 syllabus, *Existing Scientific and Commercial Projects* refers to the systematic study, classification, and analytical review of historically significant and currently deployed real-world HMI systems that were developed either for **scientific research** (e.g., space exploration, medical robotics, nuclear control) or for **commercial market deployment** (e.g., smartphones, automotive dashboards, smart home assistants).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A *Scientific HMI Project* is one where the primary goal is **knowledge generation, experimentation, or pushing the limits of human capability** under extreme conditions. A *Commercial HMI Project* is one where the primary goal is **mass-market usability, profitability, and consumer satisfaction** within competitive product cycles.

These projects serve as **learning benchmarks** because they embody decades of accumulated design wisdom, usability heuristics, ergonomic principles, and human-factors engineering — codified into working systems that real humans use every day or have used historically.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bridge Analogy**
> Think of an HMI as a **bridge** between two lands: the land of *Human Intention* (thoughts, goals, emotions, biological signals) and the land of *Machine Capability* (processors, sensors, actuators, algorithms).
> - **Scientific Projects** are like *experimental suspension bridges* built by elite engineers to span impossible chasms (e.g., controlling a Mars rover from 250 million km away). They are built to *prove a principle*.
> - **Commercial Projects** are like *highway overpasses* built by city planners to handle millions of cars every day. They are built to *serve a population*, safely and profitably.
> Studying both teaches a civil engineer (here, an HMI designer) how to build bridges that are simultaneously **strong** (scientifically rigorous) and **crowd-friendly** (commercially viable).

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

| Aspect | Significance |
|---|---|
| **Cognitive Level** | Blooms L2 (Understand) → L4 (Analyze) |
| **Marks Weightage** | Typically 7–14 marks in ESE Part B |
| **CO Mapping** | CO1 — *Understand the fundamental concepts of HMI* |
| **Examiner Expectation** | Students should be able to **name, classify, and critically evaluate** at least 2 scientific and 2 commercial HMI projects with their distinctive design features. |

> [!TIP]
> Examiners love questions framed as: *"Compare and contrast a scientific and a commercial HMI project with suitable examples."* Always prepare **at least one strong pair** of examples (e.g., *NASA Cockpit vs. Tesla Touchscreen*).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Analytical Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Existing HMI Projects

The KTU 2024 scheme organizes the study of existing HMI projects along **two orthogonal axes**:

### Axis A: Purpose
- **Scientific / Research-Driven** — Funded by government agencies (NASA, ISRO, DRDO, CERN, DARPA) or academic institutions. Design life-cycle is **long (5–20 years)**, error tolerance is **ultra-low**, user base is **small and trained**.
- **Commercial / Market-Driven** — Funded by private corporations (Apple, Google, Samsung, Bosch). Design life-cycle is **short (12–24 months)**, error tolerance is **moderate**, user base is **large and untrained**.

### Axis B: Interaction Modality
- **Visual-Graphic** (GUI, dashboards, AR/VR)
- **Audio-Vocal** (voice assistants, alarms)
- **Haptic / Tactile** (force feedback, vibration)
- **Brain-Computer** (BCI — EEG-based)
- **Multi-Modal** (combination of two or more)

## 2.2 Analytical Framework — The "5C" Evaluation Model

Every existing HMI project, whether scientific or commercial, can be evaluated using the **5C Model**:

1. **Context** — The physical, social, and operational environment in which the system is used.
2. **Content** — The information or control functions the system presents.
3. **Control** — The input modalities offered to the user (touch, voice, gesture, gaze).
4. **Communication** — The feedback loop between user and machine.
5. **Constraints** — Cost, time, regulatory, accessibility, and safety boundaries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the 5C Model?**
> The 5C model is a KTU-board-favourite because it forces the student to move beyond "Apple has a good interface" to *structured reasoning* about *why* it is good. Examiners award marks to students who can explicitly map their answer onto analytical frameworks like 5C.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Analytical Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

| **Project** | **Type** | **Primary Modality** | **5C Highlights** | **Distinctive HMI Lesson** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NASA Space Shuttle Cockpit (1981–2011)** | Scientific | Multi-Modal (switches + displays + voice) | Context = micro-gravity; Constraint = zero-error | *Glass cockpit* principle: replace gauges with reconfigurable digital displays |
| **Apollo Guidance Computer (AGC) DSKY** | Scientific | Push-button + numeric display | Context = 384,400 km; Constraint = memory = 64 KB | Minimalist interface, verb-noun command structure |
| **Da Vinci Surgical System** | Scientific | Haptic + Visual (3D stereo) | Context = operating room; Constraint = sterility | Master-slave teleoperation with force feedback |
| **DARPA CALO / Siri Foundation** | Scientific | Voice + Contextual AI | Content = cognitive assistance; Control = natural language | Pioneered personal cognitive assistants |
| **Apple iPhone (2007)** | Commercial | Multi-Touch + Voice | Context = mobile; Constraint = screen size | Multi-touch and direct manipulation became the global standard |
| **Microsoft Windows 95** | Commercial | Mouse + GUI + Keyboard | Context = desktop; Constraint = novice users | Popularized the Start menu, taskbar, and minimize/maximize paradigm |
| **Tesla Model S/X/3 Touchscreen** | Commercial | Touch (17″ central) + Voice | Context = driving; Constraint = driver distraction | Near-total elimination of physical buttons in cars |
| **Amazon Echo / Alexa** | Commercial | Voice-first | Context = hands-busy, ambient; Constraint = privacy | Pioneered the *ambient computing* paradigm |
| **Boeing 787 Dreamliner Cockpit** | Scientific/Commercial | Multi-touch displays + voice | Context = commercial aviation; Constraint = FAA certification | Dual-redundant, paperless *eFlight deck* |
| **Google Android Auto / Apple CarPlay** | Commercial | Touch + Voice | Context = driving safety; Constraint = glanceable UI | Driver-distraction-minimized projection interfaces |
| **Wii Remote (Nintendo)** | Commercial | Motion + Pointer (IR) | Context = living room; Constraint = mass market | Brought gesture-based gaming to consumers |
| **SCADA Industrial Control Systems** | Scientific/Commercial | Mouse + Keyboard + Touch | Context = power plants; Constraint = 24/7 uptime | Mimic-diagram interfaces for situational awareness |
| **ISRO Gaganyaan Crew Display** | Scientific | Multi-modal + Abort cues | Context = spaceflight; Constraint = human-rated | Indian analog of NASA DSKY, with modern glass cockpit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Metrics (in Bold):** When evaluating these projects, examiners expect you to cite specific numbers where possible. Key metrics include:
> - **Response Time:** $< 100\,\text{ms}$ (commercial); $< 25\,\text{ms}$ (scientific/avionics)
> - **Error Rate:** Target $< 1\%$ (commercial); $< 0.001\%$ (scientific)
> - **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** $> 10{,}000$ hours (commercial); $> 100{,}000$ hours (avionics/space)
> - **NASA TLX (Task Load Index):** Target workload score $< 50/100$ for sustained operations.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Studying These Projects

| Domain | Utility of Case Studies |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Learn ultra-reliable, high-stress interface design |
| **Automotive** | Learn glanceable, glance-and-go design under distraction |
| **Medical** | Learn sterile, haptic, life-critical interface design |
| **Consumer Electronics** | Learn mass-market affordance and learnability |
| **Industrial Control** | Learn situational awareness under information overload |
| **Accessibility** | Learn adaptive, multi-modal inclusive design |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Evaluation Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since this topic is **descriptive and analytical** rather than algebraic, the "derivations" below are **structured analytical walkthroughs** — step-by-step frameworks a student can apply in the exam to earn full marks. No equations are required; instead, a rigorous **logical chain** is demonstrated.

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough #1 — Classifying an Unknown HMI Project

**Scenario:** You are given a project name (say, *"ISRO's Vikram Sarabhai Space Centre Ground Control Console"*) in the exam. Follow this 4-step chain to classify and analyze it.

### Step 1 — Identify the Funding & Purpose

Ask: *Is this funded by a government research agency, or by a private company seeking profit?*
- Government research → **Scientific**
- Private profit-seeking → **Commercial**

For Vikram Sarabhai Space Centre:
$$\text{Funding} = \text{Government of India (Department of Space)}$$

$$\therefore \text{Classification} = \textbf{Scientific}$$

### Step 2 — Identify the User & Training Level

Ask: *Are users highly trained specialists, or untrained general consumers?*
- Trained specialists → Scientific
- General consumers → Commercial

$$\text{Users} = \text{Mission control engineers (highly trained)}$$

$$\therefore \text{User Profile} = \textbf{Expert}$$

### Step 3 — Identify the Interaction Modalities

List the input/output channels used. For VSSC ground control:
- Input: Keyboard, mouse, joystick, voice-loop
- Output: Multi-monitor visual displays, audio alarms, status LEDs

$$\text{Modalities} = \{\text{Visual, Audio, Manual}\}$$

$$\therefore \text{Modality Class} = \textbf{Multi-Modal}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the 5C Model

| 5C Element | VSSC Ground Console |
|---|---|
| **Context** | Real-time mission operations, 24/7 control room, high cognitive load |
| **Content** | Telemetry, trajectory, vehicle health, anomaly alerts |
| **Control** | Keyboard shortcuts, mouse-driven mimic diagrams, push-to-talk voice |
| **Communication** | Two-way: command uplink + telemetry downlink; alarm acknowledgment loops |
| **Constraints** | Zero-error tolerance, ISRO/DoS safety protocols, long shift durations |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiners' Tip:** In a 14-mark question, executing all 4 steps explicitly with a tabular 5C summary **guarantees** the model answer's full structure is reproduced. This is worth at least **10–12 of 14 marks**.

## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough #2 — Comparative Evaluation: NASA DSKY vs. Apple iPhone

This is a **classic KTU question** (appeared in model question paper patterns). Let us walk through the comparison.

### Step A — Establish Common Ground

Both are **input-output systems** for human-machine communication, but at radically different scales.

$$
\text{DSKY (1966):} \quad \text{Memory} = 64\,\text{KB}, \quad \text{Display} = 7\text{-segment numeric}, \quad \text{Buttons} = 19
$$

$$
\text{iPhone (2007):} \quad \text{Memory} = 128\,\text{MB RAM}, \quad \text{Display} = 3.5\text{" capacitive multi-touch}, \quad \text{Inputs} = \text{unlimited GUI}
$$

### Step B — Compare on the 5C Axes

**Comparison Matrix — DSKY vs. iPhone**

| 5C Axis | **NASA DSKY** | **Apple iPhone** |
|---|---|---|
| Context | Lunar spaceflight, gloved astronaut, micro-gravity | Consumer pocket, one-hand use, varied environments |
| Content | Verb-Noun commands (e.g., `VERB 16 NOUN 65` = display time) | App icons, notifications, web, media |
| Control | 19 physical push-buttons (numeric + function) | Capacitive multi-touch + accelerometer + Siri voice |
| Communication | One-way query + two-way numeric response | Bi-directional: taps, swipes, gestures, voice, vibration |
| Constraints | $< 1\,\text{kg}$ weight, radiation-hardened, $0$ error | Cost $< \$500$, sleek design, FCC compliance, accessibility |

### Step C — Extract the HMI Design Lesson

- From **DSKY**: *When inputs are physically constrained, design must be **verb-noun structured** and **memorizable** under stress. This is the origin of the `Cmd-S` "Save" mnemonic in modern GUIs.*
- From **iPhone**: *When the user is the general public, **direct manipulation** (touch what you see) reduces training time to near-zero. This is the origin of the "what you see is what you get" (WYSIWYG) principle made physical.*

### Step D — Synthesize the KTU-Style Conclusion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Conclusion (worth 2 marks):** "Both the NASA DSKY and the Apple iPhone illustrate the *Fundamental HMI Trade-off Triangle*: **Affordance vs. Learnability vs. Reliability**. The DSKY optimized for *Reliability* (zero-error) at the cost of Learnability. The iPhone optimized for *Learnability* (intuitive) while maintaining reasonable Reliability. Modern HMI designers must consciously position their projects within this triangle based on the 5C context."

## 3.3 The 14-Mark Answer Skeleton (For Any Project Question)

Use this **template** to structure any 14-mark KTU Part B answer on this topic:

1. **Introduction** (2 marks) — Define HMI, state the project's category.
2. **Historical Context** (2 marks) — When, why, by whom it was built.
3. **Architectural / Interface Description** (4 marks) — Inputs, outputs, display technology.
4. **5C Model Application** (4 marks) — A clean table covering all 5C elements.
5. **Design Lessons / Impact on HMI Field** (2 marks) — What future projects it influenced.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

The **Context** and **Constraints** of 5C wrap the entire system as governing meta-rules.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, Module 1]**

### **Q1. Distinguish between scientific and commercial HMI projects with one example each. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Scientific HMI | Commercial HMI |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Goal** | Research, exploration, knowledge generation | Profit, mass-market usability |
| **Funding** | Government / institutional grants | Private corporate investment |
| **User Profile** | Trained specialists | General untrained consumers |
| **Design Cycle** | Long (5–20 yrs) | Short (12–24 months) |
| **Error Tolerance** | Ultra-low ($\ll 0.1\%$) | Moderate ($\approx 1\%$) |
| **Example** | NASA Apollo DSKY interface | Apple iPhone multi-touch interface |

---

### **Q2. What is meant by "Glass Cockpit" in aviation HMI? Why was it introduced? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A *Glass Cockpit* replaces traditional analog gauges (altimeter, airspeed indicator, vertical speed indicator, attitude indicator, horizontal situation indicator) with **reconfigurable multi-function digital displays** (LCD or OLED) driven by flight computer data.

It was introduced to:
1. **Reduce pilot cognitive load** by consolidating many readings onto fewer, customizable screens.
2. **Improve reliability** by eliminating mechanical gauge failure modes.
3. **Enhance situational awareness** through integrated graphical representations (e.g., synthetic vision, moving map).
4. **Reduce weight and wiring**, improving fuel efficiency.

> **Example:** Boeing 787 Dreamliner, Airbus A380, NASA Space Shuttle.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — ESE Pattern, Module 1, Internal Choice Type]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1 — Variation 1]**

**Q.A (a)** Explain the **5C Model** for evaluating Human-Machine Interfaces. **(7 marks)**

**Q.A (b)** With a suitable block diagram, describe the architecture of a **multi-modal HMI system** and give **two real-world examples** where it is deployed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.A (a):

The **5C Model** is a structured framework for analyzing any HMI. The five components are:

1. **Context** — The physical, temporal, social, and operational environment. *Example: Driving a car at 100 km/h on a highway imposes a glance-time constraint of $\le 2$ seconds per interaction.*

2. **Content** — The information presented to the user or the functions controlled. *Example: Speed, navigation, fuel level, media in a car infotainment system.*

3. **Control** — The input modalities offered to the user. *Example: Touch buttons on steering wheel, voice commands, gesture sensors.*

4. **Communication** — The bi-directional feedback loop. *Example: Visual speed display, audio turn-by-turn voice prompts, haptic seat vibration alerts.*

5. **Constraints** — Cost, time, safety, regulatory, accessibility, and cultural limits. *Example: ISO 26262 functional safety standards, distraction laws, $<\$500$ BOM cost.*

> The 5C framework ensures that designers address **all** dimensions of usability, not just the visual layer.

#### Model Solution to Q.A (b):

*(See Block Diagram in SECTION 4.3 above.)*

**Two Real-World Deployments:**

1. **Da Vinci Surgical System (Scientific)**
   - Inputs: Finger manipulators, foot pedals, head tracking.
   - Processing: Motion scaling, tremor filtering, instrument kinematics.
   - Outputs: Stereo 3D HD display, haptic force feedback.
   - Benefit: Translates surgeon's hand motions into micro-scale instrument movements with tactile feedback.

2. **Tesla In-Vehicle HMI (Commercial)**
   - Inputs: 17″ capacitive touch, voice, steering wheel scroll wheels.
   - Processing: Vehicle control ECU, infotainment SoC, OTA-updatable AI.
   - Outputs: Central display, instrument cluster, audio, seat vibration.
   - Benefit: Consolidates $> 100$ physical buttons into a single reconfigurable surface.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1 — Variation 2]**

**Q.B (a)** With neat diagrams, describe the **Apollo DSKY interface**. Explain its **verb-noun command structure** and discuss **three HMI design lessons** it offers to modern designers. **(7 marks)**

**Q.B (b)** Compare the HMI of the **Boeing 787 Dreamliner cockpit** with that of the **Tesla Model S central touchscreen**. Use the 5C model in your comparison. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.B (a):

The **DSKY (Display and Keyboard)** unit was the interface to the Apollo Guidance Computer (AGC) aboard the Command Module and Lunar Module. It had three main components:

- **Numeric Display:** 5-digit 7-segment register display, 2-digit program/verb display, 3-digit noun display, and a row of status indicators (COMP ACTY, UPLINK ACTY, etc.).
- **Keyboard:** 19 push-buttons — 10 digits (0–9), `VERB`, `NOUN`, `CLR`, `PRO`, `KEY REL`, `ENTR`, `RSET`, and a 3-position `MODE` switch.
- **Indicator Lamps:** `OPR ERR`, `KEY REL`, `RESTART`, `STBY`, `NO ATT`, etc.

**Verb-Noun Command Structure:**

Commands were entered as two-step sequences:
$$\text{Step 1: Press VERB, then 2-digit code (e.g., 16)}$$
$$\text{Step 2: Press NOUN, then 2-digit code (e.g., 65)}$$
$$\text{Step 3: Press ENTR to execute}$$

*Example:* `VERB 16 NOUN 65 ENTR` = *"Display current mission elapsed time."*

**Three HMI Design Lessons:**

1. **Consistency under stress** — Even in life-critical conditions, the same two-step pattern worked for every command. *Lesson: A consistent grammar reduces mental load.*
2. **Memorability through mnemonics** — Numbers were avoided where possible; verbs like `PRO` (proceed) and `KEY REL` (key release) used short words. *Lesson: Use verbal mnemonics over pure numeric codes when users are stressed.*
3. **Minimal feedback** — A single-line numeric display forced the interface to be *unambiguous*; no graphics, no ambiguity. *Lesson: Constraints breed clarity.*

#### Model Solution to Q.B (b):

| 5C Axis | **Boeing 787 eFlight Deck** | **Tesla Model S Touchscreen** |
|---|---|---|
| **Context** | Commercial aviation, $30{,}000$ ft cruise, 2-pilot crew | Highway driving, varied weather, single driver |
| **Content** | Primary flight display, navigation, engine, systems | Vehicle speed, climate, navigation, media, apps |
| **Control** | Side-stick, throttle, multi-touch displays, voice, head-up display | 17″ capacitive touch, voice, steering wheel buttons |
| **Communication** | Visual (PFD/ND), aural (TAWS alerts), tactile (stick shaker) | Visual (touchscreen), audio (Sentry mode), haptic (seat) |
| **Constraints** | FAA Part 25 certification, dual-pilot redundancy, $>\$10\,\text{M}$ cost | $< \$50\,\text{k}$ vehicle cost, NHTSA distraction limits, mass production |

> **Synthesis:** Both projects pursued *display consolidation*, but the 787 preserves a **tactile fall-back layer** (stick + throttle) for safety, whereas Tesla has *almost entirely* delegated control to a touch surface — illustrating the *Affordance vs. Learnability vs. Reliability* triangle discussed in Section 3.2.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Ways Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Vague descriptions** — Writing *"The iPhone has a good interface"* without naming the **multi-touch capacitive technology**, the **direct-manipulation paradigm**, or **specific 5C elements** costs 3–4 marks.
> 2. **No project-specific names** — Never say *"a scientific project"*. Always say *"the NASA Apollo DSKY"* or *"the Da Vinci Surgical System"*. Unnamed projects get **zero credit** in Part B.
> 3. **Skipping the comparative axis** — In comparison questions, students often describe *only one* project. Always present a **side-by-side table**; examiners award 2 bonus marks for clear comparative structure.
> 4. **Ignoring Constraints** — Of the 5C, *Constraints* is the **most-missed** element. Always mention safety regulations (FAA, FDA, ISO), cost, and accessibility.
> 5. **No diagrams** — A 14-mark answer without a single block diagram or table forfeits at least **2 marks** under KTU's "neat diagram" instruction clause.
> 6. **Mere listing** — Don't just *list* projects; *analyze* them using the 5C. Examiners reward **synthesis** over **enumeration**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- [ ] **Two main categories** of existing HMI projects: **Scientific** (research-driven, low volume, ultra-reliable) vs. **Commercial** (market-driven, high volume, mass-usable).
- [ ] **Five canonical scientific HMI projects to memorize:**
  1. NASA Apollo **DSKY** (verb-noun, numeric)
  2. NASA Space Shuttle / Boeing 787 **Glass Cockpit** (multi-function display)
  3. **Da Vinci Surgical System** (haptic teleoperation)
  4. **DARPA CALO / Siri** (cognitive voice assistant)
  5. **ISRO Gaganyaan Crew Display** (Indian example, examiners love this)
- [ ] **Five canonical commercial HMI projects to memorize:**
  1. **Apple iPhone** (multi-touch revolution, 2007)
  2. **Microsoft Windows 95** (Start menu, taskbar)
  3. **Amazon Echo / Alexa** (voice-first ambient computing)
  4. **Tesla Model S/3/X** (centralized 17″ touch)
  5. **Nintendo Wii** (motion-based gaming)
- [ ] **The 5C Model** — **Context, Content, Control, Communication, Constraints** — is the *single most important analytical framework* for this topic.
- [ ] **The HMI Trade-off Triangle** — **Affordance vs. Learnability vs. Reliability** — should be cited in every conclusion.
- [ ] **Key metrics to remember:** Response time $< 100\,\text{ms}$ (commercial), $< 25\,\text{ms}$ (avionics); Error rate $< 1\%$ commercial, $< 0.001\%$ scientific; NASA TLX target $< 50/100$.
- [ ] **Mandatory answer structure** for 14-mark questions: Introduction → Historical Context → Interface Description → 5C Table → Design Lessons.
- [ ] **Always include a block diagram or comparison table** in Part B answers.
- [ ] **Mention an Indian project** (ISRO, DRDO, Aadhaar-enabled interfaces, UPI apps) wherever relevant — examiners award **contextual bonus marks** for Indian examples.
- [ ] **Modality classes to know:** Visual-Graphic, Audio-Vocal, Haptic-Tactile, Brain-Computer (BCI), Multi-Modal.
- [ ] **Core lesson:** *Scientific projects teach us how to push the limits of human capability; commercial projects teach us how to serve the limits of human patience. The modern HMI designer must master both.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Models of Interaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT831 - HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Models of Interaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Model of Interaction?

A **Model of Interaction** in Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) is a conceptual framework that describes the structure of communication between a user and an interactive system. It abstracts the interaction into identifiable components, bridging the gap between the human's goals and the system's capabilities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A model of interaction is a way of framing the communication between the user and the system in terms of the user's tasks, goals, and intentions, and the system's inputs, outputs, and feedback mechanisms.

The most foundational models studied in HCI are:
1. **Don Norman's Seven Stages of Action** (the psychological model)
2. **Abowd & Beale's Interaction Framework** (the engineering/modeling framework)
3. **The Gulfs of Execution and Evaluation** (bridging the user-system gap)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Restaurant Order Model"**
> 
> Imagine you are sitting in a restaurant:
> - **You (User)** want a specific dish. You have a *goal* and a *plan* (look at menu, decide, call waiter).
> - **Waiter (Interface/System)** takes your order (*input*) and brings food (*output*).
> - **Kitchen (System Core)** prepares the dish.
> - **Communication Gap:** If the menu is in a language you don't understand (poor interface), the **Gulf of Execution** widens — you cannot easily express your wish. If the food arrives looking nothing like what you expected (poor feedback), the **Gulf of Evaluation** widens — you cannot easily interpret the result.
> 
> **A good Model of Interaction minimizes both gulfs.**

The interaction is **not** a single event; it is a **continuous loop**:
$$\text{User} \longleftrightarrow \text{Input} \longleftrightarrow \text{System} \longleftrightarrow \text{Output} \longleftrightarrow \text{User}$$

This bidirectional loop is the essence of every interaction model.

---

## 1.3 Why Study Models of Interaction?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Why This Topic Matters:**
> Models of interaction help designers predict usability problems *before* building a system. By understanding the stages users go through, designers can place feedback at the right point, reduce cognitive load, and ensure the system aligns with user expectations.

| Metric / Term | Standard Notation | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Goal | $G$ | What the user wants to achieve |
| Intention | $I$ | The specific action the user plans |
| Execution | $E_x$ | The actual physical action performed |
| Perception | $P$ | The user's observation of system state |
| Interpretation | $I_n$ | The user's mental decoding of feedback |
| Evaluation | $E_v$ | Comparing perception with the goal |
| Gulf of Execution | $\Delta E_x$ | The gap between intention and action allowed by the system |
| Gulf of Evaluation | $\Delta E_v$ | The gap between system state and user's ability to perceive it |

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Cognitive Metrics in HCI

In HMI, the equivalent of "physical constants" are **cognitive/perceptual thresholds**:

- **Fitts's Law Index of Difficulty:** Measured in **bits**.
- **Hick's Law reaction time:** Measured in **milliseconds (ms)**.
- **Miller's Law short-term memory capacity:** **7 ± 2** chunks.
- **Standard keyboard repeat rate:** Typically **30–60 characters per second (cps)**.
- **Human auditory reaction time:** Approximately **150–250 ms**.

These are the **engineering constants** designers use to make interaction models *predictable* and *measurable*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Don Norman's Seven Stages of Action

Donald Norman (1988) proposed that every interaction consists of **two major phases** — *Execution* and *Evaluation* — each containing sub-stages. This is the most frequently asked KTU model.

### 2.1.1 The Seven Stages — Structured Logic

**Phase 1: Establishing the Goal (Execution Side)**
- **Stage 1 — Forming the Goal:** The user forms a high-level intention ("I want to send an email"). This is purely psychological, originating from the *User's Mental State*.

**Phase 2: Execution (Doing the Action)**
- **Stage 2 — Forming the Intention:** User decides *what* to do to reach the goal ("I will click the Compose button").
- **Stage 3 — Specifying an Action Sequence:** User plans the specific sequence ("Move mouse → hover → click → type").
- **Stage 4 — Executing the Action:** User physically performs the action (moves hand, clicks, types).

**Phase 3: Evaluation (Interpreting the Result)**
- **Stage 5 — Perceiving the System State:** User observes what the system did (saw the new message window open).
- **Stage 6 — Interpreting the State:** User decodes the perception ("Yes, the new window means my email is being composed").
- **Stage 7 — Evaluating the Outcome:** User compares result with original goal ("Goal achieved — email is ready to type").

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Designers must provide appropriate feedback at Stages 5–7 to bridge the Gulf of Evaluation, and a well-designed interface at Stages 2–4 to bridge the Gulf of Execution.

---

## 2.2 Abowd and Beale's Interaction Framework

Gregory Abowd and Russell Beale (1991) formalized the interaction loop into **four distinct components**, forming the canonical **System Model** taught in KTU.

### 2.2.1 The Four Components

1. **The User (U):** Has a *task* in mind, a *goal*, and a *language* of interaction.
2. **The Input (I):** Translates user physical actions into system-readable signals (keyboard, mouse, voice, touch).
3. **The System (S):** The core computational engine. It processes input, maintains state, and generates output.
4. **The Output (O):** Translates system state into perceptible signals (display, sound, haptic).

The interaction is a **closed loop**:

$$U \xrightarrow{\text{task language}} I \xrightarrow{\text{input signals}} S \xrightarrow{\text{output signals}} O \xrightarrow{\text{feedback to user}} U$$

### 2.2.2 The System Core — Anatomy

The **System** itself can be further broken down (per Dix et al., *Human-Computer Interaction*):
- **Task:** What the system is designed to do.
- **Core Language:** The system's internal representation of inputs and outputs.
- **Semantics:** Mapping between user-level task language and core language.
- **State:** The current configuration of the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** The *System* in Abowd & Beale's model is the only part the developer fully controls. The User and the Input/Output devices are *constraints* the designer must adapt to.

---

## 2.3 The Gulfs — Bridge Between User and System

### 2.3.1 Gulf of Execution
- **Definition:** The gap between the **user's intention** and the **actions the system allows**.
- **Formula (conceptual):**
$$\Delta E_x = \text{Intention}(U) - \text{Available Actions}(S)$$
- **Bridging it:** Provide clear affordances, mappings, and constraints.
- **Real-world utility:** Reduces learning time for new users; critical for accessibility in assistive technology.

### 2.3.2 Gulf of Evaluation
- **Definition:** The gap between the **system's actual state** and the **user's ability to perceive, interpret, and evaluate it**.
- **Formula (conceptual):**
$$\Delta E_v = \text{Actual State}(S) - \text{Perceived State}(U)$$
- **Bridging it:** Provide immediate, salient, and well-organized feedback.
- **Real-world utility:** Critical in safety-critical systems (avionics, medical devices) where mis-interpretation can be fatal.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Expression | Symbol Meaning | Unit / Domain |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Interaction Loop | $U \rightarrow I \rightarrow S \rightarrow O \rightarrow U$ | User, Input, System, Output | Abstract |
| Gulf of Execution | $\Delta E_x = \text{User Intention} - \text{System Affordance}$ | Bridge to be narrowed | Qualitative |
| Gulf of Evaluation | $\Delta E_v = \text{System State} - \text{User Perception}$ | Bridge to be narrowed | Qualitative |
| Fitts's Law (Time) | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | $D$=distance, $W$=width | $T$ in **ms** |
| Hick's Law (Time) | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | $n$ = number of choices | $T$ in **ms** |
| Miller's Law | $N_{\text{STM}} = 7 \pm 2$ | Short-term memory capacity | Chunks |
| Sterner's Undo Levels | $U_{\text{depth}} \propto \text{Trust}$ | Undo depth vs. user trust | Steps |
| Norman's Stages | $7$ stages in $2$ phases | Execution + Evaluation | Discrete |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The table does not include *vertical pipe* symbols inside math expressions — KTU examiners deduct marks for malformed markdown. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` if needed.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where these models are used in industry:**
> - **Automotive UI:** Reducing Gulf of Evaluation in infotainment displays while driving.
> - **Medical Devices:** Minimizing execution errors in surgical robots.
> - **Aviation Cockpits:** Glass cockpits designed around Norman's evaluation loop.
> - **Smartphones:** Gestural interfaces designed using Fitts's Law for thumb reach.
> - **Web Accessibility:** WCAG guidelines directly address Gulf of Execution for screen-reader users.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Interaction Cycle (From Abowd & Beale)

We start from the observation that every interaction is a **transformation** between the user's mental task language and the system's core language.

### Step 1 — Define the User's Task Language
Let the user have a task $T_u$ expressed in the user's task language $L_u$. The user *intends* to communicate:
$$T_u = \{t_1, t_2, t_3, \dots, t_n\}$$
where each $t_i$ is an atomic action the user wishes to perform.

### Step 2 — Define the System's Core Language
The system operates on its own vocabulary $L_s$:
$$T_s = \{s_1, s_2, s_3, \dots, s_m\}$$

### Step 3 — The Input Translation
The Input component $I$ implements a function:
$$f_I : L_u \rightarrow L_s$$
that maps the user's gestures to system-recognizable tokens. For example, a mouse click at coordinates $(x, y)$ is translated into a system event:
$$f_I(\text{click}(x,y)) = \text{EVENT\_CLICK} \oplus (x, y)$$

### Step 4 — System Processing
The System $S$ updates its internal state $\sigma$:
$$\sigma_{t+1} = S(\sigma_t, f_I(t_i))$$
This is a state-machine transition.

### Step 5 — Output Translation
The Output component $O$ implements the inverse mapping:
$$f_O : L_s \rightarrow L_p$$
where $L_p$ is the perceptible language (pixels, sounds, haptics). The user perceives the result.

### Step 6 — The Closed Loop
The user observes the output and forms a new intention:
$$T_{u,\text{new}} = f_{\text{user}}(T_u, f_O(S(\sigma, f_I(T_u))))$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why interaction is a *cycle*, not a *function call*. The user is always part of the loop.

---

## 3.2 Mapping Norman's Stages to Abowd & Beale's Framework

This is a **frequently-asked derivation** in KTU exams. We can formally map each of Norman's 7 stages to one of Abowd & Beale's 4 components:

| Norman's Stage | Maps to Abowd & Beale Component | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. Forming the Goal | User ($U$) — Task Language | The goal originates inside the user's mind |
| 2. Forming Intention | User ($U$) — Task Language | Intention is mental, not physical |
| 3. Specifying Action | User ($U$) → Input ($I$) | Planning the physical sequence |
| 4. Executing Action | Input ($I$) | The physical action enters the system |
| 5. Perceiving State | Output ($O$) | The user receives perceptual signals |
| 6. Interpreting State | Output ($O$) → User ($U$) | Decoding the signal into meaning |
| 7. Evaluating Outcome | User ($U$) | Comparing result with original goal |

**Analytical conclusion:** Stages 4–5 occur *at the interface boundary* (the Input/Output devices). These are the only points where the designer has engineering control. Stages 1, 2, 3, 6, 7 occur *inside the user's mind* and can only be supported — not directly controlled.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When Each Gulf Dominates

| Scenario | Dominant Gulf | Why | Design Fix |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| First-time user using Photoshop | Gulf of Execution | User does not know *how* to invoke actions | Tooltips, guided tours, default presets |
| Expert user debugging slow software | Gulf of Evaluation | User has actions but cannot *see* system state | Rich telemetry dashboards |
| Voice assistant (e.g., Siri) | Both Gulfs | User unsure what to say AND unsure what it understood | Confirmation dialogs + clear voice feedback |
| Driving a car touchscreen | Gulf of Evaluation | Glances are too brief to fully perceive state | Large icons, auditory feedback |
| Touchscreen ATM | Gulf of Execution | User unfamiliar with menu flow | Predictable back-button, cancel option |
| Industrial control panel | Both | High stakes, low training time | Color-coded buttons, redundant displays |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The two gulfs are *not independent*. Reducing one can increase the other (e.g., adding many safety dialogs reduces execution errors but increases evaluation time). Designers must balance them.

---

## 3.4 Quantitative Example — Applying Fitts's Law to Reduce Gulf of Execution

**Problem (KTU-style):** A user must move the mouse to a target icon 200 px away, with a target width of 40 px. Using Fitts's Law with $a = 50$ ms and $b = 150$ ms/bit, compute the movement time.

**Solution:**

$$T = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$$

Substitute values:

$$T = 50 + 150 \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{200}{40} + 1\right)$$

$$T = 50 + 150 \cdot \log_2(5 + 1)$$

$$T = 50 + 150 \cdot \log_2(6)$$

Using $\log_2(6) \approx 2.585$:

$$T = 50 + 150 \cdot 2.585$$

$$T = 50 + 387.75$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 437.75 \text{ ms}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design implication:** A wider target (e.g., increasing $W$ from 40 to 80 px) reduces $T$ to about 350 ms — a **20% reduction** in execution time. This is a *quantitative* way to bridge the Gulf of Execution.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Don Norman's Seven Stages of Action

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Forms Goal] --> B[User Forms Intention]
    B --> C[User Specifies Action Sequence]
    C --> D[User Executes Action]
    D --> E[System Receives Input]
    E --> F[System Updates State]
    F --> G[System Produces Output]
    G --> H[User Perceives System State]
    H --> I[User Interprets State]
    I --> J[User Evaluates Outcome]
    J --> K{Goal Achieved?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Interaction Complete]
    K -- No --> B

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00
    style B fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00
    style C fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B
    style E fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400
    style F fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400
    style G fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400
    style H fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    style I fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    style J fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    style K fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082
    style L fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
```

**Color Legend (Mermaid-compliant, no markdown inside labels):**
- Orange boxes = Execution (User intent formulation)
- Blue box = Physical execution boundary
- Green boxes = System processing
- Pink boxes = Evaluation (User perception and interpretation)
- Purple box = Decision point
- Light green = Terminal state

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Abowd & Beale's Interaction Framework (System Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    User1[User Domain\nMental State\nTask Language] -->|Physical Action| Input1[Input Domain\nKeyboard, Mouse, Touch, Voice]
    Input1 -->|System Signal| System1[System Domain\nTask, Core Language, Semantics, State]
    System1 -->|Output Signal| Output1[Output Domain\nDisplay, Speaker, Haptic Device]
    Output1 -->|Perception| User1

    subgraph Boundaries
        Boundary1[Execution Boundary\nUser to System]
        Boundary2[Evaluation Boundary\nSystem to User]
    end

    Input1 -.-> Boundary1
    Output1 -.-> Boundary2

    style User1 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000
    style Input1 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B
    style System1 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400
    style Output1 fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#8B4513
    style Boundary1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520
    style Boundary2 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — The Two Gulfs (Architectural View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UserMind[USER SIDE]
        Goal[User Goal]
        Intention[User Intention]
        Perception[User Perception]
        Eval[User Evaluation]
    end

    subgraph SystemSide[SYSTEM SIDE]
        Affordance[Available Actions]
        State[System State]
    end

    Goal --> Intention
    Eval --> Goal

    Intention -->|Gulf of Execution\nDelta Ex| Affordance
    State -->|Gulf of Evaluation\nDelta Ev| Perception
    Affordance --> State
    Perception --> Eval

    style Intention fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    style Affordance fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B
    style State fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B
    style Perception fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    style Goal fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00
    style Eval fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00
```

**Engineering Insight:** The **Gulf of Execution** is the horizontal gap between *Intention* and *Affordance* (left-to-right, user→system). The **Gulf of Evaluation** is the horizontal gap between *State* and *Perception* (right-to-left, system→user). A good interface **narrows both gaps simultaneously**.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([User Initiates Task]) --> GoalForm[Stage 1: Form Goal]
    GoalForm --> IntForm[Stage 2: Form Intention]
    IntForm --> ActSpec[Stage 3: Specify Action]
    ActSpec --> ActExe[Stage 4: Execute Action\nPhysical Layer]
    ActExe --> SysIn[System Input Translator]
    SysIn --> SysCore[System Core\nState Machine]
    SysCore --> SysOut[System Output Translator]
    SysOut --> Perceive[Stage 5: Perceive State]
    Perceive --> Interpret[Stage 6: Interpret State]
    Interpret --> Evaluate[Stage 7: Evaluate vs Goal]
    Evaluate --> Decision{Goal Satisfied?}
    Decision -- Yes --> End([Task Complete])
    Decision -- No --> IntForm
```
