# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a "Model of Interaction" in HCI. Mention any two well-known interaction models.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | CO2 | Bloom: Remember/Understand]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *Model of Interaction* is a conceptual framework that describes the structure of communication between a user and an interactive system, abstracting the dialogue into identifiable components such as the user's goals, system input, internal processing, and output.

Two well-known models are:
1. **Don Norman's Seven Stages of Action** (1988) — describes the cognitive stages a user passes through during interaction.
2. **Abowd and Beale's Interaction Framework** (1991) — an engineering model with four components: User, Input, System, and Output, connected in a closed loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's tip (1 mark allocation):** Definition = 1 mark, naming two models with one-line description = 2 marks.

---

### Question 2: What is the "Gulf of Evaluation"? How can a designer reduce it?
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 | CO2 | Bloom: Understand/Apply]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The *Gulf of Evaluation* is the gap between the **actual state of the system** and the **user's ability to perceive, interpret, and evaluate that state** with respect to their goal.

Designers can reduce it by:
1. **Providing immediate, salient feedback** (e.g., a button highlight on click).
2. **Using familiar metaphors and mappings** (e.g., a trash can icon for delete).
3. **Showing system status persistently** (e.g., progress bars, confirmation dialogs).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's tip:** A common mistake is to confuse the two gulfs. Remember — *Execution* is user→system; *Evaluation* is system→user.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1): Comprehensive Analysis of Norman's Model

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 | CO2 | Bloom: Understand/Analyze | 14 Marks]**

**(a) Explain Don Norman's Seven Stages of Action in detail. Illustrate with a real-world example. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Norman (1988) proposed that every purposeful interaction with a system can be decomposed into **seven cognitive stages**, grouped into two major phases: **Execution** and **Evaluation**.

**Phase 1 — Execution (Stages 1–4):**

- **Stage 1: Forming the Goal.** The user decides what they want to achieve. This is a high-level psychological state. *Example:* "I want to print this document."
- **Stage 2: Forming the Intention.** The user decides *what action* to take. *Example:* "I will click the Print icon."
- **Stage 3: Specifying the Action Sequence.** The user plans the exact sequence. *Example:* "Move mouse to the top toolbar → locate the printer icon → click."
- **Stage 4: Executing the Action.** The user physically performs the action — moving the hand, clicking the mouse.

**Phase 2 — Evaluation (Stages 5–7):**

- **Stage 5: Perceiving the System State.** The user observes the screen. *Example:* Sees a print dialog box appear.
- **Stage 6: Interpreting the State.** The user decodes the perception. *Example:* "The dialog confirms the system received my command."
- **Stage 7: Evaluating the Outcome.** The user compares the result with the goal. *Example:* "Yes, the print dialog is open — goal partially achieved; I now need to confirm printing."

The two phases form a continuous **feedback loop**. If the evaluation in Stage 7 does not match the goal, the user re-enters Stage 2 with a refined intention.

---

**(b) Discuss the two Gulfs of Execution and Evaluation. How do they relate to Norman's seven stages? Explain with a diagram. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Gulf of Execution ($\Delta E_x$):** This is the gap between the **user's intention** (Stages 2–3) and the **actions the system actually allows** (Stage 4). A wide Gulf of Execution means the user *cannot easily translate* their intention into physical action. Example: a user wanting to "save" but unable to find the save icon.

**Gulf of Evaluation ($\Delta E_v$):** This is the gap between the **system's actual state** and the **user's ability to perceive and interpret it** (Stages 5–6). A wide Gulf of Evaluation means the user *cannot easily tell* what the system has done. Example: clicking a button but receiving no visible feedback.

**Relationship to Norman's Stages:**

| Norman's Stages | Associated Gulf |
| :--- | :--- |
| Stages 2–4 (Intention → Action) | Gulf of Execution |
| Stages 5–6 (Perception → Interpretation) | Gulf of Evaluation |

**Designer's Mandate:** A successful interface *narrows both gulfs simultaneously*. The diagram (see Section 4.3) shows the user side (intention/perception) and system side (affordance/state), with the two gulfs as horizontal gaps bridged by the interface.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing all seven stages as a flat list without distinguishing *Execution* and *Evaluation* phases. (Loses 2 marks.)
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Reversing the gulfs — saying Gulf of Evaluation is user→system. (Loses 1 mark.)
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not linking the gulfs back to Norman's stages explicitly. (Loses 1–2 marks.)
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Skipping the diagram. A labeled diagram is **mandatory** for full 7 marks in part (b).

---

### Question B (Option 2): Abowd & Beale's Framework

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 | CO2 | Bloom: Understand/Analyze | 14 Marks]**

**(a) Explain Abowd and Beale's Interaction Framework with a neat block diagram. Describe each of the four components in detail. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Abowd and Beale (1991) proposed a generalized framework that models interaction as a **closed loop** among four components. It is sometimes called the *System Model of Interaction*.

**The Four Components:**

1. **The User ($U$):** A human operator with:
   - A *task* in mind (a goal to be achieved).
   - A *task language* — the set of concepts and actions the user is familiar with.
   - *Sensory organs* that perceive system output.
   - *Motor organs* that produce physical input.

2. **The Input ($I$):** The input domain consists of:
   - *Physical input devices* (keyboard, mouse, touchscreen, microphone).
   - An *input translator* that converts physical actions into the system's internal signal format.
   - It bridges the user's task language and the system's core language.

3. **The System ($S$):** The internal computational engine with:
   - A *task* — what the system is fundamentally designed to do.
   - A *core language* — the system's internal representation.
   - *Semantics* — the rules mapping inputs to state changes.
   - *State* — the system's current configuration.

4. **The Output ($O$):** The output domain consists of:
   - *Output devices* (monitor, speaker, haptic motor).
   - An *output translator* that converts internal state into perceptible signals.
   - It bridges the system's core language and the user's perception.

**Block Diagram (text version for exam):**

$$\boxed{\text{USER} \xrightarrow{\text{Input Device}} \text{INPUT} \xrightarrow{\text{System Signal}} \text{SYSTEM} \xrightarrow{\text{Output Signal}} \text{OUTPUT} \xrightarrow{\text{Perception}} \text{USER}}$$

(Refer to Section 4.2 for the full Mermaid diagram.)

---

**(b) Compare Norman's Seven Stages of Action with Abowd & Beale's Framework. How do they complement each other? [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Norman's Model | Abowd & Beale's Model |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Origin** | Cognitive Psychology (1988) | Software Engineering (1991) |
| **Focus** | The *user's mind* — what the user does internally | The *system architecture* — what the system contains |
| **Number of Stages** | 7 stages | 4 components |
| **Primary View** | Psychological / descriptive | Engineering / structural |
| **Key Concept** | Two gulfs (Execution, Evaluation) | Closed loop with translator devices |
| **Strength** | Explains *why* users make errors | Explains *where* in the system to fix errors |
| **Limitation** | Does not specify system internals | Does not detail cognitive stages |

**Complementarity:**

- Norman answers the question: *"What is happening inside the user's head?"*
- Abowd & Beale answer: *"What is happening inside the system?"*
- Together, they provide a **complete model** spanning cognition and computation.

A practical designer uses Norman's model to identify *which* Gulf is too wide (diagnosis), then uses Abowd & Beale's framework to identify *which component* (User, Input, System, Output) needs redesign (treatment).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Treating the two models as competitors rather than complements. (Loses 1 mark.)
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Not drawing the block diagram in part (a). (Loses 1–2 marks.)
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Naming the four components as User, Keyboard, CPU, Monitor — these are *examples*, not the *components*. The components are **User, Input, System, Output** (abstract categories). (Loses 1 mark.)
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to mention the *translators* (input/output translators) in part (a). (Loses 0.5–1 mark.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Models of Interaction**

- ✅ A **Model of Interaction** abstracts the dialogue between user and system into formal components.
- ✅ **Don Norman's Seven Stages of Action** = the foundational psychological model (1988).
  - Stages 1–4 = **Execution** (Goal → Intention → Specify Action → Execute).
  - Stages 5–7 = **Evaluation** (Perceive → Interpret → Evaluate).
- ✅ **Abowd & Beale's Framework** = the foundational engineering model (1991).
  - Four components: **User, Input, System, Output** in a closed loop.
  - Input and Output are *translators* between user and system languages.
- ✅ **Gulf of Execution ($\Delta E_x$)** = Intention ↔ Affordance (user → system).
- ✅ **Gulf of Evaluation ($\Delta E_v$)** = System State ↔ Perception (system → user).
- ✅ The two models are **complementary** — Norman describes the *user's mind*; Abowd & Beale describe the *system's structure*.
- ✅ **Fitts's Law** $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$ is a quantitative tool to *bridge* the Gulf of Execution.
- ✅ **Hick's Law** $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ governs choice reaction time — bridges Execution by limiting menu options.
- ✅ **Miller's Law** (7 ± 2 chunks) limits short-term memory — informs evaluation feedback design.
- ✅ Reducing one Gulf may widen the other — design is a *balance*, not an *optimization*.
- ✅ In KTU exams, always: (1) define clearly, (2) illustrate with example, (3) draw labeled diagram, (4) link theory to design.
- ✅ Common 1-mark traps: confusing the two gulfs, listing 4 input/output *examples* instead of *components*, omitting the loop nature of interaction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT831 - HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)** and **Interaction Design**, the **"Terms of Interaction"** refer to the **five fundamental conceptual primitives** proposed by **Donald A. Norman** in his seminal work *"The Design of Everyday Things"* (originally published as *"The Psychology of Everyday Things"*, 1988). These five terms form the core **designer–user vocabulary** that explains *how* and *why* a user understands, perceives, and operates any artifact — physical or digital.

The five canonical terms are:

1. **Affordance** — The perceived and actual properties of an object that determine *how* it can be used.
2. **Signifier** — Any perceivable indicator, cue, or signal that communicates the *where* and *how* of an interaction.
3. **Mapping** — The relationship between a control and its resulting effect in the world (or system).
4. **Feedback** — The information sent back to the user regarding the *result* of an action.
5. **Constraint** — Factors that *limit* or *restrict* the set of possible user actions, preventing error.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (OEBRT831 – Module 2):**
> Students are expected to understand each term, distinguish *affordance* from *signifier* (a very common exam pitfall), and apply them to evaluate everyday interfaces such as door handles, GUI buttons, ATM screens, and smartphone touch surfaces.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Door" Intuition

Imagine you are walking up to a **door** in a public building.

- The door has a **large flat metal plate** on one side. The plate *affords* **pushing** (it looks pushable). The plate is also a **signifier** — it visually signals *"push here"*.
- On the other side, the door has a **vertical handle bar**. The handle *affords* **pulling** (the human hand naturally wraps around it). The handle is the **signifier** for *pull*.
- The plate is on the *opposite* side of the handle — this is **poor mapping**; users will press against the handle side, fail, and walk into the glass.
- When the door unlatches and swings, you feel the air movement and hear the click — this is **feedback**.
- The **constraint** is the door frame itself: it physically prevents the door from moving sideways or upward, only allowing a rotational arc.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** Every UI design failure (the "this button doesn't look clickable" complaint, the "I clicked Submit but nothing happened" frustration) can be traced back to a violation of one of these five terms. They are the **grammar of interaction design**.

## 1.3 Norman’s Position in HCI History

Donald A. Norman joined Apple Computer in the early 1990s and is widely credited with bringing **cognitive psychology** into mainstream interaction design. His five terms are not arbitrary — they are derived from **Gibson's Ecological Theory of Visual Perception (1979)**, which originally defined *affordances* as action possibilities offered by an environment to an organism. Norman *adapted* and *extended* these ideas for **human–machine systems**.

## 1.4 The Distinction: Affordance vs. Signifier

| Term | Answers the Question | Exists In |
|------|----------------------|-----------|
| **Affordance** | *"What actions are possible?"* | The object (real or perceived) |
| **Signifier** | *"Where / how do I act?"* | The perceptual surface (signs, labels, icons) |

> [!WARNING]
> Norman himself later clarified (in the 2013 *Revised and Expanded Edition*) that **affordances are relationships, not properties**, and that **signifiers are more important than affordances** for design because users can only act on what they *perceive*. This clarification is a frequently asked question in KTU examinations.

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used in Interaction Design

While the five terms are conceptual, the *evaluation* of their quality uses quantitative HCI metrics:

- **Fitts's Law Index of Difficulty** — $ID = \log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{A}{W} + 1\right)$ bits, where $A$ = distance to target, $W$ = width of target.
- **Hick's Law** — $T = a + b \cdot \log_{2}(n+1)$ seconds, where $n$ = number of choices.
- **Steering Law** — $T = a + b \cdot \dfrac{D}{W}$ for constrained motion tasks.
- **Throughput (TP)** — measured in **bits per second (bps)**.
- **NASA-TLX Score** — cognitive workload, range **0–100**.
- **System Usability Scale (SUS)** — range **0–100**, with **> 68** considered above-average usability.
- **Moore's/Andries' Reaction Time Floor** — **~ 100 ms** for a single stimulus–response cycle in young adults.

These metrics will be referenced again in the deep analysis section as the *measurement tools* that quantify the success of a design built on the five terms.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Terms — Structured Logical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Affordance

- **Definition (Gibson, 1979; Norman, 1988):** A relationship between an object's properties and the capabilities of an agent that determines *what actions the agent can perform on the object*.
- **Two Sub-Types:**
  - *Real Affordance* — what the object actually allows (e.g., a chair supports sitting).
  - *Perceived Affordance* — what the user *thinks* is possible (a chair *looks* sittable).
- **Why it matters:** If perceived affordance ≠ real affordance, the user is confused.
- **How to apply:** A button that looks raised and shadowed *affords pressing*; a 3D-printed handle on a 2D screen *affords* being gripped (skeuomorphism).

### 2.1.2 Signifier

- **Definition:** *Any* perceivable cue — visual, auditory, tactile — that **communicates** the location, mode, or nature of an interaction.
- **Five Channels of Signifiers:**
  1. *Visual* (icons, labels, color, depth).
  2. *Auditory* (the shutter sound of a camera, a system beep).
  3. *Tactile* (vibration, the click of a physical key).
  4. *Textual* (the word "Save" under an icon).
  5. *Gestural / Spatial* (a swipe area highlighted in modern smartphones).
- **Why it matters:** Signifiers *replace* guesswork. The best signifiers are visible *at the moment of need* (just-in-time signifiers).

### 2.1.3 Mapping

- **Definition:** The *correspondence* between a **control** and its **effect** in the controlled system (or in the world).
- **Two Sub-Types:**
  - *Natural Mapping* — uses spatial analogies (e.g., a stove burner knob on the *left* controls the *left* burner).
  - *Cultural / Arbitrary Mapping* — learned by convention (e.g., red = stop, green = go).
- **Why it matters:** Good mapping reduces memory load and cognitive effort.
- **How to apply:** Volume sliders in media players that move *upward* for "louder" follow natural vertical-intensify mapping.

### 2.1.4 Feedback

- **Definition:** Information sent *back* to the user about the *result* of an action — within a meaningful time window.
- **Categories:**
  - *Visual* (button color changes on click).
  - *Auditory* (confirmation beep).
  - *Haptic* (vibration when a key is pressed).
  - *Proprioceptive* (the feel of a physical switch).
  - *Combined Multimodal Feedback* (most robust).
- **The "Five Properties of Good Feedback" (Norman):**
  1. *Immediate* — within **100 ms** for basic response confirmation.
  2. *Informative* — tells the user *what happened*.
  3. *Perceivable* — uses a salient channel.
  4. *Continuous* (where appropriate) — progress bars during long tasks.
  5. *Reversible* (where possible) — undo buttons.

### 2.1.5 Constraint

- **Definition:** Factors that *limit* the possible user actions, preventing error and guiding the user.
- **Four Sub-Types (Norman's Classification):**
  1. *Physical Constraint* — the physical world blocks invalid actions (you cannot insert a square peg into a round hole).
  2. *Semantic / Cultural Constraint* — convention (e.g., red light = stop).
  3. *Logical Constraint* — uses reasoning (e.g., a list of radio buttons implies only one selection).
  4. *Disability / Affordance-based Constraint* — user's capabilities (e.g., voice-only control for hands-free operation).

## 2.2 The Interaction Loop — How the Five Terms Co-Operate

The five terms do **not** function in isolation. They form a closed **perception–action loop**:

1. **Perceive** the **signifier** → (signifier triggers the perception of **affordance**).
2. **Map** the control to the intended effect → (using **mapping** rules).
3. **Execute** the action → (allowed by **constraint**).
4. **Receive** the **feedback** → (confirms success or signals error).
5. **Update mental model** → (next action).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This loop is the *blueprint* for every GUI controller (HTML buttons, Android `Button` widgets, iOS UIButton), every industrial HMI panel (Siemens SIMATIC, ABB 800xA), and every automotive dashboard. A defect at *any* step creates a usability failure mode.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — All Key Concepts at a Glance

| # | Term | Answers | Core Sub-Category | Classic Real-World Example | Design Anti-Pattern (Violation) |
|---|------|---------|-------------------|----------------------------|---------------------------------|
| 1 | **Affordance** | *What can I do?* | Real vs Perceived | Door handle *affords* pulling | A flat touchscreen key that *affords* touch but no visual cue |
| 2 | **Signifier** | *Where / how?* | Visual, Auditory, Tactile | "Save" icon (floppy disk) | Hidden gesture (no hint) |
| 3 | **Mapping** | *Which control for what?* | Natural vs Cultural | Volume slider up = louder | Randomly placed burner knobs |
| 4 | **Feedback** | *What happened?* | Immediate, Multimodal | Button depress animation | Submit click with no spinner |
| 5 | **Constraint** | *What is forbidden?* | Physical, Logical, Cultural, Semantic | ATM card slot accepts only correct orientation | Form accepts any text in phone-number field |

## 2.4 Mathematical & Quantitative HCI Metrics (Indirectly Linked to the Five Terms)

While the five terms are conceptual, the *quality* of their implementation is measurable. Below is the consolidated KTU formula reference.

| Metric | Formula | Variable Definitions | Typical Unit | Used to Evaluate |
|--------|---------|----------------------|--------------|------------------|
| **Fitts's Law — Time** | $T = a + b \cdot ID$ | $a$ = start/stop time, $b$ = device slope, $ID$ = index of difficulty | seconds (s) | Affordance clarity of a target |
| **Fitts's Law — Index of Difficulty** | $ID = \log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{A}{W} + 1\right)$ | $A$ = movement amplitude, $W$ = target width | bits | Signifier size adequacy |
| **Hick's Law — Time** | $T = a + b \cdot \log_{2}(n+1)$ | $n$ = number of equally-likely choices | seconds (s) | Mapping complexity |
| **Steering Law — Time** | $T = a + b \cdot \dfrac{D}{W}$ | $D$ = distance, $W$ = tunnel width | seconds (s) | Constraint effectiveness |
| **Throughput** | $TP = \dfrac{ID_{e}}{MT}$ | $ID_{e}$ = effective index of difficulty, $MT$ = mean movement time | bits/s (bps) | Overall interaction efficiency |
| **Error Rate** | $ER = \dfrac{N_{e}}{N_{t}} \times 100\%$ | $N_{e}$ = errors, $N_{t}$ = total trials | percent (%) | Constraint + Feedback quality |
| **SUS Score** | $SUS = \sum_{i=1}^{10} s_{i} \cdot 2.5$ | $s_{i}$ = individual item score (0–4) | unitless (0–100) | Holistic usability |
| **NASA-TLX** | $TLX = \dfrac{\sum_{k=1}^{6} w_{k} \cdot r_{k}}{15}$ | $w_{k}$ = weight, $r_{k}$ = raw rating | unitless (0–100) | Cognitive load from poor signifiers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** You will **not** be required to compute these values in detail, but you **must** know which term each metric primarily evaluates. For example: *Fitts's Law* is used to measure **affordance clarity** of a target; *Hick's Law* is used to measure the **mapping complexity** of a menu.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Application of the Five Terms |
|--------|-------------------------------|
| **Automotive HMI** | Touchscreen with depth (affordance) + vibration (signifier + feedback); gear shifter has physical slot (constraint) |
| **Medical Devices** | Insulin pump: bright "Inject" button (signifier) → auditory beep (feedback) → lock-out if dose > safe range (constraint) |
| **Industrial Control (SCADA)** | Red emergency stop mushroom button (cultural signifier + physical constraint) |
| **Mobile UX** | Bottom navigation bar (consistent mapping); long-press to delete (gesture signifier) |
| **Aviation Cockpit** | Yoke position maps to aircraft pitch (natural mapping); autopilot disconnect tone (feedback) |
| **Smart Home IoT** | Single button "Tap once = on, twice = off" (constrained mapping) |
| **VR / AR Interfaces** | Controller vibration + spatial audio signifiers; physical wall constraint in VR safety boundaries |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix of the Five Terms

> Since the topic of *"Terms of Interaction"* is **conceptual-descriptive** rather than **mathematical-derivation-heavy**, this section presents an **exhaustive analytical matrix** of all five terms. For every term, a **structured, step-by-step deconstruction** is provided, with a *logical expression* showing how the term functions within the perception–action loop.

## 3.1 The Foundational Logical Expression

Let:

- $U$ = User (with capabilities $C$).
- $O$ = Object / Interface Element.
- $P$ = User's Perception function.
- $A$ = Set of possible Actions.
- $E$ = Effect in the system.
- $F$ = Feedback signal.
- $R$ = Resulting mental model update.

The complete interaction loop is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1 (Signifier Perception)} &\Rightarrow S \;=\; P(O) \\
\text{Step 2 (Affordance Realization)} &\Rightarrow A \;=\; \left\{a \in \text{Actions} \mid O \text{ permits } a \text{ given } C \right\} \\
\text{Step 3 (Mapping Resolution)} &\Rightarrow m : \text{Control} \rightarrow E \\
\text{Step 4 (Action Selection under Constraint)} &\Rightarrow a^{*} \;=\; \underset{a \in A \cap \mathcal{K}}{\arg\min} \; \text{Cost}(a) \\
\text{Step 5 (Execution)} &\Rightarrow E \;=\; m(a^{*}) \\
\text{Step 6 (Feedback Reception)} &\Rightarrow F \;=\; \text{Signal}(E) \\
\text{Step 7 (Model Update)} &\Rightarrow R \;=\; \text{Update}\!\left( \text{Prior Model},\, F \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $\mathcal{K}$ denotes the **set of allowed actions under the active constraints**. The term $a^{*}$ is the user's *chosen* action — the one that minimizes the cognitive cost among the allowed set.

> Each of Norman's five terms maps to one or more of the steps above. We now deconstruct them one by one.

## 3.2 Affordance — Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown

### Step A1 — Identify the Object's Properties
An object $O$ has **physical, semantic, and digital properties**.
- *Physical*: shape, size, weight, texture, rigidity.
- *Semantic*: cultural meaning, prior associations.
- *Digital*: responsiveness, state (e.g., enabled/disabled, online/offline).

### Step A2 — Identify the User's Capabilities
A user $U$ has **sensorimotor and cognitive capabilities** $C$.
- Sensorimotor: hand size, grip strength, fine-motor precision.
- Cognitive: working memory span, prior experience.

### Step A3 — Compute the Affordance Set
$$
\begin{aligned}
A(O, U) \;=\; \big\{ a \in \text{Actions} \;\big|\; & \text{Property}(O, a) = \text{true} \\
& \text{and} \; \text{Capability}(U, a) = \text{true} \big\}
\end{aligned}
$$
The *affordance* is the set of all such actions $a$.

### Step A4 — Distinguish Real vs. Perceived
- *Real Affordance*: $A(O, U)$ as defined above.
- *Perceived Affordance*: $A(P(O), U)$ — the user perceives only a subset.

> **Design implication:** A good design *minimizes the gap* between $A(O,U)$ and $A(P(O),U)$.

### Step A5 — Failure Mode
If the perceived set is empty $\left[ A(P(O),U) = \emptyset \right]$ but the real set is non-empty, the user declares the object **"useless"** or **"broken"** — a classic usability defect.

## 3.3 Signifier — Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown

### Step S1 — Define the Signifier Set
A signifier $S$ is a perceivable signal $\in \mathcal{P}$, where $\mathcal{P}$ is the perceptual channel.

### Step S2 — Map Perceptual Channels to Signifiers
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{S} \;=\; \{ & S_{\text{visual}}, S_{\text{auditory}}, S_{\text{tactile}}, S_{\text{textual}}, S_{\text{gestural}} \}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step S3 — Signifier Strength Function
A signifier's *strength* can be modeled as a function of channel salience and contextual fit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{strength}} \;=\; w_{1}\cdot \text{Salience}(S) \;+\; w_{2}\cdot \text{ContextFit}(S, T) \;+\; w_{3}\cdot \text{Cultural}(S)
\end{aligned}
$$
where $T$ is the *task context* and the weights $w_{1}, w_{2}, w_{3}$ sum to **1**.

### Step S4 — Visibility vs. Just-in-Time Signifiers
- *Always-visible* signifiers: labels, icons.
- *Just-in-time* signifiers: hover tooltips, contextual menus.
- Norman's rule: the **best signifiers appear only at the moment of need**.

### Step S5 — Failure Mode
Invisible or misleading signifiers force the user into **guesswork**, increasing cognitive load and Hick's-Law reaction time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{with-guesswork}} \;=\; a + b \cdot \log_{2}(n+1) \quad \text{where } n \text{ is large}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Mapping — Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown

### Step M1 — Define Control-Effect Pair
A mapping $m$ is a function $m : C \rightarrow E$ where $C$ is the control space and $E$ is the effect space.

### Step M2 — Classify Mapping Type
$$
\begin{aligned}
m \;=\; \begin{cases} m_{\text{natural}} & \text{if } m \text{ follows spatial/sensory analogy} \\ m_{\text{cultural}} & \text{if } m \text{ is learned by convention} \\ m_{\text{arbitrary}} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step M3 — Measure Mapping Quality (Spatial Proximity)
A common metric is the **distance** between control and effect:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{control-to-effect}} \;=\; \sqrt{(x_{c} - x_{e})^{2} + (y_{c} - y_{e})^{2} + (z_{c} - z_{e})^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$
The smaller $D$, the better the mapping (a principle exploited in *direct manipulation* interfaces).

### Step M4 — Failure Mode
In **poor mapping**, the user's $m_{\text{mental}}$ (mental model) diverges from the system's $m_{\text{actual}}$, producing the **gulf of execution** (Norman, 1986).

## 3.5 Feedback — Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown

### Step F1 — Define the Feedback Loop
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Action } a \;\longrightarrow\; \text{System response } r \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Feedback signal } F
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step F2 — Feedback Latency Constraint
Norman's "immediate feedback" requires:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t \;=\; t_{F} - t_{a} \;<\; 100 \text{ ms (basic response)} \\
\Delta t \;<\; 1 \text{ s (perceived as instant for cognitive tasks)} \\
\Delta t \;>\; 10 \text{ s (user begins to lose attention)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step F3 — Feedback Completeness
A feedback signal should address the **four W's**:
- *What happened?*
- *Where did it happen?*
- *What does it mean?*
- *What can the user do next?*

A simple mapping:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{complete}} \;=\; \big\{ W_{1}, W_{2}, W_{3}, W_{4} \big\} \\
F_{\text{quality}} \;=\; \dfrac{\vert F_{\text{present}} \cap F_{\text{complete}} \vert}{4} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step F4 — Failure Mode
*No feedback* or *delayed feedback* is the **#1 cause** of user re-clicks, double-submissions, and trust erosion in web forms.

## 3.6 Constraint — Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown

### Step K1 — Define the Constraint Operator
A constraint $\mathcal{K}$ restricts the action set:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{permitted}} \;=\; A(O, U) \cap \mathcal{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step K2 — Classify Constraint Type
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{K} \;=\; \begin{cases} \mathcal{K}_{\text{physical}} & \text{(hardware shape, port orientation)} \\ \mathcal{K}_{\text{logical}} & \text{(radio button implies 1-of-n)} \\ \mathcal{K}_{\text{semantic}} & \text{(red = stop, green = go)} \\ \mathcal{K}_{\text{disability-aware}} & \text{(substitute input for impaired channel)} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step K3 — Constraint Strength Function
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{K}_{\text{strength}} \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{\vert A_{\text{permitted}} \vert}{\vert A(O,U) \vert}
\end{aligned}
$$
- $\mathcal{K}_{\text{strength}} = 0$: no constraint.
- $\mathcal{K}_{\text{strength}} = 1$: total constraint (only one action possible).

### Step K4 — Failure Mode
Over-constraint → user frustration, loss of agency. Under-constraint → user error. **Optimal design tunes $\mathcal{K}_{\text{strength}} \approx 0.7$–$0.9$** for safety-critical contexts.

## 3.7 Consolidated Analytical Matrix — All Five Terms in One View

| # | Term | Logical Role in Loop | Failure Mode | Primary Evaluation Metric |
|---|------|----------------------|--------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Affordance** | Defines $A(O,U)$ | Perceived $\neq$ Real | Fitts's Law $ID$ |
| 2 | **Signifier** | Defines $S = P(O)$ | Invisible / misleading | Hick's Law $T$ vs. $n$ |
| 3 | **Mapping** | Defines $m: C \rightarrow E$ | Gulf of execution | $D_{\text{control-to-effect}}$ |
| 4 | **Feedback** | Defines $F = \text{Signal}(E)$ | Missing or delayed | $\Delta t < 100$ ms |
| 5 | **Constraint** | Restricts $A$ via $\mathcal{K}$ | Over/under-constraint | $\mathcal{K}_{\text{strength}}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synthesis:** A well-designed interface makes all five terms *visible and consistent*. The hallmark of an expert designer (and an A-grade KTU answer) is the ability to look at any artifact — a door, a microwave, a smartphone app — and **name which term is violated, in which step, and propose a redesign**.
