# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Interaction Loop — Mermaid Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *closed perception–action loop* showing how all five terms interlock. The diagram is carefully designed using only alphanumeric node IDs and clean uppercase labels (no markdown bold, no HTML tables, no reserved keywords).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[USER WITH CAPABILITIES C] --> A2[PERCEIVE OBJECT O]
    A2 --> A3{SIGNIFIER VISIBLE AND DECODED}
    A3 -->|NO| A4[GUESSWORK OR ERROR PATH]
    A4 --> A5[INCREASED COGNITIVE LOAD]
    A3 -->|YES| A6[AFFORDANCE RECOGNIZED]
    A6 --> A7{MAPPING CLEAR}
    A7 -->|NO| A8[GULF OF EXECUTION]
    A7 -->|YES| A9{CONSTRAINT EVALUATED}
    A9 --> A10[ACTION PERMITTED]
    A9 -->|BLOCKED| A11[CONSTRAINT VIOLATION WARNING]
    A10 --> A12[USER EXECUTES ACTION A]
    A12 --> A13[SYSTEM PRODUCES EFFECT E]
    A13 --> A14{FEEDBACK WITHIN 100 MS}
    A14 -->|NO| A15[USER DISTRUST AND RECLICK]
    A14 -->|YES| A16[MENTAL MODEL UPDATED R]
    A16 --> A17[LEARNED BEHAVIOR REINFORCED]
    A17 --> A2
    A15 --> A2
    A11 --> A2
    A8 --> A2
    A5 --> A2
```

### 4.1.1 Reading the Diagram

- The **outer rectangle** is the *closed interaction loop*. The user returns to perception after every action.
- **Green-coded** nodes represent the *successful path*; **orange-coded** nodes represent *failure paths* that re-enter the loop.
- Every node is reachable from a *Norman term* (annotated in 4.2 below).

## 4.2 Mapping of Diagram Nodes to Norman's Five Terms

| Node ID (alpha) | Node Label | Norman Term |
|------------------|------------|-------------|
| nodeA2 | PERCEIVE OBJECT O | Pre-loop context |
| nodeA3 | SIGNIFIER VISIBLE AND DECODED | **Signifier** |
| nodeA6 | AFFORDANCE RECOGNIZED | **Affordance** |
| nodeA7 | MAPPING CLEAR | **Mapping** |
| nodeA9 | CONSTRAINT EVALUATED | **Constraint** |
| nodeA14 | FEEDBACK WITHIN 100 MS | **Feedback** |
| nodeA16 | MENTAL MODEL UPDATED R | Post-feedback learning |

## 4.3 Term-by-Term Influence Matrix — Block Diagram

The following diagram shows **how each of the five terms influences the others** in a system, viewed from a designer's perspective. Arrows indicate the *direction of design-time influence*.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[AFFORDANCE] --> S2[SIGNIFIER]
    S2 --> S3[FEEDBACK]
    S3 --> S4[CONSTRAINT]
    S4 --> S5[MAPPING]
    S5 --> S1
    S1 --> S6[USER PERCEIVES POSSIBLE ACTION]
    S2 --> S6
    S3 --> S7[USER UNDERSTANDS RESULT]
    S4 --> S8[USER AVOIDS ERROR]
    S5 --> S9[USER LINKS CONTROL TO EFFECT]
    S6 --> S10[COMPLETE INTERACTION]
    S7 --> S10
    S8 --> S10
    S9 --> S10
    S10 --> S11[USABLE SYSTEM]
```

### 4.3.1 Design Insight

The cyclical structure of `AFFORDANCE → SIGNIFIER → FEEDBACK → CONSTRAINT → MAPPING → AFFORDANCE` highlights a critical fact: **no term is an island**. Fixing one without the others yields an unusable artifact. This is why **usability evaluation must be holistic**.

## 4.4 Practical Real-World Mapping (Door Example)

The following block-level architecture flow maps the *door example* (Section 1) to all five terms — a frequently asked KTU question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[FLAT METAL PLATE ON DOOR FRONT] --> D2[PERCEIVED AFFORDANCE PUSH]
    D1 --> D3[VISUAL SIGNIFIER PUSH HERE]
    D4[VERTICAL HANDLE ON DOOR BACK] --> D5[PERCEIVED AFFORDANCE PULL]
    D4 --> D6[VISUAL SIGNIFIER PULL HERE]
    D1 --> D7[SPATIAL MAPPING SIDE TO SIDE]
    D4 --> D7
    D7 -->|GOOD MAPPING| D8[USER INSTINCTIVELY CHOOSES CORRECT SIDE]
    D7 -->|BAD MAPPING| D9[USER PRESSES AGAINST GLASS FRUSTRATION]
    D10[PHYSICAL DOOR FRAME] --> D11[PHYSICAL CONSTRAINT ARCSHAPE ONLY]
    D12[DOOR UNLATCHES SWINGS] --> D13[FEEDBACK CLICK SOUND AIR MOVEMENT]
    D12 --> D14[FEEDBACK TACTILE LATCH RELEASE]
    D12 --> D15[FEEDBACK VISUAL DOOR SWINGING]
```

### 4.4.1 Door Analysis Text

- **Affordance:** Plate affords pushing; handle affords pulling.
- **Signifier:** Plate and handle are themselves signifiers.
- **Mapping:** Plate-on-front ↔ Push-from-front is *natural*; handle-on-back ↔ Pull-from-back is *natural*. If they are swapped, mapping breaks.
- **Feedback:** Click sound + tactile release + visual swing motion.
- **Constraint:** Frame prevents lateral or vertical motion; only rotational arc permitted.

## 4.5 Mobile Touchscreen UI — Block-Level Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[FLAT GLASS SURFACE] --> M2[NO TACTILE AFFORDANCE]
    M1 --> M3[REQUIRES VISUAL SIGNIFIERS]
    M3 --> M4[RAISED LOOKING ICONS DEPTH SHADOWS]
    M3 --> M5[LABELS UNDER ICONS]
    M3 --> M6[COLOR CODING PRIMARY SECONDARY ACTIONS]
    M7[TOUCH INPUT] --> M8[HAPTIC VIBRATION FEEDBACK]
    M9[BUTTON PRESSED] --> M10[ANIMATED RIPPLE VISUAL FEEDBACK]
    M11[SOUND EFFECT] --> M12[AUDITORY FEEDBACK]
    M13[LOGICAL CONSTRAINT ONE WAY SCROLL] --> M14[PREVENTS REVERSE NAVIGATION]
    M15[FORM FIELD] --> M16[KEYBOARD TYPE CONSTRAINT EMAIL PHONE NUMERIC]
    M17[NAVIGATION BAR] --> M18[STANDARD MAPPING BACK HOME RECENTS]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The mobile example illustrates a key insight: in a **flat touchscreen**, the *real* affordance (touch) is invisible. Designers must therefore *manufacture perceived affordances* through **visual signifiers** — exactly what iOS, Android, and Material Design guidelines prescribe.

## 4.6 Constraint Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[ALL CONSTRAINTS] --> C1[PHYSICAL CONSTRAINT]
    C0 --> C2[LOGICAL CONSTRAINT]
    C0 --> C3[SEMANTIC OR CULTURAL CONSTRAINT]
    C0 --> C4[DISABILITY AWARE CONSTRAINT]
    C1 --> C1A[EXAMPLES USB SHAPE ONLY ONE ORIENTATION FIT]
    C2 --> C2A[EXAMPLES RADIO BUTTONS ONE OF N MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE]
    C3 --> C3A[EXAMPLES RED LIGHT STOP GREEN GO]
    C4 --> C4A[EXAMPLES VOICE CONTROL FOR BLIND USER HAPTIC FOR DEAF USER]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. *"Differentiate between affordance and signifier with a suitable example."*
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, OEBRT831] (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

- **Affordance** is the relationship between an object's properties and a user's capabilities that determines *what actions* are possible. It is a property of the *object–user pair*.
- **Signifier** is a *perceptible signal* (visual, auditory, tactile) that *communicates* where and how an interaction should occur.
- Affordance is the *underlying possibility*; signifier is the *perceived communication* of that possibility.
- **Example:** A door handle *affords* pulling (because the hand can grasp it), but the *signifier* is the visual shape of the handle itself, which signals *"pull here"*. In a touchscreen, a flat icon *affords* tapping, and the icon's raised appearance or label is its *signifier*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (KTU Pattern):**
> - Definition of Affordance — 1 mark
> - Definition of Signifier — 1 mark
> - Correct example with distinction — 1 mark

---

### Question 2. *"List the four types of constraints described by Donald Norman. Briefly explain any two."*
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, OEBRT831] (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The four types of constraints are:

1. **Physical Constraint** — The physical structure prevents certain actions. *Example:* A square peg physically cannot enter a round hole; a USB-C port physically rejects a USB-A plug.
2. **Logical Constraint** — Reasoning alone prevents invalid actions. *Example:* A single-select radio-button group logically permits only one choice.
3. **Semantic / Cultural Constraint** — Convention guides the user. *Example:* A red icon universally implies "stop" or "danger".
4. **Disability-aware Constraint** — Design accommodates user impairments. *Example:* Subtitles for deaf users; voice input for motor-impaired users.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (KTU Pattern):**
> - Listing the four types — 1 mark
> - Detailed explanation of two — 2 marks (1 each)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B Module question carries **14 marks**, typically with a single internal choice. The standard sub-partition is **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Both questions below are model answers.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 / Model Paper]**
**(a)** *Explain the five fundamental terms of interaction as proposed by Donald Norman. Illustrate each with a real-world example. (7 marks)*
**(b)** *With a neat block diagram, describe the perception–action loop of an interaction. Show how each of Norman's terms maps to a stage in the loop. (7 marks)*

### Model Answer A(a) — 7 Marks

The five fundamental terms of interaction as proposed by Donald A. Norman in *"The Design of Everyday Things"* (1988) are:

**1. Affordance (1 mark)**
An affordance is the *relationship* between an object's properties and the user's capabilities that determines possible actions.
*Example:* A door handle *affords* pulling; a button on a screen *affords* pressing. Gibson (1979) introduced the term in ecological psychology; Norman adapted it for design.

**2. Signifier (1 mark)**
A signifier is a *perceivable indicator* that communicates the location and mode of an interaction.
*Example:* A "Save" floppy-disk icon is a visual signifier; the click of a key is an auditory-tactile signifier.

**3. Mapping (1 mark)**
Mapping is the *correspondence* between a control and its effect in the system or world.
*Example:* The volume slider in a media player moves *up* to make the sound *louder* — a natural, vertical-intensify mapping.

**4. Feedback (1 mark)**
Feedback is the *response* sent to the user about the result of their action, ideally within **100 ms** (perception threshold).
*Example:* A button depresses and changes color when clicked, providing visual feedback; a confirmation beep is auditory feedback.

**5. Constraint (1 mark)**
A constraint *limits* the set of possible actions, preventing error.
*Example:* A USB-C port's shape is a *physical constraint*; a red traffic light is a *semantic constraint*.

**Synthesis (1 mark):**
These five terms together form the *designer–user vocabulary*. A failure in any term produces a usability defect. They are evaluated quantitatively using **Fitts's Law** (affordance clarity), **Hick's Law** (signifier visibility), and **error rate** (constraint + feedback quality).

**Diagram (1 mark):** Use the 5-row summary table from Section 2.3 of these notes as a 1-mark visual aid.

### Model Answer A(b) — 7 Marks

The perception–action loop (also called the *interaction cycle* or *Norman–Draper cycle*) consists of the following stages:

**Stage 1 — Perception (1 mark)**
The user perceives a *signifier* on the object (e.g., a glowing button, a labeled icon). Without a perceivable signifier, the user enters a *guesswork path*.

**Stage 2 — Cognition / Affordance Recognition (1 mark)**
The user recognizes an *affordance* — the perceived possibility of an action. A raised, shadowed button affords pressing.

**Stage 3 — Mapping / Intention (1 mark)**
The user selects an action by matching the *control* to the *intended effect* through a *mapping rule*. Good mapping is *natural* (spatial) or *cultural* (learned).

**Stage 4 — Execution under Constraint (1 mark)**
The user attempts the action. *Constraints* may block invalid actions (e.g., the "Delete" option is grayed out when nothing is selected).

**Stage 5 — System Effect (1 mark)**
The system produces an *effect* (e.g., file deleted, screen updated).

**Stage 6 — Feedback (1 mark)**
The system sends a *feedback* signal back to the user within ~100 ms (visual, auditory, or haptic).

**Stage 7 — Mental Model Update (1 mark)**
The user updates their *mental model* of the system. The cycle restarts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mapping the five terms to the loop:**
> - Signifier → Stage 1 (perception)
> - Affordance → Stage 2 (cognition)
> - Mapping → Stage 3 (intention)
> - Constraint → Stage 4 (execution)
> - Feedback → Stage 6 (verification)
>
> *Stage 5 is a system-internal step, and Stage 7 is a learning step; the five Norman terms govern the user-side stages.*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** *Discuss the properties of good feedback in interactive systems. How does feedback relate to Fitts's Law and the human reaction-time floor of ~100 ms? (7 marks)*
**(b)** *Choose any two of the following artifacts and analyze the five terms of interaction for each: (i) A microwave oven, (ii) A smartphone unlock screen, (iii) An ATM cash-dispenser interface. (7 marks)*

### Model Answer B(a) — 7 Marks

**Properties of Good Feedback (Donald Norman, expanded):** (3 marks)

1. *Immediate* — Delivered within the user's perceptual reaction-time window (**< 100 ms** for tactile/basic, **< 1 s** for cognitive).
2. *Informative* — Tells the user *what happened*, *what state the system is in*, and *what to do next* (the four W's).
3. *Perceivable* — Uses a *salient channel* — visual, auditory, or haptic — appropriate to the user's context.
4. *Continuous (where appropriate)* — Progress bars during long operations prevent the user from assuming the system has hung.
5. *Reversible (where possible)* — Undo buttons, confirmation dialogs for destructive actions.

**Relation to Fitts's Law (2 marks):**
Fitts's Law governs *target-acquisition* time:
$$
\begin{aligned}
T \;=\; a + b \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{A}{W} + 1\right)
\end{aligned}
$$
Good feedback must appear **at or near the target** so that the user's *perception* of the target is updated before the next movement is initiated. If feedback is delayed or far from the target, the next Fitts's-Law movement is mis-aligned, and the overall *throughput* $TP$ falls.

**Relation to the 100 ms Reaction-Time Floor (2 marks):**
The human visual reaction time has a physiological floor of approximately **100–250 ms** (depending on modality and age). If the system feedback arrives *after* this window, the user perceives the system as *laggy* or *unresponsive*, leading to *re-clicks* and *double-submissions*. In *low-latency* interfaces (e.g., gaming, music input), the design target is **< 50 ms** to remain imperceptible.

### Model Answer B(b) — 7 Marks

**Artifact 1: Smartphone Unlock Screen (3.5 marks)**

- *Affordance:* A flat screen *affords* touch; the circular pattern or PIN keypad *affords* drag or tap.
- *Signifier:* The visible dots of the pattern grid, the numerals 0–9, the biometric (fingerprint) icon — all are visual signifiers. A haptic vibration is a tactile signifier.
- *Mapping:* Dragging from a finger-position to a dot is a *natural* spatial mapping.
- *Feedback:* A line traces the user's finger, the dot lights up; success → screen unlocks with animation; failure → vibration + red flash.
- *Constraint:* The OS limits the number of attempts (5 before lockout). A grayed-out fingerprint sensor during the first second is a temporal constraint.

**Artifact 2: ATM Cash-Dispenser Interface (3.5 marks)**

- *Affordance:* The touchscreen *affords* tapping; the card slot physically *affords* card insertion.
- *Signifier:* Language toggle (English / Hindi / Malayalam etc.), large text, on-screen keypad layout.
- *Mapping:* The PIN pad layout matches phone/ATM conventions (1-2-3 on top); the cash dispenser slot is *behind* the keypad to support natural body movement.
- *Feedback:* "Processing…" spinner, audible beep per key, cash-out animation.
- *Constraint:* Card slot accepts only one orientation (physical constraint); the "Cash Withdrawal" button is disabled if balance is insufficient (logical constraint); 3 incorrect PINs block the card (security constraint).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing affordance with signifier** — This is the **#1 deduction** in KTU answer scripts. Affordance is a *relationship*; signifier is a *cue*. Always state this distinction explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the "five" in the answer** — When asked for "the five terms", students often list only 3 or 4. *Every term is worth 1 mark.* Missing one = -1 mark.
> 3. **No real-world example** — A definition without an example is treated as a *half-answer*. Always include one.
> 4. **Citing Norman without giving the source year** — Always write *"Donald A. Norman, 1988, The Design of Everyday Things"*. Examiners are strict about attribution.
> 5. **Ignoring the revision** — In 2013, Norman revised the book. The 2013 edition reframes **signifiers as more important than affordances**. Including this nuance earns **bonus marks** in 14-mark answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **"Terms of Interaction"** are **Affordance, Signifier, Mapping, Feedback, and Constraint** — proposed by **Donald A. Norman (1988)** in *"The Design of Everyday Things"*.
- **Affordance** = *What can I do?* — a relationship between object and user.
- **Signifier** = *Where / how?* — a perceivable signal.
- **Mapping** = *Which control ↔ which effect?* — natural (spatial) vs. cultural.
- **Feedback** = *What happened?* — must be **immediate (< 100 ms)**, informative, perceivable, continuous (where needed), reversible (where possible).
- **Constraint** = *What is forbidden?* — physical, logical, semantic/cultural, disability-aware.
- The five terms form a **closed perception–action loop**; the loop repeats for every interaction.
- **Common metric associations:**
  - *Affordance clarity* → **Fitts's Law**
  - *Signifier visibility / number of choices* → **Hick's Law**
  - *Constraint effectiveness* → **Steering Law**
  - *Feedback quality* → **reaction-time floor ≈ 100 ms**
  - *Holistic usability* → **SUS score**, **NASA-TLX**
- **Real-world exemplars to memorize:**
  - Door (plate vs. handle) — classic Norman example.
  - Stove burner knob layout — natural vs. cultural mapping.
  - ATM — physical constraint of card slot.
  - Touchscreen button — manufactured perceived affordance.
  - Emergency stop mushroom button — semantic + physical constraint.
- **Affordance ≠ Signifier.** This is the **single most-tested distinction** in KTU exams for this topic.
- Norman revised his framework in the **2013 edition** — signifiers are now emphasized as more important than affordances for design.
- **Fitts's Law** index of difficulty: $\,ID = \log_{2}\!\left( \dfrac{A}{W} + 1 \right)\,$ bits.
- **Hick's Law** choice reaction time: $\,T = a + b \cdot \log_{2}(n+1)\,$ seconds.
- **SUS > 68** indicates above-average usability; **NASA-TLX** is bounded 0–100.
- **The perception–action loop is cyclic**, not linear. Every successful or failed interaction updates the user's mental model and feeds back to perception.
- **In a flat touchscreen**, real affordances are weak; **perceived affordances must be manufactured** through visual signifiers (skeuomorphism, depth, labels).
- **Over-constraint** causes frustration; **under-constraint** causes error. Aim for $\mathcal{K}_{\text{strength}} \approx 0.7$–$0.9$ in safety-critical UIs.
- **Multimodal feedback** (visual + auditory + haptic) is the most robust form.
- **Cultural mapping** (red = stop, green = go) is *learned*, not universal — it can fail across cultures and must be paired with **textual signifiers** for internationalization.
- **The "Gulf of Execution"** (Norman, 1986) is the gap between the user's *intention* and the *actions permitted* by the system; it is *closed* by good mapping, signifiers, and constraints.
- **The "Gulf of Evaluation"** is the gap between the system's *state* and the user's *perception* of that state; it is *closed* by good feedback.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interaction Framework
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT831 - HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Interaction Framework

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Interaction Framework?

An **Interaction Framework** is a structured conceptual model that describes, analyzes, and prescribes how a **user (human)** and a **system (machine/computer)** communicate, translate intentions into operations, and interpret feedback. In KTU 2024 HMI parlance, it formalizes the *bridge* between the **Human Domain** (goals, intentions, cognitive processing) and the **Computer Domain** (input/output mechanisms, state variables, kernel operations).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**  
> *An interaction framework is a conceptual model that decomposes the human–computer dialogue into four orthogonal layers — **Goals, Tasks, Actions, and Operations** — together with the translation/interpretation gulfs that exist between them.*

The two classical reference frameworks prescribed in the **KTU OEBRT831 syllabus (Module 2)** are:

1. **Norman’s Seven Stages of Action** (1988) — the *execution–evaluation cycle*.
2. **Abowd & Beale’s Interaction Framework** (1991) — the *extended system image* model.

A third complementary viewpoint often tested in KTU is **Donald Norman’s Gulfs (Gulf of Execution & Gulf of Evaluation)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — “Driving Your Friend’s Car” 🚗

Imagine you usually drive your own car, but today you must drive a **manual-transmission car** (foreign system) for the first time. You have a clear **goal**: *reach the office by 9 AM*. Your brain translates that goal into **tasks** (start, accelerate, brake, signal). Tasks must be turned into **actions** (push clutch, move gear stick to 2nd), but the car’s controls are arranged differently — that mismatch is the **Gulf of Execution**.

While driving, the **dashboard** (speedometer, fuel gauge) tells you what the car is *actually* doing. If the speedometer is in mph but you think in km/h, you will misjudge your speed. That mismatch is the **Gulf of Evaluation**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **smaller the gulfs**, the more *transparent* and *usable* the system. Every HMI design principle in KTU Module 2 ultimately exists to *shrink* these two gulfs.

---

## 1.3 Why Interaction Frameworks Matter in HMI Design

| Benefit | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| **Predictive power** | Predicts *where* users will fail before prototype is built. |
| **Design Vocabulary** | Provides shared vocabulary (goal, task, action) for designers. |
| **Evaluation Lens** | Lets testers locate usability defects in the *execution–evaluation cycle*. |
| **Systematic Redesign** | Each gulf suggests a targeted redesign strategy. |
| **Cross-Disciplinary Use** | Used in UI/UX, industrial HMIs, automotive dashboards, medical devices. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Mnemonic:** The interaction framework turns a **“User ↔ System”** relationship into a **“Goal → Task → Action → Operation → State Change → Feedback”** pipeline.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Board-Exam Definitions)

| Term | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Goal** | The desired *end-state* the user wants to achieve (e.g., *“send an email”*). |
| **Task** | The set of activities required to satisfy a goal at a procedural level. |
| **Intent** | The user’s internal mental representation of *what* must be done. |
| **Action** | A concrete physical or semantic operation the user performs (e.g., click, drag). |
| **Operation** | The lowest-level system primitive executed by the machine. |
| **System State** | The current value of all internal variables of the computer system. |
| **Gulf of Execution** | The gap between the user’s intention and the allowable system actions. |
| **Gulf of Evaluation** | The gap between the system’s actual state and the user’s perception of it. |
| **Feedback** | Information returned by the system that helps the user assess progress. |
| **Direct Mapping** | The degree to which controls and effects have a *natural* relationship. |
| **Affordance** | A property of an object that suggests *how* it can be used. |
| **Mapping** | The relationship between controls and their effects in the world. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Norman’s Seven Stages of Action (1988)

Donald Norman, in *“The Design of Everyday Things”*, decomposes every purposeful human activity into **seven stages**, grouped into two halves:

### A. The Execution Side (Stages 1 → 4) — *Doing It*

| Stage | Mental Process | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Forming the Goal** | Cognitive | User decides *what* they want to accomplish. |
| **2. Forming the Intention** | Cognitive | User decides *what to do* at a high level to achieve the goal. |
| **3. Specifying an Action** | Cognitive | User selects the *specific sequence* of operations. |
| **4. Executing the Action** | Physical | User carries out the action on the system interface. |

### B. The Evaluation Side (Stages 5 → 7) — *Checking It*

| Stage | Mental Process | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **5. Perceiving the System State** | Perceptual | User observes the feedback presented by the system. |
| **6. Interpreting the State** | Cognitive | User makes sense of the perceived feedback. |
| **7. Evaluating the Outcome** | Cognitive | User compares the interpreted state with the original goal. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If **Stage 7 (Evaluating the Outcome)** shows the goal is *not* met, the user loops back to **Stage 2 or 3** and the cycle repeats. This is the **Execution–Evaluation Cycle**.

---

## 2.2 Norman’s Gulfs Model

### 2.2.1 Gulf of Execution
The **Gulf of Execution** is the psychological distance between:
- The user’s **intention (in the human domain)**, and
- The **physical actions required to invoke system behaviour (in the computer domain)**.

> *“How do I tell the system what to do?”*

**Bridging strategies:** good **affordances**, **natural mappings**, **constraints**, and **signifiers**.

### 2.2.2 Gulf of Evaluation
The **Gulf of Evaluation** is the psychological distance between:
- The **system’s actual state (computer domain)**, and
- The user’s **perception and interpretation of that state (human domain)**.

> *“What did the system just do?”*

**Bridging strategies:** immediate, **clear**, **visible**, and **well-organised feedback**.

### 2.2.3 Quantitative Conceptualisation of the Gulfs

While the gulfs are *qualitative* concepts, their **effective size** can be expressed as a conceptual engineering metric:

$$
G_{exec} = \text{distance}(\text{User Intention},\ \text{System Action Vocabulary})
$$

$$
G_{eval} = \text{distance}(\text{System State},\ \text{User Mental Model})
$$

A well-designed interface minimizes both: $G_{exec} \to 0$ and $G_{eval} \to 0$.

---

## 2.3 Abowd & Beale Interaction Framework (1991)

Abowd and Beale extended Norman’s model into a rigorous four-component model that is the **core KTU Module 2 framework**. It contains **four components** and **four translations**.

### 2.3.1 The Four Components

| Component | Domain | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **User** | Human | The human with goals, intentions, and perception. |
| **Input** | Computer | The devices & channels through which the user *transmits* intentions (keyboard, mouse, mic). |
| **System** | Computer | The computational core that processes input and maintains state. |
| **Output** | Computer | The channels through which the system *displays* state (screen, speaker, haptic). |

### 2.3.2 The Four Translations (Translations = the *gulfs*)

| Translation | What Happens | Bridged by |
|---|---|---|
| **User → Input** | User formulates an action; selects the input device. | Affordances, mappings. |
| **Input → System** | The system parses the raw input into semantic form. | Lexical & syntactic design. |
| **System → Output** | The system renders the new state via output channels. | Display design, feedback. |
| **Output → User** | The user perceives and interprets the output. | Legibility, interpretability. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** “Translation” and “Gulf” are *synonymous* in this framework. Examiners often ask: *“List the four translations in Abowd & Beale’s framework.”*

---

## 2.4 The Execution–Evaluation Cycle (Visual Logic)

A continuous loop:

$$
\underbrace{\text{Goal} \xrightarrow{\text{Plan}} \text{Intention}}_{\text{Execution (Stages 1-2)}} \xrightarrow{\text{Action}} \underbrace{\text{System State}}_{\text{Stages 3-4}} \xrightarrow{\text{Feedback}} \underbrace{\text{Perception} \to \text{Interpretation} \to \text{Evaluation}}_{\text{Evaluation (Stages 5-7)}}
$$

The cycle continues until the **goal** is reached, or the user **aborts**.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quantitative Models Used Alongside the Framework)

These are the **predictive models** KTU expects you to pair with the *qualitative* framework.

| # | Model | Equation | Use | Key Parameter | Reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fitts’ Law** | $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | Predicts pointing/movement time. | $D$ = distance, $W$ = target width, $a,b$ = empirically derived constants (ms). | Fitts (1954) |
| 2 | **Hick–Hyman Law** | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(N+1)$ | Predicts decision time for $N$ choices. | $N$ = number of equally probable choices. | Hick (1952) |
| 3 | **Keystroke-Level Model (KLM)** | $T_{task} = \sum T_{K} + \sum T_{P} + T_{H} + T_{M} + T_{R}$ | Predicts expert task time. | $T_K$ = keystroke, $T_P$ = pointing, $T_H$ = hand move, $T_M$ = mental prep, $T_R$ = system response. | Card, Moran & Newell (1980) |
| 4 | **Steering Law** | $T = a + b \cdot \dfrac{A}{W}$ | Predicts movement time in constrained paths. | $A$ = path length, $W$ = width. | Accot & Zhai (1997) |
| 5 | **Index of Difficulty (ID)** | $ID = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | Fitts’ law normalized difficulty (bits). | Unit: **bits**. | Fitts (1954) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark-Trap:** Always mention the **units of $ID$ (bits)** and the **direction of proportionality** for Fitts’ law. They are favourite 2-mark sub-part questions.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Framework |
|---|---|
| **Avionics Cockpits** | Minimizing Gulf of Execution via grouped, labelled controls; minimizing Gulf of Evaluation via glass-cockpit displays. |
| **Medical Devices (e.g., infusion pumps)** | Signifiers and constraints prevent mis-dosing — a literal life-saving reduction in $G_{exec}$. |
| **Industrial Control (SCADA)** | The four-translation model guides HMI panel layout. |
| **Automotive Infotainment** | Hick’s Law governs the *max* number of menu items to keep driver distraction low. |
| **Accessibility Tech** | Affordances & feedback help users with motor/cognitive impairments bridge the gulfs. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Mapping: Norman’s 7 Stages ↔ Abowd & Beale’s 4 Translations

The KTU examiner frequently asks: *“Show how Norman’s 7 stages map onto Abowd & Beale’s 4 translations.”* Below is the full derivation.

### Step 1 — Identify Norman’s Two Halves

Norman’s cycle has:
- **Execution Half (Stages 1–4)** = the user moving *from* intention *to* action.
- **Evaluation Half (Stages 5–7)** = the user moving *from* perception *to* judgement.

### Step 2 — Map Execution Half to Two Translations

| Norman Stage | Abowd–Beale Translation | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Forming the Goal | (Meta-level, before system) | Goal is *outside* the system boundary. |
| 2. Forming the Intention | **User → Input** | User decides *which input device* to use. |
| 3. Specifying an Action | **User → Input** (continues) | User formulates the precise interaction. |
| 4. Executing the Action | **Input → System** | Raw input (mouse click, key press) is parsed by the system. |

### Step 3 — Map Evaluation Half to Two Translations

| Norman Stage | Abowd–Beale Translation | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| 5. Perceiving the State | **System → Output** | The system renders new state to output channels. |
| 6. Interpreting the State | **Output → User** | The user perceives the rendered state. |
| 7. Evaluating the Outcome | **Output → User** (continues) | The user compares it to the original goal. |

### Step 4 — Final Mapping Summary (Board Diagram)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Norman Stage 2-4} \ &\longleftrightarrow\ \text{User} \to \text{Input} \to \text{System} \quad (\text{Execution Translations}) \\
\text{Norman Stage 5-7} \ &\longleftrightarrow\ \text{System} \to \text{Output} \to \text{User} \quad (\text{Evaluation Translations})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Stage 1 (Forming the Goal) is **pre-system** and is not part of any Abowd–Beale translation. It belongs purely to the human domain.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Applying Fitts’ Law Within the Framework

**Problem:** A designer wants a button on a touchscreen. Distance from the user’s typical finger resting point to the button is $D = 12 \text{ cm}$. The button’s edge length is $W = 1.5 \text{ cm}$. Empirical constants for the device are $a = 50 \text{ ms}$ and $b = 150 \text{ ms/bit}$. Calculate **(i)** the Index of Difficulty, **(ii)** the predicted movement time, and **(iii)** comment on the *Gulf of Execution*.

### Step 1 — Compute the Index of Difficulty (ID)

$$
ID = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)
$$

$$
ID = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{12}{1.5} + 1\right) = \log_2(8 + 1) = \log_2(9)
$$

$$
ID = \log_2(9) = \dfrac{\ln 9}{\ln 2} = \dfrac{2.1972}{0.6931} \approx 3.17 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Movement Time (MT) using Fitts’ Law

$$
MT = a + b \cdot ID
$$

$$
MT = 50 + 150 \times 3.17
$$

$$
MT = 50 + 475.5 = 525.5 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 3 — Interpret in terms of the Interaction Framework

A *larger* $W$ or *smaller* $D$ reduces $ID$, shortens $MT$, and thereby **shrinks the Gulf of Execution** because the user can invoke the system action with less cognitive/physical effort. Conversely, very small targets on touchscreens *expand* the gulf and lead to user error.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 14-Mark Pattern:** Always pair a numerical Fitts’ law computation with a paragraph explaining *which gulf* is affected and *how the designer should redesign* the interface.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Design Principles to Each Translation

| Translation | Primary Cognitive Gulf | Key Design Principles | KTU Example Question Hook |
|---|---|---|---|
| **User → Input** | Gulf of Execution (Initiation) | Affordances, Signifiers, Constraints, Natural Mapping, Consistency. | *“How can a GUI button shrink the Gulf of Execution?”* |
| **Input → System** | Gulf of Execution (Execution) | Lexical/Syntactic design, Error prevention, Input tolerance. | *“Explain how input parsing affects usability.”* |
| **System → Output** | Gulf of Evaluation (Initiation) | Visibility, Feedback, Status display, Immediate response. | *“Why is feedback critical in evaluation?”* |
| **Output → User** | Gulf of Evaluation (Execution) | Legibility, Comprehensibility, Multi-modal cues. | *“Why are redundant visual + audio cues used in cockpits?”* |

---

## 3.4 Proof-Style Derivation: Why Feedback Closes the Evaluation Loop

> **Claim:** A user can complete the evaluation side of Norman’s cycle *if and only if* the system provides perceivable feedback.

**Step A — Forward Direction (Feedback → Closure):**
If the system emits a perceivable output $O$ for every state change $\Delta S$, then the user at Stage 5 perceives $O$, at Stage 6 interprets $O \equiv \Delta S$, and at Stage 7 evaluates whether $\Delta S$ achieves the original goal. The loop is therefore *closed*.

**Step B — Backward Direction (Closure → Feedback):**
Assume the user can complete Stages 5–7 without explicit system output. Then either (i) the user is inferring the state via memory — which is **unreliable** and violates the **visibility principle** — or (ii) the system has no state change. In both cases, **effective closure requires explicit output** to the user. Hence feedback is necessary.

$$
\therefore \quad \text{User completes evaluation} \iff \text{System provides perceivable feedback}.
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Abowd & Beale Interaction Framework (Master Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HumanDomain["HUMAN DOMAIN"]
        U1["User\nGoals, Intentions, Perception"]
    end

    subgraph ComputerDomain["COMPUTER DOMAIN"]
        I1["Input Devices\nKeyboard, Mouse, Touch, Mic"]
        S1["System Core\nProcessor, State Variables"]
        O1["Output Devices\nScreen, Speaker, Haptics"]
    end

    U1 -- "T1: User to Input\nAffordance, Mapping" --> I1
    I1 -- "T2: Input to System\nLexical / Syntactic Parse" --> S1
    S1 -- "T3: System to Output\nRender State, Display" --> O1
    O1 -- "T4: Output to User\nLegibility, Interpretability" --> U1

    U1 -. "Goal-setting\n(pre-system)" .-> U1
    S1 -. "Internal State\nChange" .-> S1
```

---

## 4.2 Norman’s Seven Stages of Action (Execution–Evaluation Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Stage 1\nForming the Goal"] --> A2["Stage 2\nForming the Intention"]
    A2 --> A3["Stage 3\nSpecifying an Action"]
    A3 --> A4["Stage 4\nExecuting the Action"]
    A4 --> A5["Stage 5\nPerceiving the System State"]
    A5 --> A6["Stage 6\nInterpreting the State"]
    A6 --> A7["Stage 7\nEvaluating vs Goal"]
    A7 -- "Goal Achieved?" --> A8{"Goal Met?"}
    A8 -- "Yes" --> Aend["Task Complete"]
    A8 -- "No" --> A2
```

---

## 4.3 The Two Gulfs — Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GofE["GULF OF EXECUTION"]
        H1["User Intention"]
        H2["System Vocabulary of Actions"]
    end
    subgraph GofV["GULF OF EVALUATION"]
        C1["System State (Actual)"]
        C2["User Mental Model (Perceived)"]
    end

    H1 -- "Bridge with affordances, mapping, signifiers" --> H2
    C1 -- "Bridge with feedback, visibility, legibility" --> C2
```

---

## 4.4 Mapping Norman’s 7 Stages onto Abowd–Beale’s 4 Translations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1["N1: Goal\n(Pre-system)"] --> N2["N2: Intention\nUser to Input (T1)"]
    N2 --> N3["N3: Action\nUser to Input (T1 cont.)"]
    N3 --> N4["N4: Execution\nInput to System (T2)"]
    N4 --> N5["N5: Perceive\nSystem to Output (T3)"]
    N5 --> N6["N6: Interpret\nOutput to User (T4)"]
    N6 --> N7["N7: Evaluate\nOutput to User (T4 cont.)"]
    N7 -- "Loop back if goal unmet" --> N2
```

---

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Touchscreen HMI

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Layer1["Human Layer"]
        LH1["User Goal\nSend Email"]
        LH2["Task: Compose, Attach, Send"]
    end

    subgraph Layer2["Bridge Layer - Gulf of Execution"]
        LB1["Action: Tap Compose, Type, Tap Send"]
        LB2["Affordance: Icon shape, Label, Color"]
    end

    subgraph Layer3["System Layer - Core"]
        LS1["Input Parser\nTouch Coordinates, Text Buffer"]
        LS2["State Manager\nEmail Draft, Recipient List"]
        LS3["Output Renderer\nGUI Update, Notification"]
    end

    subgraph Layer4["Bridge Layer - Gulf of Evaluation"]
        LF1["Feedback: Status bar, Toast, Sound"]
        LF2["Visual Cue: Sent tick mark, animation"]
    end

    LH1 --> LH2 --> LB1 --> LS1 --> LS2 --> LS3 --> LF1 --> LF2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Interaction Framework. List the four components of Abowd and Beale’s interaction framework.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An *interaction framework* is a conceptual model describing the communication between a human user and a computer system via goals, tasks, actions, and operations, along with the translation barriers (gulfs) between them. (1.5 Marks)

Abowd and Beale’s framework has four components: **User, Input, System, Output**. (1.5 Marks)

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Gulf of Execution and Gulf of Evaluation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Gulf of Execution | Gulf of Evaluation |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | From user intention → system actions. | From system state → user perception. |
| **Question** | *“How do I make the system do it?”* | *“What did the system just do?”* |
| **Bridged by** | Affordances, signifiers, mappings. | Feedback, visibility, legibility. |
| **Norman stage** | Stages 1–4. | Stages 5–7. |

(Any three differences = 3 Marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice — 14 Marks)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts **(a) — 7 marks** and **(b) — 7 marks**. Either Q-A or Q-B is to be answered.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain Norman’s Seven Stages of Action model in detail with a suitable diagram. How does it describe the execution–evaluation cycle? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **Definition (1 Mark):** Norman (1988) decomposed every purposeful human activity into seven cognitive/physical stages grouped into two halves — *Execution* and *Evaluation*.
2. **Execution Stages (1.5 Marks):**
   - Stage 1: Forming the Goal
   - Stage 2: Forming the Intention
   - Stage 3: Specifying an Action
   - Stage 4: Executing the Action
3. **Evaluation Stages (1.5 Marks):**
   - Stage 5: Perceiving the System State
   - Stage 6: Interpreting the State
   - Stage 7: Evaluating the Outcome
4. **Execution–Evaluation Cycle (2 Marks):** After Stage 7, if the goal is unmet, the user loops back to Stage 2 and re-iterates. The cycle continues until goal achievement or user abort.
5. **Diagram (1 Mark):** (See Section 4.2 of these notes.)

**(b)** Define Gulf of Execution and Gulf of Evaluation. Discuss with examples how Norman’s design principles (affordance, mapping, feedback, constraints) help to bridge these gulfs. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

1. **Definitions (2 Marks):**
   - *Gulf of Execution:* the gap between the user’s intention and the system’s action vocabulary. Reduced by good **affordances, signifiers, mappings, and constraints**.
   - *Gulf of Evaluation:* the gap between the system’s actual state and the user’s perception of it. Reduced by **visible, immediate, and well-organised feedback**.
2. **Bridging the Execution Gulf (2 Marks):** *Example:* A door with a flat plate *affords* pushing; the flat plate is a *signifier* indicating the action; the mapping (push → open) is *natural*; an electrical panel with a *constraint* (keyed slot) prevents mis-connection.
3. **Bridging the Evaluation Gulf (2 Marks):** *Example:* After a file is deleted, an **“Undo” toast** appears immediately; a **green progress bar** shows download percentage. Both are forms of feedback that let the user evaluate state changes.
4. **Conclusion (1 Mark):** The two gulfs together define the *usability* of any interface; reducing both is the central goal of HMI design.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the Abowd and Beale interaction framework with a neat diagram. Explain each of the four translations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **Introduction (1 Mark):** Abowd & Beale (1991) proposed a four-component, four-translation model formalising the user–system dialogue. The four components are **User, Input, System, Output**.
2. **Diagram (1 Mark):** (See Section 4.1 of these notes.)
3. **Translation T1 — User → Input (1.5 Marks):** The user formulates an intention and chooses an input device. *Bridged by* affordances, mappings, and consistency.
4. **Translation T2 — Input → System (1.5 Marks):** The system parses the raw input. *Bridged by* lexical/syntactic design and error-tolerance.
5. **Translation T3 — System → Output (1 Mark):** The system renders the new state. *Bridged by* visibility and status display.
6. **Translation T4 — Output → User (1 Mark):** The user perceives and interprets the output. *Bridged by* legibility, multi-modal feedback, and comprehensibility.

**(b)** A designer is creating a touchscreen icon for an industrial control panel. The icon is at a distance of $D = 8 \text{ cm}$ from the operator’s resting hand, and the target icon has edge length $W = 2 \text{ cm}$. Empirical constants for the device are $a = 60 \text{ ms}$ and $b = 200 \text{ ms/bit}$. **(i)** Calculate the Index of Difficulty and predicted movement time. **(ii)** Suggest two design changes to shrink the Gulf of Execution. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Calculation (4 Marks):**

Step 1 — ID:

$$
ID = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right) = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{8}{2} + 1\right) = \log_2(5)
$$

$$
ID = \dfrac{\ln 5}{\ln 2} = \dfrac{1.6094}{0.6931} \approx 2.32 \text{ bits}
$$

Step 2 — MT:

$$
MT = a + b \cdot ID = 60 + 200 \times 2.32 = 60 + 464 = 524 \text{ ms}
$$

**(ii) Design changes to shrink the Gulf of Execution (3 Marks):**

1. **Enlarge the target** (increase $W$): Doubling $W$ from 2 cm to 4 cm reduces $ID$ from 2.32 to 1.32 bits, sharply reducing $MT$.
2. **Reposition the icon closer to the hand’s resting point** (reduce $D$): Halving $D$ from 8 cm to 4 cm reduces $ID$ to $\log_2(3) \approx 1.58$ bits.
3. **Add a strong signifier** (e.g., an obvious 3-D raised bezel and a contrasting label) so the operator perceives the action affordance before initiating the tap.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Confusing translation numbers with stage numbers:** Examiners will deduct a mark if you swap T3 and T4.
> 2. **Forgetting the unit “bits”** for $ID$ in Fitts’ law questions — *always* state units.
> 3. **Writing “feedback” without saying *when*** — KTU expects “**immediate** feedback” for full credit.
> 4. **Omitting the diagram in 7-mark sub-parts** — diagrams carry at least 1 mark each.
> 5. **Not closing the loop** in cycle diagrams — always show the “Goal unmet → loop back” arrow.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️

- ✅ An **Interaction Framework** decomposes user–system dialogue into **Goals → Tasks → Actions → Operations**, with explicit **gulfs** between them.
- ✅ **Norman’s 7 Stages** = 4 execution + 3 evaluation stages, forming the **Execution–Evaluation Cycle**.
- ✅ **Abowd & Beale’s Framework** = 4 components (User, Input, System, Output) + 4 translations.
- ✅ **Gulf of Execution** = User intention ↔ System action vocabulary. *Bridged by* affordances, signifiers, mappings, constraints.
- ✅ **Gulf of Evaluation** = System state ↔ User perception. *Bridged by* feedback, visibility, legibility.
- ✅ **Fitts’ Law** $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ — units of $ID$ are **bits**.
- ✅ **Hick’s Law** $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(N+1)$ — pairs naturally with the *decision* part of Stage 3.
- ✅ **KLM** is the predictive task-time model used for *Goal–Task–Action* decomposition.
- ✅ **Stage 1 (Forming the Goal)** lies *outside* the system boundary — it is the only pre-system stage.
- ✅ A *single* “Translation” in Abowd–Beale maps to *one or more* Norman stages.
- ✅ **Feedback must be immediate, visible, and interpretable** to fully close the evaluation loop.
- ✅ **Visibility, Feedback, Constraints, Mapping, Consistency** are the five design principles most cited in 7-mark KTU answers.
- ✅ Use a **Mermaid-style block diagram** in your answer sheet to score the dedicated *diagram mark*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ergonomics of Interaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT831 - HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ergonomics of Interaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Ergonomics (Human Factors)** is the scientific discipline concerned with the understanding of interactions among humans and other elements of a system, and the profession that applies theory, principles, data, and methods to design in order to optimize human well-being and overall system performance.

In the context of **Human–Machine Interaction (HMI)**, *Ergonomics of Interaction* refers to the application of ergonomic principles to the design, evaluation, and refinement of the interactive surfaces between a human user and a technological artifact — encompassing **input devices, output displays, physical work-stations, control layouts, and ambient environmental conditions**.

The **International Ergonomics Association (IEA)** classifies ergonomics into three broad domains, each of which is **mandatory** in the KTU 2024 syllabus mapping:

| Domain | Primary Focus | HMI Relevance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Physical Ergonomics** | Anatomical, anthropometric, biomechanical factors | Workstation, chair, keyboard, monitor positioning |
| **Cognitive Ergonomics** | Mental processes, perception, memory, reasoning | Menu structure, icon design, error messages |
| **Organizational Ergonomics** | Socio-technical systems, workflows, shift design | Team coordination, supervisory control |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Carpenter's Bench"**
> Imagine two carpenters given identical tools and wood. The *first* works on a wobbly stool at chest height in dim light. The *second* has a height-adjustable bench, an anti-fatigue mat, glare-free lighting, and tools within arm's reach. The **product** (a chair) is identical, but the *process* — and the carpenter's body after eight hours — will be radically different. **Ergonomics of interaction is the engineering of that "second workbench"** for digital and physical interfaces alike.

In other words, ergonomics answers: *"How do we make the system fit the human, instead of forcing the human to fit the system?"*

## 1.3 Why It Matters in KTU Examinations

- Every year, KTU examiners test whether students can connect **physical design metrics** (anthropometry, posture) with **cognitive limits** (Miller's 7±2, Fitts's Law) and **regulatory frameworks** (ISO 9241, NIOSH).
- A high-scoring answer must integrate *all three* domains rather than treating them as silos.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard to Remember:** **ISO 9241-210:2010** — "Ergonomics of human–system interaction — Part 210: Human-centred design for interactive systems." This is a frequently quoted framework in KTU boards.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Interaction Ergonomics

### Pillar A — Physical (Anatomical) Ergonomics
- **Posture & Biomechanics**: Neutral postures of the wrist (0°–15° extension), elbow (90°–110° flexion), and neck (<20° flexion) minimize static muscle load.
- **Anthropometry**: Design must accommodate the **5th–95th percentile** of the user population. The mean is *not* a safe design target.
- **Repetitive Strain Injury (RSI) / Work-related Musculoskeletal Disorders (WMSD)**: Cumulative trauma disorders (CTDs) caused by repetitive, forceful, or awkward movements.

### Pillar B — Cognitive Ergonomics
- **Mental Workload**: Working-memory demand of a task. Excessive workload → errors; insufficient workload → vigilance decrement.
- **Perception-Action Coupling**: Stimulus–Response compatibility (e.g., steering-wheel rotation matching visual path).
- **Situation Awareness (SA)**: Endsley's three-level model — *Perception → Comprehension → Projection*.

### Pillar C — Organizational Ergonomics
- Shift work, fatigue management, team coordination, and human-automation function allocation (Fitts's *MABA-MABA* list).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **high-yield** formulas for KTU ESE questions on Ergonomics of Interaction. Memorize the symbols and the *meaning*, not just the equation.

| # | Equation / Relation | Meaning | KTU Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right)$ | **Fitts's Law** — Movement Time to a target of width $W$ at distance $D$ | Keyboard/touch-target sizing |
| 2 | $D_2 = \log_2\!\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right)$ | **Index of Difficulty (ID)** in bits | Predicting device throughput |
| 3 | $IP = \frac{1}{b}$ | **Index of Performance (IP)** in bits/s | Comparing input devices |
| 4 | $RWL = LC \cdot HM \cdot VM \cdot DM \cdot AM \cdot FM \cdot CM$ | **NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)** in kg | Lifting-task evaluation |
| 5 | $LI = \frac{L}{RWL}$ | **Lifting Index (LI)** — risk of WMSD | $LI > 1 \Rightarrow$ hazard |
| 6 | $P = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + \mathrm{erf}\!\left(\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$ | **Anthropometric percentile** for a normally distributed dimension $x$ | 5th / 95th percentile design |
| 7 | $C = \frac{L_\text{max} - L_\text{min}}{L_\text{background}}$ | **Luminance Contrast** (Michelson) for display readability | VDU legibility |
| 8 | $V = R \cdot D \cdot P$ | **Visual Acuity** (Snellen): smallest resolvable detail = $1$ arc-minute | Display pixel pitch |
| 9 | $f_\text{Nyquist} = 2 \cdot f_\text{max}$ | **Nyquist-Shannon sampling** for display refresh | Flicker-free HMI |
| 10 | $\mathrm{SR} = \frac{\#\text{correct}}{\#\text{total}} \cdot 100\%$ | **Success Rate** in usability tests | Ergonomic validation |

> [!NOTE]
> All $a, b$ in Fitts's Law are empirically determined regression constants (e.g., for a mouse: $a \approx 50$ ms, $b \approx 150$ ms/bit). The **slope $b$** is the only one required in KTU 14-mark derivations.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **Avionics**: Boeing 787 cockpit — designers used *Fitts's Law* to place critical mode-selectors close to the pilot with a wide target face, reducing *eyes-off-time* by 30 %.
- **Medical Devices**: Infusion pumps validated via *NIOSH Lifting Index* and *cognitive walkthroughs* to prevent programming errors.
- **Consumer Electronics**: Smartphone touch-targets (44 pt × 44 pt minimum, Apple HIG) are a direct Fitts's-Law ergonomic constraint.
- **Industrial Robotics**: Anthropometric tables (DIN 33402) define the safe work-envelope around a cobot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** A 10 % reduction in wrist deviation angle can lower carpal-tunnel syndrome risk by up to **40 %** — a fact often quoted in KTU 14-mark answers on WMSD.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Fitts's Law and its Ergonomic Implication

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin:** Paul M. Fitts, 1954 — "The information capacity of the human motor system in controlling the amplitude of movement." *Journal of Experimental Psychology*, 47(6), 381–391.

### Step 1 — Information-Theoretic Foundation

The **information content** of a motor task of selecting one of $N$ equiprobable targets of equal width $W$ spanning an amplitude $A$ is given by Hartley–Shannon:

$$
H = \log_2(N) \text{ bits.}
$$

### Step 2 — Express $N$ in Spatial Terms

For a 1-D pointing task of distance $D$ and target width $W$:

$$
N = \frac{D}{W} + 1 \approx \frac{D}{W} \quad \text{(for } D \gg W\text{)}.
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute to Obtain Index of Difficulty

$$
D_2 \;=\; \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) \;\approx\; \log_2\!\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right) \text{ bits.}
$$

The factor $2$ in the numerator is the **Weiner–Fitts correction** that ensures $D_2 = 0$ when $D = W/2$ (i.e., the user is *on* the target).

### Step 4 — Linear Movement-Time Model

Empirically, average movement time scales linearly with information:

$$
T = a + b \cdot D_2.
$$

### Step 5 — Ergonomic Interpretation

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial W} = -\frac{b}{W \ln 2} < 0,
$$

so **larger targets ⇒ faster pointing**. The slope $b$ is itself an **ergonomic indicator** of device efficiency: a smaller $b$ (steeper throughput) means the device better matches the human motor channel.

### Step 6 — Index of Performance (Throughput)

$$
IP = \frac{D_2}{T} \approx \frac{1}{b} \quad \text{bits/s}.
$$

| Device | Typical $b$ (ms/bit) | IP (bits/s) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Stylus | 100 | 10.0 |
| Mouse | 150 | 6.7 |
| Touchscreen (finger) | 230 | 4.3 |
| Foot pedal | 400 | 2.5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often ask, *"Why is the mouse ergonomically superior to a foot pedal?"* The answer is grounded in the $IP$ column above — a larger $IP$ means a higher effective channel capacity, and a *better* human–machine fit.

## 3.2 Derivation: NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)

The **NIOSH Lifting Equation (1991, revised 1994)** computes the maximum recommended load under *ideal* conditions and then discounts it by six **multipliers** that quantify ergonomic risk.

### Step 1 — Load Constant

$$
LC = 23 \text{ kg} \quad \text{(Reference load under ideal conditions).}
$$

### Step 2 — Identify the Six Multipliers

Each multiplier $M \in [0, 1]$ and captures a *physical* ergonomic factor:

$$
M = \begin{cases}
HM & \text{(Horizontal Multiplier)} \\
VM & \text{(Vertical Multiplier)} \\
DM & \text{(Distance Multiplier)} \\
AM & \text{(Asymmetry Multiplier)} \\
FM & \text{(Frequency Multiplier)} \\
CM & \text{(Coupling Multiplier)}.
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 3 — Closed-Form Expression

$$
\begin{aligned}
RWL \;=\; &LC \cdot HM \cdot VM \cdot DM \cdot AM \cdot FM \cdot CM, \\
LI \;=\; &\frac{\text{Actual Load } L}{RWL}.
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Sample Numerical Walk-Through

A worker lifts $L = 18$ kg from a horizontal distance $H = 30$ cm, vertical origin $V = 25$ cm, vertical destination $V' = 110$ cm, asymmetric angle $A = 30°$, 4 lifts/min, fair coupling.

| Multiplier | Formula (1994 NIOSH) | Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $HM$ | $(25/H)$ for $H \le 25$ cm; $(25/H+0.0033(V-25))$ otherwise | 0.83 |
| $VM$ | $1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert$ | 0.85 |
| $DM$ | $0.82 + 4.5/D$ with $D = V' - V$ | 0.87 |
| $AM$ | $1 - 0.0032 A$ | 0.90 |
| $FM$ | From frequency table (4/min, 1 h) | 0.75 |
| $CM$ | Fair coupling table | 0.95 |

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
RWL &= 23 \times 0.83 \times 0.85 \times 0.87 \times 0.90 \times 0.75 \times 0.95, \\
    &\approx 23 \times 0.404, \\
    &\approx 9.3 \text{ kg}, \\[4pt]
LI &= \frac{18}{9.3} \approx 1.94.
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **$LI > 1$** indicates the worker is at *elevated* risk of WMSD. $LI > 3$ warrants *engineering control* redesign (NIOSH).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Ergonomic Domains to Design Actions

| Ergonomic Domain | Symptom if Ignored | Design Action (KTU answer ready) | Standard / Metric |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Physical (posture) | Back pain, neck strain | Adjustable chair; monitor at eye-level | ISO 9241-5 |
| Physical (force) | Tendonitis | Reduce grip force; spring-loaded keys | ANSI/HFES 100-2007 |
| Physical (anthropometry) | Reach mismatch | 5th–95th percentile reach envelopes | DIN 33402-2 |
| Cognitive (memory) | Mode errors | Recognize-not-recall; 7±2 menu depth | Miller's Law |
| Cognitive (attention) | Tunnel vision | Distinctive auditory warnings | ANSI S3.41 |
| Organizational (fatigue) | Shift-work errors | Max 12 h shift, 10 h rest | OSHA §1910 |
| Environmental (light) | Eye fatigue | 300–500 lux on VDU; matte screen | ISO 9241-307 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** When asked a 14-mark question, structure your answer as **(i) Domain, (ii) Hazard, (iii) Standard, (iv) Mitigation, (v) Verification metric.** This 5-point scaffold typically fetches full marks in KTU valuation.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Snellen Acuity ↔ Display Pixel Pitch

A 24-inch 1920×1080 monitor is viewed at 60 cm. Required *visual-acuity-limited* pixel pitch $p$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{\min} &= 1 \text{ arc-minute} = \frac{1}{60} \times \frac{\pi}{180} \text{ rad}, \\
p &= D \cdot \tan(\theta_{\min}), \\
p &= 0.60 \times \tan\!\left(\frac{\pi}{10800}\right), \\
p &\approx 1.75 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.175 \text{ mm}.
\end{aligned}
$$

The monitor's actual pixel pitch is $\approx 0.27$ mm, so **Snellen-acuity comfort is met with a safety margin of 1.55×** — an ergonomic comfort factor typical of contemporary displays.

> [!TIP]
> This derivation is a *favourite* KTU 14-mark sub-question: *"Derive the minimum pixel pitch for a 22-inch full-HD display viewed at 50 cm."* Use the same five-step scaffold.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
