# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`
**List and briefly explain any three types of sensors used for sensing finger motion in a data glove.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Flex Sensor (1 mark):** A thin polymer strip whose resistance increases with the radius of curvature. Used to measure the bend angle of finger joints (e.g., MCP, PIP, DIP). Typical resistance change: 10 kΩ (flat) to 30–40 kΩ (90° bend).

2. **Strain Gauge (1 mark):** A piezoresistive element bonded to the dorsal surface of the finger. Its resistance changes in proportion to the surface strain caused by tendon movement. Connected in a Wheatstone bridge for high sensitivity. Gauge Factor ≈ 2 for metallic types.

3. **Force-Sensitive Resistor — FSR (1 mark):** A polymer thick-film device whose resistance decreases with applied force. Mounted on the fingertip to measure grip pressure. Non-linear, but ideal for binary "contact" or "no-contact" detection.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional II]`
**What is the role of sEMG sensors in finger-gesture recognition? Mention two features extracted from the sEMG signal.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Role (2 marks):** Surface Electromyography (sEMG) measures the **summed electrical activity of motor units** in the forearm muscles (e.g., flexor digitorum superficialis) that control finger flexion. By recording these potentials via surface electrodes, an HMI can infer the user's *intended finger gesture* even when the hand is not moving — making sEMG the primary input modality for **prosthetic hand control** and **silent gesture recognition** in VR.

**Features (1 mark):**
- **Root Mean Square (RMS):** indicates contraction strength.
- **Mean Absolute Value (MAV):** average signal magnitude in a window.
- (Alternatively: Zero Crossing Rate, Waveform Length, Mean Frequency.)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a resistive flex-sensor based data glove** for capturing all five finger flexion angles. Discuss the readout circuit and the formula governing the voltage output. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flex sensor has a flat resistance of **10 kΩ** and follows the linear model $R(\theta) = 10\,(1 + 0.02\,\theta)\,\text{k}\Omega$, where $\theta$ is the bend angle in degrees. It is connected in a voltage divider with $R_{ref} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $V_{cc} = 5\,\text{V}$. **(i)** Calculate the output voltage when $\theta = 60°$. **(ii)** Find the angle at which the output voltage is exactly **2.0 V**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Architecture and Readout Circuit — 7 marks**

**Block diagram (3 marks):**

Five flex sensors — one on each finger (thumb + 4 fingers) — are mounted on the dorsal side of a glove along each finger's neutral bending axis. Each sensor's terminals are routed via a flexible flat cable (FFC) to a **central readout PCB** containing:

1. **Voltage divider network:** One reference resistor $R_{ref} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ per channel.
2. **Analog multiplexer (e.g., 74HC4051):** Time-multiplexes 5 channels into 1 ADC input.
3. **Instrumentation buffer (e.g., LM358):** High-impedance follower to prevent MUX-induced loading.
4. **16-bit ADC (e.g., ADS1115):** Digitizes the analog voltages.
5. **Microcontroller (e.g., ESP32 / STM32):** Reads ADC, applies calibration, transmits via USB/Bluetooth.
6. **Host software:** Maps voltages to joint angles, drives a VR avatar or robotic hand.

**Readout formula (2 marks):**

$$V_{out,i} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_{ref}}{R_{f,i}(\theta_i) + R_{ref}}$$

**Operating principle (2 marks):** As the finger bends, $R_f$ increases from 10 kΩ to ~30 kΩ, causing $V_{out}$ to drop from 2.5 V to ~1.25 V. The microcontroller applies a calibration polynomial (often linear after compensation) to convert $V_{out}$ back to angle in degrees.

---

**(b) Numerical Sub-parts — 7 marks**

**(i) Output voltage at $\theta = 60°$ (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $R_f$ at $\theta = 60°$:**
$$R_f(60) = 10\,(1 + 0.02 \times 60)\,\text{k}\Omega = 10 \times 2.2 = 22\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 2 — Apply voltage divider:**
$$V_{out} = 5 \cdot \frac{10}{22 + 10} = 5 \cdot \frac{10}{32}$$

$$V_{out} = \frac{50}{32} = 1.5625\,\text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = 1.5625\,\text{V} \approx 1.56\,\text{V}}$$

**(ii) Angle at which $V_{out} = 2.0\,\text{V}$ (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Set up the equation:**
$$2.0 = 5 \cdot \frac{10}{R_f + 10}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $R_f$:**
$$\frac{2.0}{5} = \frac{10}{R_f + 10} \implies 0.4 = \frac{10}{R_f + 10}$$

$$R_f + 10 = \frac{10}{0.4} = 25 \implies R_f = 15\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Use the resistance model to find $\theta$:**
$$15 = 10\,(1 + 0.02\theta)$$

$$\frac{15}{10} = 1 + 0.02\theta \implies 1.5 = 1 + 0.02\theta$$

$$0.5 = 0.02\theta \implies \theta = \frac{0.5}{0.02}$$

$$\boxed{\theta = 25°}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Unit mix-ups (–1 mark):** Students often write $R_f$ in ohms but $\theta$ in radians. Always quote units *with* the symbol.
> - **Formula mis-quote (–2 marks):** Writing $V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_f}{R_f + R_{ref}}$ is the *wrong* arm. Make sure your $R_{ref}$ corresponds to the *output tap* (the resistor *across* which you measure).
> - **Skipping the calibration step (–1 mark):** Real data gloves require a 2-point calibration (flat = 0°, fist = 90°). Mention this in part (a).
> - **Forgetting to draw the multiplexer (–1 mark):** A single ADC cannot read 5 sensors simultaneously without a MUX.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**(a)** Describe the working principle of a **strain-gauge based finger-bend sensor** deployed in a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration. Derive the small-signal output voltage expression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the use of **IMU-based finger pose estimation** in modern HMI systems. Compare it with sEMG-based gesture recognition in terms of latency, accuracy, and application. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Strain Gauge in Wheatstone Bridge — 7 marks**

**Working principle (3 marks):**
A metallic foil strain gauge is bonded to the dorsal surface of the finger using cyanoacrylate adhesive. As the finger flexes, the tendon beneath the skin pulls the surface, producing a tensile strain $\varepsilon$ along the gauge's long axis. This strain deforms the conductive grid, lengthening and thinning the resistive element, which increases its resistance by $\Delta R$.

**Wheatstone bridge deployment (2 marks):**
The active gauge is placed in one arm of a Wheatstone bridge. The other three arms are precision fixed resistors of equal value $R$. The bridge is excited by a stable DC source $V_{ex}$. The differential output $V_o$ is amplified by an instrumentation amplifier (e.g., INA128 with gain 1000).

**Derivation (2 marks):** Already derived in Section 3.2 above. Final small-signal result:

$$V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

---

**(b) IMU vs sEMG — 7 marks**

**IMU-based pose estimation (3 marks):**
Modern finger-tracking gloves (e.g., **Manomotion, Synertial Gloves, StretchSense**) use **9-DoF IMUs** (3-axis accelerometer + 3-axis gyroscope + 3-axis magnetometer) mounted on each finger segment. A **sensor fusion algorithm** (Madgwick or Mahony filter) combines gyro integration with accelerometer tilt and magnetometer heading to produce drift-corrected 3-D orientation. The resulting quaternion is mapped to skeletal joint angles.

**Comparison table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | IMU-based | sEMG-based |
|---|---|---|
| **Latency** | ~10–20 ms (suitable for real-time VR) | ~50–200 ms (windowing delay) |
| **Accuracy** | High for gross motion (±2–5°) | Moderate (gesture-level only) |
| **Information** | Absolute 3-D pose | Muscle intent |
| **Power** | Higher (sensor fusion) | Lower (after AFE) |
| **Application** | VR/AR avatar control, motion capture | Prosthetic limb control, silent speech |
| **Limitation** | Drift if magnetometer disturbed | Crosstalk, sweat, electrode shift |

**Conclusion (extra credit in KTU):** IMU is preferred for *outward* HMI (animation, VR avatar), while sEMG is preferred for *inward* intent decoding (prosthetics, hands-free control). Hybrid systems using both are the current research frontier.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this 1-page checklist the night before the exam.**

- ⭐ **Flex sensor = variable resistor**, $R$ *increases* with bend. Typical model: $R(\theta) = R_0(1 + k\theta)$.
- ⭐ **Strain gauge = Wheatstone bridge** — always. Output ≈ $\frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$.
- ⭐ **Gauge Factor** = 2.0 for metallic, **100–200** for semiconductor. **Always quote the value in your answer.**
- ⭐ **FSR** is for **grip/contact force**, not for joint angle. $R \approx k / F^n$.
- ⭐ **Capacitive sensor** uses parallel-plate equation: $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$. Sensitivity increases as $d$ decreases.
- ⭐ **sEMG** has bandwidth **20 Hz – 500 Hz**, amplitude **0.1–5 mV**. Use **RMS and MAV** as features.
- ⭐ **IMU = 9-DoF** (Accel + Gyro + Mag). Sample rate **≥ 50 Hz** to satisfy Nyquist for finger motion.
- ⭐ **Voltage divider output** is taken across the **reference** resistor (not the sensor) in KTU's standard convention.
- ⭐ **For data gloves**, a **multiplexer + single ADC** is the standard low-cost architecture; 5 flex sensors → 1 ADC channel.
- ⭐ **For precision**, use a **quarter-bridge** with an **INA128 instrumentation amplifier**, gain ~1000.
- ⭐ **KTU favourite numerical:** flex sensor + voltage divider with a *given linear model*. Practice at least 3 problems.
- ⭐ **Real-world system:** the **CyberGlove III**, **StretchSense**, and **HaptX Gloves DK2** are standard industry references — name-drop one in long answers.
- ⭐ **Dr. Stephen Hawking's cheek switch** is a famous single-sensor HMI example — useful for *elaboration* in any HMI answer.
- ⭐ **Always mention latency, drift, and calibration** in long answers — these are the buzzwords KTU examiners reward.
- ⭐ **Avoid the single biggest mistake:** writing the voltage divider formula upside-down ($R_f$ on top instead of $R_{ref}$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# MODULE 4: HANDS AND TOUCHING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> In the context of Human–Machine Interaction (HMI), *hands and touching* refers to the study of the human hand as a high-bandwidth bidirectional input/output channel — encompassing its biomechanical structure, tactile and kinesthetic sensing capabilities, manipulative dexterity, and the design of touch-enabled interfaces, haptic devices, and gestural controls that allow natural, efficient, and intuitive human interaction with digital systems.

### 1.1 The Human Hand — A Biological Input Device

The human hand is the most sophisticated and versatile manipulator in the known biological world. It is the primary conduit through which we physically engage with the physical and digital environment. From an HMI perspective, the hand is not merely a *pointer* but a complete **multimodal I/O system** with:

- **~ 17,000 touch receptors** in the palm and fingers (mechanoreceptors, thermoreceptors, nociceptors).
- **27 bones**, **27 joints**, and **over 123 ligaments** providing degrees of freedom (DOF).
- **~ 34 muscles** (intrinsic + extrinsic) enabling fine and gross motor control.
- **Precision grip (pinch) and power grip** as the two foundational grasp patterns.
- A **Fitts's Law** response time landscape dominated by sub-second target acquisition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)**
> Imagine the human hand as a *biologically engineered universal remote control* that works in 360°, never runs out of battery when warm, can sense texture, temperature, pressure, and vibration, and simultaneously acts as a feedback display. Unlike a TV remote (one-way, ~4 buttons), the hand talks *to* the system and *listens back* through touch — a closed-loop dialogue called **active touch (haptics)**. Every tap on a smartphone screen, every pinch-to-zoom, every time you feel a click on a keyboard — that is the hand-as-interface in action.

### 1.2 Why "Touch" is a Critical Modality in HMI

Donald Norman, in *The Design of Everyday Things*, identified **affordances** — perceived action possibilities. Touch surfaces *afford* pressing, sliding, and gesturing. The five reasons touch dominates modern HMI are:

| # | Reason | Engineering Implication |
|---|--------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Direct manipulation** | No intermediary device; WYSIWYG pointing. |
| 2 | **High dexterity** | Multi-finger, multi-touch input. |
| 3 | **Bidirectional** | Touch is sensed (input) and felt (output). |
| 4 | **Intuitive** | Low learning curve; users pre-trained by the physical world. |
| 5 | **Compact / portable** | Folds the keyboard, mouse, and display into one. |

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Touch receptor density (fingertip):** ~ 250 units / cm²
- **Two-point discrimination threshold (fingertip):** ~ **2 – 3 mm**
- **Two-point discrimination (palm):** ~ **10 – 15 mm**
- **Reaction time to tactile stimulus:** ~ **150 – 300 ms**
- **Minimum perceptible vibration (fingertip):** ~ **10 µm** displacement at 200–300 Hz
- **Spatial resolution of touch screens:** typically ≥ **5 mm** for Fitts' Law compliance
- **Sample rate for touch sensors:** ≥ **60 Hz** for smooth interaction

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Human Tactile System — Mechanoreceptors

The skin of the hand is embedded with four primary classes of mechanoreceptors, each tuned to a specific frequency band and function. This is the *biological analog* of a multi-band sensor array.

| Receptor | Type | Adaptation | Frequency Band | Function |
|----------|------|------------|----------------|----------|
| **Meissner corpuscle** | FA-I (Fast Adapting I) | Fast | 20 – 50 Hz | Light touch, low-frequency vibration, grip control |
| **Pacinian corpuscle** | FA-II | Fast | 100 – 300 Hz | High-frequency vibration, tool-mediated texture |
| **Merkel disk** | SA-I (Slow Adapting I) | Slow | DC – 5 Hz | Pressure, edge detection, form perception |
| **Ruffini ending** | SA-II | Slow | 7 – 15 Hz | Skin stretch, hand posture, slip detection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule for Haptic Devices**
> To convincingly simulate textures and clicks, haptic actuators must reproduce vibrations in the **20 – 300 Hz** band — the union of FA-I and FA-II response windows. Outside this band, the hand simply cannot feel the stimulus.

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Touch-Based Interaction

Following the HCI literature, touch interactions decompose along three orthogonal axes:

1. **Direct vs. Indirect touch** — finger on screen (direct) vs. stylus on graphics tablet (indirect).
2. **Static vs. Dynamic (gestural)** — single tap (static) vs. swipe/pinch (gestural).
3. **Single-touch vs. Multi-touch** — one contact point vs. simultaneous multiple contacts (≥ 2).

## 2.3 Touchscreen Technologies — Engineering Comparison

KTU examiners frequently ask for a comparative analysis of touch sensing technologies. The four dominant types are:

| Technology | Sensing Principle | Multi-touch | Cost | Typical Use |
|------------|-------------------|-------------|------|-------------|
| **Resistive** | Pressure → two conductive layers contact | No (single) | Low | ATMs, industrial |
| **Capacitive (projected)** | Disturbance in electrostatic field by conductive finger | Yes | Medium-High | Smartphones, tablets |
| **Infrared (IR)** | Break in IR light grid above screen | Yes | High | Large displays, kiosks |
| **Surface Acoustic Wave (SAW)** | Attenuation of ultrasonic waves on glass | Limited | High | Public kiosks |

> [!NOTE]
> **Capacitive touch — key equation**
> For a projected capacitive (PCAP) sensor, the finger acts as one plate of a parallel-plate capacitor with the sensor electrode as the other. The change in capacitance is approximated by:
>
> $$C \;\approx\; \varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, \frac{A}{d}$$
>
> where $\varepsilon_0$ is the permittivity of free space, $\varepsilon_r$ is the relative permittivity of the dielectric (typically glass + cover lens, $\varepsilon_r \approx 6$ to $10$), $A$ is the effective electrode area, and $d$ is the electrode-to-finger distance. When the finger approaches, the fringing-field capacitance increases, which the controller IC detects.

## 2.4 Fitts's Law — The Quantitative Heart of Pointing

For *all* hand-pointing tasks (mouse, finger, stylus, VR controller), **Fitts's Law** is the gold-standard predictive model.

### KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All symbols below are KTU-board-exam ready. Memorize the *form* of the equation, the *logarithmic* index, and the *unit* of $ID$.

| # | Equation / Term | Expression | Meaning / Units |
|---|------------------|------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Movement Time (MT)** | $MT \;=\; a + b \cdot ID$ | Total time to acquire target (s) |
| 2 | **Index of Difficulty (ID)** | $ID \;=\; \log_2 \!\left(\frac{2A}{W}\right)$ | Bits (Shannon formulation) |
| 3 | **Effective target width** | $W_e \;=\; \sqrt{4.133 \cdot \sigma^2}$ | Accounts for variability |
| 4 | **Effective ID** | $ID_e \;=\; \log_2 \!\left(\frac{2A}{W_e} + 1\right)$ | Revised (Welford) form |
| 5 | **Throughput (TP)** | $TP \;=\; \frac{ID_e}{MT}$ | Performance index (bits/s) |
| 6 | **Steering Law (Accot-Zhai)** | $MT \;=\; a + b \cdot \frac{A}{W}$ | For trail-following tasks |
| 7 | **Haptic Fitts' Law** | $MT_h \;=\; a_h + b_h \cdot ID$ | With force feedback |

> **Variable glossary:** $A$ = distance to target center, $W$ = target width along axis of motion, $a, b$ = empirically fit constants (device-dependent), $\sigma$ = standard deviation of hit points.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical tip on notation:** In KTU answer sheets, write $ID$ as $\log_2(2A/W)$ — never as $\log(2A/W)$. Marks are awarded for the explicit base-2 logarithm, since $ID$ is measured in **bits** (Shannon).

## 2.5 Haptic Feedback — From Passive to Active Touch

**Active touch** (haptics) is the bidirectional, exploratory use of the hand. The system must *push back* on the user through:

- **Tactile feedback** — skin-level stimuli (vibration, texture, pressure).
- **Kinesthetic feedback** — force, torque, joint-position cues.
- **Pseudo-haptic feedback** — sensory illusion created by visual substitution (e.g., when a virtual object's weight is conveyed only by cursor deceleration).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Realization of Haptics**
> Common haptic actuator technologies:
> - **Eccentric Rotating Mass (ERM)** motors — low-frequency buzz, used in early phones.
> - **Linear Resonant Actuators (LRA)** — sharp, crisp clicks at resonance (~150–200 Hz).
> - **Piezoelectric actuators** — broadband, ultra-fast response, used in HD touchscreens and surgical simulators.
> - **Voice-coil actuators** — variable amplitude, used in game controllers and force-feedback steering wheels.

## 2.6 Gesture-Based Interaction

A **gesture** is a movement of the body (typically the hand or fingers) that conveys meaning or intent. In HMI, gestures are classified as:

- **Manipulation gestures** — drag, pinch, rotate, swipe, fling.
- **Semantic (symbolic) gestures** — check mark, X, circle, custom sign-language symbols.
- **Deictic gestures** — pointing (the most primitive, used by infants from ~9 months).
- **Surface gestures** — confined to a 2D touch surface.
- **In-air (3D) gestures** — performed in mid-air, sensed by depth cameras or radar (e.g., Soli).

## 2.7 Engineering Applications

- **Smartphone & tablet UIs** — multi-touch capacitive displays with haptic confirmation.
- **Automotive HMI** — touchscreens + haptic touchpads to reduce driver gaze-time.
- **Medical simulators** — force-feedback surgical trainers (e.g., da Vinci skills simulator).
- **AR/VR controllers** — hand-tracking gloves (e.g., Meta Quest Touch, Manus gloves).
- **Industrial HMIs** — ruggedized resistive touch panels with gloved-hand support.
- **Assistive technology** — Braille displays, tactile graphics for the visually impaired.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Fitts's Law (Shannon Form)

The standard form $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2(2A/W)$ is *not* a derivation in the strict physics sense — it is an empirical law that Paul Fitts (1954) justified via the **Shannon-Hartley theorem** from information theory. Below is the conceptual step-by-step justification a KTU examiner expects.

### Step 1 — Position the task as a noisy communication channel

Fitts reasoned that pointing to a target of width $W$ at distance $A$ is equivalent to transmitting, through a motor-control channel, the information "I am hitting the target." The **channel capacity** of a noisy band-limited channel is given by Shannon's theorem:

$$C \;=\; B \cdot \log_2 \!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$

where $B$ is bandwidth (Hz), $S$ is signal power, $N$ is noise power.

### Step 2 — Map channel capacity to movement difficulty

Fitts *defined* the **Index of Difficulty** as the amount of information (in bits) the user must transmit to land inside the target:

$$ID \;=\; \log_2 \!\left(\frac{2A}{W}\right)$$

**Why the factor of 2?** Because the target can be approached from either direction; the *effective* spread of hit points is $2A$ (a distance of $A$ on each side of the target center, conservatively).

### Step 3 — Linear assumption for movement time

By the **linearity assumption** (later formalized in the 1990s as the *linearity-of-information* hypothesis), $MT$ grows linearly with $ID$:

$$MT \;=\; a + b \cdot ID$$

Substituting the $ID$ expression yields the canonical form:

$$MT \;=\; a + b \cdot \log_2 \!\left(\frac{2A}{W}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Throughput and effective ID

Real users show **normally distributed** hit points around the target center. Welford (1968) revised the model with **effective width** $W_e$:

$$W_e \;=\; \sqrt{4.133 \cdot \sigma^2} \;=\; 4.133 \, \sigma$$

(The constant 4.133 corresponds to the 96% target-acquisition success criterion, $z \approx 2.05$, $z^2 \approx 4.133$.)

The **effective Index of Difficulty** becomes:

$$ID_e \;=\; \log_2 \!\left(\frac{A}{W_e} + 1\right) \;=\; \log_2 \!\left(\frac{A}{4.133\,\sigma} + 1\right)$$

### Step 5 — Throughput, the device-independent performance metric

The slope $b$ carries device-specific variability. To compare devices fairly, MacKenzie (1992) defined **throughput** in bits/second:

$$TP \;=\; \frac{ID_e}{MT}$$

Typical values:

| Device | Throughput (bits/s) |
|--------|---------------------|
| Finger (touch) | 4 – 6 |
| Mouse | 4 – 6 |
| Stylus | 3 – 5 |
| Joystick | 1.5 – 3 |
| Touchpad | 3 – 5 |

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Fitts's Law for a Smartphone Tap

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A user must tap a circular icon of diameter $W = 20$ mm located $A = 80$ mm away from the user's current finger position. The empirical constants for the phone's capacitive touchscreen are $a = 250$ ms, $b = 130$ ms/bit. Compute the predicted movement time.

**Step 1 — Index of Difficulty (Shannon form).**

$$ID \;=\; \log_2 \!\left(\frac{2A}{W}\right) \;=\; \log_2 \!\left(\frac{2 \times 80}{20}\right) \;=\; \log_2(8) \;=\; 3 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Predicted Movement Time.**

$$MT \;=\; a + b \cdot ID \;=\; 250 + 130 \times 3 \;=\; 250 + 390 \;=\; 640 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**

A predicted $MT \approx 0.64$ s is in the typical range for finger taps on capacitive screens (≈ 0.5 – 0.8 s). Reducing $W$ to 10 mm (smaller icon) would double the $ID$ to 4 bits and increase $MT$ to $250 + 520 = 770$ ms — a **20 % slowdown** purely from halving the target.

## 3.3 Derivation — Effective Touch Sensor Resolution from Fitts's Law

To ensure a touchscreen's pixel grid does not bottleneck the user, the **sensor pitch** $p$ must satisfy:

$$p \;\le\; \frac{W_{\min}}{2}$$

where $W_{\min}$ is the smallest meaningful target the UI will offer. Using the minimum perceptible finger-spread for a pinch gesture (~ 8 mm) as $W_{\min}$:

$$p \;\le\; \frac{8 \text{ mm}}{2} \;=\; 4 \text{ mm}$$

This justifies modern smartphone pixel pitches of ~ 0.4 mm — orders of magnitude finer than the human finger's acuity, ensuring the *display* (not the sensor) is the limiting factor.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Trade-offs in Touch-Based HMI

| Design Lever | Effect on $MT$ | Engineering Cost | User Benefit |
|--------------|----------------|------------------|--------------|
| Increase $W$ (bigger targets) | Decreases $ID$ → lower $MT$ | Less information per screen | Faster, fewer errors |
| Increase haptic confirmation | Faster target *verification*, may shorten $MT$ | Adds actuator + driver | Confidence, accessibility |
| Add multi-touch | Enables 2-DOF manipulation in parallel | More complex firmware | Powerful gestures (pinch, rotate) |
| Use Fitts-compliant menu | Aligns $W$ with task frequency | Reflow / redesign | 30 – 50 % faster expert use |
| Add on-screen keyboard | Indirect touch, slower per key | None | Free-text input |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Hand as a Multi-Channel HMI Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PERIPHERAL["HAND AS PERIPHERAL"]
        A1[Mechanoreceptors in Skin]
        A2[Joint Angle Sensors]
        A3[Muscle Spindles]
    end

    subgraph SPINAL["SPINAL / CORTICAL PATHWAYS"]
        B1[Somatosensory Cortex S1]
        B2[Motor Cortex M1]
        B3[Cerebellum Coordination]
    end

    subgraph SENSOR["DIGITAL SENSE LAYER"]
        C1[Capacitive Touch Sensor]
        C2[Force Sensitive Resistor]
        C3[IMU and Flex Sensor]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE["COMPUTE LAYER"]
        D1[Touch Controller IC]
        D2[Gesture Classifier]
        D3[Haptic Rendering Engine]
    end

    subgraph ACTUATE["ACTUATION LAYER"]
        E1[Piezo Actuator]
        E2[Linear Resonant Actuator]
        E3[Voice Coil Force Feedback]
    end

    PERIPHERAL --> SPINAL
    SPINAL --> SENSOR
    SENSOR --> COMPUTE
    COMPUTE --> ACTUATE
    ACTUATE -->|Feedback| PERIPHERAL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** The flow is closed-loop. The biological hand senses the world, the brain plans, the digital sensor detects intent, the compute layer interprets and renders a response, the actuator delivers the response, and the skin feels it — completing the haptic loop in ~ 30 – 50 ms for crisp feedback.

## 4.2 Touchscreen Stack — Cross-Section (Functional)

```mermaid

flowchart LR

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> *Define haptic feedback and distinguish between tactile and kinesthetic feedback with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Haptic feedback** is the use of the sense of touch to provide information to a user about an interaction with a computer system. It is a closed-loop modality that uses mechanical stimulation (vibration, force, motion) to communicate with the user.
>
> 1. **Tactile feedback** stimulates the skin's mechanoreceptors (Meissner, Pacinian, Merkel, Ruffini) and conveys *surface-level* sensations such as texture, vibration, and pressure. *Example:* A smartphone's Taptic Engine vibrating when a key is tapped.
> 2. **Kinesthetic feedback** stimulates receptors in the muscles, joints, and tendons to convey *force, weight, and position*. *Example:* The resistance felt in the joystick of a flight simulator when pushing against aerodynamic load.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Expected]**
> *State Fitts's Law and explain the meaning of its two key parameters, A and W.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Fitts's Law models the **time required to move to and select a target** as a linear function of the *index of difficulty* of the task. The canonical form is
>
> $$MT \;=\; a + b \cdot \log_2 \!\left(\frac{2A}{W}\right)$$
>
> - **A (Amplitude / Distance):** the distance from the starting point of the hand (or cursor) to the center of the target. Larger $A$ ⇒ more time.
> - **W (Width):** the size of the target measured along the axis of motion. Larger $W$ ⇒ less time.
> - The term $\log_2(2A/W)$ is the **Index of Difficulty (ID)** measured in *bits*; it represents the information content of the motor task.
> - The constants $a$ and $b$ are determined empirically for each input device (finger, mouse, stylus, etc.).

---

## Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Q3 (A). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
> *(a)* Describe the four types of mechanoreceptors found in the human hand and explain how their frequency-response characteristics influence the design of haptic actuators. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* With a neat block diagram, explain the closed-loop architecture of a haptic touch interface, identifying the roles of the capacitive sensor, controller IC, and piezoelectric actuator. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Q3(A)(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Define the role of mechanoreceptors.**
Mechanoreceptors are specialized nerve endings in the skin that transduce mechanical deformation (pressure, vibration, stretch) into neural signals. They are the *biological sensors* of the hand.

**Step 2 (3 marks) — Tabulate the four types.**

| Receptor | Adaptation | Best Frequency | Function |
|----------|------------|----------------|----------|
| Meissner | FA-I | 20 – 50 Hz | Light touch, low-frequency vibration, grip slip |
| Pacinian | FA-II | 100 – 300 Hz | High-frequency vibration, fine texture |
| Merkel | SA-I | DC – 5 Hz | Static pressure, edge detection, form |
| Ruffini | SA-II | 7 – 15 Hz | Skin stretch, hand posture |

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Connect to haptic design.**

To simulate a *texture*, the actuator must reproduce high-frequency vibrations in the **100 – 300 Hz** band (Pacinian). To simulate a *button click*, a sharp transient with a frequency component near the **20 – 50 Hz** range (Meissner) is required. To convey *grip force* or *firmness*, a low-frequency, sustained stimulus in the **0 – 15 Hz** band (Merkel, Ruffini) is appropriate. **Therefore, a broadband haptic actuator (e.g., piezoelectric)** is preferred over a single-frequency ERM motor because it can span all four receptor bands.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Concluding remark.**
Modern HD touchscreens use piezo actuators precisely because they can deliver energy across the entire 0 – 300 Hz tactile band, faithfully matching the four receptor windows.

### Model Solution — Q3(A)(b) — 7 marks

**Block diagram (drawn by student, 3 marks):**

The student is expected to draw the closed loop with the following six labelled blocks:

1. **User's finger** (with mechanoreceptors in skin).
2. **Capacitive sensor layer** (ITO grid) on the touchscreen.
3. **Touch controller IC** (e.g., focal-plane ASIC that scans the electrode matrix).
4. **Host processor / Gesture classifier** (software).
5. **Haptic driver circuit** (high-voltage amplifier for piezo).
6. **Piezoelectric actuator** bonded under the screen.

Arrows must form a loop: finger → sensor → controller → processor → driver → actuator → finger.

**Explanatory text (4 marks):**

- **Capacitive sensor:** Detects the change in fringing-field capacitance caused by the conductive finger. A self- or mutual-capacitance scan resolves the (x, y) contact position and supports multi-touch.
- **Touch controller IC:** Periodically scans the electrode matrix (typically 60 – 240 Hz), filters noise, computes touch coordinates, and reports them to the host over I²C or SPI.
- **Host processor / Gesture classifier:** Identifies user intent (tap, swipe, pinch, rotate) using a gesture-recognition algorithm.
- **Haptic driver + piezo actuator:** When the system decides to provide a *click*, the driver applies a sharp high-voltage step to the piezo, causing the cover glass to deform briefly. The deformation stimulates the user's mechanoreceptors, closing the loop.

> **Concluding sentence (must appear in answer):** The latency of this closed loop must remain below ~ 30 ms for the haptic confirmation to feel *synchronous* with the visual response; otherwise users perceive a mismatch and report the device as "laggy."

---

> **Q3 (B). [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Expected Alternative Choice]**
> *(a)* Compare resistive, capacitive (projected), infrared, and SAW touchscreen technologies with respect to sensing principle, multi-touch support, durability, and cost. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* Apply Fitts's Law to predict the movement time for a stylus-tap task on a graphics tablet, where the stylus must travel $A = 100$ mm to a target of width $W = 8$ mm. The empirically measured constants for a stylus on a Wacom tablet are $a = 220$ ms, $b = 145$ ms/bit. Also compute the throughput given the standard deviation of hit points $\sigma = 1.5$ mm. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Q3(B)(a) — 7 marks

**Comparison table (5 marks) — must be present in the answer:**

| Property | Resistive | Capacitive (PCAP) | Infrared (IR) | SAW |
|----------|-----------|-------------------|---------------|-----|
| Sensing principle | Two conductive layers touch under finger pressure | Disturbance in electrostatic field by conductive finger | Break in grid of IR beams above screen | Attenuation of ultrasonic waves travelling on glass |
| Multi-touch | No (single) | Yes (≥ 5 points typical) | Yes (unlimited in theory) | Limited (1 – 2) |
| Durability | Moderate (top PET layer wears) | High (glass surface) | High (no contact layer) | High (glass surface) |
| Cost | Low | Medium-High | High | High |
| Works with gloves | Yes | Sometimes (special tuning) | Yes | Yes |
| Optical clarity | Low (extra layers) | High | High | Very high |

**Discussion (2 marks):**
PCAP dominates consumer devices due to multi-touch + glass durability, but resistive remains in industrial HMIs and ATMs because it works with *any* input (gloved finger, stylus, pen). IR is reserved for very large displays (> 40") and SAW for high-end public kiosks where optical clarity is paramount.

### Model Solution — Q3(B)(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Write Fitts's Law.**

$$MT \;=\; a + b \cdot \log_2 \!\left(\frac{2A}{W}\right)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Compute $ID$.**

$$ID \;=\; \log_2 \!\left(\frac{2 \times 100}{8}\right) \;=\; \log_2(25) \;\approx\; 4.644 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Compute $MT$.**

$$MT \;=\; 220 + 145 \times 4.644 \;\approx\; 220 + 673.4 \;\approx\; 893.4 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Compute $W_e$.**

$$W_e \;=\; 4.133 \cdot \sigma \;=\; 4.133 \times 1.5 \;\approx\; 6.20 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Compute $ID_e$ (Welford form).**

$$ID_e \;=\; \log_2 \!\left(\frac{A}{W_e} + 1\right) \;=\; \log_2 \!\left(\frac{100}{6.20} + 1\right) \;=\; \log_2(17.13) \;\approx\; 4.099 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Compute throughput.**

$$TP \;=\; \frac{ID_e}{MT} \;=\; \frac{4.099}{0.8934} \;\approx\; 4.59 \text{ bits/s}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Interpret.** A throughput of ~ 4.6 bits/s is typical for stylus input and is comparable to a finger on a capacitive touchscreen, confirming that *precision* pointing devices do not necessarily yield higher throughput than direct touch.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Base of the logarithm:** Always write $\log_2$, not $\ln$ or $\log_{10}$. Losing 1 mark per part is common.
> 2. **Units of $ID$:** State explicitly "in bits." A student who writes "$ID = 4.6$" without units loses a half mark.
> 3. **Effective width formula:** Welford's $W_e = 4.133\sigma$ — the constant is $4.133$, not $4$ or $2$. Memorize the value.
> 4. **Receptor-to-actuator mapping:** Examiners expect a clear *frequency band* match, not a vague "the actuator should vibrate."
> 5. **Touchscreen comparison table:** Every cell must be filled. An empty cell = lost marks.
> 6. **Closed-loop diagram:** Arrows must be drawn. Boxes without arrows receive partial credit only.
> 7. **Throughput units:** Always quote throughput in **bits/second**.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] The human hand is the **primary HMI manipulator** — 27 bones, ~ 17 000 receptors, two-point discrimination ~ 2–3 mm at the fingertip.
- [x] Four mechanoreceptors — **Meissner (FA-I, 20–50 Hz)**, **Pacinian (FA-II, 100–300 Hz)**, **Merkel (SA-I, DC–5 Hz)**, **Ruffini (SA-II, 7–15 Hz)** — dictate the design band of haptic actuators.
- [x] Touchscreen technologies: **Resistive** (cheap, single-touch), **Capacitive / PCAP** (multi-touch, dominant), **IR** (large displays), **SAW** (high clarity kiosks).
- [x] **Fitts's Law:** $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2(2A/W)$, with $ID$ in **bits**.
- [x] **Effective width** $W_e = 4.133\sigma$; **Throughput** $TP = ID_e / MT$ in **bits/s**.
- [x] Haptic feedback is **bidirectional** — *tactile* (skin) vs. *kinesthetic* (joints/muscles) — with a closed-loop latency budget of **≤ 30 ms**.
- [x] Gestures classified along **direct / indirect, static / dynamic, single / multi-touch**, with surface vs. in-air distinction.
- [x] Modern smartphones use **piezoelectric actuators** for broadband (0–300 Hz) haptic response matching all four receptor windows.
- [x] Haptic Fitts's Law variant: $MT_h = a_h + b_h \cdot ID$ — applies when force feedback is active.
- [x] Three pillars of any touch-HMI exam answer: **(1) sensing, (2) processing, (3) actuation** — always include the closed loop.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interactive Digital Surfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT831 - HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Interactive Digital Surfaces ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interactive Digital Surfaces** are flat, planar, or near-planar physical interfaces that integrate sensing, computation, and display capabilities to detect, recognize, and respond to direct human inputs—such as touch, gestures, pressure, hover, or tangible object manipulation—thereby enabling a seamless two-way dialogue between the user and the underlying digital system. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEBRT831 - Human Machine Interface)**, interactive digital surfaces form a critical sub-domain of **post-WIMP (Windows, Icons, Menus, Pointer)** interaction paradigms. They represent the physical embodiment of **direct manipulation interfaces**, where the surface itself becomes both the input device and the output display. ### 1.2 Key Taxonomy of Interactive Digital Surfaces | Surface Category | Form Factor | Example Use Case | |------------------|-------------|------------------| | **Interactive Tabletops** | Horizontal, table-sized | Microsoft Surface, SMART Table | | **Interactive Walls** | Large vertical displays | SMART Board, Google Jamboard | | **Interactive Whiteboards** | Wall-mounted | Education & corporate collaboration | | **Tabletop Tangible Interfaces** | Object-tagged surfaces | Reactable, Sensetable | | **Smart Surfaces** | Embedded in furniture/walls | Microsoft PixelSense, Displax | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of an Interactive Digital Surface as a "smart piece of paper"** — Imagine your dining table, but every square inch of it can sense where your fingers are touching, recognize what objects you place on it, and instantly project a responsive image at those very locations. The line between "the thing you touch" and "the thing that responds" disappears completely. A more relatable analogy: - A **traditional desktop computer** = typing commands *at* a machine (indirect) - A **smartphone touchscreen** = directly tapping the icons you see - An **interactive digital surface** = the **entire room's wall/table** becomes that smartphone — you can drag virtual windows, share content physically, and have multiple users interacting simultaneously without needing a keyboard or mouse. ### 1.4 Core Properties (KTU 2024 Learning Outcomes) An interactive digital surface must satisfy the following **fundamental properties**: 1. **Co-location of Input and Output** — The same physical space serves as both display and sensor. 2. **Multi-user Capability** — Supports simultaneous, parallel interactions by multiple users. 3. **Multi-modal Sensing** — Detects touch, gestures, pressure, hover, and object recognition. 4. **Direct Manipulation** — Objects on the surface can be directly grasped, moved, and rotated by hand. 5. **Tangible Affordance** — Physical objects placed on the surface carry digital meaning. ### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Frame Rate for Smooth Display**: $\geq 60 \text{ Hz}$ (minimum perceptual threshold) - **Touch Sampling Rate**: Typically **100–250 Hz** - **Standard Touch Latency**: **< 10 ms** (acceptable) to **< 5 ms** (premium) - **Multi-touch Points**: **2 points (basic)** to **100+ points (advanced surfaces)** - **Display Resolution**: **4K UHD (3840 × 2160)** standard, **8K (7680 × 4320)** emerging - **Minimum Touch Target Size**: **9 mm × 9 mm** (per ISO 9241-9 ergonomics) - **Activation Force (Capacitive)**: **0.1 N – 0.2 N** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification by Sensing Technology Interactive digital surfaces are categorized based on the **underlying sensor technology** used to detect user input. Each has distinct advantages, limitations, and ideal use-cases. #### 2.1.1 Capacitive Sensing Surfaces **Operating Principle**: Capacitive surfaces detect touch by measuring changes in an **electrostatic field** projected across the surface. A human finger (being a conductor) disturbs this field, and the change in capacitance $C$ is measured at each electrode intersection. $$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$ Where: - $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$ (vacuum permittivity) - $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity of the dielectric - $A$ = effective electrode overlap area - $d$ = distance between electrodes **Why it works**: When a finger approaches, it forms a virtual capacitor with the electrodes, modifying the local field. **Limitations**: Cannot detect inputs through thick gloves, doesn't work with non-conductive styli (unless active). #### 2.1.2 Resistive (Pressure-Based) Surfaces **Operating Principle**: Two conductive layers separated by micro-spacers touch when pressed, completing a circuit. Voltage dividers compute the touch coordinate. $$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$ Where $R_1$ and $R_2$ are the resistance values from the touch point to each edge electrode. **Strengths**: Works with any input (finger, glove, stylus). **Weaknesses**: Poor optical clarity (~75%), limited multi-touch (requires sophisticated electronics). #### 2.1.3 Infrared (IR) Optical Surfaces **Operating Principle**: An array of **IR LEDs** along the surface perimeter emits beams, and **photodetectors** on the opposite side detect interruptions. **Theoretical Detection**: An object is detected when: $$I_{detected} = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha x} < I_{threshold}$$ Where $I_0$ is the incident IR intensity, $\alpha$ is the attenuation coefficient, and $x$ is the obstruction path length. **Why useful**: Scalable to enormous sizes (walls, floors), no surface coating required, supports any object. #### 2.1.4 Frustrated Total Internal Reflection (FTIR) **Operating Principle**: IR light is injected into an acrylic/glass slab. When a finger touches the surface, it **frustrates** total internal reflection at the contact point, scattering light downward toward a camera. The **critical angle** for total internal reflection is given by: $$\theta_c = \arcsin\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$$ For acrylic ($n_1 \approx 1.49$) and air ($n_2 = 1.0$): $$\theta_c = \arcsin\left(\frac{1.0}{1.49}\right) \approx 42.2°$$ #### 2.1.5 Diffused Illumination (DI) **Operating Principle**: IR LEDs illuminate the surface from below; diffuse reflection is captured by a camera. Touches appear as **bright spots** against a darker background. #### 2.1.6 Vision-Based (Camera Tracking) **Operating Principle**: Multiple cameras track shadows, finger blobs, or markers. Computer vision algorithms (blob detection, contour tracking) compute touch coordinates. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter / Concept | Formula / Value | Application | |---------------------|-----------------|-------------| | Capacitance (parallel plate) | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Capacitive touchscreens | | Critical angle (TIR) | $\theta_c = \arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$ | FTIR surfaces | | Voltage divider (resistive) | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Resistive touchscreens | | Refresh rate threshold | $f \geq 60 \text{ Hz}$ | Perceptual smoothness | | Touch latency (acceptable) | $t_l < 10 \text{ ms}$ | Real-time interaction | | Touch target size (ISO) | $9 \text{ mm} \times 9 \text{ mm}$ | Ergonomic standard | | Snell's law (refraction) | $n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$ | Optical surface design | | IR attenuation | $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha x}$ | IR sensing surfaces | | Touch sampling rate | $100 - 250 \text{ Hz}$ | High-precision surfaces | | Frame time at 60 Hz | $t_f = \dfrac{1}{60} \approx 16.67 \text{ ms}$ | Display system design | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip**: Always state the **sensing technology**, its **working principle**, and at least **two advantages/limitations** when answering comparison questions on interactive surfaces. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Interactive digital surfaces are deployed across multiple high-impact domains: - **Medical Surgery**: Operating room touch interfaces for sterile image manipulation. - **Education**: SMART Boards, Google Jamboard for collaborative learning. - **Retail & Museums**: Interactive product catalogs and exhibits. - **Control Rooms**: Air traffic control, military command centers. - **Automotive**: In-vehicle infotainment and dashboard surfaces. - **Architecture & Design**: Tangible interfaces for spatial design (e.g., MIT's "Second Surface"). - **Banking**: Self-service kiosks and ATM multi-touch interfaces. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Touch Coordinate Calculation in a 5-Wire Resistive Surface A **5-wire resistive touchscreen** uses four corner electrodes and one central sensing wire. The X-coordinate is determined by applying voltage to the left and right corners and sensing via the center wire. **Step 1: Apply excitation voltage $V_{in}$ across the X-axis (left ↔ right).** A uniform potential gradient is established across the surface: $$V(x) = V_{in} \cdot \frac{x}{W}$$ Where $W$ is the total width of the surface. **Step 2: Measure the voltage $V_x$ at the touch point via the center sensing wire.** Because the center wire is in contact with the top resistive layer at coordinate $(x_t, y_t)$, the voltage read at the wire equals the local potential: $$V_x = V_{in} \cdot \frac{x_t}{W}$$ **Step 3: Solve for the touch X-coordinate.** $$x_t = W \cdot \frac{V_x}{V_{in}}$$ **Step 4: Repeat the process with Y-axis electrodes to obtain $y_t$.** Switch the excitation to the top ↔ bottom electrodes, and read $V_y$ at the center wire: $$V_y = V_{in} \cdot \frac{y_t}{H}$$ $$\therefore y_t = H \cdot \frac{V_y}{V_{in}}$$ **Step 5: Final coordinate pair.** $$\boxed{(x_t, y_t) = \left( W \cdot \frac{V_x}{V_{in}}, \quad H \cdot \frac{V_y}{V_{in}} \right)}$$ **Interpretation**: The resistive layer behaves as a *continuous voltage divider*, allowing the controller to compute a unique (x, y) coordinate for any single touch point. This is why the technology is cost-effective but limited in multi-touch capability. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Multi-Touch Detection via Mutual Capacitance Modern capacitive surfaces (used in iPads, Microsoft Surface Hubs) use a **mutual capacitance** grid of rows and columns. **Step 1: Define the mutual capacitance between a row electrode $R_i$ and a column electrode $C_j$.** $$M_{ij} = C_{ij} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A_{ij}}{d_{ij}}$$ Where $A_{ij}$ is the overlap area and $d_{ij}$ is the dielectric gap at intersection $(i,j)$. **Step 2: Drive row $R_i$ with an AC excitation signal.** The drive signal at angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$ is: $$v(t) = V_p \sin(\omega t)$$ **Step 3: Measure the resulting current $i_{ij}$ flowing into column $C_j$.** For an ideal mutual capacitor: $$i_{ij}(t) = C_{ij} \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} = C_{ij} \cdot V_p \omega \cos(\omega t)$$ **Step 4: Detect the touch effect.** When a finger approaches intersection $(i,j)$, it draws charge, reducing $C_{ij}$ to $C'_{ij}$. The change in current magnitude is: $$\Delta i_{ij} = (C_{ij} - C'_{ij}) \cdot V_p \omega$$ **Step 5: Threshold detection and coordinate mapping.** A touch is registered at $(i,j)$ if: $$\vert \Delta i_{ij} \vert > \Delta i_{threshold}$$ A typical threshold is **5–10% of the baseline current**. **Step 6: Multi-touch reconstruction.** Each intersection $(i,j)$ of the $M \times N$ grid is scanned independently, yielding a **touch map**: $$T[i,j] = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \vert \Delta i_{ij} \vert > \Delta i_{threshold} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ This binary map is then processed by the **touch controller's gesture recognition engine** to identify taps, swipes, pinches, and rotations. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Sensing Technologies | Feature | Capacitive | Resistive | Infrared | FTIR | Vision-Based | |---------|-----------|-----------|----------|------|--------------| | **Multi-touch** | Yes (10+) | Limited (2) | Yes (unlimited) | Yes (unlimited) | Yes | | **Optical Clarity** | Excellent (>90%) | Poor (~75%) | N/A (no overlay) | N/A | N/A | | **Works with Gloves** | Limited | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | | **Works with Stylus** | Active only | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | | **Latency** | Very Low | Low | Low | Medium | Medium-High | | **Scalability** | Medium | Medium | Excellent | Medium | Excellent | | **Cost** | Medium | Low | High | Medium | High | | **Object Recognition** | No | No | Limited | Limited | Yes | | **Outdoor Use** | Limited | Good | Good | Limited | Limited | --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A 5-wire resistive touchscreen has dimensions $W = 300 \text{ mm}$ and $H = 200 \text{ mm}$. The excitation voltage is $V_{in} = 5 \text{ V}$. The measured voltages at the touch point are $V_x = 1.8 \text{ V}$ and $V_y = 3.2 \text{ V}$. Compute the touch coordinates. **Step 1: Compute $x_t$.** $$x_t = W \cdot \frac{V_x}{V_{in}} = 300 \cdot \frac{1.8}{5.0} = 300 \cdot 0.36 = 108 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2: Compute $y_t$.** $$y_t = H \cdot \frac{V_y}{V_{in}} = 200 \cdot \frac{3.2}{5.0} = 200 \cdot 0.64 = 128 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 3: Express the coordinate.** $$\boxed{(x_t, y_t) = (108 \text{ mm}, \, 128 \text{ mm})}$$ **Step 4: Verify normalization.** $$\frac{x_t}{W} + \frac{y_t}{H} = 0.36 + 0.64 = 1.0 \text{ (independent — coordinate is valid)}$$ This means the touch is at **36% of the width** and **64% of the height** — approximately the lower-right quadrant of the surface. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Interactive Digital Surface System ```mermaid graph TD nodeA[User Input Layer
Finger, Stylus, Tangible Object] nodeB[Sensor Subsystem
Capacitive / IR / FTIR / Vision] nodeC[Signal Conditioning
Filtering, Amplification, ADC] nodeD[Touch Controller MCU
Coordinate Reconstruction] nodeE[Gesture Recognition Engine
Pattern Matching Algorithm] nodeF[Application Logic Layer
Event Handlers, State Machine] nodeG[Graphics Rendering Engine
Scene Graph, Compositor] nodeH[Display Subsystem
LCD / OLED / Projection] nodeI[Output to User
Visual, Audio, Haptic Feedback] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI nodeI -.-> nodeA ``` **Description of the flow**: - The user touches the surface with a finger, stylus, or physical object. - The **sensor subsystem** detects this contact using one of the technologies discussed. - **Signal conditioning** removes noise, amplifies the signal, and digitizes it via an **ADC**. - The **touch controller MCU** reconstructs the (x, y) coordinates and timestamp. - The **gesture recognition engine** interprets multi-finger patterns as gestures (e.g., pinch, rotate). - The **application logic** dispatches events to the appropriate UI components. - The **rendering engine** updates the display in real time. - The updated display is shown back to the user, **closing the feedback loop**. --- ### 4.2 Sensing Technology Decision Flow ```mermaid graph TD start{Start
Choose Sensing Technology} q1{Need High
Optical Clarity?} q2{Multi-touch
Required?} q3{Large Area
Over 65 inches?} q4{Detect Physical
Objects?} cap[Capacitive Sensing] res[Resistive Sensing] ir[Infrared Grid] ftir[FTIR with Camera] vis[Vision-Based System] start --> q1 q1 -->|Yes| q2 q1 -->|No| res q2 -->|Yes 10plus| cap q2 -->|No 2 finger| res start --> q3 q3 -->|Yes| q4 q3 -->|No| cap q4 -->|Yes| vis q4 -->|No| ir start --> q4 q4 -->|Yes Hover| ftir ``` --- ### 4.3 Interactive Digital Surface — Layered Architecture ```mermaid graph TD L1[Layer 5 - Application Layer
Collaboration Apps, Games, Control Systems] L2[Layer 4 - Middleware
Input Manager, Gesture Library, TUIO Protocol] L3[Layer 3 - Processing Layer
Multi-touch Tracking, Blob Detection, Kalman Filter] L4[Layer 2 - Sensor Abstraction Layer
Device Drivers, Calibration, Coordinate Mapping] L5[Layer 1 - Hardware Layer
Sensor Grid, Display Panel, Embedded MCU] L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 ``` > [!NOTE] > **TUIO (Tangible User Interface Objects)** is a standard UDP-based protocol used to transmit touch and tangible object data from interactive surfaces to client applications. It is highly relevant for KTU exams. --- ### 4.4 Multi-Touch Event Lifecycle ```mermaid graph TD s1[Touch Down Event
TOUCH_BEGIN] s2[Touch Update Event
TOUCH_MOVE] s3[Touch Up Event
TOUCH_END] s4[Touch Cancel Event
TOUCH_CANCEL] s5[Gesture Recognition
Pinch, Rotate, Swipe] s6[Action Dispatched to App] s1 --> s2 s2 --> s2 s2 --> s3 s1 --> s4 s3 --> s5 s4 --> s5 s5 --> s6 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define an interactive digital surface and list any **three** key properties that distinguish it from a traditional WIMP interface. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > An **interactive digital surface** is a flat physical surface that integrates sensing, computation, and display technologies to enable direct, multi-user manipulation of digital content using touch, gestures, or tangible objects. **Three distinguishing properties (1 mark each):** 1. **Co-location of input and output** — The surface is both display and input device simultaneously. 2. **Multi-user, multi-touch capability** — Supports simultaneous interactions by multiple users without mode switching. 3. **Tangible and direct manipulation** — Physical objects and bare-hand gestures interact directly with on-screen content, eliminating the indirection of a mouse/keyboard. --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Differentiate between **FTIR** and **Diffused Illumination (DI)** as optical sensing techniques for interactive surfaces. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | FTIR | Diffused Illumination | |--------|------|------------------------| | **Light Source Position** | IR LEDs at edges of acrylic slab | IR LEDs beneath the surface | | **Detection Principle** | Touch *frustrates* total internal reflection | Touch causes *diffuse reflection* of upward IR light | | **Visual Appearance** | Touches appear **bright** on dark background | Touches appear **bright** on dim background | | **Best For** | High-contrast touch detection | Pressure estimation, finger shape analysis | | **Sensitivity** | Excellent for light touches | Better for heavier, sustained contact | --- ### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`** **(a)** Explain in detail the **working principle of a capacitive touchscreen**, including the self-capacitance and mutual-capacitance methods. Compare the two methods with respect to multi-touch performance. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **architecture of an interactive tabletop** with a neat block diagram. Discuss how multi-user collaboration is supported. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A **(a) Capacitive Touchscreen Working Principle (7 marks):** **1. Background and Principle (2 marks):** A capacitive touchscreen relies on the **change in capacitance** caused by a conductive object (typically a finger) approaching or touching the surface. The surface is overlaid with a transparent electrode grid made of **Indium Tin Oxide (ITO)**. When a finger approaches the surface, it acts as one plate of a capacitor, with the ITO electrode acting as the other. The dielectric is the cover glass and air gap. The change in capacitance is detected and used to compute the touch coordinate. **2. Self-Capacitance Method (2 marks):** - Each electrode (row or column) is measured **independently** with respect to ground. - A finger touch increases the capacitance of the electrodes nearest to it. - **Limitations**: Suffers from the **"ghosting problem"** in multi-touch — when two fingers touch, the controller cannot uniquely determine the touch points because the same set of electrodes is activated regardless of which diagonal the fingers are on. - Example: Used in basic single-touch devices (older smartphones). **3. Mutual-Capacitance Method (2 marks):** - Every **intersection of a row and column electrode** has a measurable mutual capacitance $C_{ij}$. - A driving signal is applied to each row, and the resulting current is measured at each column. - A finger touch **reduces the local mutual capacitance** at that intersection, which is uniquely identifiable. - Enables **true multi-touch** with no ghosting. - Used in modern smartphones, tablets, and high-end interactive surfaces (Apple iPad, Microsoft Surface). **4. Comparison Table (1 mark):** | Property | Self-Capacitance | Mutual-Capacitance | |----------|------------------|---------------------| | Multi-touch | Limited (ghost touches) | True multi-touch | | Cost | Lower | Higher | | Sensing granularity | Per electrode | Per intersection | | Use case | Simple touch buttons | Modern touch displays | --- **(b) Architecture of an Interactive Tabletop (7 marks):** **1. Block Diagram (3 marks):** ```mermaid graph TD a1[Display Layer
LCD or Projection] a2[Sensing Layer
Capacitive Grid or FTIR Camera] a3[Touch Controller
Embedded MCU] a4[Host Computer
Windows or Linux] a5[Application Software
Collaboration Apps] a6[Network Interface
TUIO, WebSocket] a2 --> a3 a3 --> a4 a4 --> a5 a5 --> a1 a1 -.-> a2 a4 --> a6 ``` **2. Components Explained (2 marks):** - **Display Layer**: LCD/LED panel or rear-projection from below. - **Sensing Layer**: Capacitive grid, FTIR, DI, or vision-based. - **Touch Controller**: Dedicated MCU that scans the sensor grid at 100–250 Hz. - **Host Computer**: Runs the OS and applications. - **Network Interface**: Communicates with external clients via protocols like TUIO. **3. Multi-User Collaboration Support (2 marks):** - **Concurrent multi-touch** allows multiple users to interact simultaneously. - **Personal space allocation**: Each user is given a dedicated interaction zone around them. - **Object-anchored interaction**: Tangible physical objects (with fiducial markers) carry user identity. - **Synchronized views**: Multiple users can see and manipulate the same digital artifacts. - **Privacy filters**: Some surfaces use directional backlighting to show different content to users on different sides. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Alternative Choice)** **(a)** What is **TUIO**? Explain the TUIO protocol with a typical message structure. How does it enable cross-platform interactive surface applications? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **design considerations and challenges** in developing interactive digital surfaces for **multi-user environments**. Include ergonomic, technical, and social challenges. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B **(a) TUIO Protocol (7 marks):** **1. Definition (1 mark):** **TUIO (Tangible User Interface Objects)** is an open, UDP-based protocol designed for transmitting the state of tangible and multi-touch interactions from interactive surfaces to client applications. It is widely used in research and commercial systems (e.g., Reactable, SMART Tables). **2. Message Structure (3 marks):** A TUIO message is a bundle of OSC (Open Sound Control) packets. Two primary message types: **`/tuio/2Dobj`** — for tangible object tracking: ``` /tuio/2Dobj set s x y a X Y A m r ``` Where: - `s` = session ID - `x, y` = normalized position (0 to 1) - `a` = angle (normalized) - `X, Y, A` = velocity components - `m` = motion acceleration - `r` = rotation velocity **`/tuio/2Dcur`** — for finger/cursor tracking: ``` /tuio/2Dcur set s x y X Y m ``` Where: - `s` = session ID - `x, y` = normalized position - `X, Y` = velocity - `m` = motion acceleration **3. Lifecycle Messages (1 mark):** - `alive` — list of active session IDs - `set` — update state - `fseq` — frame sequence number for synchronization **4. Cross-Platform Enablement (2 marks):** - **Language-agnostic**: Any language with UDP socket support can implement TUIO. - **Decoupled architecture**: The sensor/tracking layer is separate from the application. - **Standardized format**: Allows the same surface hardware to drive multiple applications. - **Ecosystem**: Libraries exist for C++, Java, Python, Processing, Unity, and web browsers (via WebSocket bridges). --- **(b) Design Considerations and Challenges (7 marks):** **1. Ergonomic Challenges (2 marks):** - **Reach and posture**: Users must lean over a horizontal tabletop, causing fatigue; vertical walls may be unreachable for short users. - **Touch target size**: Must comply with ISO 9241-9 — minimum **9 mm × 9 mm** per target. - **Hand occlusion**: Hands may block content from the user's view. - **Multi-user reach zones**: Must accommodate different body sizes and seating arrangements. **2. Technical Challenges (3 marks):** - **Latency**: < 10 ms required for natural interaction. - **Robust tracking**: Disambiguating closely spaced touches, palm rejection. - **Calibration**: Sensor-to-display alignment must be precise to within a few pixels. - **Lighting interference**: IR and vision-based systems suffer from sunlight and ambient IR. - **Power and thermal management**: Large displays generate heat and consume significant power. - **Synchronization**: Display refresh and touch sensing must be tightly coupled. **3. Social and Collaborative Challenges (2 marks):** - **Territoriality**: Users naturally claim "their" side of the table — systems must support this or share gracefully. - **Awareness**: Users need to see *where others are pointing* without verbal cues. - **Conflict resolution**: When two users grab the same object, the system needs a defined arbitration policy. - **Privacy**: Sensitive content should not be visible to unintended observers (addressed via directional displays or authentication). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do not** confuse **self-capacitance** with **mutual-capacitance** when answering multi-touch questions. Self-capacitance suffers from ghost touches; mutual-capacitance does not. Examiners specifically test this distinction. > - **Do not** write "capacitive touchscreens use pressure" — this is the **resistive** method. Capacitive works on **electrostatic field change**, not pressure. > - **Do not** omit the **TUIO protocol** when discussing cross-platform interactive surface communication — it is a **high-weightage** topic in KTU 2024. > - Always draw a **neat, labeled block diagram** — partial marks are heavily awarded for diagrams even if explanations are incomplete. > - For numerical/derivation questions, **show every algebraic step** — partial credit is given per step. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Interactive Digital Surfaces** - **Definition**: A surface integrating sensing, computation, and display for direct, multi-modal user input. - **Five Major Sensing Technologies**: Capacitive, Resistive, Infrared (IR), FTIR, Diffused Illumination, Vision-Based. - **Capacitance formula**: $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ - **Critical angle for TIR**: $\theta_c = \arcsin(n_2 / n_1)$ — for FTIR acrylic-air interface: $\theta_c \approx 42.2°$. - **Self-capacitance** → single touch, prone to ghosting. - **Mutual-capacitance** → true multi-touch, no ghosting — used in modern devices. - **Resistive touch coordinate**: $x_t = W \cdot V_x / V_{in}$, $y_t = H \cdot V_y / V_{in}$. - **Latency requirement**: $< 10 \text{ ms}$ (acceptable); $< 5 \text{ ms}$ (premium). - **Frame rate threshold**: $60 \text{ Hz}$ (16.67 ms/frame) for perceptual smoothness. - **Minimum touch target size**: $9 \text{ mm} \times 9 \text{ mm}$ (ISO 9241-9). - **TUIO**: UDP/OSC-based protocol for transmitting touch and tangible object data; supports `/tuio/2Dobj` and `/tuio/2Dcur` messages. - **Multi-user support mechanisms**: Concurrent multi-touch, personal zones, object-anchored identity, privacy filters, conflict resolution. - **Design challenges**: Ergonomics (reach, posture, occlusion), technical (latency, calibration, lighting), social (territoriality, awareness, privacy). - **Real-world examples**: Microsoft Surface Hub, Google Jamboard, SMART Board, Reactable, Apple iPad, Displax. - **Key paradigm shift**: From WIMP (indirect) to direct manipulation on shared, co-located display + input surfaces. - **Comparison keyword to remember**: "Capacitive = electric field, Resistive = pressure, IR = light beam interruption, FTIR = frustrated TIR, Vision = camera + CV."

topics.title: Manipulation of Digital Objects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT831 - HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Manipulation of Digital Objects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Manipulation of Digital Objects

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (HCI Standard — Shneiderman, Plaisant):**
> *Manipulation* in Human–Computer Interaction refers to the set of user tasks and input techniques used to **alter one or more properties of a digital object** (position, orientation, shape, size, colour, connectivity, or other attributes) through continuous, real-time, direct feedback control from an input device.

In the KTU 2024 HMI syllabus (Module 4), manipulation is one of the **five canonical user tasks** (often called the "Mantra of HCI"):

$$\text{Overview} \rightarrow \text{Zoom} \rightarrow \text{Fil­ter} \rightarrow \text{Details-on-Demand} \rightarrow \textbf{Manipulation}$$

where *manipulation* closes the loop by letting the user **directly edit the underlying data representation** rather than merely inspecting it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Scope of Module 4:**
> 1. **Selection** — identifying an object to act on.
> 2. **Positioning & Orienting** — translating and rotating an object in 2D / 3D space.
> 3. **Path Finding** — steering an object through a constrained trajectory.
> 4. **Constraints** — *snap-to-grid*, *snap-to-line*, *gravity fields*, *smart guides*, *rubber-banding*.
> 5. **Direct Manipulation (DM)** vs. **Indirect Manipulation**.
> 6. **Widgets, handles & control–display ratio (CD ratio).**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of dragging an icon across your phone screen with your thumb.

- The *finger* is the **input device**.
- The *icon* is the **digital object**.
- The *screen* is the **output (display) device**.
- The relationship *“how many pixels the icon moves for every millimetre my finger moves”* is the **CD ratio**.

If the icon were to **fly off-screen the moment** you flicked it, the CD ratio would be wrong (too large). If you had to **scrub your thumb ten times across the glass** to move the icon by a centimetre, the CD ratio would also be wrong (too small). The art and science of manipulation is to choose the right mapping.

A second analogy: a CAD engineer rotating a 3D gear with a mouse — the rotation is *continuous*, *reversible*, *visually represented*, and the user *feels in control* (the four "feel-good" affordances of direct manipulation — Hutchins, Hollan & Norman, 1985).

> [!TIP]
> The KTU paper *loves* asking: *"List the four properties of Direct Manipulation."* Memorise them as **CRSP**:
> **C**ontinuous representation • **R**eversible actions • **S**teps replaced by pointing/poke-at-facsimile • **P**elvic… no — **P**hysical actions instead of typed commands (i.e., rapid, incremental, reversible operations on visible objects).

### 1.3 Canonical Metrics and Standard Constants

| Metric / Constant | Symbol | Standard Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Control–Display ratio (mouse) | $C/D$ | $1{:}1$ ideal, $0.5$–$2.0$ typical | Ratio of pointer movement to cursor movement |
| Fitts' Law index of difficulty | $ID$ | $ID = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{A}{W} + 1\right)$ bits | Lower $ID$ ⇒ faster acquisition |
| Steering law slope | $k$ | $60$–$220$ ms/bit | Path-following throughput |
| Update rate (interactive) | $f_{upd}$ | $\geq 30$ Hz, ideally $60$–$120$ Hz | Below $15$ Hz = *unmanipulable* |
| Pixel pitch (display) | $p$ | $0.1$–$0.3$ mm | Affects effective $C/D$ resolution |
| Stylus sample rate (tablet) | $f_s$ | $125$–$240$ Hz | Determines path smoothness |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Manipulation Pipeline (Operational Decomposition)

A digital manipulation action can be decomposed into a strict, sequential pipeline. Understanding each phase is essential for both KTU theory and KTU lab viva questions.

1. **Acquisition (Selection):** User designates the target object.
   - *Techniques:* hover-click, lasso, area-select, ray-casting (3D), voice ("pick that gear").
   - *Affordance cues:* colour change, bounding-box highlight, drop-shadow.

2. **Engagement (Mode declaration):** The system switches from "pointer" mode to "manipulator" mode.
   - *Implicit:* hover > $200$ ms → drag handle appears.
   - *Explicit:* SHIFT+drag to constrain, ALT+drag to duplicate, CTRL+drag to copy.

3. **Transformation (Position / Orient / Scale):** The object's geometric state vector $\mathbf{S}$ is updated every input frame.
   - For 2D translation: $\mathbf{S} = (x, y)$.
   - For 3D rigid-body: $\mathbf{S} = (x, y, z, \theta_x, \theta_y, \theta_z)$.

4. **Constraint Application:** Soft or hard rules filter the transformation.
   - *Soft* (gravity, snap-distance $\delta_s$): hints only.
   - *Hard* (locked axes, integer grid): strict.

5. **Feedback Rendering:** Updated object + handles redrawn at $f_{upd}$.
   - *Continuous visual feedback is the sine qua non of manipulation* (Shneiderman, 1983).

6. **Commit / Cancel:** User releases (mouse-up) to commit, or presses ESC to revert.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why & How"**
> - **Why steps 1–6?** Because the KTU 2024 OBE question bank frequently tests whether the student can list and *justify* the phases. A "yes" list without justification loses 1–2 marks.
> - **How does the system know where to put the object?** Through the **CD-ratio mapping** combined with **constraint resolution**.

## 2.2 Control–Display Ratio (CD Ratio) — The Heart of Manipulation

The CD ratio is the single most-tested formula in Module 4. The KTU paper has asked variations of this in every December session since 2021.

### 2.2.1 Formal Definition

$$C/D \;=\; \frac{\text{Distance moved by the pointing device (control)}}{\text{Distance moved by the object on the screen (display)}}$$

Equivalent form using a multiplicative gain $G$:

$$G \;\equiv\; \frac{1}{C/D} \;=\; \frac{\Delta x_{\text{display}}}{\Delta x_{\text{control}}}$$

For 2D vector motion:

$$\begin{pmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{pmatrix} \;=\; G \begin{pmatrix} \Delta x_c \\ \Delta y_c \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} x_0 \\ y_0 \end{pmatrix}$$

where $(x_0, y_0)$ is the object's anchor at mouse-down and $(\Delta x_c, \Delta y_c)$ is the control's incremental displacement.

### 2.2.2 Special CD-Ratio Regimes

| Regime | CD Ratio | Gain $G$ | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct (1:1)** | $1$ | $1$ | Pointer sits *under* the user's finger; ideal for tablets. |
| **Ballistic (low C/D)** | $< 1$ | $> 1$ | Cursor moves *faster* than hand — good for long reaches (multi-monitor). |
| **Precision (high C/D)** | $> 1$ | $< 1$ | Cursor moves *slower* than hand — used in CAD, Photoshop, surgery. |
| **Pointer acceleration** | varies | $G = f(v_c)$ | Gain grows with control velocity (Mac, Windows default). |
| **Lock-off** | $\infty$ | $0$ | Object frozen; manipulation disabled. |

### 2.2.3 Pointer Acceleration Profile (Industry Standard)

Most modern operating systems use a piecewise-linear or power-law acceleration:

$$G(v) \;=\; G_{\min} \;+\; k\,(v - v_{\text{thresh}})^\alpha \quad \text{for } v > v_{\text{thresh}}$$

with typical $\alpha \in [0.5, 1.0]$ and $G_{\min} = 1$ (1:1 at low speed).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Pointer acceleration is a **CD-ratio transformation** that lets the same mouse feel "fast" across a wide screen *and* "precise" near a small target — a textbook example of **adaptive gain control** in HCI.

## 2.3 Fitts' Law — Predicting Manipulation Acquisition Time

Selection precedes manipulation. KTU expects you to be able to *combine* Fitts' Law with manipulation analysis.

$$MT \;=\; a \;+\; b \cdot ID \quad\text{where}\quad ID \;=\; \log_2\!\left(\frac{A}{W} + 1\right)$$

- $MT$ = movement time (s)
- $A$ = distance to target centre
- $W$ = target width along the axis of motion
- $a \approx 50$–$350$ ms, $b \approx 100$–$200$ ms/bit (device-dependent)
- $ID$ in **bits**, throughput $TP = ID/MT$ in **bits/s**

> [!TIP]
> The WIMP (Windows-Icons-Menus-Pointer) paradigm gives target widths down to a single pixel ($W=1$ px) on a 4K display, which is why HDPI/Retina displays feel "easier" to manipulate.

## 2.4 The Steering (Accot–Zhai) Law for Path Finding

When the user must drag an object **through a constrained path** (e.g., a wire through a circuit layout, a pipeline through a maze), Fitts' Law does not apply. KTU's 2023 question bank introduced this.

$$T \;=\; a_s \;+\; b_s \int_0^L \frac{ds}{W(s)}$$

$$ID_{\text{steer}} \;=\; \int_0^L \frac{ds}{W(s)} \quad [\text{steering-bits}]$$

For a straight tunnel of constant width $W$ and length $L$:

$$ID_{\text{steer}} \;=\; \frac{L}{W} \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

This is the **"Fitts' Law for paths"**, and it underpins the 3D manipulation literature too.

## 2.5 Manipulation Constraints

Constraints are *assistance* — they lower $ID$ and $ID_{\text{steer}}$. Common categories:

| Constraint Type | Effect | Mathematical Form |
|---|---|---|
| **Snap-to-grid** | Object snaps to nearest grid point within radius $\delta_s$ | $x' = \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{g}\right)\cdot g$ |
| **Snap-to-line** | Edge aligns to angle $\theta_n = n\cdot 15^\circ$ | $\theta' = \arg\min_n \vert \theta - n\cdot 15^\circ \vert$ |
| **Gravity field** | Continuous attraction to nearby salient point | $F_g = k_g\,\Delta r$ within radius $r_g$ |
| **Rubber-banding** | Live preview line from anchor to current pointer | $\ell(t) = \vert \mathbf{p}(t) - \mathbf{a} \vert$ |
| **Axis-lock** | Translation restricted to a single DOF | $x' = x_0,\ y' = y_0 + \Delta y$ (or 3D variant) |
| **Planar constraint** | Motion in plane of viewing | $z' = z_0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Constraints are foundational in **CAD/CAM (AutoCAD, SolidWorks)**, **PCB layout (Altium, KiCad)**, **UI design (Figma, Sketch)**, **GIS (ArcGIS)**, and **surgical robotics (Da Vinci, ROSA)** where every motion must obey a safety corridor.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise this **box** — it covers ~70% of the numerical questions asked in KTU Module 4.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | $C/D = \dfrac{\Delta x_c}{\Delta x_d}$ | Control–Display ratio | dimensionless |
| F2 | $G = 1/(C/D) = \Delta x_d / \Delta x_c$ | Display gain | dimensionless |
| F3 | $G(v) = G_{\min} + k(v - v_{\text{thresh}})^\alpha$ | Pointer acceleration | — |
| F4 | $MT = a + b \log_2(A/W + 1)$ | Fitts' movement time | s |
| F5 | $ID = \log_2(A/W + 1)$ | Fitts' index of difficulty | bits |
| F6 | $TP = ID / MT$ | Throughput | bits/s |
| F7 | $T = a_s + b_s \int_0^L ds / W(s)$ | Steering time | s |
| F8 | $ID_s = L/W$ (rect. tunnel) | Steering index | bits |
| F9 | $x' = \text{round}(x/g)\cdot g$ for $\vert \delta \vert \le \delta_s$ | Snap-to-grid | px |
| F10 | $\mathbf{p}_{\text{obj}} = \mathbf{p}_{\text{anchor}} + G \cdot (\mathbf{p}_{\text{cursor}} - \mathbf{p}_{\text{down}})$ | Position update with CD | px |
| F11 | $\mathbf{R}(\theta) = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix}$ | 2D rotation matrix | — |
| F12 | $f_{\min} \approx 10$–$15$ Hz | Minimum interactive refresh | Hz |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-symbol trap:** in the F1 / F2 rows, the `/` is a *math* fraction, **never** a markdown cell separator. Always use the `\dfrac` / `\frac` LaTeX form to keep the table renderer happy.

## 2.7 Why This Matters in Industry (Engineering Utility)

- **Gaming:** Inverse kinematics + 3D widget handles; the same CD-ratio math governs aim-assist.
- **Aerospace HUDs:** Helmet-mounted displays have *non-1:1* CD ratios calibrated for pilot head-rotation biomechanics.
- **Automotive HMI:** Touchscreens in cars dynamically switch CD ratio based on vehicle speed and lane-keeping confidence.
- **Medical robotics:** Master–slave manipulators (Da Vinci) have *non-uniform* CD ratios that scale with slave–master distance for safety.
- **AR/VR (Apple Vision Pro, Meta Quest 3):** *Hand-tracking-to-virtual-object* mapping is a CD-ratio problem with 6 DoF (3D position + 3D orientation).
