# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pointer Position Update with a General CD Ratio

We will derive, **step-by-step**, the closed-form expression that updates a digital object's screen-coordinate every input event.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

> *Given a mouse-down anchor at $(x_0, y_0)$ and the current cursor at $(x_c, y_c)$ on a 1:1 pixel coordinate system, find the new object position $(x', y')$ for a configurable display gain $G$.*

### 3.1.2 Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the control displacement vector.**
The control's incremental motion is the difference between the current cursor position and the cursor position at mouse-down (the *anchor cursor position*).

$$\Delta \mathbf{p}_c \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} \Delta x_c \\ \Delta y_c \end{pmatrix} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} x_c - x_{c,\text{down}} \\ y_c - y_{c,\text{down}} \end{pmatrix}$$

*Conversion logic:* we use the *delta* (not absolute cursor position) because manipulation is a *relative* action; this is what makes it robust to pointer jumps at screen edges.

**Step 2 — Apply the display gain.**

The screen displacement of the object is the control displacement scaled by the gain $G \equiv 1/(C/D)$.

$$\Delta \mathbf{p}_d \;=\; G \cdot \Delta \mathbf{p}_c \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} G(x_c - x_{c,\text{down}}) \\ G(y_c - y_{c,\text{down}}) \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Add the anchor.**

The absolute new position of the object is the anchor position at mouse-down plus the scaled delta.

$$\begin{aligned} x' &\;=\; x_0 \;+\; G(x_c - x_{c,\text{down}}) \\ y' &\;=\; y_0 \;+\; G(y_c - y_{c,\text{down}}) \end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the special case $G = 1$.**

$$\begin{aligned} x' &\;=\; x_0 + (x_c - x_{c,\text{down}}) \\ &\;=\; x_0 + x_c - x_{c,\text{down}} \end{aligned}$$

If we choose the anchor so that $x_0 = x_{c,\text{down}}$ (object starts *under* the cursor), then $x' = x_c$ — a 1:1 direct manipulation. ✓

**Step 5 — General vector form.**

$$\boxed{\;\mathbf{p}' \;=\; \mathbf{p}_0 \;+\; G\bigl(\mathbf{p}_c - \mathbf{p}_{c,\text{down}}\bigr)\;}$$

**Step 6 — Add pointer acceleration.**

If the OS uses an adaptive gain, replace $G$ with $G(v)$ from §2.2.3.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Snap-to-Grid Coordinate Update

We will derive the snap rule for a square grid of spacing $g$.

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

> *A user drags a rectangle to raw position $x = 137.4$ px on a grid with $g = 10$ px. What is the snapped position? Show the derivation.*

### 3.2.2 Derivation

**Step 1 — Normalise to grid units.**

$$x_n \;=\; \frac{x}{g} \;=\; \frac{137.4}{10} \;=\; 13.74$$

**Step 2 — Round to nearest integer.**

$$k^\star \;=\; \mathrm{round}(x_n) \;=\; \mathrm{round}(13.74) \;=\; 14$$

**Step 3 — Map back to pixel space.**

$$x' \;=\; k^\star \cdot g \;=\; 14 \cdot 10 \;=\; 140 \text{ px}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the snap-distance gating (KTU expects this).**

The snap should *only* fire when the raw position is within $\delta_s$ of a grid point.

$$\begin{aligned} \delta &\;=\; x - \mathrm{round}(x_n)\cdot g \;=\; 137.4 - 140 \;=\; -2.6 \text{ px} \\ \text{Snap?} &\;=\; \begin{cases} \text{Yes} & \text{if } \vert \delta \vert \le \delta_s \\ \text{No} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \end{aligned}$$

With $\delta_s = 4$ px (default in Figma), $\vert -2.6 \vert = 2.6 \le 4$ → **snap fires** → $x' = 140$ px. ✓

**General closed form:**

$$\boxed{\;x' \;=\; \begin{cases} g \cdot \mathrm{round}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{g}\right) & \text{if } \left\vert x - g\cdot\mathrm{round}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{g}\right) \right\vert \le \delta_s \\[4pt] x & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}\;}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — 2D Rotation of an Object About an Anchor

For *orienting* an object, the KTU paper often asks for the rotation matrix and the resulting coordinates.

**Step 1 — Define the local coordinates relative to the anchor.**

$$x'_{\text{local}} \;=\; x - x_0, \qquad y'_{\text{local}} \;=\; y - y_0$$

**Step 2 — Translate to origin, apply rotation, translate back.**

$$\begin{aligned} x_{\text{rot}} &\;=\; x_0 \;+\; x'_{\text{local}}\cos\theta \;-\; y'_{\text{local}}\sin\theta \\ y_{\text{rot}} &\;=\; y_0 \;+\; x'_{\text{local}}\sin\theta \;+\; y'_{\text{local}}\cos\theta \end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent matrix form.**

$$\begin{pmatrix} x_{\text{rot}} \\ y_{\text{rot}} \end{pmatrix} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} x_0 \\ y_0 \end{pmatrix} \;+\; \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x - x_0 \\ y - y_0 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example (KTU-favourite).**

Rotate the point $(3, 0)$ about the origin by $\theta = 90^\circ = \pi/2$ rad.

$$\begin{aligned} \cos(90^\circ) &\;=\; 0, \quad \sin(90^\circ) \;=\; 1 \\ x_{\text{rot}} &\;=\; 0\cdot 3 - 1\cdot 0 \;=\; 0 \\ y_{\text{rot}} &\;=\; 1\cdot 3 + 0\cdot 0 \;=\; 3 \\ \text{Result:} &\; (0, 3) \;\checkmark \end{aligned}$$

This is the classic rotation-by-90° counter-clockwise — examiners love it because the student must know both the matrix *and* the sign convention.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Technique vs. Property

This is a high-yield **2-D table** that KTU paper-setters often paraphrase as: *"Compare the following manipulation techniques along the given dimensions."*

| Technique | DoF | Continuous? | Reversible? | Domain | CD-Ratio | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Direct touch | 2D / 3D | ✓ | ✓ (re-tap) | Mobile | $1{:}1$ | Smartphones, tablets |
| Mouse drag | 2D | ✓ | ✓ (re-drag) | Desktop | configurable | CAD, GUI |
| Stylus + tablet | 2D / pressure | ✓ | ✓ | Wacom | configurable | Drawing, handwriting |
| Trackball | 2D (rate) | ✓ | ✓ | Kiosk | variable | Accessibility |
| Eye-gaze dwell | 2D | discrete | ✓ | Assistive | $0{:}1$ (lock-off) | Severe motor impairment |
| 3D widget (arcball) | 3 rotation | ✓ | ✓ | 3D CAD | $1{:}1$ | 3D model posing |
| Voice | 6DoF | ✗ | ✗ | Anywhere | n/a | Hands-busy tasks |
| Joystick | 2D (rate) | ✓ | ✓ | Games, wheelchairs | variable | Vehicle-like control |
| 6-DOF controller (VR) | 6 | ✓ | ✓ | VR/AR | configurable | Immersive 3D |

**Analytical takeaway:** there is *no* universally "best" technique. The KTU 2024 OBE expects you to **justify** the technique choice with reference to *task*, *user population*, and *environment* — i.e., the **Nielsen-Shneiderman usability heuristics**.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (ESE 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem (KTU-pattern):** A CAD engineer is rotating a 3D bracket. The widget reports $\theta = 47.3^\circ$. The system applies a 15°-step angle-snap. The mouse-down anchor in world space is $(x_0, y_0) = (100, 200)$ px. A vertex of the bracket is at local offset $(\Delta x, \Delta y) = (30, -20)$ px. Compute the new world-space position of this vertex after snap.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Snap the angle.**

$$\theta' \;=\; \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{47.3}{15}\right) \cdot 15 \;=\; \mathrm{round}(3.15) \cdot 15 \;=\; 3 \cdot 15 \;=\; 45^\circ$$

**Step 2 — Convert to radians.**

$$\theta'_{\text{rad}} \;=\; 45 \cdot \frac{\pi}{180} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\sin$ and $\cos$.**

$$\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{4}\right) \;=\; \sin\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{4}\right) \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \;\approx\; 0.7071$$

**Step 4 — Apply the rotation–translation.**

$$\begin{aligned} x_{\text{new}} &\;=\; 100 \;+\; 30\cdot(0.7071) \;-\; (-20)\cdot(0.7071) \\ &\;=\; 100 \;+\; 21.21 \;+\; 14.14 \\ &\;=\; 135.36 \text{ px} \\[4pt] y_{\text{new}} &\;=\; 200 \;+\; 30\cdot(0.7071) \;+\; (-20)\cdot(0.7071) \\ &\;=\; 200 \;+\; 21.21 \;-\; 14.14 \\ &\;=\; 207.07 \text{ px} \end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check with the distance invariant.**

Rotation preserves length:

$$\begin{aligned} \vert \mathbf{v}_\text{old} \vert &\;=\; \sqrt{30^2 + (-20)^2} \;=\; \sqrt{1300} \;\approx\; 36.06 \text{ px} \\ \vert \mathbf{v}_\text{new} \vert &\;=\; \sqrt{(35.36)^2 + (7.07)^2} \;=\; \sqrt{1300} \;\approx\; 36.06 \text{ px} \;\checkmark \end{aligned}$$

**Final answer:** the vertex moves from world $(130, 180)$ to world $(135.36, 207.07)$ px after the 45°-snapped rotation.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Manipulation Pipeline — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Device - Mouse/Touch/Stylus] --> B[Event Sampler - 60 to 240 Hz]
    B --> C{Selection Test - Hit Testing}
    C -- Miss --> D[No-op - Cursor moves only]
    C -- Hit --> E[Object Acquired - Highlight]
    E --> F[Mode Switch - Pointer to Manipulator]
    F --> G[CD Ratio Module - Gain Computation]
    G --> H[Constraint Solver - Snap/Lock/Gravity]
    H --> I[Transform Engine - Translate or Rotate or Scale]
    I --> J[Feedback Renderer - 60 Hz redraw]
    J --> K{User Action}
    K -- Release --> L[Commit - Write Model]
    K -- Press ESC --> M[Cancel - Restore State]
    L --> N[End]
    M --> N
    D --> N
```

*Reading guide:* the **CD-Ratio Module** (block G) and the **Constraint Solver** (block H) are the two KTU-favourite blocks — every numerical question ultimately passes through them.

## 4.2 CD-Ratio Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start - Pointer Velocity v] --> Q{v greater than v-thresh}
    Q -- No --> R[G equals 1 - Direct 1:1 Mode]
    Q -- Yes --> S[Compute G = G-min + k times v - v-thresh power alpha]
    S --> T[Apply Gain to Delta Cursor]
    R --> T
    T --> U[Compute New Object Position]
    U --> V[Apply Constraint Filter]
    V --> W[Render to Display]
```

## 4.3 Manipulation Technique Selection Matrix (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Task2D[2D Task]
        T1A[Touch]
        T1B[Mouse]
        T1C[Stylus]
    end
    subgraph Task3D[3D Task]
        T2A[6-DOF Controller]
        T2B[Arcball Widget]
        T2C[Mid-air Hand Tracking]
    end
    subgraph TaskPath[Path-Finding Task]
        T3A[Steering with Tunnel]
        T3B[Waypoint Editor]
        T3C[Auto-router with Override]
    end
    Task2D --> CD1[CD Ratio 1:1 to 1:4]
    Task3D --> CD2[CD Ratio configurable with DOF coupling]
    TaskPath --> CD3[Steering Law governs timing]
```

## 4.4 Feedback Loop Schematic (Cybernetics View of Manipulation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[User Brain and Hand] -->|motor command| C[Control - Input Device]
    C -->|electrical signal| S[System - OS Kernel]
    S -->|display command| D[Display - Screen]
    D -->|visual + haptic| U
    S -->|state read| M[World Model - Object State Vector]
    M --> S
```

*Reading guide:* this is the classical **closed-loop cybernetic** model of manipulation (Sheridan, Verplank). The *loop delay* $\tau$ is what makes or breaks a manipulation feel — at $\tau > 100$ ms, even a perfect CD ratio feels sluggish.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1. Define *Control–Display (CD) ratio*. Explain with a suitable example how a CD ratio different from 1:1 can be useful in a graphics application.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Control–Display ratio is defined as the ratio of the distance moved by the *control* (input device) to the corresponding distance moved by the *displayed* object (cursor).
>
> $$C/D \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta x_{\text{control}}}{\Delta x_{\text{display}}}$$
>
> When $C/D = 1$, a unit movement of the control causes a unit movement on the screen (direct 1:1 manipulation, e.g., finger on a touch screen).
>
> When $C/D > 1$ (display gain $G < 1$), the cursor moves *slower* than the hand — useful in **CAD and Photoshop** for pixel-precise selection of small targets.
>
> When $C/D < 1$ ($G > 1$), the cursor moves *faster* than the hand — useful for **long traverses** such as a 4K monitor or moving across a 3D world in a game. (3 marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q2. List the four properties of Direct Manipulation (DM). Why does DM feel "satisfying" to the user?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Following Hutchins, Hollan & Norman (1985), the four properties of DM are:
> 1. **Continuous representation** of the object of interest.
> 2. **Physical actions** (poking, dragging) instead of typed commands.
> 3. **Rapid, incremental, reversible** operations whose impact is immediately visible.
> 4. **Layered approach** to learning — beginners try one step at a time.
>
> DM is satisfying because it provides *immediate, reversible, low-risk* feedback that matches the user's mental model of the system; errors are cheap to correct, building user confidence and engagement. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 4, ESE Pattern]**
>
> ### Question A (14 Marks)
>
> **(a)** Define *Control–Display ratio*. With a clear diagram, derive the position-update equation for a 2D drag operation in terms of the control's incremental displacement and the display gain. Explain how **pointer acceleration** modifies this equation in modern operating systems. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A graphics tablet has a sensor surface of $310 \times 216$ mm and a screen of $1920 \times 1080$ pixels. The user enables *precision mode*, which sets $C/D = 2$ (cursor moves at half the stylus speed). The stylus is currently at sensor position $(150, 90)$ mm; the mouse-down anchor sensor position was $(20, 30)$ mm. Compute the cursor's current pixel position on the screen, assuming the anchor cursor is $(400, 300)$ px. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) [7 marks]**
1. **Definition (2 marks):** CD ratio is $C/D = \Delta x_c / \Delta x_d$ with display gain $G = 1/(C/D) = \Delta x_d / \Delta x_c$. (See §2.2.1.)
2. **Diagram (1 mark):** Block diagram as in §4.1, blocks G and I.
3. **Derivation (3 marks):** As in §3.1, we obtain
   $$\mathbf{p}' \;=\; \mathbf{p}_0 \;+\; G\bigl(\mathbf{p}_c - \mathbf{p}_{c,\text{down}}\bigr).$$
4. **Pointer acceleration (1 mark):** $G$ is replaced by $G(v) = G_{\min} + k(v - v_{\text{thresh}})^\alpha$ so that the *same* mouse feels fast across large screens and precise near small targets. macOS, Windows, X11 all implement this.

**(b) [7 marks]**

*Step 1 — Convert sensor millimetres to screen pixels.*

Sensor pitch:

$$p_x \;=\; \frac{1920 \text{ px}}{310 \text{ mm}} \;\approx\; 6.194 \text{ px/mm}, \quad p_y \;=\; \frac{1080 \text{ px}}{216 \text{ mm}} \;=\; 5.000 \text{ px/mm}$$

*Step 2 — Compute the control displacement in pixels.*

$$\Delta x_c = (150 - 20) \cdot p_x = 130 \cdot 6.194 = 805.22 \text{ px}$$

$$\Delta y_c = (90 - 30) \cdot p_y = 60 \cdot 5.000 = 300.00 \text{ px}$$

*Step 3 — Apply the CD ratio.*

$C/D = 2 \Rightarrow G = 1/2 = 0.5$.

$$\Delta x_d = G \cdot \Delta x_c = 0.5 \cdot 805.22 = 402.61 \text{ px}$$

$$\Delta y_d = G \cdot \Delta y_c = 0.5 \cdot 300.00 = 150.00 \text{ px}$$

*Step 4 — Add the anchor.**

$$\begin{aligned} x' &= 400 + 402.61 = 802.61 \text{ px} \\ y' &= 300 + 150.00 = 450.00 \text{ px} \end{aligned}$$

**Final answer:** the cursor is at **(802.6, 450.0) px** on the screen. ✓ (7 marks)

> ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice to Question A*
>
> **(a)** Explain the *Steering Law* of Accot & Zhai. Derive the steering index of difficulty $ID_s$ for a **straight rectangular tunnel** of length $L$ and constant width $W$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A user is dragging a wire through a printed-circuit-board routing tool. The corridor is a piecewise-straight tunnel: a 60-mm horizontal stretch, then a 90°-arc of radius 8 mm sweeping into a 40-mm vertical stretch. Tunnel width is $W = 4$ mm throughout. Using the Steering Law, compute the *steering index of difficulty* in bits. Take $\pi \approx 3.1416$. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) [7 marks]**
1. **Statement (2 marks):** The Steering Law is the Fitts'-Law analogue for path-following tasks. It states
   $$T \;=\; a_s + b_s \int_0^L \frac{ds}{W(s)}$$
   where $W(s)$ is the *local tunnel width* at arc-length $s$ along the path.
2. **Derivation for rectangular tunnel (3 marks):** Since $W(s) = W = $ const and $L$ is constant,
   $$ID_s \;=\; \int_0^L \frac{ds}{W} \;=\; \frac{1}{W}\int_0^L ds \;=\; \frac{L}{W} \;\;[\text{steering-bits}].$$
3. **Engineering utility (2 marks):** Steering indices govern drag-through-tunnel tasks in PCB routers, video editing timelines, security-tool menu sliders, 3D model constraint corridors, and VR locomotion paths.

**(b) [7 marks]**

*Step 1 — Horizontal stretch (constant $W = 4$ mm).*

$$ID_{s,1} = \frac{L_1}{W} = \frac{60}{4} = 15 \text{ bits}$$

*Step 2 — Curved arc.*

Arc length: $L_2 = r \cdot \theta = 8 \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{2} = 4\pi \approx 12.566$ mm.

$$ID_{s,2} = \frac{L_2}{W} = \frac{4\pi}{4} = \pi \approx 3.1416 \text{ bits}$$

*Step 3 — Vertical stretch.*

$$ID_{s,3} = \frac{L_3}{W} = \frac{40}{4} = 10 \text{ bits}$$

*Step 4 — Total steering index (linear additivity of $ID_s$).**

$$ID_{s,\text{total}} = 15 + 3.1416 + 10 = 28.1416 \text{ bits}$$

**Final answer:** $\boxed{ID_{s,\text{total}} \approx 28.14 \text{ steering-bits}}$. (7 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where students LOSE marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the gain-to-CD inversion.** Writing $G = C/D$ instead of $G = 1/(C/D)$ costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing *selection* and *manipulation* Fitts' Law.** Selection uses $ID = \log_2(A/W + 1)$; steering uses $ID_s = L/W$ — do **not** interchange them.
> 3. **Sign convention in 2D rotation.** $y$-axis points *down* on most computer displays (screen coordinates), so the visual rotation is *clockwise* for positive $\theta$ in the standard math formula. Always state the convention explicitly.
> 4. **Snap-distance gating.** Snap rules only fire *inside* $\delta_s$. Writing $x' = g \cdot \mathrm{round}(x/g)$ without the $\delta_s$ check loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Units in the formula table.** Always state $A, W, L$ in **consistent units** (mm, px, or cm) — examiners check this strictly.
> 6. **Vague "Direct Manipulation" answers.** Listing only 1–2 properties loses 1 mark; you must give all **four** (CRSP).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4, Manipulation of Digital Objects**

- ☐ Manipulation = **altering properties** (position, orientation, scale, attributes) of a digital object with **real-time visual feedback**.
- ☐ Five canonical user tasks (Shneiderman): **Overview → Zoom → Filter → Details-on-Demand → Manipulation**.
- ☐ Four properties of Direct Manipulation: **Continuous, Reversible, Steps→Poke, Physical not lexical** (CRSP).
- ☐ **CD ratio** = control distance / display distance; **Gain** $G = 1/(C/D)$.
- ☐ $G > 1$ → *ballistic* (long throws, multi-monitor); $G < 1$ → *precision* (CAD, surgery).
- ☐ Pointer acceleration: $G(v) = G_{\min} + k(v - v_{\text{thresh}})^\alpha$, $\alpha \in [0.5, 1.0]$.
- ☐ **Fitts' Law:** $MT = a + b \log_2(A/W + 1)$, $TP = ID/MT$ in bits/s.
- ☐ **Steering Law:** $T = a_s + b_s \int_0^L ds/W(s)$; for a rectangle $ID_s = L/W$ bits.
- ☐ **Snap-to-grid** $x' = g\cdot \mathrm{round}(x/g)$ inside snap distance $\delta_s$.
- ☐ **Snap-to-angle** $\theta' = 15^\circ \cdot \mathrm{round}(\theta/15^\circ)$.
- ☐ 2D rotation matrix: $\mathbf{R}(\theta) = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix}$.
- ☐ Interactive refresh rate must be $\geq 30$ Hz (ideally $60$–$120$ Hz); below $10$–$15$ Hz is unmanipulable.
- ☐ Six-DoF manipulation: 3 translation + 3 rotation, dominant in VR/AR and 3D CAD.
- ☐ Constraints *reduce* $ID$ and $ID_s$, making manipulation easier and less error-prone.
- ☐ Closed-loop manipulation latency $\tau \le 100$ ms is the rule of thumb for "feel right".
- ☐ Technique choice must be justified against **task, user, environment** (Nielsen heuristics).

> **Closing examiner's note:** Module 4 is *the* most formula-rich module in HMI. The KTU 2024 paper typically allocates **2 questions** to manipulation in Part A and **1 long question (with internal choice)** in Part B. Master the CD ratio, the two Fitts' variants, and the snap formulas — those four items alone routinely decide whether you score 7 or 14 in the long answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Displays with Rear Projection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT831 - HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Displays with Rear Projection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rear Projection (RP) Display** is a large-format display technology in which the image-forming light beam is projected from a light engine located *behind* the viewing screen (relative to the observer) onto the rear surface of a specially engineered translucent screen, which then re-directs, diffuses, and re-emits the light toward the audience seated on the opposite side.

In the context of **Human–Machine Interface (HMI)**, rear-projection displays are classified as *shared / co-located group-view displays* and are widely deployed in **control rooms, ATC towers, traffic management centers, simulation rigs, and command-and-control consoles** where multiple operators must observe a single high-fidelity information surface simultaneously without parallax shadowing.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 — Must-Know Distinction**
> In **front projection**, the projector and observer are on the *same* side of the screen (the image is reflected). In **rear projection**, the projector is on the *opposite* side of the screen (the image is transmitted and re-diffused).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Aquarium Analogy"

Imagine standing in front of a large **fish tank (aquarium)** at a zoo:

- The **fish** swimming inside represent the **pixels of the image**.
- The **glass wall** of the aquarium represents the **rear-projection screen**.
- The **water and lighting inside the tank** represent the **light engine + optics** hidden behind the screen.
- You (the observer) see a clear, bright image through the glass without ever seeing the lamp, lens, or wiring that produced it.

Just like the aquarium keeps the messy machinery hidden behind a clean transparent panel while you enjoy a vivid view, a **rear-projection display hides its projector, lamp, and electronics behind an opaque-to-the-back but bright-to-the-front screen**, giving the audience a clean viewing surface free from shadows, glare, or "presenter obstruction."

---

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Industrial Values)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Screen Diagonal** | $d$ | 50″ – 200″+ | Defines viewing class |
| **Screen Gain** | $G$ | 1.0 – 4.0 | Brightness amplification |
| **Half-Gain Viewing Angle** | $\theta_{HG}$ | 30° – 70° | Defines audience spread |
| **Contrast Ratio** | $CR$ | 1500:1 – 100000:1 (Laser) | Image legibility |
| **Throw Ratio** | $TR$ | 0.2 – 1.5 | Cabinet depth design |
| **Native Resolution** | $R$ | XGA, 1080p, 4K UHD | Information density |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Brightness:** On-axis luminance for control-room rear-projection walls is engineered for **$L \approx 150$ to $400 \text{ cd/m}^2$** as per ISO 9241-307 ambient contrast guidelines.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Rear-Projection Display — Six Logical Stages

A rear-projection system is fundamentally a **light-modulation + optical relay** chain. The operational pipeline can be decomposed as follows:

1. **Light Generation (Source Stage)**
   - A high-intensity source — historically UHP/Metal-Halide lamp, modernly **solid-state Laser Phosphor**, RGB Laser, or high-power LED — emits broadband or spectrally pure light.
   - Governing design constraint: **luminous flux $\Phi_v$ (lumens)** and **spectral gamut** (sRGB / Rec.709 / DCI-P3 coverage).

2. **Light Conditioning (Illumination Optics)**
   - The raw source light is homogenized via a **fly-eye integrator** or **light-pipe tunnel** so that the imager receives a *uniform* rectangular patch of light — critical for eliminating hot-spots.

3. **Image Modulation (Imager Stage)**
   - The uniform light is spatially modulated pixel-by-pixel by an imaging device:
     - **3 × LCD (Transmissive LCD)** — polarisation-based light gating.
     - **DMD / DLP (Digital Micromirror Device)** — millions of micro-mirrors tilt $\pm 12°$ at kHz rate.
     - **LCoS (Liquid Crystal on Silicon)** — reflective, high-fill-factor.
     - **CRT** (legacy) — raster-scanned electron beam on phosphor.

4. **Projection Lens (Relay Stage)**
   - A multi-element lens (typically 11–17 elements including aspheric and low-dispersion glass) magnifies the imager output onto the rear surface of the screen. **Throw ratio** and **lens shift** are the key parameters.

5. **Screen Re-direction (Diffuser Stage)**
   - The modulated light strikes the *rear* of a **two-layer screen**:
     - **Fresnel Lens Layer** — converts diverging projection light into a collimated beam perpendicular to the screen.
     - **Lenticular Lens Layer** — controls horizontal diffusion so the image is visible across a wide audience arc but rejects ambient room light via black stripes.

6. **Audience Perception (Viewing Stage)**
   - The diffused image is observed under ambient control-room lighting. Performance is governed by **luminance ($L$)**, **contrast ratio ($CR$)**, and **half-gain angle ($\theta_{HG}$)**.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Rear Projection — Engineering Justification

| Problem with Front Projection in HMI | How Rear Projection Solves It |
|---|---|
| Operator casts shadow on screen | Projector is behind screen — no obstruction |
| Stray light washes out image | Fresnel + lenticular screen rejects off-axis ambient light |
| Bulky ceiling mounting required | All optics self-contained in floor console |
| Glare from room lights | Screen gain concentrates light toward audience |
| Maintenance hard in mission-critical rooms | Modular rear-access lamp/engine replacement |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize these equations. They are the **only** quantitative tools needed for numerical questions on rear-projection in the KTU ESE.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $TR = \dfrac{D_t}{W_s}$ | **Throw Ratio** = Throw Distance / Screen Width | dimensionless |
| 2 | $G = \dfrac{L_\theta}{L_{Lambert}}$ | **Screen Gain** = On-axis luminance / Lambertian reference luminance | dimensionless |
| 3 | $L = \dfrac{E \cdot G}{\pi}$ | Luminance from screen illuminance (Lambertian approximation) | $\text{cd/m}^2$ |
| 4 | $\theta_{HG}$ : $G(\theta_{HG}) = \dfrac{G_{0}}{2}$ | Definition of **Half-Gain Angle** | degrees |
| 5 | $CR = \dfrac{L_{white}}{L_{black}}$ | **Contrast Ratio** | dimensionless |
| 6 | $f_{lens} = \dfrac{D_t}{\left(M + 1\right)}$ | Thin-lens conjugate relation (with magnification $M = W_s / W_i$) | m |
| 7 | $\eta_{system} = \eta_{source} \cdot \eta_{imager} \cdot \eta_{lens} \cdot \eta_{screen}$ | **System optical efficiency** | dimensionless |
| 8 | $A_{screen} = W_s \cdot H_s$ | Screen area (4:3 vs 16:9 scaling) | $\text{m}^2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Safety Note:** In the markdown table above, absolute-value bars and conditional bars were intentionally written as **plain text words** ("ratio", "condition") to avoid breaking the markdown table pipe-syntax. In handwritten/printed KTU answer sheets, always use proper $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Air Traffic Control (ATC):** Large 4m × 1.5m rear-projection video walls (Barco, eyevis) form the **radar situational display**, where 24/7 readability under dim ambient is non-negotiable.
- **Industrial Control Rooms (SCADA):** Used as the **common operational picture (COP)** screens in power-grid dispatch centers (POSOCO/POWERGRID India deployments).
- **Automotive & Aerospace Simulation:** Full-scale cockpit mockups use **curved rear-projection cylinders** for 180°/360° field-of-view training (e.g., flight simulators).
- **Medical Imaging Walls:** Multi-modality fusion displays in radiology reading rooms.
- **Smart City Command Centers:** Vehicle tracking, surveillance, and emergency dispatch dashboards.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Throw Ratio and Cabinet Depth

**Problem Setup (KTU-standard):** A 16:9 rear-projection display has a screen diagonal of 84 inches. The projection lens has a fixed throw ratio of $TR = 0.25$. Find the (a) screen width, (b) throw distance, and (c) total minimum cabinet depth.

**Step 1 — Convert diagonal to width using the 16:9 ratio.**

The diagonal of a 16:9 rectangle is the hypotenuse of a right triangle whose legs are $W_s$ and $H_s = \frac{9}{16}W_s$.

$$
d = \sqrt{W_s^2 + H_s^2} = \sqrt{W_s^2 + \left(\tfrac{9}{16}W_s\right)^2}
$$

$$
d = W_s \sqrt{1 + \left(\tfrac{9}{16}\right)^2} = W_s \sqrt{1 + \tfrac{81}{256}} = W_s \sqrt{\tfrac{337}{256}}
$$

$$
W_s = \dfrac{d}{\sqrt{337/256}} = \dfrac{84 \text{ in}}{\sqrt{1.3164}} = \dfrac{84 \text{ in}}{1.1473}
$$

$$
\boxed{W_s \approx 73.2 \text{ inches} \;(\approx 1.86 \text{ m})}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the throw distance using the throw ratio definition.**

$$
TR = \dfrac{D_t}{W_s} \;\Longrightarrow\; D_t = TR \times W_s
$$

$$
D_t = 0.25 \times 73.2 \text{ in}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_t \approx 18.3 \text{ inches} \;(\approx 0.465 \text{ m})}
$$

**Step 3 — Add the clearance for the lens + imager + light-source envelope.**

Standard rear-projection engine envelope behind the rear focal point requires $\approx 30\%$ additional depth for the light path folding and electrical connectors.

$$
D_{cabinet} = D_t \times 1.30 = 0.465 \times 1.30
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{cabinet} \approx 0.605 \text{ m}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight:** An **ultra-short-throw (UST)** lens with $TR \le 0.2$ allows the entire RP display to be built into a slim floor-stand cabinet under 25 cm deep — a major usability win for office and control-room environments.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Luminance From Screen Illuminance and Gain

**Problem:** A rear-projection screen with peak gain $G_0 = 3.5$ receives an on-axis illuminance of $E = 600$ lux from the projector. Compute the on-axis screen luminance (assuming a near-Lambertian diffuser).

**Step 1 — State the governing relationship.**

For a diffuser with gain $G$, the relationship between on-screen illuminance $E$ and the emitted luminance $L$ along the gain axis is:

$$
L = \dfrac{E \cdot G}{\pi}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
L = \dfrac{600 \;\text{lux} \times 3.5}{\pi}
$$

$$
L = \dfrac{2100}{\pi}
$$

$$
\boxed{L \approx 668.5 \text{ cd/m}^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret against ISO 9241-307 ambient guidelines.**

Control-room ergonomic standards recommend $L \ge 100$ cd/m² for normal ambient and $L \ge 200$ cd/m² for high-ambient rooms. The computed value comfortably satisfies mission-critical viewing.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Off-Axis Gain & Half-Gain Angle (Analytical Model)

A standard empirically-fitted model for the angular roll-off of a lenticular screen gain is:

$$
G(\theta) = G_0 \cdot \cos^{n}(\theta)
$$

where $n$ is a shaping exponent determined by the lenticular pitch. The half-gain angle $\theta_{HG}$ is defined as the angle at which the gain falls to half the on-axis value:

$$
G(\theta_{HG}) = \dfrac{G_0}{2} = G_0 \cos^{n}(\theta_{HG})
$$

**Step-by-step reduction:**

$$
\cos^{n}(\theta_{HG}) = \dfrac{1}{2}
$$

$$
n \cdot \ln\!\left(\cos\theta_{HG}\right) = \ln\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right) = -\ln 2
$$

$$
\cos\theta_{HG} = 2^{-1/n}
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta_{HG} = \arccos\!\left(2^{-1/n}\right)}
$$

**Numerical Example:** For $n = 3$ (typical lenticular screen):

$$
\cos\theta_{HG} = 2^{-1/3} = 0.7937
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta_{HG} \approx 37.4^{\circ}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off Visualized:** A high-gain screen ($G_0 \uparrow$) has a *narrower* half-gain cone ($n \uparrow$, $\theta_{HG} \downarrow$). For wide control rooms with many side-seated operators, a **mid-gain ($G_0 \approx 1.5$) wide-angle screen** is preferred.

---

## 3.4 KTU Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Table

| Display Need | Recommended RP Configuration |
|---|---|
| Bright control room, narrow seating | High-gain Fresnel ($G_0 \approx 4$, $\theta_{HG} \approx 25°$) |
| Wide control room, many operators | Mid-gain Lenticular ($G_0 \approx 1.5$, $\theta_{HG} \approx 50°$) |
| 24/7 mission-critical, low maintenance | Laser-phosphor light engine (30000 h MTBF) |
| Tight space (shallow room) | Ultra-short-throw mirror-folded optics |
| 4K/UHD content | DLP (0.9″ DMD) or 3LCD with 4K e-Shift |
| High ambient light (daylight) | Black-stripe contrast-enhancement screen |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Rear-Projection Display Optical Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SOURCE]:::source --> B[ILLUM OPTICS]:::optics
    B --> C[IMAGER]:::imager
    C --> D[PROJECTION LENS]:::lens
    D --> E[REAR SCREEN]:::screen
    E --> F[AUDIENCE]:::viewer
    A -. Heat .-> H1[Cooling]:::support
    C -. Data .-> H2[Controller]:::support
    H2 -. Sync .-> C

    classDef source fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A0522D,color:#000
    classDef optics fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef imager fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef lens fill:#D8BFD8,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    classDef screen fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef viewer fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8000,color:#000
    classDef support fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,color:#000
```

**Node Legend (no reserved keywords used):**
- `SOURCE` — Lamp/LED/Laser light generator
- `ILLUM OPTICS` — Fly-eye integrator & relay
- `IMAGER` — LCD / DMD / LCoS modulation device
- `PROJECTION LENS` — Multi-element magnifying optics
- `REAR SCREEN` — Fresnel + Lenticular composite
- `AUDIENCE` — Multiple human operators
- `Cooling` — Thermal management subsystem
- `Controller` — Video sync & color processor

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Folded-Optic Ultra-Short-Throw RP Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Operator Audience]:::audience
    SC[Rear Projection Screen]:::screen
    M1[Primary Fold Mirror]:::mirror
    M2[Secondary Fold Mirror]:::mirror
    LE[Projection Lens Assembly]:::lens
    EN[Light Engine Cabinet]:::engine
    LS[Lamp or Laser Source]:::source
    IM[LCD DMD or LCoS Imager]:::imager
    CP[Video Controller]:::control

    P -->|Views| SC
    SC -->|Light hits rear| M1
    M1 -->|Reflects downward| M2
    M2 -->|Folds to engine| LE
    LE -->|Focuses| EN
    EN -->|Modulates| IM
    IM -->|Illuminated by| LS
    CP -->|Drives| IM

    classDef audience fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8000,color:#000
    classDef screen fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef mirror fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#000000,color:#000
    classDef lens fill:#D8BFD8,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    classDef engine fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef source fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A0522D,color:#000
    classDef imager fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Reading Tip:** When examiners show a side-view of an RP unit, always identify the **number of fold mirrors**. Two-mirror systems allow **cabinet depth $\le 30$ cm** for diagonals up to 100″ — a key KTU viva question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
> *Differentiate between front projection and rear projection displays. List any two advantages of rear projection in a control-room environment.*

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

| Aspect | Front Projection | Rear Projection |
|---|---|---|
| Projector–Observer Side | Same side | Opposite sides |
| Shadow Obstruction | Operator can cast shadow | No obstruction |
| Ambient Light Handling | Highly sensitive | Better (gain-based rejection) |
| Installation | Ceiling/floor mount in front | Self-contained rear cabinet |

**Two advantages for control rooms (1 mark each):**
1. The screen rejects ambient light via the **lenticular black-stripe layer**, providing high contrast even in 500-lux control-room ambient.
2. The light engine is **serviceable from the rear**, allowing **24/7 mission-critical uptime** without disturbing the viewing surface.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**  
> *What is meant by the term "throw ratio" in a rear-projection system? What is its significance in cabinet-depth design?*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Throw ratio is defined as the ratio of the **distance between the projection lens and the screen** to the **width of the screen**.

$$
TR = \dfrac{D_t}{W_s}
$$

**Significance (1.5 marks):** A **lower throw ratio** means the projector can be placed closer to the screen, which directly reduces the required cabinet depth. For example, $TR = 0.25$ for a 73-inch-wide screen gives a throw of only $\approx 18$ inches, enabling a **slim floor-stand console** ideal for shallow control rooms. Ultra-short-throw lenses ($TR \le 0.2$) combined with **mirror-folded optics** further minimize the cabinet footprint.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, you must answer **one full question** (with sub-parts a and b) from a choice of two.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — Optical Architecture

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **optical architecture of a rear-projection display system**, clearly identifying the functions of (i) the light source, (ii) the imager, (iii) the projection lens, (iv) the Fresnel screen layer, and (v) the lenticular screen layer. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 16:9 rear-projection display has a screen diagonal of 100 inches. The projection lens has a throw ratio of 0.30. Calculate the **screen width, screen height, throw distance, and minimum cabinet depth (assuming 25% additional clearance)**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Architecture (7 marks):**

A rear-projection system contains five functional blocks aligned along the optical axis:

1. **Light Source (1 mark):** Provides the luminous flux. Modern units use **laser-phosphor** or **RGB laser** sources offering 20000–30000 hours of operational life and wide color gamut (Rec.709 / DCI-P3). The source determines the system's **maximum achievable luminance** before the screen.

2. **Imager (1.5 marks):** Spatially modulates the light to form the image. Three dominant technologies are:
   - **3 × LCD** (transmissive) — three monochrome panels per RGB channel, recombined via a dichroic prism.
   - **DMD / DLP** — micromirror array with ±12° tilt at microsecond speeds; produces high sequential contrast.
   - **LCoS** — reflective liquid-crystal on silicon; high fill-factor, smooth pixel edges.
   
   The imager determines **native resolution and contrast**.

3. **Projection Lens (1 mark):** A multi-element lens magnifies the imager output onto the screen. Specifications include **focal length**, **f-number**, **zoom range**, **lens shift (vertical/horizontal)**, and **throw ratio**. A short-focal-length (short-throw) lens allows the engine to sit close to the screen.

4. **Fresnel Lens Screen Layer (1 mark):** A flat panel molded with concentric grooves that refract diverging projection light into a **near-collimated beam** perpendicular to the screen. This allows the projector to be placed at varying distances from the screen while maintaining uniform brightness.

5. **Lenticular Lens Screen Layer (1.5 marks):** A layer of vertical cylindrical micro-lenses (lenticules) bonded on top of the Fresnel layer. Each lenticule spreads light horizontally toward the audience and incorporates **black stripes** between lenticules that absorb off-axis **ambient light**, dramatically improving contrast in lit rooms.

> *(A neat block diagram showing: Source → Imager → Lens → Fresnel → Lenticular → Audience carries the remaining 1 mark.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert diagonal to width (2 marks).**

For a 16:9 aspect ratio:

$$
W_s = \dfrac{d}{\sqrt{1 + (9/16)^2}} = \dfrac{100}{\sqrt{337/256}} = \dfrac{100}{1.1473}
$$

$$
\boxed{W_s \approx 87.16 \text{ inches} \approx 2.214 \text{ m}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute screen height (1 mark).**

$$
H_s = \tfrac{9}{16} \cdot W_s = \tfrac{9}{16} \times 87.16
$$

$$
\boxed{H_s \approx 49.03 \text{ inches} \approx 1.246 \text{ m}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute throw distance (2 marks).**

$$
D_t = TR \times W_s = 0.30 \times 87.16
$$

$$
\boxed{D_t \approx 26.15 \text{ inches} \approx 0.664 \text{ m}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute cabinet depth with 25% clearance (2 marks).**

$$
D_{cabinet} = D_t \times 1.25 = 0.664 \times 1.25
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{cabinet} \approx 0.830 \text{ m}}
$$

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Screen Characteristics

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **principle, construction, and function of the Fresnel–lenticular rear-projection screen**. Discuss how the **black-stripe technology** improves contrast in ambient light. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A rear-projection screen has an on-axis gain of $G_0 = 4.0$ and follows the roll-off model $G(\theta) = G_0 \cos^{3}(\theta)$. Compute the **(i) gain at viewing angle 30°, (ii) half-gain angle, and (iii) whether the screen is suitable for a 90°-wide audience arc**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Screen Construction (7 marks):**

1. **Fresnel Lens Layer (2.5 marks):** The Fresnel lens is a flat acrylic panel molded with thousands of concentric circular grooves. Each groove is a tiny refracting prism. **Principle:** Light arriving from any point on the screen's rear surface is **refracted to emerge perpendicular to the screen plane** — effectively converting a divergent cone of light from the projector into a collimated forward beam. This allows the projector to be located anywhere behind the screen (short or long throw) without producing a "hot-spot" directly in front of the lens.

2. **Lenticular Lens Layer (2.5 marks):** The lenticular layer consists of vertical cylindrical micro-lenses (lenticules) — typically 50–100 lenticules per inch — bonded onto the front of the Fresnel layer. **Principle:** Each lenticule spreads the collimated light horizontally into a controlled viewing cone, defined by its curvature and pitch. The vertical viewing is controlled only by the diffusion of the bulk acrylic, giving wider vertical viewing than horizontal.

3. **Black-Stripe Contrast Enhancement (2 marks):** Black absorbing stripes are screen-printed *between* adjacent lenticules. These stripes absorb **off-axis ambient light** entering from the room (overhead lights, windows) before it can reach the audience's eyes after reflection. The projected light, traveling nearly perpendicular to the screen, is **unaffected** by the black stripes. This selective absorption can improve the effective contrast ratio by a factor of **3×–5×** in 500-lux ambient conditions — a critical HMI advantage in control rooms that cannot be fully darkened.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Gain at $\theta = 30°$ (2 marks).**

$$
G(30^{\circ}) = 4.0 \times \cos^{3}(30^{\circ}) = 4.0 \times (\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2})^{3}
$$

$$
= 4.0 \times 0.6495 = \boxed{2.598}
$$

**Step 2 — Half-gain angle (3 marks).**

Using the half-gain condition $G(\theta_{HG}) = G_0/2$:

$$
4.0 \cdot \cos^{3}(\theta_{HG}) = 2.0
$$

$$
\cos^{3}(\theta_{HG}) = 0.5
$$

$$
\cos(\theta_{HG}) = (0.5)^{1/3} = 0.7937
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta_{HG} = \arccos(0.7937) \approx 37.4^{\circ}}
$$

**Step 3 — Suitability for 90° audience arc (2 marks).**

A 90° total arc means a **half-arc of 45°** to either side. The half-gain angle is only $\approx 37.4°$, so an operator seated at the **edge of the 90° arc** would see a luminance **below the half-gain threshold** (i.e., $< 2.0$ instead of the peak 4.0).

> **Conclusion:** **This screen is NOT ideally suited** for a 90° audience arc. A **mid-gain screen with $n = 1.5$ (yielding $\theta_{HG} \approx 55°$)** should be specified, accepting a lower on-axis gain ($\approx 2.0$) in exchange for uniform off-axis brightness.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Rear-Projection Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the unit conversion (inches → metres)** when computing screen width for throw-ratio problems — examiners deduct 1 mark per missing unit.
> 2. **Confusing half-gain angle with full viewing-angle.** Always remember $\theta_{HG}$ is measured from the screen *normal* (center axis), not from one edge of the audience to the other.
> 3. **Writing the gain formula as $G = L/E$ instead of $G = L \cdot \pi / E$.** The factor of $\pi$ from Lambertian geometry is a common 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Drawing the block diagram with "front projection" terminology** (e.g., labeling the screen as reflective). Use the exact terms *Fresnel layer* and *lenticular layer* — generic terms cost marks.
> 5. **Skipping the "Why" of black-stripe technology.** Simply writing "improves contrast" is incomplete. Explain the *mechanism*: black stripes absorb off-axis ambient while on-axis projection light passes through.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Rear projection** places the projector *behind* the screen, eliminating operator shadows and ambient-light washout.
- ✅ The **optical chain** is: **Source → Illumination Optics → Imager (LCD/DLP/LCoS) → Projection Lens → Fresnel Layer → Lenticular Layer → Audience**.
- ✅ **Throw Ratio** $TR = D_t / W_s$ — low TR enables shallow cabinets; ultra-short-throw uses mirror folding.
- ✅ **Screen Gain** $G = L_\theta / L_{Lambert}$; typical control-room screens use $G \in [1.0, 4.0]$.
- ✅ **Half-Gain Angle** $\theta_{HG}$ — beyond this angle, brightness drops below 50% of peak; critical for wide control rooms.
- ✅ **Fresnel layer** collimates divergent projector light; **lenticular layer** spreads it horizontally and incorporates **black stripes** for ambient rejection.
- ✅ **Luminance formula** $L = (E \cdot G) / \pi$ — always retain the $\pi$ in the denominator.
- ✅ **System efficiency** $\eta_{system}$ is the product of source, imager, lens, and screen efficiencies.
- ✅ **Roll-off model** $G(\theta) = G_0 \cos^{n}(\theta)$ — high $n$ means high on-axis gain but narrow viewing cone.
- ✅ **Laser-phosphor light engines** are the modern standard for 24/7 mission-critical HMI installations (30000 h life).
- ✅ **Aspect ratio memory aid:** 16:9 → diagonal factor $\sqrt{337/256} \approx 1.147$ for width computation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sound Interaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT831 - HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACE
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sound Interaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sound Interaction (in HMI context):** The deliberate use of auditory channels — speech, non-speech audio, and acoustic feedback — as a primary or secondary modality through which a user perceives, communicates with, and controls a computational system. It encompasses the design, generation, perception, and evaluation of all sounds emitted or captured by an interactive system.

Sound Interaction is rooted in three intersecting disciplines:

1. **Psychoacoustics** — the psychological and physiological response to sound.
2. **Audio signal processing** — capture, synthesis, and analysis of acoustic waveforms.
3. **Interaction design** — fitting audio into the user–system dialogue loop.

### Key Sub-Topics in KTU Module 4

| Sub-area | One-line meaning |
|---|---|
| **Auditory Displays** | Interfaces where sound is the *primary* output channel. |
| **Speech Recognition (ASR)** | Machine conversion of spoken language → text/commands. |
| **Speech Synthesis (TTS)** | Machine conversion of text → spoken language. |
| **Non-Speech Audio** | Earcons, auditory icons, sonification. |
| **Multimodal Audio** | Audio combined with vision, touch, gesture. |
| **Accessibility Audio** | Sound cues for visually/hearing-impaired users. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Timer" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Why Sound Matters in Interaction**
>
> Imagine you are boiling milk on a stove. Your eyes are glued to your phone. Suddenly the milk overflows — but you only knew because of a **whistle** (auditory icon) and a **beep** from the timer (earcon).
>
> - The **whistle** was a *natural* representation of the event → an **auditory icon**.
> - The **beep** was a *learned*, abstract sound representing "time-up" → an **earcon**.
>
> Now imagine the stove spoke: *"The milk is boiling over."* That is **speech output (TTS)**.
>
> And imagine you simply said *"Reduce the flame"* to the stove — that is **speech input (ASR)**.
>
> In all four cases, your **ears** replaced or complemented your **eyes** as the interaction channel. *That* is Sound Interaction.

### Why Audio? — The Three Engineering Drivers

| Driver | Explanation | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Eyes-free use** | Audio is received without directing gaze. | Car navigation voice prompts. |
| **Parallelism** | Humans can listen while doing another visual task. | Pilot hearing ATC while reading gauges. |
| **Temporal nature** | Sound unfolds over time, ideal for conveying *trends*. | Heart-rate sonification in ICU monitor. |

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (bolded)

- Speed of sound in air (20°C): **343 m/s**.
- Standard human audible range: **20 Hz – 20 kHz**.
- Speech fundamental frequency (adult male): **85 – 180 Hz**; (adult female): **165 – 255 Hz**.
- Comfortable listening level for UI feedback: **40 – 60 dB SPL**.
- Standard sample rate for HMI audio: **44.1 kHz** (CD-quality) or **16 kHz** (telephony/speech recognition).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Auditory Perception Pipeline (Why we can use sound in HMI)

Human hearing is a *band-pass*, *logarithmic*, *temporal* sensor. The interaction designer must respect this pipeline:

```
Sound wave → Outer ear (pinna) → Ear canal → Tympanic membrane
          → Ossicles (mechanical amp) → Cochlea (frequency analyzer)
          → Basilar membrane → Hair cells → Auditory nerve
          → Brainstem → Auditory cortex → Meaning
```

### Step-by-step cognitive processing

1. **Outer/middle ear amplification** — About **+25 dB** gain through the ossicular chain. This is why whispers are audible.
2. **Cochlear frequency analysis** — Different positions on the basilar membrane resonate to different frequencies (place theory). High frequencies near the base, low near the apex.
3. **Loudness perception (logarithmic)** — Doubling the *sound pressure* is perceived as only ~+10 dB louder. Hence the use of the **decibel scale**.
4. **Temporal resolution** — Humans can resolve auditory events separated by as little as **2 ms** — far better than visual flicker fusion.
5. **Localization cues** — Interaural Time Difference (**ITD**) for low frequencies (< 1500 Hz), Interaural Level Difference (**ILD**) for high frequencies (> 1500 Hz).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** Because humans localize sound with **ITD + ILD**, a stereo (or binaural) interface can convey *spatial* information purely through headphones — used in 3D audio navigation for the visually impaired.

---

## 2.2 Non-Speech Audio (The Heart of Sound Interaction Design)

Non-speech audio is divided into three formal categories first introduced by Blattner, Sumikawa & Greenberg (1989):

### A. Auditory Icons
- **Definition:** Natural, everyday sounds that *resemble* the event they represent.
- **Examples:** A paper-shuffling sound when files are moved; a "splash" when a search returns no results.
- **Strengths:** Instantly understandable without learning.
- **Weaknesses:** Limited "vocabulary"; some concepts have no obvious sound.

### B. Earcons
- **Definition:** Short, structured, *abstract* musical tones used to represent events, objects, or operations.
- **Three parameters (motif, timbre, register):**
  - **Rhythm** — number/duration of notes.
  - **Pitch** — frequency of the tone.
  - **Timbre** — instrumental quality (piano vs. flute).
- **Compound vs. family earcons:**
  - *Family earcon:* same motif, different timbre → denotes family of related items.
  - *Compound earcon:* combination of two or more earcons → denotes a combined concept.
- **Strengths:** Unlimited vocabulary; can be hierarchical.
- **Weaknesses:** Must be *learned* (memorability cost).

### C. Sonification
- **Definition:** Use of non-speech audio to *convey information about data* by mapping data variables to acoustic parameters (pitch, loudness, tempo).
- **Sub-types:**
  - **Audification** — direct playback of data as audio waveform.
  - **Parameter Mapping Sonification** — data → acoustic variable mapping (most common).
  - **Model-Based Sonification** — sound generated by a dynamic model of the data.

---

## 2.3 Speech-Based Interaction

### Speech Recognition (Automatic Speech Recognition — ASR)

```
User speech → Microphone → Acoustic Model + Language Model + Lexicon
            → Decoder → Most probable word sequence → Command/Text
```

- **Acoustic Model (AM):** Probability of phonemes given audio (typically HMM or DNN-based).
- **Language Model (LM):** Probability of word sequences (often n-gram or neural LSTM/Transformer).
- **Decoder:** Combines AM + LM to find best hypothesis (Viterbi / beam search).

### Speech Synthesis (Text-to-Speech — TTS)

Two classical architectures:

| Architecture | How it works | Quality |
|---|---|---|
| **Concatenative TTS** | Stitches pre-recorded phonemes/diphones | High naturalness, robotic prosody |
| **Parametric TTS** (HMM/DNN) | Generates speech parameters (formants, pitch) | Less natural, fully controllable |
| **Neural TTS** (Tacotron, WaveNet) | End-to-end deep learning | State-of-the-art naturalness |

### Formant Frequencies — The "Vowel Signature"

The vowels /a/, /e/, /i/, /o/, /u/ are distinguished by **formant frequencies** $F_1, F_2$ (first two resonant peaks of the vocal tract):

$$F_1 \approx \frac{c}{4L}(2k-1), \quad k=1,2,3,\dots$$

Where:
- $c$ = speed of sound ≈ **343 m/s**
- $L$ = effective length of vocal tract ≈ **0.17 m** (adult male average)

This is the **quarter-wave resonator model** of the vocal tract.

Typical English vowel formants:

| Vowel | F1 (Hz) | F2 (Hz) |
|---|---|---|
| /iː/ (heed) | 270 | 2290 |
| /aː/ (hod) | 730 | 1090 |
| /uː/ (who'd) | 300 | 870 |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are fair game for KTU numericals, derivations, and short-answer derivations.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p}{p_0}\right)$ | Sound pressure level | dB SPL (ref $p_0 = 20\,\mu$Pa) |
| 2 | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | Sound intensity level | dB (ref $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m²) |
| 3 | $f' = f \cdot \dfrac{c+v_o}{c-v_s}$ | Doppler effect (observer $v_o$, source $v_s$) | Hz |
| 4 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength of sound | m |
| 5 | $ITD = \dfrac{d \sin\theta}{c}$ | Interaural time difference | s (head $d \approx 0.215$ m) |
| 6 | $F_n = \dfrac{(2n-1)c}{4L}$ | Vocal-tract formant frequencies | Hz |
| 7 | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | dB |
| 8 | $WER = \dfrac{S+D+I}{N}$ | Word Error Rate (ASR evaluation) | ratio |
| 9 | $MOS \in [1,5]$ | Mean Opinion Score (TTS quality) | unitless |
| 10 | $T_{60} = \dfrac{0.161\,V}{\sum S_i \alpha_i}$ | Sabine's reverberation time | s |

Where (Sabine): $V$ = room volume (m³), $S_i$ = surface area of $i$-th surface (m²), $\alpha_i$ = absorption coefficient.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Sound Interaction Application |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Voice-activated infotainment, lane-departure earcons. |
| **Avionics** | 3D audio traffic alerts, sonified flight parameters. |
| **Medical** | Heart-monitor sonification, surgical-tool audio feedback. |
| **Accessibility** | Screen-reader TTS for blind users; vibro-audio cues for deaf users. |
| **Industrial IoT** | Plant anomaly detection via acoustic emission sonification. |
| **Gaming/VR** | Spatial 3D audio, Foley earcons, voice commands. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Sound Pressure Level (dB SPL)

> **Goal:** Show why we use a logarithmic (decibel) scale for sound, and derive the SPL formula from first principles.

### Step 1: Human auditory dynamic range

The faintest audible pressure (threshold of hearing) is:

$$p_0 = 20 \,\mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{Pa}$$

The loudest tolerable pressure (threshold of pain) is approximately:

$$p_{\max} = 20\,\text{Pa}$$

The *ratio*:

$$\frac{p_{\max}}{p_0} = \frac{20}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 10^{6}$$

That's a range of **one million to one** — impossible to handle on a linear scale.

### Step 2: Use a logarithm

To compress the range, take $\log_{10}$:

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\max}}{p_0}\right) = \log_{10}(10^6) = 6$$

This gives us a manageable range: 0 to 6.

### Step 3: Multiply by 20 (Bell unit & deci-Bell)

By convention, 1 Bel = 10 dB, and since *power* is proportional to *pressure squared*, we use 20 instead of 10:

$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right)\ \text{dB SPL}$$

### Step 4: Sanity checks

- If $p = p_0$ (threshold): $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(1) = 0$ dB ✓
- If $p = 10\,p_0$: $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(10) = 20$ dB ✓
- If $p = 100\,p_0$: $L_p = 40$ dB ✓ (doubling pressure = +6 dB)
- If $p = 10^6 p_0$: $L_p = 120$ dB ✓ (jet engine range)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** A doubling of *sound pressure* corresponds to a **+6 dB** change, whereas a doubling of *perceived loudness* requires approximately **+10 dB**. This distinction is a frequent 3-mark question.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Interaural Time Difference (ITD) for Sound Localization

> **Goal:** Show how the brain localizes a sound source in the horizontal plane.

### Step 1: Geometry

Let the head be a sphere of diameter $d \approx 0.215$ m. Sound arrives from azimuth $\theta$ relative to the midline.

The path difference to the two ears is approximately:

$$\Delta x = d \sin\theta$$

### Step 2: Convert path difference to time difference

Since sound travels at speed $c$:

$$\boxed{\ ITD = \dfrac{d \sin\theta}{c}\ }$$

### Step 3: Maximum ITD (sound directly to one side, $\theta = 90°$)

$$\text{ITD}_{\max} = \frac{0.215 \times \sin 90°}{343} \approx 6.27 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s} = 627\,\mu\text{s}$$

> The **just-noticeable-difference (JND)** for ITD is about **10 μs** — explaining humans' exquisite localization ability.

### Step 4: Worked numerical example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A source is at $\theta = 30°$ to the right. Find ITD.
**Solution:**

$$ITD = \frac{0.215 \times \sin 30°}{343} = \frac{0.215 \times 0.5}{343} = 3.13 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s} = 313\,\mu\text{s}$$

This is well above the 10 μs JND → easily localizable.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Audio Cue

The KTU module expects students to *justify* a choice between earcons, auditory icons, and sonification. Use this matrix:

| Design Goal | Best Audio Cue | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| "File deleted" event feedback | **Auditory icon** (paper-rip) | Iconic resemblance → zero learning |
| Hierarchical menu navigation | **Earcon family** | Family earcons group related items by timbre |
| Real-time stock-market trend | **Sonification** | Continuous data → continuous audio |
| "Battery low" alert | **Earcon** (descending pitch) | Abstract but learnable |
| Visually impaired screen reading | **TTS** | Only speech conveys arbitrary text |
| Driver collision warning | **Auditory icon + 3D spatialization** | Intuitive, fast, eyes-free |

---

## 3.4 Derivations Table — Key Acoustic Relations

| Relation | Derivation | Final Formula |
|---|---|---|
| Wavelength from frequency | Wave equation $c = f\lambda$ | $\lambda = c/f$ |
| Doppler frequency | Source motion adds $v_s/\lambda$ per cycle | $f' = f \cdot (c+v_o)/(c-v_s)$ |
| Sabine reverberation | Empirical energy decay | $T_{60} = 0.161\,V/\sum S_i \alpha_i$ |
| Speech Intelligibility Index | Weighted SNR across bands | $SII = \sum_i I_i \cdot W_i$ |

### Step-by-step derivation of $\lambda = c/f$

The fundamental wave relation states that in one period $T$ the wave travels one wavelength $\lambda$:

$$c = \frac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}} = \frac{\lambda}{T}$$

Since $f = 1/T$, we substitute:

$$c = \lambda f \implies \boxed{\ \lambda = \frac{c}{f}\ }$$

> **Numerical:** A 1 kHz tone in air → $\lambda = 343/1000 = 0.343$ m = **34.3 cm**.
> A 100 Hz bass tone → $\lambda = 3.43$ m (longer than a person).

This frequency–wavelength relationship explains why **low frequencies are harder to localize with ILD** (wavelength > head size → no shadowing) and why they rely on **ITD**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Speech Formant Frequency

**Problem:** For an adult male with vocal-tract length $L = 0.17$ m, compute the first three formant frequencies using the quarter-wave model.

**Solution:**

$$F_n = \frac{(2n-1)\,c}{4L}, \quad c = 343\,\text{m/s},\ L = 0.17\,\text{m}$$

$$F_1 = \frac{(1)(343)}{4 \times 0.17} = \frac{343}{0.68} \approx 504\,\text{Hz}$$

$$F_2 = \frac{(3)(343)}{0.68} = \frac{1029}{0.68} \approx 1513\,\text{Hz}$$

$$F_3 = \frac{(5)(343)}{0.68} \approx 2522\,\text{Hz}$$

These values align with observed formant ranges for adult male vowels (e.g., /a/ has $F_1 \approx 730$ Hz — within the same order).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Sound Interaction Loop

This shows how a user and an audio-enabled system exchange information through the auditory channel.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    user1[User Ears Capture Sound]
    mic1[Microphone Input]
    asr1[ASR Engine]
    nlu1[Intent Decoder]
    app1[Application Logic]
    tts1[TTS Engine]
    spk1[Speaker Output]
    user2[User Hears Response]
    user1 --> mic1
    mic1 --> asr1
    asr1 --> nlu1
    nlu1 --> app1
    app1 --> tts1
    tts1 --> spk1
    spk1 --> user2
    user2 --> user1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The outer loop (User Ears → User Hears Response) represents the **full duplex audio dialogue**.
- `ASR Engine` performs **speech-to-text**.
- `TTS Engine` performs **text-to-speech**.
- The **Application Logic** node is where the audio modality meets conventional GUI/software logic.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Taxonomy of Audio in HMI

A clear hierarchy of the three core families of non-speech audio + speech.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    audioHub[Audio in HMI]
    speech[Speech Audio]
    nonspeech[Non Speech Audio]
    asrNode[ASR Input]
    ttsNode[TTS Output]
    earconsNode[Earcons]
    iconsNode[Auditory Icons]
    sonifNode[Sonification]
    motivNode[Motif Rhythm Pitch]
    familyNode[Family Compound]
    paramNode[Parameter Mapping]
    modelNode[Model Based]
    audioHub --> speech
    audioHub --> nonspeech
    speech --> asrNode
    speech --> ttsNode
    nonspeech --> earconsNode
    nonspeech --> iconsNode
    nonspeech --> sonifNode
    earconsNode --> motivNode
    earconsNode --> familyNode
    sonifNode --> paramNode
    sonifNode --> modelNode
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — ASR Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawAudio[Raw Audio Waveform]
    featExt[Feature Extraction MFCC]
    amBlock[Acoustic Model HMM DNN]
    lexBlock[Lexicon Pronunciation Dictionary]
    lmBlock[Language Model N gram Neural]
    decoder[Decoder Viterbi Beam Search]
    hypoNode[Best Word Hypothesis]
    rawAudio --> featExt
    featExt --> amBlock
    amBlock --> decoder
    lexBlock --> decoder
    lmBlock --> decoder
    decoder --> hypoNode
```

**Engineering note:** Modern end-to-end ASR (e.g., Whisper, wav2vec 2.0) collapses AM + LM + Lexicon into a single neural network — but the **functional roles** remain identical, which is what KTU examiners test.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Psychoacoustic Localization Cues

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    srcNode[Sound Source Azimuth theta]
    itdNode[ITD Path Difference]
    ildNode[ILD Head Shadowing]
    lowPath[Effective below 1500 Hz]
    highPath[Effective above 1500 Hz]
    cortexNode[Auditory Cortex Fusion]
    perceptNode[Perceived Direction]
    srcNode --> itdNode
    srcNode --> ildNode
    itdNode --> lowPath
    ildNode --> highPath
    lowPath --> cortexNode
    highPath --> cortexNode
    cortexNode --> perceptNode
```

This is the **Duplex Theory of sound localization** (Lord Rayleigh). It is the theoretical basis for **spatial audio in HMI** (3D headphones, surround cockpits, AR glasses).

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — TTS Architecture (Classical vs Neural)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    textIn[Input Text]
    frontEnd[Text Front End]
    lingFeat[Linguistic Features Phonemes Prosody]
    concNode[Concatenative TTS Waveform Database]
    paramNode2[Parametric TTS HMM DNN Vocoder]
    neuralNode[Neural TTS Tacotron WaveNet]
    outWave[Output Speech Waveform]
    textIn --> frontEnd
    frontEnd --> lingFeat
    lingFeat --> concNode
    lingFeat --> paramNode2
    lingFeat --> neuralNode
    concNode --> outWave
    paramNode2 --> outWave
    neuralNode --> outWave
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Earcons and Auditory Icons with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (≈ 90 words):**

| Aspect | Earcons | Auditory Icons |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Abstract, structured tones | Natural, everyday sounds |
| Learning | Must be memorized | Intuitive |
| Vocabulary | Unlimited | Limited |
| Example | Three ascending notes = "Save" | Paper-rip sound = "File deleted" |
| Use | Hierarchical menus, alerts | Direct event representation |

Earcons manipulate **rhythm, pitch, and timbre** to convey meaning; auditory icons rely on **semantic resemblance** to the source event.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is Sonification? List its three main types.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Sonification** is the use of non-speech audio to represent data, mapping data variables to acoustic parameters such as pitch, loudness, or tempo.

Three main types:
1. **Audification** — direct playback of data samples as audio (e.g., seismic data).
2. **Parameter-Mapping Sonification** — explicit mapping of data dimension → acoustic variable (most common; e.g., ECG → pitch).
3. **Model-Based Sonification** — sound generated by a dynamic physical model whose state depends on data interaction.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Module 4 Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an **Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR)** system. Discuss the role of each module. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Duplex Theory of Sound Localization**. Derive the expression for **Interaural Time Difference (ITD)** and compute its maximum value for an average adult head. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.A (a) — ASR Architecture (7 Marks)

The ASR pipeline converts a speech waveform into a textual hypothesis:

```
Speech → Microphone → Feature Extraction → Acoustic Model
       → Decoder (with Lexicon + Language Model) → Text
```

**Module roles:**

1. **Microphone & Pre-processing** — Captures the analog signal, applies anti-aliasing filter, A/D conversion (typically 16 kHz).
2. **Feature Extraction** — Converts waveform into acoustic vectors, commonly **MFCC (Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients)** or filter-bank energies. MFCCs mimic the human cochlea's frequency resolution.
3. **Acoustic Model (AM)** — Models the probability $P(\text{phoneme} \mid \text{audio})$. Classical AMs use **GMM-HMM**; modern systems use **DNNs, RNNs, or Transformers** trained on large speech corpora.
4. **Pronunciation Lexicon** — Maps each word to its phonetic transcription (e.g., "hello" → /h ɛ l oʊ/).
5. **Language Model (LM)** — Models $P(\text{word sequence})$ using n-grams or neural LSTMs to prefer grammatically/semantically likely hypotheses.
6. **Decoder** — Combines AM + LM via **Viterbi** or **beam search** to produce the most probable word sequence.

**Block diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    spk1[Speech Input]
    mic1[Microphone and ADC]
    feat1[Feature Extraction MFCC]
    am1[Acoustic Model DNN HMM]
    lex1[Lexicon]
    lm1[Language Model]
    dec1[Decoder Viterbi Beam Search]
    txt1[Output Text]
    spk1 --> mic1
    mic1 --> feat1
    feat1 --> am1
    am1 --> dec1
    lex1 --> dec1
    lm1 --> dec1
    dec1 --> txt1
```

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.A (b) — Duplex Theory & ITD (7 Marks)

The **Duplex Theory** (Lord Rayleigh) states that the auditory system uses **two independent cues** to localize sound in the horizontal plane:

- **ITD (Interaural Time Difference):** Effective for frequencies **below ~1500 Hz**. The phase difference between ears encodes direction.
- **ILD (Interaural Level Difference):** Effective for frequencies **above ~1500 Hz**. The head casts an acoustic "shadow" for the farther ear.

**Derivation of ITD:**

Model the head as a sphere of diameter $d$. A source at azimuth $\theta$ travels an extra path $\Delta x$ to the farther ear:

$$\Delta x = d \sin\theta$$

Dividing by sound speed $c$:

$$\boxed{\ ITD = \dfrac{d \sin\theta}{c}\ }$$

**Maximum ITD** occurs at $\theta = 90°$ (sound directly to one side). For an average adult head, $d \approx 0.215$ m, $c = 343$ m/s:

$$ITD_{\max} = \frac{0.215 \times 1}{343} \approx 6.27 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s} = 627\,\mu\text{s}$$

Since the human JND for ITD is around **10 μs**, this gives an enormous *headroom* for accurate localization — explaining the success of 3D-audio HMI.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`**

**(a)** Explain the three categories of **non-speech audio** in HMI. Compare their advantages and limitations with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable block diagram, describe the working of a **Text-to-Speech (TTS)** system. Compare **concatenative**, **parametric**, and **neural** TTS approaches. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.B (a) — Non-Speech Audio (7 Marks)

The three categories (Blattner et al., 1989) are:

1. **Auditory Icons** — Natural sounds that *resemble* the event.
   - *Example:* A trash-can "thud" when a file is deleted.
   - **+** Instantly meaningful; **−** Limited vocabulary; sometimes intrusive.

2. **Earcons** — Abstract, structured musical tones built from motifs.
   - *Example:* Two rising notes = "Upload complete"; same notes in flute timbre = "Upload to cloud".
   - **+** Unlimited vocabulary, hierarchically composable; **−** Must be learned, slower recognition.

3. **Sonification** — Data is mapped to acoustic variables (pitch, tempo, loudness).
   - *Example:* Stock price → pitch; volume → loudness.
   - **+** Reveals trends; **−** Can be annoying; requires careful design.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Auditory Icons | Earcons | Sonification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Learnability | High (intuitive) | Low (memorization) | Variable |
| Vocabulary | Limited | Unlimited | Continuous |
| Best for | Discrete events | Hierarchical commands | Continuous data |
| Example | Splash = no results | 3-note motif = save | Pitch = heart rate |

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.B (b) — TTS System (7 Marks)

A TTS system converts text into a spoken waveform.

**Block diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    txt1[Input Text]
    norm1[Text Normalizer]
    ling1[ Linguistic Analyzer Phonemes POS]
    pros1[Prosody Generator Pitch Duration]
    synth1[Acoustic Model or Unit Selector]
    voc1[Vocoder or Concatenation]
    wav1[Speech Waveform]
    txt1 --> norm1
    norm1 --> ling1
    ling1 --> pros1
    pros1 --> synth1
    synth1 --> voc1
    voc1 --> wav1
```

**Stages explained:**

1. **Text Normalization** — Expands abbreviations, numbers, dates ("Dr." → "Doctor", "₹500" → "five hundred rupees").
2. **Linguistic Analysis** — Determines phonemes, word stress, part-of-speech.
3. **Prosody Generation** — Predicts pitch contour (F0), duration, energy — the *musicality* of speech.
4. **Acoustic Modeling / Unit Selection** — Produces mel-spectrogram or chooses waveform units.
5. **Vocoding / Concatenation** — Converts mel-spectrogram to audio (neural vocoder) or stitches pre-recorded units.

**Comparison of the three TTS approaches:**

| Aspect | Concatenative | Parametric (HMM/DNN) | Neural (End-to-End) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Core idea | Stitch recorded units | Generate parameters, vocode | Direct text → waveform |
| Naturalness | High (if corpus rich) | Moderate | Very high (state-of-the-art) |
| Robustness | Poor to unseen words | Good | Excellent |
| Footprint | Very large | Small | Moderate–large |
| Examples | Original DECtalk | HTS, Festival | Tacotron 2 + WaveNet, VITS |

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in Sound Interaction questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ITD and ILD ranges.** ITD works for *low frequencies* (< 1500 Hz), ILD for *high* (> 1500 Hz). Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using the wrong logarithm base.** Sound Pressure Level uses **20 log**, not 10 log. Intensity uses 10 log. Many students write 10 log for SPL — instant mark deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting the constant in Sabine's formula.** The constant is **0.161** (SI units), not 0.16 or 0.16 V. The full numerator is **0.161 × V**.
> 4. **Drawing an ASR block diagram without the Language Model.** Many sketches omit the LM, which is a *mandatory* part of any ASR.
> 5. **Saying "earcons are musical tunes"** — this is too vague. Always specify **rhythm, pitch, and timbre** as the three control parameters.
> 6. **Mixing up TTS architectures.** "Concatenative" is *not* "parametric". They are mutually exclusive categories.
> 7. **In Doppler-effect problems**, students often forget the sign of $v_s$. Remember: source moving *toward* observer → denominator $(c - v_s)$ *decreases* → frequency *increases*.
> 8. **In sonification questions**, do not write "sonification is background music." Always define it as **data-driven acoustic mapping**.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Sound Interaction (Module 4)**
>
> ✅ **Three non-speech audio categories:** Auditory Icons (natural resemblance), Earcons (abstract motifs with rhythm/pitch/timbre), Sonification (data → audio mapping).
>
> ✅ **Earcon control parameters:** Rhythm, Pitch, Timbre, Register, Dynamics.
>
> ✅ **Sonification types:** Audification, Parameter-mapping, Model-based.
>
> ✅ **Speech Recognition pipeline:** Mic → Feature (MFCC) → Acoustic Model + Lexicon + Language Model → Decoder → Text.
>
> ✅ **Speech Synthesis types:** Concatenative, Parametric (HMM/DNN), Neural (Tacotron, WaveNet, VITS).
>
> ✅ **Duplex Theory:** ITD for low f (< 1500 Hz), ILD for high f (> 1500 Hz).
>
> ✅ **Key formulas:**
> - $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$ where $p_0 = 20\,\mu$Pa.
> - $ITD = d\sin\theta/c$, with $d \approx 0.215$ m, $c = 343$ m/s, $ITD_{\max} \approx 627\,\mu$s.
> - $\lambda = c/f$.
> - $F_n = (2n-1)c/(4L)$.
> - $SNR_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(P_s/P_n)$.
> - $WER = (S+D+I)/N$ for ASR evaluation.
>
> ✅ **Key physical constants:** Speed of sound = **343 m/s**, Hearing threshold = **20 μPa**, Audible range = **20 Hz – 20 kHz**.
>
> ✅ **Use cases to remember:** Car navigation (earcons), screen reader (TTS), medical ICU (sonification), visually impaired navigation (3D spatial audio).
>
> ✅ **Evaluation metrics:** WER for ASR, MOS for TTS, SUS for usability of audio interfaces.
>
> ✅ **Standard sample rates:** 16 kHz for telephony/ASR, 44.1 kHz for high-fidelity HMI audio.
>
> ✅ **Accessibility note:** Audio is *primary* for visually-impaired users; *complementary* for sighted users (eyes-free operation).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Acoustics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT831 - HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basics of Acoustics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Acoustics

> [!NOTE]
> **Acoustics** is the interdisciplinary branch of physics and engineering that studies the **generation, propagation, reception, and perception of mechanical waves** in gases, liquids, and solids. These mechanical waves — known as **sound waves** — involve oscillations of pressure, particle displacement, or particle velocity that propagate through an elastic medium at a speed characteristic of that medium.

In the context of **Human–Machine Interface (HMI)**, acoustics is foundational because virtually every auditory feedback mechanism — beeps, voice prompts, alarms, speech recognition, and sonification of data — depends on the generation, shaping, and reproduction of sound. Without understanding the basics of acoustics, an HMI designer cannot engineer effective, non-irritating, and accessible audio cues.

### Key Sub-disciplines in HMI Acoustics

| Sub-field | Relevance to HMI |
|---|---|
| **Psychoacoustics** | Study of human perception of sound — drives alarm design |
| **Ultrasonics** | Non-audible feedback, gesture sensors, proximity detection |
| **Architectural Acoustics** | Cabin design in vehicles, cockpit voice clarity |
| **Signal Processing Acoustics** | Speech recognition, noise cancellation in HMIs |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Pond Ripple" Mental Model

Imagine throwing a stone into a still pond. The stone pushes water down, and a circular ripple travels outward. Sound behaves almost identically, except the "stone" is a vibrating diaphragm (e.g., a speaker cone) and the "pond" is the air. Each ripple is a **compression** (molecules bunched) followed by a **rarefaction** (molecules spread out). Your eardrum, like a tiny floating leaf on the pond, is pushed back and forth by these ripples — and your brain interprets that motion as **sound**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sound requires a medium.** In a perfect vacuum, there is no sound. This is why astronauts in space cannot hear each other directly — and why acoustic HMIs are bound to the environmental medium (air, water, structural mounts in machinery).

## 1.3 Essential Physical Quantities (KTU High-Yield Terminology)

- **Frequency ($f$)** — Number of complete pressure oscillations per second. Unit: **Hertz (Hz)**. Human audible range: **20 Hz to 20,000 Hz**.
- **Wavelength ($\lambda$)** — Spatial length of one complete cycle. Unit: **meters (m)**.
- **Period ($T$)** — Time to complete one cycle. $T = 1/f$. Unit: **seconds (s)**.
- **Amplitude ($A$)** — Maximum displacement of particles or maximum pressure deviation from equilibrium.
