- **Sound Pressure ($p$)** — Instantaneous local pressure deviation caused by the wave. Unit: **Pascal (Pa)**.
- **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** — Logarithmic measure in **decibels (dB)** referenced to the threshold of human hearing $p_0 = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$.
- **Speed of Sound ($c$)** — In air at **20 °C**, $c \approx \mathbf{343\ m/s}$. In water $\approx 1480\ m/s$. In steel $\approx 5000\ m/s$.

> [!TIP]
> Memorize $c \approx 343\ m/s$ for air at room temperature — it is the **standard reference value** used in virtually all KTU problems unless stated otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Sound Wave

A sound wave is a **longitudinal mechanical wave**. The disturbance is *parallel* to the direction of propagation (unlike a wave on a string, which is transverse). In air, the wave consists of alternating **compressions** (high-pressure regions) and **rarefactions** (low-pressure regions).

### 2.1.1 The Wave Equation (1-D, Lossless)

For a 1-D plane acoustic wave traveling in the $+x$ direction, the instantaneous sound pressure satisfies:

$$\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2} = \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2}$$

This is the **acoustic wave equation**, derived from the linearized continuity, momentum, and adiabatic gas equations. Its general solution is:

$$p(x,t) = p_0 \cos(\omega t - kx + \phi)$$

where:
- $p_0$ = pressure amplitude
- $\omega = 2\pi f$ = angular frequency (rad/s)
- $k = 2\pi / \lambda$ = wave number (rad/m)
- $\phi$ = initial phase

## 2.2 Fundamental Acoustic Relationships

The following are the **non-negotiable formulas** for any KTU acoustics problem:

### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Use in HMI |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Period | $T = 1/f$ | s | Timing of periodic alerts |
| 2 | Wavelength | $\lambda = c / f$ | m | Speaker enclosure sizing |
| 3 | Angular frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | rad/s | DSP filter design |
| 4 | Wave number | $k = 2\pi / \lambda = \omega / c$ | rad/m | Beam-forming in mic arrays |
| 5 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p / p_0)$ | dB | Specifying alarm loudness |
| 6 | Sound Intensity Level | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I / I_0)$ | dB | Acoustic power of speakers |
| 7 | Intensity–Pressure relation | $I = p_{\text{rms}}^2 / (\rho c)$ | W/m² | Microphone sensitivity |
| 8 | Acoustic Impedance | $Z = \rho c$ | Pa·s/m | Matching transducers |
| 9 | Speed of sound in air | $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T / M}$ | m/s | Environmental compensation |
| 10 | Inverse square law | $L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 20 \log_{10}(r_2 / r_1)$ | dB | Far-field speaker placement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The reference values used in SPL are: $p_0 = 20\,\mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{Pa}$ and $I_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W/m}^2$. These are the **standard ISO 389-7 values** and are *expected to be used* in KTU numerical problems.

## 2.3 The Decibel Scale — Why Logarithms?

Human hearing spans an enormous range — from the threshold of hearing ($\sim 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$) to the threshold of pain ($\sim 200\ \text{Pa}$), a ratio of **10 million to 1**. A linear scale is impractical, so we use the **decibel (dB)**, a logarithmic ratio:

$$L_p\ (\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right)$$

The factor of **20** (not 10) comes from the fact that power is proportional to pressure squared, and $\log_{10}(x^2) = 2\log_{10}(x)$.

### Useful Decibel Quick-Reference

| SPL (dB) | Source | HMI Implication |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | Threshold of hearing | Silence — no cue possible |
| 30 | Quiet library | Soft confirmation chimes |
| 60 | Normal conversation | Voice prompt level |
| 80 | Heavy traffic | Sustained exposure hazardous |
| 100 | Jackhammer | Must wear hearing protection |
| 120 | Threshold of pain / rock concert | Immediate hearing damage |
| 140 | Jet engine (close) | Physical pain, OSHA limit |

> [!TIP]
> A useful **engineering shortcut**: a **3 dB increase ≈ doubling of sound intensity** (power), while a **10 dB increase ≈ doubling of perceived loudness** and a **10× increase in intensity**. A 20 dB increase = 100× intensity.

## 2.4 Acoustic Impedance and Transmission

**Acoustic impedance** $Z = \rho c$ is the acoustic analog of electrical impedance. It governs how much of a wave is **reflected** vs **transmitted** at a boundary between two media. The reflection coefficient for normal incidence is:

$$R = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$$

This is why ultrasound gel is used in medical imaging — it eliminates the air gap and minimizes reflection at the skin-transducer interface.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in HMI

- **In-Vehicle HMIs:** Engineers use the inverse-square law to position speakers so that voice prompts are intelligible from the driver's seat without being overwhelming to rear passengers.
- **Medical Alarms (IEC 60601-1-8):** Acoustic alarms in hospital equipment are specified in dB above ambient, exploiting psychoacoustic principles.
- **Speech Recognition Front-Ends:** Noise-robustness relies on the SPL of interfering sound relative to the speech signal — the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**.
- **Bone-Conduction Headsets:** Use solids (high $c$) to deliver audio without blocking the ear canal — critical for situational awareness in industrial HMIs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From the Acoustic Wave Equation to the Pressure Wave

We start from three governing equations for sound in a fluid (no viscosity, no heat conduction):

**Continuity (mass conservation):**

$$\frac{\partial \rho'}{\partial t} + \rho_0 \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0$$

**Linearized momentum (Euler equation):**

$$\rho_0 \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial p'}{\partial x}$$

**Adiabatic equation of state:**

$$p' = c^2 \rho'$$

where $\rho'$ is the density perturbation, $u$ is the particle velocity, $p'$ is the acoustic pressure, and $c$ is the speed of sound.

**Step 1:** Differentiate continuity with respect to time:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} + \rho_0 \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x \partial t} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate the momentum equation with respect to $x$:

$$\rho_0 \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x \partial t} = -\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial x^2}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute Step 2 into Step 1:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \rho'}{\partial t^2} = \frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial x^2}$$

**Step 4:** Use $p' = c^2 \rho'$ to eliminate $\rho'$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2} = c^2 \frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial x^2}$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange into the standard 1-D wave equation form:

$$\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial x^2} = \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p'}{\partial t^2}$$

> This confirms the acoustic pressure $p'$ satisfies the wave equation with phase speed $c$. Any solution of this PDE is a sound wave; the simplest is the monochromatic plane wave $p' = p_0 \cos(\omega t - kx + \phi)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relation Between Wavelength, Frequency, and Speed of Sound

**Step 1:** By definition, wavelength $\lambda$ is the distance over which the wave pattern repeats. The phase of the wave is $\theta = \omega t - kx$.

**Step 2:** For one full cycle, the phase increases by $2\pi$. So when the wave moves a distance $\lambda$ in time $T$:

$$\omega T = k \lambda$$

**Step 3:** But $\omega = 2\pi / T$ and $k = 2\pi / \lambda$. Substituting:

$$\frac{2\pi}{T} \cdot T = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \lambda \quad\Rightarrow\quad 2\pi = 2\pi\ \checkmark$$

**Step 4:** Divide both sides of $\omega T = k\lambda$ by $kT$:

$$\frac{\omega}{k} = \frac{\lambda}{T} = c$$

**Step 5:** Since $\omega = 2\pi f$:

$$c = \frac{\omega}{k} = \frac{2\pi f}{2\pi / \lambda} = f \lambda$$

$$\boxed{\ c = f \lambda\ }$$

> This is the **most fundamental equation in acoustics** and appears in nearly every KTU question. Always cross-check units: $f$ in Hz × $\lambda$ in m = m/s ✓.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Speed of Sound in an Ideal Gas

For an ideal gas undergoing adiabatic (fast) compression, $PV^{\gamma} = \text{const}$, where $\gamma$ is the ratio of specific heats.

**Step 1:** Bulk modulus $B = -V \dfrac{dP}{dV}$. From $PV^{\gamma} = K$:

$$P = K V^{-\gamma} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{dP}{dV} = -\gamma K V^{-\gamma - 1} = -\gamma \frac{P}{V}$$

**Step 2:** Therefore:

$$B = -V \cdot \left(-\gamma \frac{P}{V}\right) = \gamma P$$

**Step 3:** The general formula for speed of sound in a fluid is $c = \sqrt{B / \rho}$. Using ideal gas law $P = \rho R T / M$ (where $M$ is molar mass):

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma P}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma \rho R T / M}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T}{M}}$$

$$\boxed{\ c = \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma R T}{M}}\ }$$

**Numerical check (air at 293 K):**
- $\gamma = 1.4$ (diatomic)
- $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- $M_{\text{air}} = 0.029\ \text{kg/mol}$
- $T = 293\ \text{K}$

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{1.4 \times 8.314 \times 293}{0.029}} = \sqrt{117{,}635} \approx 343\ \text{m/s}\ \checkmark$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Intensity from RMS Pressure

The instantaneous intensity of a plane wave is $I(t) = p(t) \cdot u(t)$, where $u$ is particle velocity. For a plane wave, $p / u = \rho c$, so $u = p / (\rho c)$ and:

$$I(t) = \frac{p^2(t)}{\rho c}$$

**Step 1:** For a sinusoid $p(t) = p_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$:

$$I(t) = \frac{p_0^2 \cos^2(\omega t - kx)}{\rho c}$$

**Step 2:** The time-averaged intensity uses the RMS value $p_{\text{rms}} = p_0 / \sqrt{2}$:

$$\langle I \rangle = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{\rho c}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $p_{\text{rms}} = p_0 / \sqrt{2}$:

$$\langle I \rangle = \frac{p_0^2}{2 \rho c}$$

**Step 4:** The intensity level in dB is:

$$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\langle I \rangle}{I_0}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_0^2}{2 \rho c \, I_0}\right)$$

Using $I_0 = p_0^2 / (2 \rho c)$ evaluated at $p_0 = p_{\text{ref}} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$:

$$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{p_{\text{ref}}^2}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) = L_p$$

> This explains why SPL and SIL are numerically equal for the same physical disturbance in free field.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A factory floor has an SPL of 90 dB. An operator stands 1 m from a machine alarm. If the operator walks to 10 m away, what is the new SPL? Assume a point source radiating spherically.

**Step 1:** The inverse-square law in dB:

$$L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Plug in values:

$$L_{p2} = 90 - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10}{1}\right) = 90 - 20(1) = 90 - 20 = 70\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation:

$$\Delta L = -20\ \text{dB} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{Intensity drops by factor } 10^{20/10} = 100\times$$

The operator now hears the alarm at one-hundredth the original intensity. **HMI design implication:** alarm volume must be loud enough at the operator's typical working distance — not just 1 m away.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Acoustic HMI Signal Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how acoustic information flows from a physical event, through the HMI system, to the human operator.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Event Source] --> B[Transducer Microphone]
    B --> C[Analog Front End Preamplifier]
    C --> D[ADC Analog to Digital Converter]
    D --> E[Digital Signal Processor DSP]
    E --> F[Feature Extraction MFCC or Spectral]
    F --> G{Decision Module HMI Logic}
    G -->|Normal| H[Background Tone Speaker]
    G -->|Warning| I[Alarm Tone Speaker 85 dB SPL]
    G -->|Critical| J[Voice Prompt Speaker]
    H --> K[Operator Ear Cochlea]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Perception and Response]
    L --> M[Feedback to Machine via HMI Display or Keypad]
```

> This block architecture is the canonical model for **acoustic feedback loops in industrial and vehicular HMIs**, where machine states are encoded as sound and operator responses close the loop.

## 4.2 Acoustic Field Zoning Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NF[Near Field Zone]
        NF1[Complex pressure field] --> NF2[Geometry dependent]
        NF2 --> NF3[Distance less than wavelength]
    end
    subgraph FF[Far Field Zone]
        FF1[Spherical spreading 1 over r] --> FF2[Inverse square law applies]
        FF2 --> FF3[Distance greater than 2 times D squared over lambda]
    end
    NF -->|Transition boundary| FF
    FF -->|SPL prediction| ENG[HMI Engineer uses FF model for speaker placement]
```

## 4.3 Frequency-Band Reference Chart (for HMI Audio Cues)

| Band | Range | HMI Sound Type |
|---|---|---|
| Infrasound | < 20 Hz | Not audible — used in industrial vibration sensing |
| Low | 20–200 Hz | Sub-bass rumble cues in simulators |
| Speech fundamental | 85–255 Hz | Voice male fundamental |
| Mid | 250 Hz–2 kHz | Most speech intelligibility |
| High | 2 kHz–8 kHz | Consonant clarity in voice prompts |
| Presence | 4 kHz–6 kHz | Alarm beep "attention" band |
| Brilliance | 8 kHz–20 kHz | Ear-cons — not useful for HMIs |
| Ultrasound | > 20 kHz | Used in gesture sensors |

> [!TIP]
> For HMI alarms, design peak energy in the **2–4 kHz** range. The human ear is most sensitive near 3–4 kHz (Fletcher-Munson curve minimum), so lower SPLs are perceived as louder.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Define Sound Pressure Level. A sound has an RMS pressure of 0.2 Pa. Calculate its SPL in dB. Take reference pressure $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Sound Pressure Level (SPL) is the logarithmic measure of the RMS sound pressure of a sound wave, expressed in decibels, relative to a standard reference pressure $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$.

> **Calculation:**

$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.2}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right)$$

$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.2}{2 \times 10^{-5}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(10{,}000) = 20 \times 4 = 80\ \text{dB}$$

> **Result: SPL = 80 dB** — comparable to a noisy restaurant.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Explain the term "acoustic impedance" and state its SI unit. Why is impedance matching important in HMI speaker design?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Acoustic Impedance ($Z$):** It is the product of the density of the medium ($\rho$) and the speed of sound in it ($c$), i.e., $Z = \rho c$. SI unit: **Pa·s/m³** (or rayl, though not SI).

> **Importance in HMI:** When sound travels from one medium to another (e.g., speaker diaphragm to air), an impedance mismatch causes reflection and energy loss. Matching $Z$ maximizes power transfer. In HMI speakers, this is achieved using horn-loaded enclosures that match the diaphragm's high impedance to the air's low impedance.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the relationship between the speed of sound, frequency, and wavelength. A siren emits a 1000 Hz tone. Find the wavelength in air at 20 °C.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** The general 1-D acoustic wave solution is:

$$p(x,t) = p_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$$

**Step 2:** For one full period $T$, the wave advances by one wavelength $\lambda$. This gives the equality $\omega T = k\lambda$.

**Step 3:** Since $\omega = 2\pi / T$ and $k = 2\pi / \lambda$:

$$\frac{2\pi}{T} T = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \lambda \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{\omega}{k} = c$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $\omega = 2\pi f$:

$$\boxed{c = f\lambda}$$

**Step 5:** At 20 °C, $c = 343\ \text{m/s}$, $f = 1000\ \text{Hz}$:

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{343}{1000} = 0.343\ \text{m} = 34.3\ \text{cm}$$

> **Result:** Wavelength = 0.343 m, which is why a 1 kHz siren has wavelengths comparable to the size of a human head — a critical fact in HRTF (head-related transfer function) modeling for 3D audio in HMIs.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**The intensity of a sound is $10^{-6}\ \text{W/m}^2$. Find (i) the intensity level in dB, and (ii) the RMS sound pressure in air, given $\rho c = 413\ \text{kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Intensity Level:**

$$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10^{-6}}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(10^6) = 60\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 2 — RMS Pressure from Intensity:**

$$I = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{\rho c} \quad\Rightarrow\quad p_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{I \cdot \rho c}$$

$$p_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{10^{-6} \times 413} = \sqrt{4.13 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.0203\ \text{Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Verification via SPL formula:**

$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.0203}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(1016) \approx 60.14\ \text{dB}\ \checkmark$$

> This confirms that in a free field, $L_p = L_I$.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the inverse square law for sound propagation. A speaker produces 86 dB SPL at 1 m. What is the SPL at 5 m and at 20 m?**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept:** For a point source radiating uniformly into free space, the acoustic power is spread over the surface of an expanding sphere. As radius $r$ increases, intensity falls as $1/r^2$:

$$I(r) = \frac{W}{4\pi r^2}$$

**Step 2 — In Decibels:**

$$L_p(r) = L_p(r_0) - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r}{r_0}\right)$$

**Step 3 — At 5 m:**

$$L_p(5) = 86 - 20 \log_{10}(5) = 86 - 20(0.699) = 86 - 13.98 = 72.02\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 4 — At 20 m:**

$$L_p(20) = 86 - 20 \log_{10}(20) = 86 - 20(1.301) = 86 - 26.02 = 59.98\ \text{dB} \approx 60\ \text{dB}$$

> **HMI Implication:** A driver 20 m from a roadside alarm (e.g., emergency vehicle) hears it at ~60 dB — quieter than in-car music at 80 dB. This affects emergency-vehicle HMIs significantly.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the significance of psychoacoustics in HMI alarm design. Why is the 2–4 kHz band preferred for alarm tones?**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Psychoacoustics Definition:** It is the study of how the human brain interprets sound, including pitch perception, loudness curves, masking, and the precedence effect.

**Step 2 — Fletcher-Munson Curves:** The human ear is most sensitive in the 2–5 kHz range. A tone at 3 kHz at 40 dB sounds as loud as a 100 Hz tone at 70 dB.

**Step 3 — Why 2–4 kHz for alarms:**
- Maximum ear sensitivity → perceived as urgent even at low SPL
- Less masking by low-frequency machinery noise
- Falls in the band critical for speech consonant perception, so alarms remain intelligible alongside voice prompts

**Step 4 — Design Recommendations:**
- Use **modulated tones** (warbling, pulsed) at 2.5–3.5 kHz for warning
- Use **intermittent bursts** for advisory, **continuous** for critical (IEC 60601-1-8 standard)
- Maintain at least **10 dB above ambient noise level** at the operator's ear

> **Example:** Hospital cardiac alarm — three short pulses at 2.5 kHz, then pause — universally recognized as "critical" because of this acoustic design.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** The most common mistakes students make in acoustics problems are:
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 20** (not 10) in SPL formulas — using $10\log$ instead of $20\log$.
> 2. **Mixing up** $p_0 = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$ with $I_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W/m}^2$ — they are related but **not numerically equivalent** in dB across all media.
> 3. **Ignoring the reference conditions** for $c$ — using 330 m/s instead of 343 m/s, leading to ~4% error in $\lambda$.
> 4. **Assuming spherical spreading indoors** — reflections from walls violate the inverse square law.
> 5. **Confusing period $T$ with frequency $f$** — remember $T = 1/f$ and $T$ is in seconds.
> 6. **Writing the wave equation in 3-D** when the problem only requires 1-D — KTU board questions specify the dimension.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember

- **Sound = longitudinal pressure wave** in an elastic medium. No medium → no sound.
- **Speed of sound in air at 20 °C = 343 m/s** (memorize this).
- **Master equation:** $c = f\lambda$. From this, every other relationship follows.
- **SPL formula:** $L_p = 20\log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}}/p_0)$ with $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$.
- **SIL formula:** $L_I = 10\log_{10}(I/I_0)$ with $I_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W/m}^2$.
- **Acoustic impedance** $Z = \rho c$ governs reflection and transmission at boundaries.
- **Inverse square law** for point sources: $L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 20\log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$.
- **HMI alarm design** uses 2–4 kHz for maximum psychoacoustic impact at low SPL.
- **Threshold of hearing** is 0 dB SPL; **threshold of pain** is ~120–140 dB SPL.
- **3 dB ≈ 2× intensity**, **10 dB ≈ 2× perceived loudness**, **20 dB = 100× intensity**.
- **Speed of sound in ideal gas:** $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T / M}$ — depends on temperature.
- **Plane wave relation:** Intensity $I = p_{\text{rms}}^2/(\rho c)$.
- **Longitudinal waves** involve particle oscillation *parallel* to propagation direction.
- **HMI applications** of acoustics: alarms, voice prompts, audio feedback, speech recognition, sonification, gesture sensing via ultrasonics.

---

> **End of Module 4 — Basics of Acoustics | OEBRT831 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Psychoacoustics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT831 - HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Psychoacoustics # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Psychoacoustics** is the scientific study of the psychological and physiological responses associated with sound. It is a branch of psychophysics that investigates how the human auditory system perceives, interprets, and responds to acoustic stimuli. The discipline bridges the gap between the physical properties of sound waves (measurable quantities such as frequency, amplitude, and spectrum) and the subjective perceptual experience of the listener (qualities such as loudness, pitch, timbre, and spatial location). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (OEBRT831 - Module 4):** > Psychoacoustics is defined as the study of the relationship between the physical characteristics of sound waves and their subjective perception by the human auditory system. It encompasses the study of sound perception attributes (loudness, pitch, timbre), auditory thresholds, masking phenomena, and the equal-loudness contours that govern human hearing. ### 1.1.1 The Three Pillars of Psychoacoustics The discipline is built upon three fundamental pillars that engineers and designers must understand: 1. **Physical Acoustics (Stimulus Domain):** Deals with the measurable, objective properties of sound such as Sound Pressure Level (SPL), frequency, and wavelength. 2. **Physiological Acoustics (Receptor Domain):** Concerns the mechanical and neural functioning of the outer, middle, and inner ear, including the cochlea and auditory nerve pathways. 3. **Psychological Acoustics (Perceptual Domain):** Studies the subjective experience of sound, including loudness perception, pitch discrimination, auditory masking, and spatial hearing. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The "Audio-Mail" System** > > Imagine your ear as a sophisticated postal system: > - **The Sound Wave (The Letter):** A physical pressure wave travelling through the air, carrying energy and information. Just like a letter, it has measurable properties—size (amplitude) and handwriting style (frequency). > - **The Ear (The Post Office):** The outer and middle ear receive the letter (sound wave), the cochlea translates it into a neural "telegram" (electrical signals sent via the auditory nerve). > - **The Brain (The Recipient):** Receives the telegram and interprets it as a meaningful experience—perhaps a soft whisper, a high-pitched violin, or the roar of a jet engine. > > **Psychoacoustics** is the science of studying how that "letter" gets translated by the "post office" and then *understood* by the "recipient." It tells us why the *same* physical sound can feel "softer" to one person and "louder" to another, or why a whisper can become completely inaudible when a truck passes by (a phenomenon called *masking*). ### 1.3 Foundational Acoustic Quantities | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Metric Unit | Description | |-----------|--------|----------------------|-------------| | Sound Pressure | $p$ | Pascal ($\text{Pa}$) | Root-mean-square deviation of atmospheric pressure caused by the sound wave | | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p$ or SPL | Decibel ($\text{dB}$) | Logarithmic measure of sound pressure relative to a reference of **20 µPa** | | Frequency | $f$ | Hertz ($\text{Hz}$) | Number of pressure oscillations per second | | Intensity | $I$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ | Acoustic power per unit area | | Wavelength | $\lambda$ | Meter ($\text{m}$) | Spatial period of the wave | > [!TIP] > **Critical Reference Value to Memorise:** > The **threshold of human hearing** is defined at $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ (20 micro-Pascals) at $f = 1 \, \text{kHz}$. This is the **standard reference pressure** for all SPL calculations and is one of the most-tested values in KTU examinations. ### 1.4 The Human Auditory Range The human ear is an extraordinarily sensitive biological instrument, but it has clearly defined operational boundaries: - **Frequency Range:** Approximately **20 Hz to 20,000 Hz (20 kHz)** for young, healthy adults. This range decreases with age—a condition called **presbycusis**. - **Dynamic Range:** From the **threshold of hearing** (≈ 0 dB SPL) to the **threshold of pain** (≈ 120–140 dB SPL). This represents an intensity ratio of approximately **$10^{12}$:1** (one trillion to one). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Sound Pressure Level (SPL) — The Foundation Sound is a logarithmic phenomenon because the human ear responds to a *ratio* of pressures, not absolute differences. This necessitates the use of the **decibel (dB)** scale. The Sound Pressure Level is defined as: $$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right) \, \text{dB}$$ where: - $L_p$ = Sound Pressure Level in decibels (dB) - $p_{\text{rms}}$ = Root-mean-square sound pressure in Pascals (Pa) - $p_0$ = Reference sound pressure = $20 \, \mu\text{Pa} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{Pa}$ ### 2.1.1 Why a Logarithmic Scale? **The Why:** The human ear can detect pressures ranging from $20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ to $20 \, \text{Pa}$ — a ratio of **1,000,000:1**. On a linear scale, this range is impossible to plot meaningfully. The logarithmic (dB) scale compresses this enormous range into a manageable 0–120 dB scale that aligns closely with human perception. **The How:** Every **+10 dB** increase represents a **10× increase** in acoustic power and a **3.16× increase** in sound pressure (since power is proportional to pressure squared). Every **+20 dB** represents a **10× increase** in sound pressure. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** > - **+3 dB** = doubling of sound **intensity** (power) > - **+6 dB** = doubling of sound **pressure** (amplitude) > - **+10 dB** = perceived as approximately **twice as loud** (this is the famous "S-magical" rule of human loudness perception) ## 2.2 Sound Intensity Level (SIL) When dealing with acoustic intensity (power per unit area), the formula uses a factor of 10 instead of 20: $$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) \, \text{dB}$$ where: - $L_I$ = Sound Intensity Level in dB - $I$ = Acoustic intensity in $\text{W/m}^2$ - $I_0$ = Reference intensity = $10^{-12} \, \text{W/m}^2$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why 20 vs 10?** Since acoustic intensity is proportional to the *square* of sound pressure ($I \propto p^2$), the logarithm of a squared quantity introduces a factor of 2. This factor of 2 is moved to the front: $10 \log_{10}(p^2/p_0^2) = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$. ## 2.3 Pitch and Frequency **Pitch** is the subjective perception of frequency. While frequency is a physical measurement (Hz), pitch is a psychological experience. The relationship is **non-linear**: - **Doubling frequency** (e.g., 100 Hz → 200 Hz) is perceived as **one octave higher**, not twice as high. - Pitch perception is **logarithmic** with respect to frequency, which is why musical scales use ratios (e.g., a perfect fifth = 3:2 ratio). The relationship between pitch sensation and frequency can be expressed using the **mel scale** (a psychoacoustic scale of perceived pitch): $$m = 1127.01048 \cdot \ln\left(1 + \frac{f}{700}\right)$$ where $m$ is the pitch in *mels* and $f$ is the frequency in Hz. A $1 \, \text{kHz}$ tone at **40 dB SPL** is defined as 1000 mels by convention. ## 2.4 Loudness — The Critical Concept **Loudness** is the subjective intensity of a sound. It depends on: 1. The Sound Pressure Level (SPL) 2. The frequency of the sound 3. The duration of the sound 4. The context (background noise) ### 2.4.1 Loudness Level (Phon) The **phon** scale normalizes loudness perception to a $1 \, \text{kHz}$ reference tone: $$L_N \, (\text{phon}) = L_p \, \text{at} \, 1 \, \text{kHz} \, \text{judged equally loud}$$ By definition, the **phon** value at $1 \, \text{kHz}$ is numerically equal to the SPL in dB. At other frequencies, the phon and dB values **diverge** significantly. ### 2.4.2 Loudness (Sone) The **sone** scale provides a *linear* measure of perceived loudness: $$N = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$$ where: - $N$ = Loudness in sones - $L_N$ = Loudness level in phons > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** A sound judged as **twice as loud** corresponds to a sone value that is **twice** as large (e.g., 2 sones = "twice as loud" as 1 sone). A **10-phon increase** corresponds to a **doubling of loudness in sones** — this is the foundation of the "10 dB = 2× louder" rule of thumb. ## 2.5 Equal-Loudness Contours (Fletcher-Munson Curves) The **Fletcher-Munson curves**, formally known as **equal-loudness contours**, are among the most important graphical tools in psychoacoustics. They plot the SPL required at different frequencies to produce the *same perceived loudness*. > [!NOTE] > **Key Observations from Equal-Loudness Contours:** > 1. The human ear is **most sensitive** at frequencies between **2 kHz and 5 kHz** (corresponding to the resonant frequency of the ear canal). > 2. Low frequencies (below 500 Hz) require significantly **higher SPL** to be perceived as equally loud. > 3. High frequencies (above 10 kHz) also require higher SPL. > 4. The contours flatten at higher loudness levels (> 80 phons) — meaning the ear becomes more "flat" in its response at loud volumes. > 5. **A-weighting** in sound level meters approximates the 40-phon contour, correcting low-frequency energy to match human perception. ## 2.6 Auditory Masking **Masking** occurs when the perception of one sound is affected by the presence of another sound. This is a critical phenomenon in audio engineering, MP3 compression (perceptual audio coding), and hearing aid design. - **Simultaneous Masking (Frequency Masking):** A loud sound at one frequency raises the threshold of hearing for nearby frequencies. The masked frequency becomes inaudible. - **Temporal Masking:** A loud sound can mask quieter sounds that occur *just before* (pre-masking, ~5–20 ms) or *just after* (post-masking, up to 200 ms) it. ## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}}/p_0)$ | Sound Pressure Level | dB | | 2 | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$ | Sound Intensity Level | dB | | 3 | $I = p_{\text{rms}}^2 / (\rho c)$ | Acoustic Intensity | $\text{W/m}^2$ | | 4 | $L_N (\text{phon}) = L_p @ 1\text{kHz}$ | Loudness Level | phon | | 5 | $N = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$ | Loudness (Sones) | sone | | 6 | $m = 1127.01 \cdot \ln(1 + f/700)$ | Pitch in Mels | mel | | 7 | $L_w = 10 \log_{10}(W/W_0)$ | Sound Power Level | dB | | 8 | $f_{\text{critical}} = 25 + 75 \cdot [1 + 1.4(f/1000)^2]^{0.69}$ | Critical Bandwidth (Hz) | Hz | | 9 | $L_{\text{dBA}} = L_p + A(f)$ | A-weighted SPL | dBA | | 10 | $\text{Octave bandwidth} = f_u - f_l$ | Octave band calculation | Hz | **Key Reference Values (MEMORISE):** - $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ (Reference Sound Pressure) - $I_0 = 10^{-12} \, \text{W/m}^2$ (Reference Intensity) - $W_0 = 10^{-12} \, \text{W}$ (Reference Power) - Threshold of Hearing ≈ **0 dB SPL** at 1 kHz - Threshold of Pain ≈ **120–140 dB SPL** - Conversational Speech ≈ **60 dB SPL** at 1 m - Jet Engine (close) ≈ **140 dB SPL** > [!TIP] > **Engineering Application:** These formulas are foundational in designing audio systems (equalizers, compressors), noise-cancelling headphones, hearing aids, MP3 codecs (MP3 exploits simultaneous masking to discard inaudible data), and architectural acoustics (concert hall design, soundproofing). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Sound Pressure to Decibels ### 3.1.1 The Starting Point — Why a Logarithmic Scale? Consider two sound pressures: $p_1 = 0.00002 \, \text{Pa}$ (whisper) and $p_2 = 20 \, \text{Pa}$ (jet engine). The ratio $p_2/p_1 = 1,000,000$. To map this enormous ratio to a perceptual scale, we use the **Bel**, defined as $\log_{10}(\text{ratio})$: $$\text{Bels} = \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right) = \log_{10}(1{,}000{,}000) = 6 \, \text{Bels}$$ The **decibel (dB)** is one-tenth of a Bel, giving us $6 \times 10 = 60 \, \text{dB}$. This is the core logic behind the dB scale. ### 3.1.2 Derivation of the SPL Formula **Step 1:** Define the ratio of the measured pressure to the reference pressure: $$R = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the base-10 logarithm (since we use Bels): $$\log_{10}(R) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right)$$ **Step 3:** Convert Bels to decibels by multiplying by 10. However, since pressure is squared when computing intensity, we use 20: $$L_p = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right) \, \text{dB}$$ This is the **standard SPL formula** used universally in acoustics. ### 3.1.3 Worked Example — Calculating SPL of a Sound **Problem:** A vacuum cleaner produces a sound pressure of $p_{\text{rms}} = 0.02 \, \text{Pa}$. Calculate its Sound Pressure Level. **Step 1:** Identify the given values. $$p_{\text{rms}} = 0.02 \, \text{Pa}, \quad p_0 = 20 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{Pa}$$ **Step 2:** Compute the pressure ratio. $$\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0} = \frac{0.02}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.02}{0.00002} = 1000$$ **Step 3:** Apply the SPL formula. $$L_p = 20 \cdot \log_{10}(1000) = 20 \times 3 = 60 \, \text{dB SPL}$$ **Conclusion:** A vacuum cleaner typically operates at around **60 dB SPL**, which matches published noise data. This calculation demonstrates the practical use of the dB formula. ## 3.2 Derivation: The Sone Scale from Phons ### 3.2.1 Establishing the Reference Point By definition, a $1 \, \text{kHz}$ tone at **40 dB SPL** has a loudness of exactly **1 sone** and a loudness level of exactly **40 phons**. This is the **bipolar anchor** of the sone scale. ### 3.2.2 The Doubling Rule Psychophysical experiments (Stevens, 1956) established that a sound judged to be **twice as loud** corresponds to a **+10 phon** increase. Mathematically, every 10-phon increase doubles the sone value: $$N = N_0 \cdot 2^{\Delta L_N / 10}$$ where $\Delta L_N$ is the change in loudness level in phons. ### 3.2.3 Deriving the Closed-Form Equation **Step 1:** Let the reference loudness be $N_0 = 1$ sone at $L_N = 40$ phons. **Step 2:** Substitute into the doubling relation: $$N = 1 \cdot 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$$ **Step 3:** This gives the standard form: $$N = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$$ **Verification at reference point:** When $L_N = 40$ phons, $N = 2^0 = 1$ sone ✓ **Verification of doubling rule:** When $L_N = 50$ phons, $N = 2^1 = 2$ sones (twice as loud) ✓ ### 3.2.4 Worked Example — Converting Phons to Sones **Problem:** A sound has a loudness level of 70 phons. What is its loudness in sones? **Step 1:** Apply the sone formula directly. $$N = 2^{(70 - 40)/10} = 2^{30/10} = 2^3 = 8 \, \text{sones}$$ **Step 2:** Interpret the result. A 70-phon sound is perceived as **8 times louder** than the 1-sone reference tone. ## 3.3 Derivation: Pitch in Mels from Frequency ### 3.3.1 The Reference Point A $1 \, \text{kHz}$ tone at 40 dB SPL is defined as 1000 mels. This anchors the mel scale. ### 3.3.2 Anchoring the Equation We require $m = 1000$ when $f = 1000 \, \text{Hz}$. The general logarithmic form is: $$m = a \cdot \ln\left(1 + \frac{f}{b}\right)$$ **Step 1:** Apply the anchor condition $m(1000) = 1000$: $$1000 = a \cdot \ln\left(1 + \frac{1000}{b}\right)$$ **Step 2:** The standard solution (Stevens & Volkman, 1940) uses $b = 700$ Hz: $$1000 = a \cdot \ln\left(1 + \frac{1000}{700}\right) = a \cdot \ln(2.4286) \approx a \cdot 0.8629$$ **Step 3:** Solve for the constant: $$a = \frac{1000}{0.8629} \approx 1127.01$$ **Step 4:** This yields the **standard mel formula**: $$m = 1127.01 \cdot \ln\left(1 + \frac{f}{700}\right)$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Scales | Scale | Domain | Type | Reference Point | Range | |-------|--------|------|-----------------|-------| | **dB SPL** | Physical (pressure) | Logarithmic | $20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ | 0 to 140 dB | | **Phon** | Perceptual (loudness level) | Logarithmic | Equal to dB SPL at 1 kHz | 0 to 130 phons | | **Sone** | Perceptual (loudness) | Linear (in doubling) | 1 sone = 40 phons @ 1 kHz | 0.01 to ~256 sones | | **Mel** | Perceptual (pitch) | Logarithmic | 1000 mel = 1 kHz | 0 to ~3400 mel | | **Hz** | Physical (frequency) | Linear | — | 20 to 20,000 Hz | ## 3.5 Derivation: Combining Multiple Sound Sources ### 3.5.1 The Problem When two or more sound sources operate simultaneously, their dB levels do **not** add linearly. They add in the *power domain* (intensity), then convert back to dB. ### 3.5.2 Step-by-Step Procedure **Step 1:** Convert each dB value to its linear pressure-squared ratio. For source $i$: $\frac{p_i^2}{p_0^2} = 10^{L_{p,i}/10}$ **Step 2:** Sum the linear power ratios (incoherent sources): $$\sum \frac{p_i^2}{p_0^2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} 10^{L_{p,i}/10}$$ **Step 3:** Convert the sum back to dB: $$L_{p,\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} 10^{L_{p,i}/10}\right)$$ ### 3.5.3 Worked Example — Two Identical Machines **Problem:** Two machines each produce 70 dB SPL. What is the combined SPL? **Step 1:** Each source contributes in linear units: $$10^{70/10} = 10^7$$ **Step 2:** Two sources sum to: $$\sum = 10^7 + 10^7 = 2 \times 10^7$$ **Step 3:** Convert back to dB: $$L_{p,\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}(2 \times 10^7) = 10 \left[\log_{10}(2) + 7\right] = 10(0.301 + 7) = 73.01 \, \text{dB}$$ **Conclusion:** **Doubling the number of identical sound sources increases the SPL by only 3 dB.** This is a critical insight for noise control engineering. ## 3.6 Derivation: Equal-Loudness Contour — 40 Phon The 40-phon contour defines the SPL required at each frequency to be perceived as equal in loudness to a $1 \, \text{kHz}$ tone at 40 dB SPL. The contour is constructed empirically by: 1. Play a $1 \, \text{kHz}$ tone at 40 dB SPL (reference). 2. Present a test tone at a different frequency. 3. Adjust the test tone's SPL until the listener judges it equally loud. 4. Record the SPL value at that frequency. 5. Repeat across all frequencies from 20 Hz to 20 kHz. 6. Plot the SPL values → this is the 40-phon contour. This procedure is repeated for loudness levels of 0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, and 120 phons to produce the full family of **Fletcher-Munson curves**. > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** In KTU examinations, you are frequently asked to interpret the Fletcher-Munson curves. Memorize the shape: a U-shaped curve with maximum sensitivity (lowest SPL required) at 2–5 kHz, and rising curves at both low and high frequencies. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Psychoacoustic Processing Chain This diagram illustrates the complete flow from a physical sound wave to the listener's perception, showing the transformation stages within the human auditory system. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Sound Source
Physical Stimulus] --> B[Outer Ear
Pinna and Ear Canal
Collects and Channels Sound] B --> C[Middle Ear
Tympanic Membrane and Ossicles
Impedance Matching] C --> D[Inner Ear
Cochlea
Frequency Analysis via Basilar Membrane] D --> E[Auditory Nerve
Neural Transduction
Spike Train Encoding] E --> F[Brainstem and Auditory Cortex
Central Processing] F --> G[Perceptual Output
Loudness Pitch Timbre Localization] A2[Amplitude Frequency Spectrum] --> A D2[Place Theory of Hearing
Tonotopic Mapping] --> D F2[Masking Temporal Integration
Auditory Memory] --> F G2[Subjective Experience
Phon Sone Mel Scales] --> G ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Equal-Loudness Contour Construction Process This diagram illustrates the experimental methodology used to construct Fletcher-Munson curves. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Set Reference Tone
1 kHz at 40 dB SPL] --> B[Listener Adjusts Test Tone
At Different Frequency] B --> C{Is Test Tone
Equally Loud?} C -->|No| D[Listener Modifies SPL
Up or Down] D --> C C -->|Yes| E[Record SPL Value
at this Frequency] E --> F[Move to Next Frequency
20 Hz to 20 kHz] F --> G{All Frequencies
Tested?} G -->|No| B G -->|Yes| H[Plot SPL vs Frequency
40 Phon Contour] H --> I[Repeat for 0 20 60 80 100 120 Phon] I --> J[Complete Fletcher-Munson
Equal Loudness Contour Family] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Auditory Masking Mechanism This diagram shows how a loud sound (masker) suppresses the perception of a quieter sound (maskee) at a nearby frequency. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Masker Sound
Loud Tone at f1] --> B[Raises Auditory Threshold
in Critical Band] C[Maskee Sound
Quiet Tone at f2] --> D{Is f2 within
Critical Band of f1?} D -->|No| E[Maskee Remains Audible
No Masking Effect] D -->|Yes| F[Maskee Falls Below
New Elevated Threshold] F --> G[Maskee Becomes
Inaudible] B --> D H[Temporal Masking
Post Masking up to 200 ms] --> G I[Frequency Masking
Simultaneous Masking] --> B ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram: Human Ear Anatomy and Functional Block Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TD A[External Acoustic Field
Sound Pressure Wave] --> B[Outer Ear
Pinna and Auditory Canal
Gain about 10 to 15 dB at 2 to 5 kHz] B --> C[Tympanic Membrane
Ear Drum
Vibrates in Response to Pressure] C --> D[Ossicles
Malleus Incus Stapes
Mechanical Amplification] D --> E[Oval Window
Entry to Cochlea
Impedance Matching] E --> F[Cochlea
Fluid Filled Spiral
Basilar Membrane Vibration] F --> G[Organ of Corti
Hair Cell Transduction
Mechanical to Electrical] G --> H[Auditory Nerve
VIII Cranial Nerve
Spike Train to Brain] H --> I[Brain
Auditory Cortex
Perception and Interpretation] ``` ## 4.5 Schematic Table: Comparison of Auditory Phenomena ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Physical Domain] --> B[Physiological Domain] --> C[Psychological Domain] A1[Frequency Hz] --> B1[Basilar Membrane Place] --> C1[Pitch Mel] A2[SPL dB] --> B2[Neural Firing Rate] --> C2[Loudness Sone] A3[Spectrum Harmonics] --> B3[Cochlear Filtering] --> C3[Timbre] A4[Interaural Time Difference] --> B4[Binaural Neural Processing] --> C4[Spatial Localization] A5[Duration Seconds] --> B5[Temporal Integration Window] --> C5[Perceived Duration] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Psychoacoustics. List any four subjective attributes of sound perception. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Psychoacoustics is the scientific study of the psychological and physiological responses associated with sound. It examines the relationship between the physical properties of acoustic stimuli (such as frequency, intensity, and spectrum) and their subjective perception by the human auditory system. **Four Subjective Attributes (1.5 Marks):** 1. **Loudness** — The perceived intensity of a sound, measured in sones or phons. 2. **Pitch** — The perceived frequency of a sound, measured in mels. 3. **Timbre** — The quality or "colour" of a sound that distinguishes different instruments playing the same note. 4. **Spatial Localization** — The ability to identify the direction and distance of a sound source. --- ### Question 2: What is the threshold of human hearing? Define the decibel and explain why a logarithmic scale is used for measuring sound. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Threshold of Hearing (1 Mark):** The threshold of human hearing is the minimum Sound Pressure Level (SPL) that the average human ear can detect, defined as $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ (20 micro-Pascals) at a frequency of $1 \, \text{kHz}$. This corresponds to **0 dB SPL**. **Definition of Decibel (1 Mark):** The decibel (dB) is a logarithmic unit that expresses the ratio of two sound pressures. The Sound Pressure Level is defined as $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}}/p_0) \, \text{dB}$. **Why Logarithmic? (1 Mark):** The human ear responds to sound pressures ranging from $20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ to $20 \, \text{Pa}$ — a ratio of **1,000,000:1**. A logarithmic scale compresses this enormous range into a manageable 0–140 dB scale that more closely matches human perception. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q. (a)** Define Sound Pressure Level (SPL). A jackhammer produces a sound pressure of 2 Pa at a distance of 5 metres. Calculate its SPL in dB. **[7 Marks]** **Q. (b)** Explain the concept of equal-loudness contours (Fletcher-Munson curves) with a neat sketch. Discuss their significance in audio engineering and noise measurement. **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution for Q. (a) — 7 Marks **Definition (2 Marks):** Sound Pressure Level (SPL) is the logarithmic measure of the RMS sound pressure of a sound wave, expressed in decibels (dB), relative to a reference pressure of $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$: $$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right) \, \text{dB}$$ **Given Data (1 Mark):** - $p_{\text{rms}} = 2 \, \text{Pa}$ - $p_0 = 20 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{Pa}$ **Step 1: Calculate the pressure ratio (1 Mark)** $$\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0} = \frac{2}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 10^5 = 100{,}000$$ **Step 2: Apply the SPL formula (1 Mark)** $$L_p = 20 \log_{10}(10^5) = 20 \times 5 = 100 \, \text{dB SPL}$$ **Step 3: Interpret the result (2 Marks):** A jackhammer at 5 m distance produces **100 dB SPL**, which is well above the safe occupational exposure limit of 85 dB (8-hour average) set by OSHA. Prolonged exposure can cause **noise-induced hearing loss (NIHL)**. --- #### Model Solution for Q. (b) — 7 Marks **Definition (2 Marks):** Equal-loudness contours, also called **Fletcher-Munson curves** (after the pioneering researchers Harvey Fletcher and W.A. Munson, 1933), are a family of curves that plot the Sound Pressure Level (dB SPL) required at different frequencies to produce the *same perceived loudness*. **Construction (1 Mark):** The contours are constructed experimentally: 1. A reference tone of $1 \, \text{kHz}$ is presented at a fixed SPL (e.g., 40 dB). 2. Test tones at other frequencies are adjusted in SPL until they are judged by listeners to be equally loud. 3. The SPL values are plotted against frequency on a log-frequency axis. 4. The procedure is repeated for multiple loudness levels (0, 20, 40, ..., 120 phons). **Key Observations from the Curves (2 Marks):** 1. The human ear is **most sensitive** at frequencies between **2 kHz and 5 kHz**, which corresponds to the resonant frequency of the ear canal. 2. Low frequencies (below 500 Hz) require **significantly higher SPL** to be perceived as equally loud — the 100-Hz tone must be about 30 dB louder than a 1-kHz tone to sound equally loud. 3. The contours **flatten at high loudness levels** (> 80 phons) — at very loud volumes, the ear's response becomes more uniform across frequencies. **Significance in Audio Engineering (2 Marks):** 1. **Equalizer Design:** Recording and mixing engineers use the curves to compensate for the ear's non-flat frequency response at low listening levels. 2. **A-Weighting Filter:** Sound level meters apply the A-weighting filter, which approximates the 40-phon contour, to correlate meter readings with human perception. 3. **Loudness Compensation:** Modern audio systems use "loudness" buttons that boost bass and treble at low volumes, mimicking the contours. --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q. (a)** Explain the concepts of Loudness Level (phon) and Loudness (sone). Derive the mathematical relationship between phons and sones. **[7 Marks]** **Q. (b)** A recording studio monitors audio at 85 dB SPL. Two speakers each output 85 dB SPL. Calculate the total SPL. Also explain the phenomenon of auditory masking with examples. **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution for Q. (a) — 7 Marks **Loudness Level — Phon (2 Marks):** The **phon** scale measures loudness level by comparing a sound to a $1 \, \text{kHz}$ reference tone. The phon value of any sound is numerically equal to the SPL (in dB) of a $1 \, \text{kHz}$ tone that is judged equally loud. By definition, the phon scale coincides with the dB scale at $1 \, \text{kHz}$. **Loudness — Sone (2 Marks):** The **sone** scale provides a linear measure of perceived loudness. By definition, 1 sone = 40 phons at 1 kHz. A 2-sone sound is perceived as **twice as loud** as a 1-sone sound. **Derivation of the Relationship (3 Marks):** Let the reference loudness be $N_0 = 1$ sone at $L_N = 40$ phons. The doubling rule states that every 10-phon increase doubles the loudness: $$N = N_0 \cdot 2^{\Delta L_N / 10} = 1 \cdot 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$$ $$\boxed{N = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}}$$ **Verification:** - At $L_N = 40$ phons: $N = 2^0 = 1$ sone ✓ - At $L_N = 50$ phons: $N = 2^1 = 2$ sones (twice as loud) ✓ - At $L_N = 60$ phons: $N = 2^2 = 4$ sones (four times as loud) ✓ --- #### Model Solution for Q. (b) — 7 Marks **Calculation of Total SPL (3 Marks):** **Given:** Two identical speakers each producing $L_{p,1} = L_{p,2} = 85 \, \text{dB SPL}$. **Step 1:** Convert each dB value to its linear power ratio. $$10^{85/10} = 10^{8.5} \approx 3.162 \times 10^8$$ **Step 2:** Sum the two linear ratios. $$\text{Total} = 3.162 \times 10^8 + 3.162 \times 10^8 = 6.324 \times 10^8$$ **Step 3:** Convert the sum back to dB. $$L_{p,\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}(6.324 \times 10^8) = 10(\log_{10}(6.324) + 8) = 10(0.801 + 8) = 88.0 \, \text{dB SPL}$$ **Conclusion:** **Doubling the number of identical sound sources increases SPL by exactly 3 dB.** This is a critical noise-control rule: to gain a 3 dB reduction, you must halve the number of sound sources. **Auditory Masking (4 Marks):** **Definition:** Auditory masking is the phenomenon in which the perception of one sound (the *maskee*) is reduced or eliminated by the presence of another sound (the *masker*). **Two Types:** 1. **Simultaneous (Frequency) Masking (2 Marks):** A loud tone at one frequency raises the hearing threshold for nearby frequencies. The new threshold is described by the **psychoacoustic tuning curve**. For example, a 1-kHz tone at 80 dB can completely mask a 1.1-kHz tone at 40 dB. **Engineering application:** MP3 and AAC codecs use psychoacoustic models to discard audio data that would be masked, achieving 10:1 compression with no perceived loss of quality. 2. **Temporal Masking (2 Marks):** A loud sound can mask quieter sounds that occur shortly before (*pre-masking*, ~5–20 ms) or shortly after (*post-masking*, up to 200 ms). For example, a hand clap can mask the sound of a finger snap that occurs within 100 ms after it. **Engineering application:** Perceptual audio coders exploit post-masking to compress silent intervals immediately following loud transients. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Reference Pressure Error:** Students often confuse $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ (sound pressure reference) with $I_0 = 10^{-12} \, \text{W/m}^2$ (intensity reference). Memorise both distinctly. > > 2. **Factor 20 vs 10:** Using $10 \log$ for SPL (correct is $20 \log$ for pressure; $10 \log$ is for intensity/power). Forgetting the factor of 2 from $p^2$ costs full marks. > > 3. **dB Addition Error:** A common mistake is adding dB values arithmetically (e.g., $60 + 60 = 120$ dB). The correct method is to convert to linear, sum, and convert back. Two 60-dB sources combine to **63 dB**, not 120 dB. > > 4. **Pitch vs Frequency Confusion:** Pitch is *perceived* frequency, measured in mels. Frequency is the *physical* stimulus, measured in Hz. They are not interchangeable. > > 5. **Sone vs Phon:** Phon is a logarithmic scale (like dB). Sone is a linear scale (in doubling). Students often mix up the equations. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Psychoacoustics (Module 4)** > > - **Definition:** Psychoacoustics = Psychology + Acoustics; the study of how humans *perceive* sound. > - **Reference Pressure:** $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ at 1 kHz → **0 dB SPL**. > - **Reference Intensity:** $I_0 = 10^{-12} \, \text{W/m}^2$. > - **SPL Formula:** $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}}/p_0)$ dB. > - **Intensity Formula:** $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$ dB. > - **+3 dB** = Doubling of sound intensity (power). > - **+6 dB** = Doubling of sound pressure (amplitude). > - **+10 dB** = Perceived as approximately **twice as loud** (Stevens' power law). > - **Human Hearing Range:** **20 Hz to 20 kHz**; **0 to 140 dB SPL**. > - **Threshold of Pain:** **120–140 dB SPL**. > - **Phon:** Loudness level — equals dB SPL at 1 kHz by definition. > - **Sone:** Linear loudness — $N = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$; 1 sone = 40 phons. > - **Mel:** Perceived pitch — $m = 1127.01 \ln(1 + f/700)$; 1000 mel = 1 kHz. > - **Fletcher-Munson Curves:** Equal-loudness contours; ear is most sensitive at **2–5 kHz**. > - **A-Weighting:** Approximates the 40-phon contour for noise measurements. > - **Masking:** Loud sound raises hearing threshold for nearby frequencies/times. > - **Critical Band:** Frequency range over which masking is effective. > - **Two Identical Sources:** Add **3 dB** (e.g., 70 dB + 70 dB = **73 dB**). > - **Engineering Applications:** Audio codecs (MP3, AAC), hearing aids, noise-cancelling headphones, concert hall design, sound level meters.

topics.title: Analysis and Synthesis of Sound
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT831 - HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analysis and Synthesis of Sound

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Sound Analysis** is the systematic decomposition of a complex acoustic signal into its constituent elementary components (sinusoids, transients, noise) to extract meaningful information about its frequency content, temporal evolution, and perceptual characteristics. Mathematically, it is the process of mapping a time-domain waveform $x(t)$ into a frequency-domain representation $X(\omega)$ using transforms such as the **Fourier Transform**.

**Sound Synthesis** is the inverse process of artificially generating a sound signal by combining elementary waveforms, mathematical models, or physical-acoustic rules to produce a desired acoustic output. It is the process of constructing $x(t)$ from a specification of parameters, models, or spectral descriptions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> In the context of Human-Machine Interaction (HMI), analysis enables machines to *interpret* acoustic input (e.g., speech recognition, environmental sonification), while synthesis enables machines to *produce* meaningful auditory feedback (e.g., alerts, earcons, auditory icons, speech output).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a glass of seawater. **Analysis** is like pouring the water through a fine chemical filter to discover *what* salts, minerals, and impurities it contains and *how much* of each. **Synthesis** is like taking purified table salt, magnesium, and water, and mixing them in a chemistry lab to *recreate* a chemically similar glass of seawater.

- **Analysis** = "What is in this sound?" (decomposition, reverse engineering)
- **Synthesis** = "How do I build this sound from scratch?" (composition, engineering)

In HMI, think of your smartphone's voice assistant: it **analyses** your voice to understand the command, and the OS **synthesizes** a reply voice. The spectrum analyzer in audio software is a real-time analysis tool; the synthesizer keyboard is a real-time synthesis tool.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Quantity | Symbol | Unit | Notes |
|----------|--------|------|-------|
| Frequency | $f$ | **Hertz (Hz)** | Human hearing range: **20 Hz – 20,000 Hz** |
| Angular Frequency | $\omega$ | rad/s | $\omega = 2\pi f$ |
| Period | $T$ | seconds | $T = 1/f$ |
| Wavelength | $\lambda$ | meters | $\lambda = c/f$, where $c \approx 343$ m/s (speed of sound in air at 20°C) |
| Sound Pressure Level | SPL | **decibels (dB)** | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_{ref})$, $p_{ref} = 20\,\mu Pa$ |
| Intensity Level | $I$ | dB | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_{ref})$ |
| Sampling Rate | $f_s$ | Hz (samples/s) | CD quality: **44,100 Hz** |
| Bit Depth | $n$ | bits | CD quality: **16 bits** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference for KTU 2024:** Always assume standard atmospheric conditions ($T = 20°C$, $p = 101.325$ kPa) unless otherwise specified. The threshold of human hearing is **0 dB SPL** and the threshold of pain is approximately **120–130 dB SPL**.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Sound Signals

**Deterministic Signals** — can be described by an explicit mathematical function.
- **Periodic signals:** $x(t) = x(t + T)$ (musical notes, vowels, humming)
- **Aperiodic (non-periodic) signals:** $x(t) \neq x(t + T)$ for all $T$ (percussion hits, consonants, noise bursts)

**Random Signals** — cannot be described deterministically; described statistically.
- **Stationary noise:** statistical properties invariant with time (e.g., white noise, pink noise)
- **Non-stationary noise:** statistics change with time (e.g., crowd chatter, traffic noise)

In HMI, the vast majority of natural and machine sounds are **non-stationary**, which is why time-frequency analysis (Short-Time Fourier Transform) is essential.

## 1.5 Why Analysis and Synthesis Matter in HMI

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Relevance in HMI:**
> 1. **Auditory Displays:** Designing audio feedback for medical devices, aviation cockpits, vehicle infotainment.
> 2. **Sonification:** Converting scientific data (e.g., seismic, ECG) into sound for monitoring.
> 3. **Speech Synthesis (TTS):** Generating spoken output for screen readers and virtual assistants.
> 4. **Audio-Based Authentication:** Voice biometrics uses spectral analysis of voice.
> 5. **Earcon & Auditory Icon Design:** Non-speech audio cues synthesized to convey information.
> 6. **Active Noise Control (ANC):** Synthesizing anti-phase sound to cancel noise — requires real-time analysis.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Time-Domain vs Frequency-Domain Analysis

The same sound can be represented in two complementary domains:

| Domain | Representation | What it reveals | Tools |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|-------|
| **Time domain** | $x(t)$ — amplitude vs time | Envelope, transients, attack/decay, timing | Oscilloscope, waveform editor |
| **Frequency domain** | $X(\omega)$ — magnitude/phase vs frequency | Pitch, harmonic content, timbre | Spectrum analyzer, spectrogram |

The **Fourier Transform** is the bridge between these two representations.

## 2.2 The Fourier Transform Family

### 2.2.1 Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT)

For an integrable, continuous signal $x(t)$:

$$X(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$$

$$x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(\omega)\, e^{j\omega t}\, d\omega$$

The inverse CTFT reconstructs the time-domain signal from the frequency-domain representation.

### 2.2.2 Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)

For a discrete sequence $x[n]$:

$$X(e^{j\Omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\Omega n}$$

The DTFT is periodic with period $2\pi$ in $\Omega$ (normalized angular frequency).

### 2.2.3 Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)

For a finite sequence of $N$ samples:

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}, \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k]\, e^{j 2\pi k n / N}$$

The DFT is the computable, discrete version of the Fourier Transform used in all digital audio systems.

### 2.2.4 Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)

The FFT is **not** a different transform — it is an efficient algorithm to compute the DFT in $O(N \log_2 N)$ instead of $O(N^2)$ operations. The Cooley-Tukey algorithm (1965) is the most common form.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Point:** When asked for spectral analysis of digital audio, always mention **DFT/FFT** specifically. The DFT maps a sequence of $N$ samples to $N$ complex frequency bins; the frequency resolution is $\Delta f = f_s / N$.

## 2.3 Spectrum, Magnitude, and Phase

For each frequency bin $k$:
- **Magnitude spectrum:** $\vert X[k] \vert = \sqrt{\text{Re}(X[k])^2 + \text{Im}(X[k])^2}$
- **Phase spectrum:** $\angle X[k] = \arctan2(\text{Im}(X[k]), \text{Re}(X[k]))$
- **Power spectrum:** $P[k] = \frac{\vert X[k] \vert^2}{N}$

In HMI perception, the **magnitude spectrum** determines what we hear as pitch and timbre, while the phase spectrum affects transient perception and signal localization.

## 2.4 Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) and Spectrograms

Since most real sounds (speech, music, machine noise) are non-stationary, a single global FFT is insufficient. The **STFT** slides a window $w[n-m]$ across the signal:

$$X(m, k) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, w[n-m]\, e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}$$

The result is a 2D time-frequency map $X(m,k)$ called a **spectrogram** — a heat map of frequency content vs time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Heisenberg-Gabor Uncertainty Principle in audio:** A short window gives good time resolution but poor frequency resolution. A long window gives good frequency resolution but poor time resolution. This trade-off is fundamental in HMI audio design.

## 2.5 Power Spectral Density (PSD)

The PSD describes how the signal's power is distributed across frequency:

$$S_{xx}(f) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \mathbb{E}\left[\, \vert X_T(f) \vert^2 \,\right]$$

where $X_T(f)$ is the Fourier Transform of a finite-duration segment.

**Welch's Method** (a KTU-favorite topic) estimates PSD by averaging periodograms of overlapping windowed segments — reducing variance at the cost of frequency resolution.

## 2.6 Autocorrelation Function

For a real signal $x(t)$, the autocorrelation is:

$$R_{xx}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, x(t+\tau)\, dt$$

For periodic signals, $R_{xx}(\tau)$ is also periodic with the same period — this is how pitch detection algorithms work in HMI. The **Wiener-Khinchin theorem** links autocorrelation to PSD via Fourier Transform:

$$S_{xx}(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_{xx}(\tau)\}$$

## 2.7 Sound Synthesis Methods

### 2.7.1 Additive Synthesis (Fourier Synthesis)

Builds complex sound by summing sine waves (partials):

$$x(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} A_k \cos(2\pi k f_0 t + \phi_k)$$

- $A_k$ — amplitude of $k$-th partial (harmonic or inharmonic)
- $f_0$ — fundamental frequency
- $\phi_k$ — phase of $k$-th partial

**Advantages:** Physically accurate; ideal for sustained, harmonic tones (organ, flute, vowel).
**Disadvantages:** Requires many parameters; inefficient for evolving timbres.

### 2.7.2 Subtractive Synthesis

Starts with a harmonically rich waveform (sawtooth, square, pulse) and **filters** out unwanted frequencies:

$$Y(\omega) = X(\omega) \cdot H(\omega)$$

- $X(\omega)$ — spectrum of source waveform
- $H(\omega)$ — filter transfer function (low-pass, high-pass, band-pass)

**Classic subtractive voice:** Oscillator → Filter → Amplifier → Output (the "analog synthesizer" architecture).

### 2.7.3 Frequency Modulation (FM) Synthesis

A carrier oscillator of frequency $f_c$ is modulated by a modulator of frequency $f_m$:

$$x(t) = A_c \sin\!\left(2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\right)$$

where $\beta$ is the **modulation index**. The carrier spectrum contains sidebands at $f_c \pm n f_m$ with Bessel-function amplitudes $J_n(\beta)$.

**Advantages:** Rich, evolving timbres from just two oscillators; computationally cheap.
**Disadvantage:** Spectrum is mathematically complex; hard to predict by ear.

### 2.7.4 Amplitude Modulation (AM) Synthesis

$$x(t) = \bigl[1 + m \cos(2\pi f_m t)\bigr] \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

where $m$ is the modulation depth. Produces two sidebands at $f_c \pm f_m$ — useful for tremolo and ring-modulation effects.

### 2.7.5 Wavetable Synthesis

A pre-stored single cycle of a waveform (the "wavetable") is read cyclically. Wavetable position can be modulated for evolving timbres.

### 2.7.6 Granular Synthesis

Sound is built from thousands of short grains (typically 10–100 ms) of audio, each with envelope, pitch, and position. Pioneered by **Iannis Xenakis** and **Curtis Roads**. Excellent for textures and time-stretching in HMI.

### 2.7.7 Physical Modeling Synthesis

Simulates the physical laws of sound-producing objects:
- **Mass-spring models** for strings and membranes.
- **Digital waveguides** for strings, winds.
- **Modal synthesis** for resonating bodies.

Highly realistic but computationally intensive.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula | Domain | Units |
|---------|---------|--------|-------|
| Sampling Theorem | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Digital audio | Hz |
| Frequency resolution | $\Delta f = f_s / N$ | DFT | Hz |
| Period | $T = 1/f$ | Time | s |
| Wavelength | $\lambda = c/f$, $c \approx 343$ m/s | Acoustics | m |
| Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_{ref})$ | dB | dB |
| Intensity Level | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_{ref})$ | dB | dB |
| DFT | $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$ | Frequency | complex |
| IDFT | $x[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] e^{j2\pi kn/N}$ | Time | samples |
| Additive Synthesis | $x(t) = \sum_k A_k \cos(2\pi k f_0 t + \phi_k)$ | Synthesis | Pa |
| FM Synthesis | $x(t) = A_c \sin(2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin 2\pi f_m t)$ | Synthesis | Pa |
| AM Synthesis | $x(t) = [1 + m\cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Synthesis | Pa |
| Autocorrelation | $R_{xx}(\tau) = \int x(t) x(t+\tau) dt$ | Time | signal² |
| Wiener-Khinchin | $S_{xx}(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_{xx}(\tau)\}$ | Frequency | W/Hz |
| STFT | $X(m,k) = \sum_n x[n] w[n-m] e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$ | Time-Freq | complex |
| Sideband frequencies (FM) | $f_c \pm n f_m$, $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ | FM | Hz |
| Bessel sideband amplitude | $A_n = A_c J_n(\beta)$ | FM | Pa |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Recovering a Signal from its DFT

We will derive the **Inverse DFT** (IDFT) starting from the **forward DFT** to show the orthogonality of the complex exponentials.

**Given:** The forward DFT is defined as
$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}, \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

**Step 1:** Start with the right-hand side of the IDFT,
$$x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k]\, e^{j 2\pi k n / N}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the expression for $X[k]$ from the forward DFT into the IDFT formula:
$$x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \left[\sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m]\, e^{-j 2\pi k m / N}\right] e^{j 2\pi k n / N}$$

**Step 3:** Swap the order of summation (finite sums commute):
$$x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m] \left[\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j 2\pi k (n-m) / N}\right]$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the inner sum. Let $N_s = n - m$. This is a **geometric series**:
$$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j 2\pi k N_s / N} = \frac{1 - e^{j 2\pi N_s}}{1 - e^{j 2\pi N_s / N}}$$

**Step 5:** Since $N_s$ is an integer, $e^{j 2\pi N_s} = 1$, so the numerator is zero, *unless* the denominator is also zero.

**Step 6:** The denominator is zero when $e^{j 2\pi N_s / N} = 1$, i.e., when $N_s = n - m$ is a multiple of $N$. Since $0 \leq m, n \leq N-1$, this only happens when $m = n$ (with $N_s = 0$).

**Step 7:** In that single case ($m = n$), every term of the sum equals 1, so the sum equals $N$.

**Step 8:** Substitute back:
$$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j 2\pi k (n-m)/N} = N \cdot \delta_{nm}$$

where $\delta_{nm}$ is the Kronecker delta.

**Step 9:** Final result:
$$x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m] \cdot N \cdot \delta_{nm} = x[n] \quad \blacksquare$$

This confirms that the IDFT inverts the DFT exactly — establishing that analysis and synthesis are perfect mathematical inverses.

## 3.2 Derivation: Additive Synthesis of a Square Wave

A square wave is a classic example of Fourier synthesis in action. The Fourier series of a square wave of amplitude $A$ and frequency $f_0$ is:

$$x_{\text{square}}(t) = \frac{4A}{\pi} \sum_{k=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{k} \sin(2\pi k f_0 t)$$

**Step-by-step construction:**

**Step 1:** Fundamental only ($k=1$): $x_1(t) = \frac{4A}{\pi} \sin(2\pi f_0 t)$

This is a pure sine — not at all square.

**Step 2:** Add the 3rd harmonic ($k=3$, amplitude $1/3$):
$$x_3(t) = \frac{4A}{\pi}\left[\sin(2\pi f_0 t) + \frac{1}{3}\sin(2\pi \cdot 3 f_0 t)\right]$$

Shape begins to flatten at the peaks.

**Step 3:** Add 5th harmonic ($k=5$, amplitude $1/5$):
$$x_5(t) = \frac{4A}{\pi}\left[\sin(2\pi f_0 t) + \frac{1}{3}\sin(6\pi f_0 t) + \frac{1}{5}\sin(10\pi f_0 t)\right]$$

Sharpening of edges and overshoot (Gibbs phenomenon) appears.

**Step 4:** General partial-sum expression:
$$x_N(t) = \frac{4A}{\pi} \sum_{\substack{k=1 \\ k \text{ odd}}}^{2N-1} \frac{1}{k} \sin(2\pi k f_0 t)$$

**Step 5:** As $N \to \infty$, $x_N(t)$ converges to the ideal square wave except at the discontinuities, where it overshoots by ~9% — the **Gibbs phenomenon**, a key KTU concept.

This is a textbook example of how **synthesis** builds a recognizable timbre from a sum of pure tones.

## 3.3 Derivation: FM Synthesis Sideband Frequencies

**Given:** A single FM equation
$$x(t) = A_c \sin\!\left(2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\right)$$

**Step 1:** Use the Bessel function expansion identity:
$$e^{j\beta \sin\theta} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta)\, e^{j n \theta}$$

**Step 2:** Rewrite the FM signal in complex exponential form. First expand the sine:
$$x(t) = A_c \, \text{Im}\!\left\{ e^{j[2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)]} \right\}$$

**Step 3:** Factor the exponential:
$$x(t) = A_c \, \text{Im}\!\left\{ e^{j 2\pi f_c t} \cdot e^{j\beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)} \right\}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the Bessel identity with $\theta = 2\pi f_m t$:
$$x(t) = A_c \, \text{Im}\!\left\{ e^{j 2\pi f_c t} \cdot \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta)\, e^{j 2\pi n f_m t} \right\}$$

**Step 5:** Distribute and combine exponents:
$$x(t) = A_c \, \text{Im}\!\left\{ \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta)\, e^{j 2\pi (f_c + n f_m) t} \right\}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to real sines using $J_{-n}(\beta) = (-1)^n J_n(\beta)$:
$$x(t) = A_c \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) \sin\!\left(2\pi(f_c + n f_m) t\right)$$

**Step 7:** Symmetrize using $J_{-n} = (-1)^n J_n$:
$$x(t) = A_c \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left[ J_n(\beta)\, \sin(2\pi(f_c + n f_m) t) + (-1)^{n+1} J_n(\beta)\, \sin(2\pi(f_c - n f_m) t) \right]$$

**Conclusion:** FM synthesis generates sidebands at frequencies $f_c + n f_m$ (upper) and $f_c - n f_m$ (lower) for $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$, with amplitudes $A_c J_n(\beta)$. The **modulation index** $\beta$ controls the spectral richness:

| $\beta$ value | Spectral character | Approx. # of significant sidebands |
|---------------|-------------------|-----------------------------------|
| $\beta < 0.5$ | Nearly pure tone | 1–2 |
| $\beta \approx 1$ | Bell-like, mellow | 3 |
| $\beta \approx 3$ | Bright, brassy | 5–6 |
| $\beta > 5$ | Noisy, complex | $>8$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** When asked to compute the sideband structure of an FM tone, students often forget that $J_n(\beta)$ is non-zero for **all** integer $n$ (not just the first few). However, $|J_n(\beta)|$ decays as $n$ grows, so only a finite number of sidebands are "audible" (i.e., above the threshold of perception).

## 3.4 Worked Example: Sampling and DFT Frequency Resolution

**Problem:** A 4-second audio signal is sampled at $f_s = 16{,}000$ Hz. The DFT is computed on the entire signal.

**Step 1:** Number of samples:
$$N = f_s \times T = 16{,}000 \times 4 = 64{,}000 \text{ samples}$$

**Step 2:** Frequency resolution:
$$\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N} = \frac{16{,}000}{64{,}000} = 0.25 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3:** Maximum recoverable frequency (Nyquist):
$$f_{max} = \frac{f_s}{2} = 8{,}000 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4:** Frequency corresponding to bin $k$:
$$f_k = k \cdot \Delta f = 0.25 k \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 5:** To resolve a frequency of 440 Hz (musical A4), the bin index is:
$$k = \frac{440}{0.25} = 1760$$

**Conclusion:** Higher $N$ (longer signals, padded with zeros, or higher $f_s$) gives finer frequency resolution — critical for accurate pitch detection in HMI applications.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Synthesis Methods

| Method | Computational Cost | Spectral Control | Best for | Real-Time Feasibility |
|--------|-------------------|------------------|----------|----------------------|
| Additive | Very high (many partials) | Excellent (per partial) | Bells, organ, vowels | Yes (with care) |
| Subtractive | Low (1 osc + filter) | Indirect (via filter) | Woodwinds, strings, analog synths | Excellent |
| FM | Very low (2 oscs) | Indirect (via $\beta$, $f_m$) | Bells, brasses, electric piano | Excellent |
| AM | Very low (2 oscs) | Limited | Tremolo, ring mod | Excellent |
| Wavetable | Low (memory lookup) | Pre-stored | Digital synths, pads | Excellent |
| Granular | Medium–High | Per-grain | Textures, time-stretch | Yes (with optimization) |
| Physical Modeling | High | Via physical params | Realistic instruments | Challenging |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Audio Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Sound x(t)] --> B[Anti-Aliasing Filter]
    B --> C[ADC Sampler fs]
    C --> D[Windowing Function w]
    D --> E[FFT / DFT Engine]
    E --> F[Bin Conversion: f kHz and Magnitude vert X k vert]
    F --> G[Spectrogram Display]
    F --> H[Feature Extraction: pitch, MFCC, formants]
    H --> I[HMI Decision Module: classification, recognition]
    style A fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2c5282,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#2c5282,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#2c5282,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#c05621,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#2f855a,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#2f855a,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#2f855a,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#742a2a,color:#ffffff
```

**Explanation:** The pipeline begins with analog sound, passes through a low-pass anti-aliasing filter (must cut above $f_s/2$), gets digitized, windowed (to reduce spectral leakage), and transformed via FFT. The output bins are converted to Hz and decibels, then visualized as a spectrogram or passed to feature extractors that feed the HMI decision system.

    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
